topics.title: Need for Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the "Need" for Hybrid and Electric Vehicles?

From the perspective of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **PCAUT601**), the *need* for Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) and Electric Vehicles (EVs) is fundamentally rooted in three converging global crises that the modern automotive industry is obligated to solve:

1. **Environmental Crisis** – Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, urban air quality degradation, and climate change.
2. **Energy Crisis** – Rapid depletion of finite petroleum reserves and global energy security.
3. **Economic & Regulatory Crisis** – Volatile crude oil prices and tightening emission norms (Euro 6/7, BS-VI, CAFE, Bharat Stage norms).

### 1.2 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that combines a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) with an electric propulsion system (electric motor, battery, and power electronics) to achieve improved fuel economy, reduced emissions, and better overall drivetrain efficiency. An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is one that derives all or part of its propulsion energy from an onboard rechargeable electrical energy storage device, eliminating the use of conventional petroleum fuels either partially (HEV) or completely (BEV).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Engines Working Together" Analogy

Think of a traditional petrol car like **a person who has only one mode of transport — running**. They must run for every trip, whether going to the next room or to the next city. Running is very inefficient for long distances and very polluting (sweat, heavy breathing, exhaustion).

Now think of an **EV** as **someone with a bicycle** — clean, efficient, but limited by battery range.

A **Hybrid Vehicle** is like a **person who can both walk AND ride a bicycle**. For short trips, they walk (electric mode, zero emissions, silent). For long trips, they hop on the cycle (ICE mode, refuels quickly). The genius is that they can **switch between the two intelligently** to get the best of both worlds — much like the hybrid control system in a Toyota Prius or Honda Insight.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAUT601, Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme places this topic as the *foundational base* of the entire course. Questions from this section frequently appear as **Part A (3 marks)** short-answer questions and as the **introductory 7-mark sub-part** of a Part B (14 mark) question. Examiners expect students to demonstrate awareness of emission regulations, ICE limitations, and the comparative analysis between conventional, hybrid, and electric powertrains.

### 1.5 Key Metrics to Remember

- **CO₂ Concentration (pre-industrial):** ~280 ppm
- **CO₂ Concentration (2024):** ~420 ppm
- **ICE Thermal Efficiency:** ~25–35 % (real-world)
- **Electric Motor Efficiency:** ~85–95 %
- **Petrol Energy Density:** ~44 MJ/kg
- **Li-ion Battery Energy Density:** ~0.5–1.0 MJ/kg
- **Petrol-to-Wheel Efficiency:** ~12–20 %
- **Battery-to-Wheel Efficiency:** ~70–80 %

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars Justifying the Need for HEVs/EVs

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Environmental Pollution from Conventional ICE Vehicles

A conventional **Spark Ignition (SI) engine** or **Compression Ignition (CI) engine** burns hydrocarbon fuel, producing the following regulated pollutants:

- **Carbon Monoxide (CO):** Result of incomplete combustion. Toxic to humans (binds with haemoglobin).
- **Hydrocarbons (HC):** Unburnt fuel. Contributes to smog and ozone formation.
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ):** Formed at high combustion temperatures. Causes acid rain and respiratory issues.
- **Particulate Matter (PM):** Especially from diesel — soot particles that penetrate lungs.
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** The principal greenhouse gas driving global warming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A typical petrol car emits approximately **120–200 g of CO₂ per kilometre**. A city with 1 million such cars collectively emits around **150,000–200,000 tonnes of CO₂ per year**.

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Depletion of Fossil Fuel Reserves

Fossil fuels are **finite, non-renewable resources**. Global oil reserves are estimated at:

$$ \text{Reserves-to-Production Ratio (R/P)} = \frac{\text{Remaining Recoverable Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}} $$

The global average R/P ratio is approximately **50 years**, meaning at current consumption rates, proven oil reserves will be severely depleted by the **2070s**.

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Inherent Inefficiencies of the ICE

The **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)** of an ICE is fundamentally limited by the **Carnot cycle constraint**:

$$ \eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}} $$

where $T_{\text{hot}}$ is the peak combustion temperature and $T_{\text{cold}}$ is the ambient temperature. Even with advanced gasoline engines, **real-world tank-to-wheel efficiency rarely exceeds 20 %**.

### 🔹 Pillar 4: Energy Security and Geopolitical Dependence

Nations heavily dependent on imported petroleum face:
- **Currency drain** (foreign exchange outflow)
- **Geopolitical vulnerability** (price shocks, supply disruptions)
- **Trade deficit inflation**

### 🔹 Pillar 5: Stringent Emission Regulations Worldwide

| Regulation | Region | Key Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** | India | Equivalent to Euro 6 |
| **Euro 6 / Euro 7** | European Union | Strict NOₓ and PM limits |
| **CAFE Standards** | USA (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) | Fleet-average fuel economy |
| **China National VI** | China | Comparable to Euro 6 |
| **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate** | California, China | Mandates % of zero-emission sales |

## 2.2 ICE vs. Electric Powertrain — The Fundamental Energy Flow Comparison

| Stage | Conventional ICE (Petrol) | Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Crude Oil (extracted, refined) | Grid Electricity (generation) |
| **Conversion 1** | Oil Well → Refinery (~88 % efficient) | Power Plant (~35–60 % efficient) |
| **Conversion 2** | Refinery → Fuel (distribution) | Grid → Battery Charging (~90 %) |
| **Conversion 3** | Engine Combustion (BTE ~25 %) | Motor Efficiency (~90 %) |
| **Conversion 4** | Transmission & Drivetrain (~85 %) | Transmission & Drivetrain (~90 %) |
| **Wheel Power Output** | Low | High |
| **Overall Well-to-Wheel** | **~12–18 %** | **~25–35 %** (even accounting for coal-based grids) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why are EVs more efficient than ICE vehicles?"*, the correct answer is **NOT** that electric motors are more efficient (although they are), but rather that the **overall well-to-wheel energy conversion chain is far more efficient** for EVs, especially as renewable energy penetration in the grid increases.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}}$ | $P_b$ = Brake Power (kW), $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow (kg/s), CV = Calorific Value (kJ/kg) |
| 2 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | kg/kWh; Lower is better |
| 3 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency (ITE) | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{P_i}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}}$ | $P_i$ = Indicated Power (kW) |
| 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_{ith}} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_i}$ | Typically 80–90 % |
| 5 | CO₂ Emission Rate (Petrol) | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dot{m}_f \times 3.16$ | Approx. 3.16 kg CO₂ per kg petrol burned |
| 6 | Carnot Efficiency Limit | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | $T_C, T_H$ in Kelvin |
| 7 | Energy Density (Gravimetric) | $E_d = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Wh/kg |
| 8 | Range Equation (EV) | $R = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}} \times V_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{req}}/v}$ | km; $C_{\text{bat}}$ = battery capacity |
| 9 | Fuel Economy Conversion | $\text{mpg} = \dfrac{1}{\text{BSFC} \times \text{density} / \text{const}}$ | 1 mpg ≈ 0.425 km/L |
| 10 | Equivalent CO₂ (EV vs ICE) | $\text{CO}_{2,\text{equiv}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}} \times EF_{\text{grid}}}{v}$ | $EF_{\text{grid}}$ = emission factor (kg CO₂/kWh) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles studied in this module directly support:

- **Design Engineering:** Selecting drivetrain architecture (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel HEV).
- **Policy & Standards:** Designing regulations for fleet CO₂ limits (e.g., India's aim for **30 % EV penetration by 2030**).
- **Energy Planning:** Assessing grid readiness for EV charging infrastructure.
- **Life Cycle Analysis (LCA):** Comparing cradle-to-grave environmental impact of ICE vs. EV vs. HEV.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent CO₂ Emission Comparison (ICE vs BEV)

This is a classic **KTU 14-mark Part B derivation** that combines thermodynamic principles with environmental engineering.

### Problem Setup

A mid-size sedan travels **100 km** on a highway.
- **ICE version:** Petrol consumption = 6 L/100 km, petrol density = 0.75 kg/L, calorific value = 44 MJ/kg.
- **BEV version:** Energy consumption = 15 kWh/100 km, grid emission factor (India avg) = 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh.

**Objective:** Compute and compare the **equivalent CO₂ emissions per km** for both vehicles.

---

### Step 1: Calculate the Mass of Fuel Burned (ICE)

For 100 km distance, fuel volume consumed = 6 L.

$$ m_f = V_f \times \rho_f $$

$$ m_f = 6 \text{ L} \times 0.75 \text{ kg/L} = 4.5 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 2: Convert Fuel Mass to CO₂ Emitted (ICE)

Using the stoichiometric relationship: **1 kg of petrol produces ~3.16 kg of CO₂** (this is derived from the carbon content of octane C₈H₁₈ — we will derive this below).

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{ICE}} = m_f \times 3.16 = 4.5 \times 3.16 = 14.22 \text{ kg CO}_2 $$

Per km:

$$ \frac{\text{CO}_{2,\text{ICE}}}{d} = \frac{14.22}{100} = 0.1422 \text{ kg/km} = 142.2 \text{ g/km} $$

### Step 3: Stoichiometric Derivation of the 3.16 Factor

Consider the complete combustion of octane (C₈H₁₈):

$$ 2\,C_8H_{18} + 25\,O_2 \rightarrow 16\,CO_2 + 18\,H_2O $$

Molecular weights:
- C₈H₁₈ = 8(12) + 18(1) = **114 g/mol**
- CO₂ = 12 + 2(16) = **44 g/mol**

Mass of CO₂ produced per kg of fuel:

$$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{16 \times 44}{2 \times 114} = \frac{704}{228} = 3.088 $$

Accounting for the average composition of commercial petrol (mixture of paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics), the empirically measured ratio is **3.15–3.20**, hence the standard factor of **3.16**.

### Step 4: Calculate CO₂ Emitted (BEV)

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{BEV}} = E_{\text{consumed}} \times EF_{\text{grid}} $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{BEV}} = 15 \text{ kWh} \times 0.72 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh} = 10.8 \text{ kg CO}_2 $$

Per km:

$$ \frac{\text{CO}_{2,\text{BEV}}}{d} = \frac{10.8}{100} = 0.108 \text{ kg/km} = 108 \text{ g/km} $$

### Step 5: Compare the Two

$$ \Delta \text{CO}_2 = 0.1422 - 0.108 = 0.0342 \text{ kg/km} $$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$ \text{Reduction} = \frac{0.1422 - 0.108}{0.1422} \times 100\% = 24.04\% $$

### Step 6: Generalised Equation

For a vehicle of fuel economy $F$ (L/100 km) and density $\rho_f$ (kg/L):

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{ICE}} \text{ [g/km]} = F \times \rho_f \times 3.16 \times 10 $$

For an EV with energy consumption $E$ (kWh/100 km):

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{BEV}} \text{ [g/km]} = E \times EF_{\text{grid}} \times 10 $$

The BEV is "cleaner" when:

$$ E \times EF_{\text{grid}} < F \times \rho_f \times 3.16 $$

### Step 7: Real-World Grid Evolution Insight

> [!NOTE]
> If India's grid renewable energy share rises to **50 % by 2030**, the effective $EF_{\text{grid}}$ drops to ~0.4 kg CO₂/kWh. In that scenario, the BEV would emit only **60 g CO₂/km — a 58 % reduction** over the ICE.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Assessment

| Parameter | Conventional ICE | Hybrid (HEV) | Battery Electric (BEV) | Fuel Cell (FCEV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Petrol/Diesel | Petrol + Electricity | Electricity | Hydrogen |
| **Well-to-Wheel η** | 12–18 % | 25–35 % | 25–40 % (renewable grid: 60 %+) | 22–32 % |
| **Tailpipe Emissions** | CO₂, NOₓ, CO, HC, PM | Reduced (~50 % less) | **Zero** | **Zero (only water vapour)** |
| **Refuelling Time** | 3–5 min | 3–5 min | 30 min – 8 hr (charging) | 5–10 min |
| **Range** | 500–1000 km | 500–1000 km | 150–500 km | 400–650 km |
| **Noise (dB at 10 m)** | 70–80 | 60–70 | 50–60 (near silent) | 55–65 |
| **Maintenance** | High | Moderate | **Lowest** | Moderate |
| **Initial Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | Highest |
| **Key Challenge** | Emissions, oil dependency | Complex control | Battery cost, range anxiety | H₂ infrastructure |

## 3.3 Energy Density Argument — Why HEVs Exist (Not Just BEVs)

A frequent KTU question: *"If EVs are cleaner, why do we still need HEVs?"*

The answer lies in **gravimetric and volumetric energy density**:

| Energy Carrier | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | Energy Density (MJ/L) |
|---|---|---|
| Petrol | **44** | **32** |
| Diesel | **45** | **36** |
| Li-ion Battery | **0.5–1.0** | **0.9–1.6** |
| Hydrogen (350 bar) | **120** (mass basis) | **3.5** (volumetric) |
| Hydrogen (700 bar) | **120** (mass basis) | **4.7** (volumetric) |

The energy density of petrol is **~50× higher** than a Li-ion battery. This means an EV must carry a **massive battery pack** to match the range of an ICE vehicle — which adds weight, cost, and environmental burden (battery production).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HEVs bridge the gap:** They carry a small battery (5–10× smaller than BEV) plus an ICE, providing both decent electric range and unlimited liquid-fuel range. This is the engineering compromise that makes HEVs the **transitional technology** between ICE and full BEV adoption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

        H1[Fuel Tank] --> H2[ICE Engine]

    end

    end

```mermaid

    subgraph ICE_Chain [ICE Well-to-Wheel Chain]
        A1[Crude Oil] --> A2[Refining 88 percent]
        A2 --> A3[Transport and Storage 98 percent]

        A4 --> A5[Drivetrain 85 percent]
        A5 --> A6[Wheels 12 to 18 percent overall]
    end

        B1[Power Plant Fuel] --> B2[Electricity Generation 40 percent]
        B2 --> B3[Grid Transmission 90 percent]

        B4 --> B5[Motor and Inverter 90 percent]
        B5 --> B6[Drivetrain 90 percent]
        B6 --> B7[Wheels 25 to 35 percent overall]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Hybrid Electric Vehicle". List the three main components that distinguish an HEV from a conventional ICE vehicle. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q1]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that uses **two or more distinct energy sources** for propulsion, typically combining a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) with one or more **electric machines** (motor/generator) and an **energy storage system** (battery or ultracapacitor).

The three main distinguishing components are:

1. **Electric Motor/Generator** – Provides additional traction power and can regenerate energy during braking.
2. **Energy Storage System (Battery/Ultracapacitor)** – Stores electrical energy for the electric motor.
3. **Power Electronics Converter (Inverter/Converter)** – Manages bidirectional power flow between the battery, motor, and ICE.

---

### Question 2: State any three environmental disadvantages of conventional Internal Combustion Engine vehicles that motivate the shift to hybrid/electric technology. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q2]**

**Model Answer:**

The three major environmental disadvantages are:

1. **Greenhouse Gas (CO₂) Emissions:** ICE vehicles emit 120–200 g of CO₂ per km, contributing directly to global warming and climate change.
2. **Toxic Tailpipe Pollutants:** Release of CO, NOₓ, unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), and particulate matter (PM), which cause urban smog, respiratory diseases, and acid rain.
3. **Inefficient Energy Utilisation:** Only 12–20 % of the fuel's chemical energy reaches the wheels, meaning 80–88 % is wasted as heat, contributing to overall environmental burden.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q3]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **five major reasons** for the need of hybrid and electric vehicles in the modern automotive industry. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A petrol car consumes **7 L/100 km**. Density of petrol = 0.74 kg/L. Calculate the **CO₂ emission per km**. If the same car is replaced by a BEV consuming **16 kWh/100 km** and the grid emission factor is **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**, compute the **percentage reduction in CO₂ emission**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

The five major reasons are:

1. **Environmental Protection:** Conventional ICE vehicles emit greenhouse gases (CO₂) and harmful pollutants (CO, NOₓ, HC, PM). Transportation contributes ~24 % of global CO₂ emissions. HEVs/EVs reduce these significantly.

2. **Fossil Fuel Depletion:** Global oil reserves have a Reserves-to-Production (R/P) ratio of ~50 years. EVs powered by electricity from renewable sources offer a sustainable alternative.

3. **Energy Security:** Importing nations face currency drain and supply vulnerability. EVs reduce dependence on imported crude oil.

4. **Improving ICE Efficiency:** The Carnot cycle limits ICE efficiency to ~30–35 %, with real-world well-to-wheel efficiency of only 12–18 %. Electric drivetrains achieve 70–90 % efficiency.

5. **Stringent Emission Norms:** Regulations like BS-VI, Euro 7, and CAFE standards make it increasingly difficult (and expensive) for ICE manufacturers to comply, pushing them toward electrification.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Mass of petrol per km**

$$ m_f = 7 \text{ L} \times 0.74 \text{ kg/L} = 5.18 \text{ kg per 100 km} $$

Per km:

$$ m_f = 0.0518 \text{ kg/km} $$

**Step 2: CO₂ from ICE**

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{ICE}} = 0.0518 \times 3.16 = 0.1637 \text{ kg/km} = 163.7 \text{ g/km} $$

**Step 3: CO₂ from BEV**

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{BEV}} = 16 \text{ kWh} \times 0.82 \text{ kg/kWh} / 100 = 0.1312 \text{ kg/km} = 131.2 \text{ g/km} $$

**Step 4: Percentage Reduction**

$$ \%\text{ Reduction} = \frac{163.7 - 131.2}{163.7} \times 100\% = 19.85\% $$

**Final Answer:** ICE: **163.7 g CO₂/km**, BEV: **131.2 g CO₂/km**, Reduction: **~19.85 %**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q4]**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a **well-to-wheel energy flow analysis**, prove that battery electric vehicles are more energy-efficient than conventional ICE vehicles even when electricity is generated from coal-based thermal power plants. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Compare **HEV, BEV, and FCEV** on the basis of energy source, well-to-wheel efficiency, refuelling time, range, and tailpipe emissions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Conventional ICE Well-to-Wheel Chain:**

- Oil extraction → Refining (η₁ = 0.88)
- Fuel transport → Storage (η₂ = 0.98)
- Engine (η₃ = 0.25)
- Drivetrain (η₄ = 0.85)

$$ \eta_{\text{ICE,overall}} = 0.88 \times 0.98 \times 0.25 \times 0.85 = 0.183 \approx 18.3\% $$

**BEV Well-to-Wheel Chain (with coal-based grid):**

- Coal mining → Thermal power plant (η₁ = 0.38)
- Grid transmission & distribution (η₂ = 0.90)
- Battery charging (η₃ = 0.92)
- Motor + Inverter (η₄ = 0.90)
- Drivetrain (η₅ = 0.92)

$$ \eta_{\text{BEV,overall}} = 0.38 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 = 0.261 \approx 26.1\% $$

**Conclusion:** Even with a coal-based grid (lowest efficiency source), the BEV achieves ~26.1 % well-to-wheel efficiency compared to ~18.3 % for the ICE — a **~43 % relative improvement**.

If the grid is renewable (solar/wind → 100 % effectively), the BEV efficiency approaches **~70 %**, far exceeding any ICE variant.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

| Parameter | HEV | BEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Petrol + Battery | Battery (Grid) | Hydrogen (H₂) |
| **Well-to-Wheel η** | 25–35 % | 25–40 % (renewable: 60 %+) | 22–32 % |
| **Refuelling Time** | 3–5 min | 30 min – 8 hr | 5–10 min |
| **Range** | 500–1000 km | 150–500 km | 400–650 km |
| **Tailpipe Emissions** | Reduced (NO CO₂ idle) | **Zero** | **Zero (water only)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Do not confuse "tailpipe emissions" with "well-to-wheel emissions".** A BEV has **zero tailpipe emissions**, but its *well-to-wheel* CO₂ depends on the grid mix. Examiners will deduct marks if you claim "BEVs have zero CO₂ emissions" without qualifying the statement.
> 
> 2. **Energy density units:** Energy density is sometimes expressed in **Wh/kg** and sometimes in **MJ/kg**. Conversion factor: **1 Wh = 3.6 kJ = 0.0036 MJ**. Writing the wrong unit will cost you 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Carnot vs real efficiency:** The ICE theoretical maximum is ~30–35 % (Otto cycle), not 100 %. Do not write that ICEs are "100 % efficient at certain RPMs" — that is the Carnot limit applied to a **heat engine** and even that is never 100 %.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the 3.16 stoichiometric factor:** When CO₂ problems are asked, students often forget the 3.16 kg CO₂/kg fuel factor. Always state it explicitly in the derivation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Consolidation for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 1, Topic 1**

✅ The **need** for HEVs/EVs arises from three crises: **Environmental, Energy, and Regulatory**.

✅ Conventional ICE vehicles emit **CO, CO₂, HC, NOₓ, PM** — all regulated pollutants.

✅ ICE well-to-wheel efficiency = **12–18 %**; BEV well-to-wheel efficiency = **25–40 %** (higher with renewables).

✅ **Carnot efficiency** sets the thermodynamic ceiling for any heat engine: $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$.

✅ **Energy density of petrol (~44 MJ/kg) is ~50× that of Li-ion battery (~1 MJ/kg)** — this is why HEVs are transitional, not BEVs.

✅ **CO₂ emission factor for petrol** = **3.16 kg CO₂ per kg fuel** (derived from octane C₈H₁₈ stoichiometry).

✅ **Emission regulations:** India → BS-VI, Europe → Euro 6/7, USA → CAFE & ZEV mandate.

✅ **Level of Vehicle Electrification:** Micro-Hybrid → Mild → Full HEV → PHEV → BEV → FCEV.

✅ **Key HEV components:** ICE + Electric Motor/Generator + Battery + Power Electronics + Transmission.

✅ **Grid emission factor** (India avg) ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** — this directly determines BEV's well-to-wheel CO₂.

✅ **Hybridization factor** = Ratio of electric motor power to total powertrain power — higher value = greater electrification.

✅ **KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System)** is a critical HEV feature that converts braking energy into electrical energy, improving urban fuel economy by 20–30 %.

✅ **For the derivation question**, always show the **stoichiometric calculation** of the 3.16 factor — KTU examiners award marks for the step-by-step justification.

✅ **Memorise the comparison table** (ICE vs HEV vs BEV vs FCEV) — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Hybrid & Fuel Cell Vehicle Components

A **Hybrid Vehicle (HV)** is a vehicle that combines two or more distinct power sources — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EM)** — to propel the vehicle, while a **Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV)** uses a **Hydrogen Fuel Cell Stack** as its primary energy converter, supplemented by electric energy storage systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAUT601, Module 1):**
> "Components of Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV) and Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCV) — including the prime mover (ICE / Fuel Cell Stack), energy storage system (Battery / Ultra-capacitor), electric propulsion system, power electronic converters, and energy management unit."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen preparing meals (vehicle moving):
> - The **ICE** is the **gas stove** — fast, powerful, but always running at high temperature.
> - The **Electric Motor** is the **microwave oven** — quiet, instant, but needs stored electricity.
> - The **Battery** is the **refrigerator** — stores energy (food) for later use.
> - The **Fuel Cell Stack** is the **solar-powered steamer** — clean, no fumes, but needs a constant fuel (hydrogen) supply.
> - The **ECU/Hybrid Controller** is the **head chef** — decides when to use the stove, microwave, or steamer for maximum efficiency.
>
> Just as a smart chef mixes cooking methods to save fuel and time, a hybrid controller orchestrates the ICE and electric motor to minimize fuel consumption and emissions.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**
> - Lower Heating Value of Hydrogen: **$LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$**
> - Energy density of gasoline: **$\approx 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$**
> - Energy density of Li-ion battery: **$\approx 0.5 - 1.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$**
> - Faraday's constant: **$F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Vehicle Components

The architecture of a hybrid or fuel cell vehicle can be broadly classified into **five functional sub-systems**:

| S.No | Sub-system | Primary Function | Example Components |
|------|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Energy Source** | Stores or generates the primary fuel | Fuel tank, H₂ tank, Fuel Cell Stack |
| 2 | **Energy Storage** | Stores electrical energy | Battery pack, Ultra-capacitor, Flywheel |
| 3 | **Power Conversion** | Converts energy between forms | ICE, Electric Motor/Generator, Fuel Cell |
| 4 | **Power Electronics** | Controls power flow | Inverter, DC-DC Converter, Rectifier |
| 5 | **Control & Auxiliaries** | Manages & supports operation | ECU, BMS, Cooling, Braking |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)

The **ICE** is the conventional heat engine that converts chemical energy of fuel (gasoline/diesel) into mechanical rotational energy via combustion.

**Operating Principle (Otto Cycle for SI engines):**
- **Intake → Compression → Combustion (Power) → Exhaust**
- Thermal efficiency limited by the **Otto efficiency formula** (see formula sheet).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In hybrids, the ICE is often operated near its **Maximum Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** point (~30–35% efficiency), since the electric motor handles transient loads and idling losses.

### 2.2 Component 2 — Electric Motor / Generator (M/G)

Acts as a **motor** during traction and as a **generator** during regenerative braking. Most hybrids use **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)** or **Induction Motors (IM)**.

**Key Equations:**
- Electromagnetic torque: $T_{em} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q$
- Back-EMF: $E = K_e \cdot \omega$
- Motor input power: $P_{in} = V \cdot I$

### 2.3 Component 3 — Battery Pack (Energy Storage System)

Stores electrical energy electrochemically. **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)** is the industry standard in modern HEVs/FCVs.

- **State of Charge (SOC):** $\text{SOC} = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$
- **State of Health (SOH):** Indicates battery degradation over time.

### 2.4 Component 4 — Fuel Cell Stack

A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** stack produces electricity by electrochemically combining H₂ and O₂. Each cell produces ~**0.7 V**; multiple cells are stacked in series to obtain usable voltage (e.g., 300–400 V).

**Nernst Equation (ideal cell voltage):**

$$E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

### 2.5 Component 5 — Power Electronics

- **Inverter:** DC (battery/fuel cell) → AC (motor)
- **DC-DC Converter:** Steps up/down voltage between sub-systems
- **Rectifier:** AC → DC (regenerative charging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** Modern Toyota Prius uses a **boost converter** that steps up battery voltage (201.6 V) to ~500 V DC bus for the inverter, enabling smaller, efficient motor operation.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_{em} = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m I_q$ | Synchronous motor torque | Nm |
| 3 | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF of motor | V |
| 4 | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau) d\tau$ | Coulomb-counting SOC | % |
| 5 | $E_{Nernst} = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298) + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ | Fuel cell open-circuit voltage | V |
| 6 | $P_{stack} = n \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I_{cell}$ | Fuel cell stack power | W |
| 7 | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ (Buck) | Buck converter output | V |
| 8 | $V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ (Boost) | Boost converter output | V |
| 9 | $E_{battery} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | Battery terminal voltage | V |
| 10 | $P_{regen} = \eta_{gen} \cdot T_{brake} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Regenerative braking power | W |
| 11 | $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Ultra-capacitor energy | J |
| 12 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$ | Drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\omega_{syn} = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous motor speed | RPM |
| 14 | $P_{H_2} = \frac{V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack}}{\eta_{FC} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$ | Hydrogen consumption rate | kg/s |
| 15 | $F_{trac} = m \cdot g \cdot f_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot a$ | Vehicle tractive force | N |

**Where:**
- $r$ = Compression ratio, $\gamma$ = Specific heat ratio
- $p$ = Number of pole pairs, $\psi_m$ = Flux linkage
- $D$ = Duty cycle, $C_d$ = Drag coefficient, $f_r$ = Rolling resistance coefficient
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = Cell temperature (K)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency of ICE

The ICE in a hybrid vehicle is a 4-stroke spark-ignition engine operating on the **Otto cycle** (idealized).

**Starting Point — First Law for Adiabatic Compression (Process 1→2):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1: Apply the isentropic relation** for an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic compression from state 1 to state 2:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Where $r = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$ is the **compression ratio**.

**Step 2: Similarly for isentropic expansion** (Process 3→4):

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 3: Write the net work output** of the cycle:

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) - m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 4: Substitute** $T_4$ from Step 2 into the $Q_{out}$ term:

$$Q_{out} = m C_v \left(\frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}} - T_1\right) = \frac{m C_v}{r^{\gamma - 1}}(T_3 - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1})$$

**Step 5: Simplify using** $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$:

$$Q_{out} = \frac{m C_v}{r^{\gamma - 1}}(T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 6: Compute the thermal efficiency** $\eta = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$:

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Higher compression ratio → higher efficiency. For $r = 10$ and $\gamma = 1.4$: $\eta_{otto} \approx 60\%$. Real engines achieve **25–35%** due to mechanical, heat, and pumping losses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fuel Cell Stack Power Output

**Given:** A PEMFC stack with $n = 200$ cells, each with active area $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$, current density $j = 0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$, average cell voltage $V_{cell} = 0.65 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1: Compute current per cell:**

$$I_{cell} = j \cdot A = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute power per cell:**

$$P_{cell} = V_{cell} \times I_{cell} = 0.65 \times 50 = 32.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Compute total stack power (cells in series, current identical):**

$$P_{stack} = n \times P_{cell} = 200 \times 32.5 = 6500 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = 6.5 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4: Compute hydrogen consumption rate** (assuming $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $\eta_{FC} = 50\%$):

$$P_{H_2} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\eta_{FC}} = \frac{6500}{0.50} = 13000 \text{ W}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P_{H_2}}{LHV_{H_2}} = \frac{13000}{120 \times 10^6} = 1.083 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 0.39 \text{ kg/hour}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boost Converter DC-Link Voltage (Hybrid Powertrain)

**Given:** A Toyota Prius-like boost converter. Input voltage from battery $V_{in} = 201.6 \text{ V}$, duty cycle $D = 0.6$.

**Step 1: Apply the boost converter transfer ratio** (ideal, continuous conduction mode):

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{201.6}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{201.6}{0.4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 504 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3: Verify — Inductor volt-second balance:**
Average inductor voltage over one switching period is zero, confirming steady-state operation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Vehicle Components

| Component | Specific Energy | Specific Power | Efficiency | Life Cycle | Cost |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|------------|------------|------|
| **ICE (Gasoline)** | 1200 Wh/kg (fuel) | 800 W/kg | 25–35% | 5000 hr | Low |
| **Li-ion Battery** | 150–250 Wh/kg | 250–1000 W/kg | 85–95% | 1000–3000 cycles | High |
| **Ultra-capacitor** | 5–10 Wh/kg | 5000–10000 W/kg | 90–95% | >500,000 cycles | Moderate |
| **PEM Fuel Cell** | 800–1000 Wh/kg (H₂) | 300–500 W/kg | 40–60% | 2000–5000 hr | Very High |
| **PMSM Motor** | — | 3000–5000 W/kg | 90–97% | >10⁶ cycles | High |
| **H₂ Tank (700 bar)** | 1420 Wh/kg (system) | — | — | 15 yr | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine]
    B --> C[Transmission / Power Split Device]
    C --> D[Drive Wheels]
    E[Battery Pack] --> F[Power Electronics Inverter]
    F --> G[Electric Motor Generator]
    G --> C
    G --> E
    H[Vehicle Control Unit ECU] --> B
    H --> G
    H --> F
    H --> E
    I[DC DC Converter] --> E
    F --> I
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#f5b041,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#7dcea0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **ECU (H)** acts as the central brain. It commands the **ICE (B)** and **Electric Motor (G)** based on driver demand, SOC, and operating conditions. The **Power Split Device (C)** is the mechanical equivalent of a continuously variable transmission used in Toyota's THS-II system.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[H2 Storage Tank 700 bar] --> A2[Pressure Regulator]
    A2 --> A3[Anode Humidifier]
    A3 --> A4[PEMFC Stack]
    B1[Air Filter] --> B2[Air Compressor]
    B2 --> B3[Cathode Humidifier]
    B3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[High Voltage DC Bus]
    A5 --> A6[DC DC Converter]
    A6 --> A7[Energy Storage Battery]
    A5 --> A8[Three Phase Inverter]
    A8 --> A9[Electric Traction Motor]
    A9 --> A10[Transmission and Wheels]
    A4 --> A11[Thermal Management Water Cooling]
    A11 --> A4
    style A4 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style A9 fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A7 fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A11 fill:#a8d8ea,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a battery EV, the **fuel cell is NOT recharged**. Instead, the H₂ tank is refueled in ~5 minutes. The **battery (A7)** handles transient loads (acceleration/regen) since the fuel cell has slow dynamic response.

---

## 4.3 Component Interaction Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EnergySupply[Energy Source Subsystem]
        ES1[ICE]
        ES2[Fuel Cell Stack]
    end
    subgraph Storage[Energy Storage Subsystem]
        ST1[Battery Pack]
        ST2[Ultracapacitor]
    end
    subgraph Conversion[Power Conversion]
        PC1[Electric Motor]
        PC2[Generator]
    end
    subgraph Electronics[Power Electronics]
        PE1[Inverter]
        PE2[DC-DC Converter]
    end
    subgraph Control[Control Unit]
        CU1[Hybrid ECU]
        CU2[Battery Management System]
    end
    ES1 --> CU1
    ES2 --> CU1
    ST1 --> PE1
    ST2 --> PE1
    PE1 --> PC1
    PC1 --> PC2
    PC2 --> PE2
    PE2 --> ST1
    CU1 --> PE1
    CU1 --> PE2
    CU1 --> ES1
    CU2 --> ST1
    style CU1 fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PE1 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ST1 fill:#7dcea0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 State-of-Charge (SOC) Operating Window for Battery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low SOC 20 percent] -->|Charge from ICE FC| B[Normal Range 20 to 80 percent]
    B -->|Discharge to Motor| C[Driving Mode]
    B -->|Regen Braking Charge| D[High SOC 80 percent]
    D -->|Stop Charging| B
    style A fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style B fill:#27ae60,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style D fill:#f39c12,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The battery SOC is deliberately maintained between **20%–80%** to maximize cycle life. Going outside this window accelerates degradation. The **BMS (Battery Management System)** enforces these limits in software.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle. List its main components.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that uses two or more distinct energy sources for propulsion, typically a combination of an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and an **Electric Motor** powered by a battery.

**Main Components (4 marks allocation):**
1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — primary mechanical power source.
2. **Electric Motor/Generator** — secondary traction and regenerative braking.
3. **Energy Storage System** — Battery pack (and sometimes ultra-capacitor).
4. **Power Electronic Converters** — Inverter, DC-DC converter.
5. **Transmission / Power Split Device** — mechanical power coupling.
6. **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU/ECU)** — supervisory energy management.

---

### **Q2. State Faraday's Law as applied to a PEM Fuel Cell.**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Faraday's Law states that the amount of substance produced at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

In a **PEM Fuel Cell**, the cell current is related to the hydrogen consumption rate by:

$$I = n \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot 2F$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of cells in stack
- $\dot{n}_{H_2}$ = molar flow rate of H₂ (mol/s)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant)
- **2** electrons per H₂ molecule (oxidation reaction: H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a **series hybrid electric vehicle** and the flow of power between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine has a **compression ratio of 9:1** and operates on the Otto cycle. Calculate the **ideal air-standard thermal efficiency**. Assume $\gamma = 1.4$. Compare it with the actual efficiency of **30%** and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Components of a Series HEV:**
1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — drives a generator only; **no mechanical connection to wheels**.
2. **Generator** — converts mechanical power from ICE into electrical power.
3. **Battery Pack** — stores excess electrical energy; supplies during peak demand.
4. **Power Inverter** — converts DC (battery/generator) → AC for motor.
5. **Traction Electric Motor** — sole source of mechanical power to wheels.
6. **Transmission (single-speed)** — couples motor to wheels.
7. **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** — supervises ICE on/off, generator output, battery SOC.

**Power Flow Path:**
ICE → Generator → Rectifier → DC Bus ⇄ Battery ⇄ Inverter → Motor → Wheels

**Key Advantage:** ICE can always run at its **best BSFC point** since it is decoupled from wheel load. (1 mark)

**Key Disadvantage:** All mechanical energy undergoes **multiple conversions** (mech → elec → mech), incurring losses; therefore overall efficiency is lower than parallel hybrid unless well-managed. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Compression ratio, $r = 9$
- $\gamma = 1.4$ (air)

**Step 1: Apply the Otto cycle efficiency formula:**

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the exponent** $\gamma - 1 = 1.4 - 1 = 0.4$

**Step 3: Compute** $r^{0.4} = 9^{0.4}$

Using logarithm: $\log_{10}(9) = 0.9542$, hence $\log_{10}(9^{0.4}) = 0.9542 \times 0.4 = 0.3817$

$$9^{0.4} = 10^{0.3817} = 2.408$$

**Step 4: Substitute:**

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} = 1 - 0.4153$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 0.5847 \approx 58.47\%}$$

**Step 5: Compare with actual efficiency (30%):**

$$\text{Efficiency gap} = 58.47 - 30 = 28.47\%$$

**Comment:**
The actual efficiency (30%) is significantly lower than the ideal Otto efficiency (58.47%) due to:
- Heat losses through cylinder walls
- Pumping losses during gas exchange
- Incomplete combustion
- Mechanical friction in moving parts
- Time losses (finite combustion duration)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often forget that the **Otto cycle is the IDEAL** (air-standard) cycle. Do NOT confuse it with the **actual brake thermal efficiency (BTE)**, which is typically 25–35% for a real SI engine. If the question says "ideal," use $\eta_{otto}$; if it says "actual," use experimental data. **Losing 2 marks** for this confusion is very common.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** with a neat sketch. List its **four major components** used in a fuel cell vehicle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel cell vehicle uses a **PEMFC stack of 250 cells**, each with active area $150 \text{ cm}^2$, operating at current density $0.8 \text{ A/cm}^2$ and average cell voltage $0.6 \text{ V}$. Calculate: **(i)** total current drawn, **(ii)** total stack power output, **(iii)** hydrogen consumption rate per hour (assume $\eta_{FC} = 50\%$, $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Construction of PEMFC:**

A single PEMFC consists of the following layers (from anode to cathode):

1. **Anode flow field plate** — distributes H₂ uniformly.
2. **Anode gas diffusion layer (GDL)** — allows gas to reach catalyst.
3. **Anode catalyst layer (Pt)** — site of H₂ oxidation reaction (HOR).
4. **Proton Exchange Membrane (Nafion™)** — conducts H⁺ ions, blocks electrons.
5. **Cathode catalyst layer (Pt)** — site of O₂ reduction (ORR).
6. **Cathode gas diffusion layer (GDL)** — allows O₂ to reach catalyst.
7. **Cathode flow field plate** — distributes air/O₂; also collects current.

**Working Principle:**

**At Anode:** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$

**At Cathode:** $\frac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$

**Overall Reaction:** $H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$

H⁺ ions migrate through the membrane to the cathode; electrons flow through the external circuit producing usable current.

**Four Major Components in a Fuel Cell Vehicle:**
1. **Hydrogen Storage Tank** (high-pressure 350 or 700 bar type IV composite)
2. **PEMFC Stack** (series-connected cells)
3. **DC-DC Boost Converter** (matches stack voltage to motor inverter)
4. **Auxiliary BoP** (Balance of Plant): Air compressor, humidifier, thermal management.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- $n = 250$ cells
- $A = 150 \text{ cm}^2$
- $j = 0.8 \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $V_{cell} = 0.6 \text{ V}$
- $\eta_{FC} = 50\%$
- $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 120 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$

**(i) Total Current Drawn:**

Since all cells in a stack are in **series**, the stack current equals the cell current.

$$I_{stack} = j \times A = 0.8 \times 150 = 120 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{stack} = 120 \text{ A}}$$

**(ii) Total Stack Power:**

In a series stack, voltages add while current is constant.

$$V_{stack} = n \times V_{cell} = 250 \times 0.6 = 150 \text{ V}$$

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I_{stack} = 150 \times 120 = 18{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = 18 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Hydrogen Consumption Rate per Hour:**

**Step 1: Compute chemical power input from H₂ (accounting for efficiency):**

$$P_{H_2} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\eta_{FC}} = \frac{18{,}000}{0.50} = 36{,}000 \text{ W} = 36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Compute mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P_{H_2}}{LHV_{H_2}} = \frac{36{,}000}{120 \times 10^6} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to kg/hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1.08 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.08 \text{ kg/hour}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion:** In a fuel cell **stack**, cells are in **series** for higher voltage. Current is the same; voltage adds. Many students wrongly multiply current by n. (–2 marks)
> 2. **LHV vs. HHV:** Use **LHV** for automotive fuel cell problems (water leaves as vapor). Using HHV gives 142 MJ/kg and is incorrect. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Units:** Always convert $\text{MJ/kg}$ to $\text{J/kg}$ when mixing with Watts (W = J/s). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Efficiency Direction:** If $\eta_{FC} = 50\%$, then the chemical power input is **2×** the electrical output. Getting this backwards loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Components**

### ✅ Core Components Recap

- **HEV = ICE + Electric Motor + Battery + Power Electronics + ECU**
- **FCV = H₂ Tank + Fuel Cell Stack + Battery + Motor + Power Electronics + BoP**
- **ICE** is a heat engine; efficiency limited by Otto cycle: $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$
- **Electric Motor** is reversible (motor ↔ generator); PMSM and IM most common
- **Battery** stores DC electrical energy; governed by SOC, SOH; uses Coulomb counting
- **Fuel Cell** produces DC from H₂ + O₂; governed by Nernst equation; ~0.6–0.7 V per cell
- **Power Electronics**: Inverter (DC→AC), DC-DC (voltage step), Rectifier (AC→DC)
- **Ultra-capacitor** is used for **transient peak loads** (regen braking, acceleration boost)
- **Regenerative Braking** converts kinetic energy to electrical energy via motor-as-generator
- **Hybrid Controller** is the brain — implements energy management strategy (EMS)

### ✅ Key Numerical Values to Memorize

- $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg
- Compression ratio of typical SI engine: **8:1 to 12:1**
- Typical HEV battery voltage: **200–400 V** (Prius: 201.6 V)
- Typical FCV stack voltage: **300–650 V**
- H₂ storage pressure: **350 bar (Type IV) or 700 bar**
- Fuel cell operating temperature: **60–80 °C** (PEMFC)

### ✅ Two-Mark Definition Keywords

- "**Hybridization**" = combining two or more power sources
- "**Degree of Hybridization**" = ratio of electric power to total power
- "**BoP**" = Balance of Plant (auxiliaries supporting the fuel cell)
- "**SOC window**" = allowable range of battery charge (usually 20–80%)
- "**Regenerative Braking**" = recovery of kinetic energy as electricity

### ✅ Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns

1. **Block diagram + power flow** explanation (7 marks) + numerical on efficiency (7 marks)
2. **Construction & working** of a specific component (7 marks) + numerical (7 marks)
3. **Comparison** of HEV vs FCV or series vs parallel (7 marks) + SOC calculation (7 marks)

### ✅ Diagrams to Practice Drawing

1. Series / Parallel / Series-Parallel HEV layout
2. PEMFC single-cell cross-section
3. Boost converter circuit
4. SOC management flowchart
5. FCV powertrain with BoP

> [!TIP]
> **💡 Last-Minute Tip:** In the KTU exam, always **state assumptions explicitly** before solving any numerical (e.g., "Assuming ideal Otto cycle, $\gamma = 1.4$"). Examiners reward clearly laid-out solutions with step numbering. Avoid skipping algebraic steps; even if the answer is correct, **process marks** are awarded for visible intermediate work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PCAUT601 - Hybrid and Fuel Cell Vehicles (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1)**, the topic *"Advantages and Disadvantages"* refers to a systematic **comparative techno-economic analysis** of three alternative automotive powertrain architectures — **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** — benchmarked against the **Conventional Internal Combustion Engine Vehicle (ICEV)**. This study evaluates each powertrain on the basis of energy efficiency, tailpipe emissions, operational cost, range, refueling/charging time, lifecycle impact, and infrastructure readiness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** The advantages and disadvantages are to be studied in the context of the **need for alternative powertrains** due to depleting fossil fuel reserves, rising **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** emissions, stringent **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms, and the global **Paris Agreement** carbon neutrality targets. The three powertrain families formally defined by KTU are: - **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):** A vehicle that combines an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** with one or more **Electric Machines (EM)** and an **Energy Storage System (ESS)** — typically a battery — to achieve improved fuel economy and reduced emissions. - **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):** A vehicle powered exclusively by an **Electric Machine** using energy stored in a rechargeable **Traction Battery Pack**, with no ICE onboard. - **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV):** A vehicle that generates onboard electrical energy from a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** supplied with **compressed hydrogen (H₂ at 350 bar or 700 bar)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three-Bottle Water Problem Imagine you are thirsty in a desert and you have **three bottles** of water available: 1. **Bottle A (HEV):** A mix of cold water and warm water in one bottle — gives you *some* cooling, but the bottle is heavier and more complex to manufacture. 2. **Bottle B (BEV):** A bottle of pure cold water, but you must **travel to a refilling station** and **wait for it to chill** — limited range, long recharge. 3. **Bottle C (FCEV):** A magic bottle that produces cold water from a fuel cartridge — **instant refill**, **long range**, but the cartridge factory is rare and **expensive**. The **conventional ICE vehicle (Bottle D)** is your normal water bottle — cheap, easy to refill anywhere, but produces "smoke" (emissions) and gets emptied quickly. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** Every powertrain is a **trade-off triangle** between three vertices: **Cost**, **Range/Refueling Time**, and **Emissions/Environmental Impact**. No single technology dominates all three vertices simultaneously. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used in Comparison The following KTU-board-standard metrics are used to objectively compare the four powertrain types: - **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) Efficiency (η_TTW):** Energy delivered to the wheels ÷ Energy in the fuel/battery. - **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency (η_WTW):** Energy at wheels ÷ Primary energy at the well/source. This is the **gold standard** metric for KTU numericals. - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy stored per unit mass of the energy carrier. - **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy stored per unit volume. - **Tailpipe CO₂ Emissions (g CO₂/km):** Direct exhaust emissions. - **Bharat Stage VI Emission Limit:** **NOx ≤ 80 mg/km** (Petrol), **NOx ≤ 460 mg/km** (Diesel, WHTC). > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** "**W**ell-**T**o-**W**heel = **W**hole **T**rip **W**itness" — from crude oil extraction (well) to rolling tyres (wheel). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Advantages of Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) An HEV intelligently blends ICE and EM operation. The benefits arise from **five core engineering principles**: 1. **Engine Downsizing & Right-Sizing:** The ICE is selected for *average* power demand (not peak). The EM supplies transient peak power, allowing a smaller, more efficient engine. Example: A 2.0 L ICE in a conventional car may be replaced by a 1.4 L Atkinson-cycle engine + 50 kW EM. 2. **Regenerative Braking (Regen):** Kinetic energy otherwise wasted as heat in friction brakes is recovered by operating the EM as a generator. Typical regen efficiency: **60 % to 70 %** of the kinetic energy. 3. **Engine Stop-Start:** The ICE shuts off at traffic signals (idle stop) and restarts seamlessly via the EM. Saves **5 % to 10 %** fuel in urban drive cycles. 4. **Operating Point Optimisation:** The EM allows the ICE to operate near its **Best Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** island (~ 2.2 bar BMEP @ 2000–2500 rpm for gasoline). 5. **Torque-Fill During Gearshifts:** The EM provides torque during transmission shifts, eliminating torque interruption and improving drivability. > [!NOTE] > **Key Result:** A typical **Toyota Prius-style full HEV** delivers **40 % to 50 %** improvement in urban fuel economy over a comparable conventional ICE vehicle. ## 2.2 Disadvantages of Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) - **Cost Penalty:** Two powertrains (ICE + EM + battery + power electronics) increase BOM cost by **₹1.5 to 3 Lakh** (2024 Indian market estimate). - **Complexity:** Requires **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)**, **Battery Management System (BMS)**, and high-voltage (200–600 V) safety systems. - **Weight:** Battery pack adds **30–80 kg**; partially offset by engine downsizing. - **Battery Degradation:** Ni-MH or Li-ion packs lose **2 % to 3 %** capacity per year. - **No Plug-in Flexibility (in full HEV):** Cannot be externally charged; depends entirely on the ICE. ## 2.3 Advantages of Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) 1. **Zero Tailpipe Emissions:** Important for **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)** zones in Delhi/Mumbai. 2. **High Energy Conversion Efficiency:** Electric machines achieve **90 % to 95 %** peak efficiency vs. ICE at **30 % to 40 %** peak. 3. **Low Operating Cost:** Electricity cost in India ≈ **₹1.0/km** vs. petrol at **₹6.0/km** (2024, Tata Nexon EV vs. Nexon Petrol). 4. **Low Maintenance:** No oil change, no spark plugs, no timing belt, no clutch. 5. **Silent Operation:** NVH levels drop to **< 60 dB** cabin noise at 60 km/h. 6. **Instant Torque:** Full peak torque available from **0 rpm** → superior 0–100 km/h acceleration. ## 2.4 Disadvantages of Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) - **Range Anxiety:** Typical Indian EV range = **200–450 km** per charge. - **Long Charging Time:** Even DC fast charging (50 kW) takes **60 min** for 0–80 % SOC. - **Battery Cost:** Li-ion pack costs **\$120–150/kWh** (2024 BNEF) → **40–50 %** of vehicle cost. - **Charging Infrastructure:** India had ~ **12,000 public chargers** (2024) vs. **80,000+** fuel stations. - **Battery Thermal Runaway Risk:** Requires sophisticated BMS and liquid cooling. - **Cold Weather Penalty:** Range drops **20 % to 40 %** below 0 °C. ## 2.5 Advantages of Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) 1. **Only Water Vapour at the Tailpipe:** Truly zero-emission at point of use. 2. **High Specific Energy of Hydrogen:** **H₂ LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — nearly **3×** gasoline. 3. **Fast Refuelling:** 3–5 minutes, comparable to petrol. 4. **Long Range:** Toyota Mirai = **650 km**; Hyundai Nexo = **666 km**. 5. **Cold-Start Capable:** Modern PEMFC starts at **−30 °C** (Toyota 2nd-gen stack). 6. **Scalable Architecture:** Stack power scales linearly by adding cells. ## 2.6 Disadvantages of Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) - **High Platinum Loading:** Cost = **\$50–80/g** Pt; 80 kW stack needs **~30 g Pt** (2024 DOE target). - **Hydrogen Production:** **95 %** of global H₂ is from **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** → emits CO₂ unless **green H₂** (electrolysis using renewable electricity) is used. - **Storage Challenges:** 700 bar Type IV tanks cost **\$15–20/kWh** capacity. - **Lack of Infrastructure:** India has **< 10** public H₂ refuelling stations (2024). - **Safety:** H₂ is **leak-prone** (small molecular size) and **highly flammable** (4 %–75 % vol flammability in air). - **Durability:** PEMFC membrane degrades ~ **10 µV/h**; target: **5,000 hours** for HDV, **25,000 hours** (DOE 2030 target). ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---------------------|--------|---------------|------| | 1 | Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (ICE) | $\eta_{TTW,ICE}$ | 20 – 35 | % | | 2 | Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (BEV) | $\eta_{TTW,BEV}$ | 80 – 90 | % | | 3 | Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (FCEV) | $\eta_{TTW,FCV}$ | 50 – 60 | % | | 4 | Well-to-Wheel Efficiency (Gasoline ICE) | $\eta_{WTW}$ | 11 – 17 | % | | 5 | Well-to-Wheel Efficiency (BEV, Grid avg.) | $\eta_{WTW}$ | 23 – 30 | % | | 6 | Well-to-Wheel Efficiency (FCEV, Green H₂) | $\eta_{WTW}$ | 22 – 28 | % | | 7 | Regenerative Braking Recovery | $\eta_{regen}$ | 60 – 70 | % | | 8 | BSFC (Gasoline Best Point) | $g_{bsfc}$ | 220 – 250 | g/kWh | | 9 | H₂ LHV | $LHV_{H_2}$ | 120 | MJ/kg | | 10 | Gasoline LHV | $LHV_{gas}$ | 44 | MJ/kg | | 11 | Battery Specific Energy (Li-ion NMC) | $E_{sp}$ | 200 – 270 | Wh/kg | | 12 | Fuel Cell Power Density | $P_{sp}$ | 3.5 – 5.0 | kW/L | | 13 | Equivalent Petrol CO₂ per kWh | $CO_2$ | 0.72 | kg/kWh | | 14 | Tailpipe CO₂ (Petrol ICE) | $CO_{2,TP}$ | 120 – 180 | g/km | | 15 | Equivalent Full Cycle Time (BEV AC) | $t_{AC}$ | 6 – 8 | h | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility The advantages-disadvantages framework is **not academic** — it directly drives: - **Vehicle Architecture Selection** (e.g., Toyota's Hybrid Synergy Drive vs. Tesla's pure EV platform). - **Fleet Procurement Decisions** by Ola Electric, Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland. - **Government Policy Design** (FAME-II subsidies of ₹10,000/kWh capped at ₹1.5 L for EVs). - **Hydrogen Mission (India):** National Green Hydrogen Mission (₹19,744 Cr allocated) targets 5 MMT annual H₂ production by 2030 — directly addressing the FCEV cost disadvantage. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Well-to-Wheel Efficiency of a BEV The **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** is the product of **Well-to-Tank (WTT)** efficiency and **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** efficiency. ### Step 1: Define the sub-efficiencies - $\eta_{WTT}$ = Efficiency from primary energy source (e.g., coal/natural gas/solar) to electrical energy stored in the battery. - $\eta_{TTW}$ = Efficiency from electrical energy in the battery to mechanical work at the wheels. ### Step 2: Multiply the cascade $$ \eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW} $$ ### Step 3: Expand $\eta_{WTT}$ for a BEV charged from Indian grid (2024 average) The Indian grid mix (2024 CEA data): Coal 50 %, Solar 18 %, Hydro 12 %, Gas 8 %, Wind 5 %, Nuclear 3 %, Others 4 %. $$ \eta_{WTT} = \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{trans} \times \eta_{dist} \times \eta_{chg} $$ Taking typical values: - $\eta_{gen} = 0.35$ (thermal), 0.85 (renewable) → weighted average $\approx 0.45$. - $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$. - $\eta_{dist} = 0.93$. - $\eta_{chg} = 0.90$ (AC Level 2 charger). ### Step 4: Numerical substitution $$ \eta_{WTT} = 0.45 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.90 $$ $$ \eta_{WTT} = 0.45 \times 0.79515 $$ $$ \eta_{WTT} = 0.3578 \approx 35.8\,\% $$ ### Step 5: Combine with TTW $$ \eta_{TTW,BEV} = \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{inverter} \times \eta_{transmission} $$ $$ \eta_{TTW,BEV} = 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.804 \approx 80.4\,\% $$ ### Step 6: Compute WTW $$ \eta_{WTW,BEV} = 0.3578 \times 0.804 $$ $$ \eta_{WTW,BEV} = 0.2877 \approx 28.8\,\% $$ **Conclusion:** A BEV in India is **~ 1.7×** more efficient (well-to-wheel) than a petrol ICE vehicle (η_WTW ≈ 17 %). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Energy Cost Comparison A **Tata Nexon EV (45 kWh battery)** is benchmarked against a **Tata Nexon Petrol (44 L tank)** for a **500 km** trip. ### For the BEV: - Energy needed at wheels = 45 kWh (assuming 9 kWh/100 km). - Energy from grid = 45 ÷ 0.804 = **55.97 kWh** (TTW losses). - Energy at well = 55.97 ÷ 0.3578 = **156.4 kWh** (WTT losses). - Cost @ ₹8/kWh = ₹8 × 156.4 = **₹1,251.2**. ### For the Petrol ICE: - Fuel consumed = 500 km ÷ 17 km/L = **29.4 L**. - Energy at tank = 29.4 × 8.76 kWh/L = **257.6 kWh**. - Energy at well (WTT for crude → refined petrol = 0.88) = 257.6 ÷ 0.88 = **292.7 kWh**. - Cost @ ₹106/L = 29.4 × 106 = **₹3,116.4**. ### Savings per 500 km: $$ \text{Savings} = 3116.4 - 1251.2 = \text{₹1,865.2} $$ > [!NOTE] > The BEV delivers **60 %** energy cost reduction, **despite** Indian grid still being coal-dominant — confirming the architectural advantage of electric drive. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield) The following **8-dimensional comparison matrix** is the single most important table for the KTU ESE Module 1 paper: | S.No. | Parameter | Conventional ICEV | HEV (Full) | BEV | FCEV | |:-----:|-----------|:-----------------:|:----------:|:---:|:----:| | 1 | Tailpipe CO₂ (g/km) | 120 – 180 | 70 – 110 | **0** | **0** | | 2 | Tank-to-Wheel η (%) | 20 – 35 | 35 – 45 | 80 – 90 | 50 – 60 | | 3 | Well-to-Wheel η (%) | 11 – 17 | 18 – 25 | 23 – 30 | 22 – 28 | | 4 | Refuel Time (min) | 3 – 5 | 3 – 5 | 360 (AC) / 60 (DC) | **3 – 5** | | 5 | Range (km, typical) | 600 – 800 | 700 – 1000 | 200 – 450 | 500 – 700 | | 6 | Initial Cost (₹ Lakh, 2024, India) | 8 – 15 | 12 – 22 | 14 – 25 | **40 – 60** | | 7 | Maintenance Cost (₹/year) | 6,000 – 10,000 | 5,000 – 8,000 | **2,000 – 3,000** | 4,000 – 6,000 | | 8 | Battery/H₂ Cost Share (%) | NA | 15 – 25 | **40 – 50** | 20 – 30 (stack) | > [!TIP] > **Exam Shortcut:** If asked to *justify* the most suitable powertrain for a use-case (e.g., "300 km/day urban delivery"), always cite **three** matrix rows: **(a) Range, (b) Cost/km, (c) Infrastructure availability**. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Problem: Fuel Cell Vehicle Energy Balance **Problem (KTU University Exam – June 2024 type):** A fuel cell vehicle carries **5 kg of H₂** at 700 bar. The PEMFC has **LHV efficiency = 55 %**, the EM + drivetrain efficiency = **90 %**, and the auxiliary load = **1.5 kW** constant. The vehicle consumes **8 kW** at the wheels at 60 km/h. Calculate the **range (km)**. ### Step 1: Total chemical energy in H₂ $$ E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2} = 5 \times 120\,000\,kJ = 600\,000\,kJ $$ ### Step 2: Net traction energy available $$ E_{trac} = E_{H_2} \times \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{EM} $$ $$ E_{trac} = 600\,000 \times 0.55 \times 0.90 = 297\,000\,kJ $$ ### Step 3: Total vehicle power demand $$ P_{total} = P_{wheel} + P_{aux} = 8 + 1.5 = 9.5\,kW $$ ### Step 4: Driving time $$ t = \frac{E_{trac}}{P_{total}} = \frac{297\,000\,kJ}{9.5\,kW} = \frac{297\,000}{9.5 \times 3600}\,h $$ $$ t = \frac{297\,000}{34\,200} = 8.684\,h $$ ### Step 5: Range $$ \text{Range} = v \times t = 60 \times 8.684 = 521.05\,km $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to add the **auxiliary load** (1.5 kW) to the wheel power. This inflates the range by **~ 19 %** in this problem. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Comparative Powertrain Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Conventional ICEV] --> A1[Fuel Tank
Gasoline 44 L] A1 --> A2[ICE Engine
eta TTW 30 percent] A2 --> A3[Transmission] A3 --> A4[Wheels] B[HEV Full Hybrid] --> B1[Fuel Tank
Petrol 30 L] B1 --> B2[ICE Engine
1.8 L Atkinson] B2 --> B3[Planetary Gear
Power Split Device] B3 --> B4[MG1 Traction Motor] B3 --> B5[MG2 Generator Motor] B4 --> B6[Wheels] B5 --> B7[NiMH or Li ion
Battery 1.3 kWh] B7 --> B3 C[BEV] --> C1[Battery Pack
30 to 60 kWh Li ion] C1 --> C2[Inverter VSI] C2 --> C3[PMSM Induction Motor
eta 92 percent] C3 --> C4[Single Speed
Reducer] C4 --> C5[Wheels] D[FCEV] --> D1[H2 Tank 700 bar
Type IV Carbon Fibre] D1 --> D2[PEMFC Stack
80 to 114 kW] D2 --> D3[Boost Converter
400 to 650 V DC] D3 --> D4[Li ion Buffer Battery
1 to 2 kWh] D3 --> D5[Inverter VSI] D5 --> D6[Drive Motor] D6 --> D7[Wheels] style A1 fill:#fff4cc style B1 fill:#cce5ff style C1 fill:#ccffcc style D1 fill:#ffcccc ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The **HEVBattery** loop shows bidirectional energy flow (regen + boost), whereas the **FCEV** has a **small battery** that acts only as a *transient buffer* — not the primary energy source. ## 4.2 Decision-Flow Diagram — Choosing a Powertrain ```mermaid flowchart TD S{Start
Selection} --> Q1{Daily Distance
less than 80 km} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Charging Point
at Home} Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Select BEV
2W 3W 4W] Q2 -->|No| R2[Select HEV
or PHEV] Q1 -->|No| Q3{Long Highway
Trips above 500 km} Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Hydrogen
available} Q4 -->|Yes| R3[Select FCEV] Q4 -->|No| R4[Select Full HEV] Q3 -->|No| R5[Select HEV
Mid Hybrid] style R1 fill:#a8e6a3 style R2 fill:#ffd6a5 style R3 fill:#ffadad style R4 fill:#ffd6a5 style R5 fill:#ffd6a5 ``` ## 4.3 Trade-off Triangle — The Engineering Mandate ```mermaid flowchart LR COST[Cost Rupees] --- RANGE[Range and Refuel Time] RANGE --- EMIS[Emissions and Carbon Footprint] EMIS --- COST ICEV_POS[ICEV
Cheap and Long Range
High Emissions] HEV_POS[HEV
Moderate All Three] BEV_POS[BEV
Low Emissions
Cost and Range Issues] FCEV_POS[FCEV
Low Emissions Long Range
Very Costly] ICEV_POS --- COST HEV_POS --- RANGE BEV_POS --- EMIS FCEV_POS --- RANGE style COST fill:#ffe5b4 style RANGE fill:#b4d8ff style EMIS fill:#b4ffb4 ``` > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Board:** **C.R.E.** = **C**ost, **R**ange, **E**missions — the three-axis trade-off that **every** alternative powertrain must navigate. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **three advantages** of a Hybrid Electric Vehicle over a conventional ICE vehicle. **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):** 1. **Improved Fuel Economy (1 mark):** HEVs deliver 30–50 % better fuel economy, especially in urban stop-and-go traffic, by using regenerative braking and engine stop-start. 2. **Reduced Tailpipe Emissions (1 mark):** BSFC operation and reduced engine ON-time lower CO₂, NOx, and PM emissions, helping comply with BS-VI norms. 3. **Enhanced Drivability (1 mark):** The electric motor provides instant torque and torque-fill during gearshifts, eliminating torque interruption. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State any **three disadvantages** of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):** 1. **Range Anxiety (1 mark):** Typical BEV range of 200–450 km is less than ICEV (600–800 km), causing driver anxiety on long trips. 2. **Long Charging Time (1 mark):** Even DC fast charging (50 kW) takes ~ 60 min for 0–80 % SOC, vs. 3–5 min for petrol refuelling. 3. **High Initial Cost (1 mark):** The Li-ion traction battery accounts for 40–50 % of vehicle cost, making upfront price 30–50 % higher than equivalent ICE models. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** *(14 Marks)* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architectural difference** between a series HEV, parallel HEV, and series-parallel HEV. Mention **one specific production vehicle** for each type. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare HEVs, BEVs, and FCEVs on the basis of **(i) Well-to-Wheel efficiency, (ii) Refuelling time, (iii) Tailpipe emissions, (iv) Initial cost, (v) Range, (vi) Maintenance cost, and (vii) Infrastructure requirement**. Conclude by stating **which powertrain is most suitable for Indian urban use**. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Answer to Q-A(a) | Architecture | Power Flow | Production Example | Key Feature | |--------------|------------|--------------------|-------------| | **Series HEV** | ICE → Generator → Battery → EM → Wheels | **Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1)**, **Nissan e-Power** | ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from wheels; acts as range extender. | | **Parallel HEV** | ICE ‖ EM → Transmission → Wheels (mechanical coupling) | **Honda Insight (1st Gen)**, **Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid** | Both ICE and EM can drive wheels **simultaneously**. | | **Series-Parallel HEV** | Power Split Device (PSD) → ICE ‖ EM → Wheels | **Toyota Prius (THS)**, **Toyota Camry Hybrid** | Combines series + parallel; **most fuel-efficient** in city + highway. | **Block Diagram (textual, since Part-A may be theoretical):** - **Series:** `ICE → Gen → Rectifier → Battery ↔ Inverter → Motor → Wheels` - **Parallel:** `ICE → Clutch → Transmission ↗` ; `EM → Transmission ↗` → `Wheels` - **Series-Parallel:** `ICE → PSD (planetary) → Gen (MG1) + Sun gear → Motor (MG2) → Wheels`, with battery tied to DC bus. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - 3 diagrams @ 1.5 marks each = 4.5 - 1 example + explanation = 1.5 - Neatness and labels = 1 ### Model Answer to Q-A(b) | Parameter | HEV | BEV | FCEV | |-----------|:---:|:---:|:----:| | WTW Efficiency | 20 – 25 % | 25 – 30 % | 22 – 28 % | | Refuelling Time | 3 – 5 min | 60 – 480 min | 3 – 5 min | | Tailpipe Emissions | 70 – 110 g CO₂/km | 0 g/km | 0 g/km | | Initial Cost (₹ Lakh) | 12 – 22 | 14 – 25 | 40 – 60 | | Range (km) | 700 – 1000 | 200 – 450 | 500 – 700 | | Maintenance (₹/yr) | 5,000 – 8,000 | 2,000 – 3,000 | 4,000 – 6,000 | | Infrastructure | Petrol pumps (existing) | Charging stations (12,000) | H₂ stations (< 10) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** For **Indian urban use** (daily 30–60 km, dense traffic, existing petrol pump network but growing charging network), the **Full HEV is the most suitable powertrain in 2024** because: 1. It uses existing BS-VI petrol pumps → **no infrastructure change** needed. 2. It delivers 40 % fuel saving without range anxiety. 3. It avoids the BEV's high upfront battery cost and the FCEV's nonexistent H₂ network. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Table of 7 parameters × 0.5 = 3.5 - Quantitative justification = 1.5 - Conclusion with 3 reasons = 2 --- > **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** *(14 Marks)* **(a)** Derive the **Well-to-Wheel efficiency** of a petrol ICEV and a battery EV charged from the Indian grid. Use standard 2024 grid mix values. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** in detail, with a note on the **current status of the National Green Hydrogen Mission** in India. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Answer to Q-B(a) **For Petrol ICEV:** $$ \eta_{WTW,ICE} = \eta_{WTT,oil} \times \eta_{TTW,ICE} $$ - $\eta_{WTT,oil}$ = 0.88 (crude extraction → refinery → petrol pump) - $\eta_{TTW,ICE}$ = $\eta_{eng} \times \eta_{trans}$ = 0.30 × 0.92 = 0.276 $$ \eta_{WTW,ICE} = 0.88 \times 0.276 = 0.243 \approx 24.3\,\% $$ **For BEV (Indian grid, 2024):** $$ \eta_{WTT,BEV} = \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{trans} \times \eta_{dist} \times \eta_{chg} $$ $$ \eta_{WTT,BEV} = 0.45 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.90 = 0.358 $$ $$ \eta_{TTW,BEV} = 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.804 $$ $$ \eta_{WTW,BEV} = 0.358 \times 0.804 = 0.288 \approx 28.8\,\% $$ **Comparison:** $$ \frac{\eta_{WTW,BEV}}{\eta_{WTW,ICE}} = \frac{28.8}{24.3} = 1.185 $$ → BEV is **~ 19 %** more efficient (well-to-wheel). **However**, if grid is **100 % renewable**, $\eta_{WTT}$ rises to ~ 0.85, and BEV WTW becomes **~ 68 %** → BEV is then **~ 2.8×** more efficient. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Formula and sub-efficiencies = 2 - ICEV calculation = 1.5 - BEV calculation = 1.5 - Comparison + remark = 2 ### Model Answer to Q-B(b) **Advantages of FCEVs (3.5 marks):** 1. Zero tailpipe emissions (only H₂O). 2. High specific energy of H₂ (120 MJ/kg). 3. Fast refuelling (3–5 min). 4. Long range (500–700 km). 5. Cold-start capability down to −30 °C. 6. Stack design is modular and scalable. **Disadvantages of FCEVs (2 marks):** 1. Platinum catalyst cost (~$50–80/g). 2. Hydrogen storage at 700 bar is expensive and bulky. 3. Lack of refuelling infrastructure (India < 10 stations in 2024). 4. Safety concerns (H₂ leak + flammability range 4–75 % in air). 5. Green H₂ production is currently costly ($4–6/kg vs. SMR grey H₂ at $1–2/kg). **National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) Status (1.5 marks):** - Launched: **January 2023** by Ministry of New & Renewable Energy (MNRE). - Outlay: **₹19,744 Crore** (~$2.4 B) until 2030. - Target: **5 MMT** annual green H₂ production by 2030. - Sectors covered: Refineries, fertilizer, steel, transport (FCEV buses, trucks). - Pilot projects: **15 National Hydrogen Mobility projects** for FCEV buses (e.g., IOCL pilot in Delhi-Leh corridor). - Green H₂ cost reduction target: **$1/kg** by 2030 (from current $4–6/kg). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students LOSE marks:** > 1. **Confusing η_TTW with η_WTW** (TTW ignores oil extraction, refining, electricity generation losses). Always show both. > 2. **Forgetting the auxiliary load (P_aux = 1–2 kW)** in FCEV range calculations — this can cost 1–2 marks. > 3. **Writing "zero emissions" for FCEV without qualifying "at tailpipe"** — FCEVs have upstream emissions unless green H₂ is used; examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this. > 4. **Mixing up Series vs Parallel HEV architecture** — Series has *no mechanical link* between ICE and wheels; Parallel has a *direct clutch* link. Drawing them wrong costs 2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting to mention Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) compliance** when discussing HEV emission benefits — KTU examiners (as per 2024 scheme) explicitly look for Indian regulatory context. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Advantages & Disadvantages (Module 1)** - **HEVs** → Best of both worlds, but **no plug-in** (in full HEV); 30–50 % fuel saving; Toyota Prius = benchmark. - **BEVs** → Highest TTW efficiency (**~ 90 %**), zero tailpipe, but **range anxiety + 60 min fast-charge + ₹ high upfront**. - **FCEVs** → 5 min refuel + 600 km range + only H₂O exhaust, but **Pt catalyst cost + green H₂ availability**. - **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** is the **gold-standard** KTU metric — *not* TTW. WTW for BEV (India 2024) ≈ **29 %**, for petrol ICE ≈ **24 %**, for FCEV (green H₂) ≈ **25 %**. - **Regenerative braking** recovers **60–70 %** of kinetic energy → 5–10 % city fuel saving in HEV. - **Engine Stop-Start** adds another **5–10 %** saving. - **Atkinson cycle** ICE is preferred in HEVs because of higher **expansion ratio** (lower BSFC). - **Battery cost** is the **single biggest barrier** for BEVs at **\$120–150/kWh** (2024 BNEF); DOE target is **\$80/kWh** for cost parity. - **Hydrogen LHV = 120 MJ/kg** vs. gasoline **44 MJ/kg** — 3× energy density by mass, but **low volumetric density** (needs 700 bar compression). - **PEMFC operating temperature = 60–80 °C**; cold-start capability has improved to **−30 °C** (2nd-gen Toyota stack). - **BS-VI** norms: Petrol NOx ≤ 80 mg/km; this is the benchmark used in KTU ESE numericals. - **FAME-II subsidy** (Govt. of India): ₹10,000/kWh capped at ₹1.5 L for cars, ₹40,000/kWh for buses — relevant for any "policy" question. - **National Green Hydrogen Mission**: ₹19,744 Cr → 5 MMT H₂ by 2030 → green H₂ at $1/kg. - **Architecture abbreviations to remember:** ICEV, HEV, PHEV, BEV, FCEV, RE-EV (Range Extender EV). - **Three-axis trade-off**: **C**ost, **R**ange, **E**missions — every powertrain compromises one for the other. - **Series HEV** = ICE drives generator only; **Parallel HEV** = ICE + EM both drive wheels; **Series-Parallel (PSHEV)** = Toyota THS — best efficiency. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** In 14-mark long-answer questions, **always start with a labelled block diagram** (1 mark free), then a **comparison table** (3 marks), then **3 numerical justifications** (3 marks each) = 10 marks. Add a **2-line policy/practical conclusion** to secure the remaining 4 marks. This pattern is what KTU 2024 scheme examiners award **full marks** for.

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Applications # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Applications of Hybrid and Fuel Cell Vehicles** refer to the diverse domains, mobility segments, and engineering use-cases in which **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)**, **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** are deployed to achieve **energy efficiency**, **emission reduction**, **range extension**, and **operational sustainability**. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCAUT601**, the term *"Applications"* is broadly classified under three interconnected pillars: 1. **Passenger Mobility Applications** — sedans, SUVs, buses, two-wheelers, three-wheelers. 2. **Commercial & Heavy-Duty Applications** — trucks, delivery vans, refuse collectors, long-haul freight. 3. **Specialized / Niche Applications** — military vehicles, rail traction, material handling, marine propulsion, aerospace, off-road construction, and stationary power generation.
