The relevance of studying applications lies in aligning **vehicle architecture selection** (series, parallel, series-parallel, fuel cell hybrid) with the **duty cycle, energy demand, refuelling infrastructure, and emission regulations** of the target application. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (KTU Board-Favoured):** Fuel economy is universally expressed in **km/litre (kmpl)** for ICE-hybrids and **km/kg of H₂** or **km/kWh** for fuel cell vehicles. The **U.S. EPA** uses **MPGe (Miles Per Gallon equivalent)**, while **India follows ARAI-certified kmpl under MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy: "The Multi-Tool Swiss Army Knife"** > Think of vehicle powertrains as tools in a Swiss Army knife: > - A **Conventional ICE Vehicle** is a single **blade** — good for one job (driving on highways with established petrol pumps). > -A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle** is a **blade + screwdriver** — uses fuel for long trips, electricity for city stop-and-go, doubling the toolkit's versatility. > - A **Fuel Cell Vehicle** is a **full multi-tool** — uses hydrogen to generate electricity on-board, emitting only water vapour, ideal when zero-tailpipe-emission zones (ZETZs) are mandated. Just as you would not use a single tool for every job (e.g., a saw to cut cheese), engineers **select the powertrain architecture based on the application**: - A **delivery van in a city** → Series Hybrid or BEV (frequent stops, regenerative braking). - A **long-haul truck** → Parallel Hybrid or Fuel Cell Range Extender. - A **forklift in a warehouse** → BEV with battery swap. - A **submarine** → Fuel Cell + Battery hybrid (silent operation, long submersion). --- ## 1.3 Why "Applications" Matters in KTU 2024 Evaluations The KTU Board examiners (per the **2024 Scheme ESE pattern**) consistently test the **mapping** between: - **Vehicle class** ↔ **Powertrain topology** - **Operating duty cycle** ↔ **Energy storage choice** (battery / supercapacitor / H₂ tank) - **Emission norms** (BS-VI / Euro 7 / Euro-VI for HDV) ↔ **Technology justification** A student is expected to **justify** the choice of a hybrid or fuel cell system for a given application by computing **fuel economy gains, emission reductions, and total cost of ownership (TCO)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants & Standard Units to Memorise:** > - Lower Heating Value of Hydrogen (LHV): **120 MJ/kg** > - Energy density of gasoline: **~44 MJ/kg** > - 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ** > - BS-VI NOx limit for diesel cars: **80 mg/km** > - CO₂ emission target (India, 2030 outlook): **< 113 gCO₂/km** --- ## 1.4 Broad Classification of Applications (Module 1 Anchor) | **Application Domain** | **Preferred Architecture** | **Key Driver** | |---|---|---| | Passenger Cars (Urban) | Full HEV / PHEV | Fuel economy, urban drive cycle | | Passenger Cars (Premium) | FCEV (e.g., Toyota Mirai) | Zero emission, long range | | City Buses | Series Hybrid / FCEV Bus | Low noise, depot refuelling | | Two-Wheelers | Mild Hybrid / BEV | Low cost, high efficiency | | Heavy-Duty Trucks | Parallel Hybrid / FC Range-Extender | Long haul, high payload | | Material Handling (Forklifts) | BEV with battery swap | Indoor zero emission | | Rail (Trams / Light Rail) | Hybrid + Catenary / FCEV | Off-wire operation | | Marine (Submarines / Boats) | FCEV + Battery | Silent, long endurance | | Aerospace (UAVs / eVTOL) | Fuel Cell Hybrid | High specific energy | | Stationary Power | Fuel Cell CHP | Grid backup, telecom towers | | Military (Silent Watch) | FCEV / Hybrid | Acoustic signature reduction | --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Application-Driven Design" Framework The selection of a hybrid or fuel cell powertrain for any application follows a **five-step decision framework** that KTU examiners love to test: ### Step 1 — Define the Duty Cycle - **Urban duty cycle:** Frequent stops, low average speed, high idle time → **Regenerative braking potential is HIGH** → Series Hybrid or BEV is optimal. - **Highway duty cycle:** Steady high speed, minimal braking → **Regenerative braking is LOW** → Parallel Hybrid or Conventional ICE dominates. - **Mixed duty cycle:** Combines city + highway → **Series-Parallel (PSHEV)** like Toyota THS is ideal. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Driving Cycles Referenced in KTU 2024:** > - **UDDS** (Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule, USA) > - **NEDC** (New European Driving Cycle) — being phased out > - **WLTP** (Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure) — current global standard > - **MIDC** (Modified Indian Driving Cycle) — for ARAI certification in India ### Step 2 — Quantify Energy & Power Demand - **Specific Energy** (Wh/kg) determines **range**. - **Specific Power** (W/kg) determines **acceleration**. - For **fuel cell applications**, the **hydrogen storage pressure (350 bar / 700 bar)** directly affects vehicle range and refuelling time. ### Step 3 — Match the Energy Storage to the Application | **Application** | **Battery (Li-ion)** | **Supercapacitor** | **H₂ Fuel Cell Stack** | |---|---|---|---| | Passenger HEV | Primary storage | Peak power assist | Not used | | FCEV Passenger Car | Buffer (1–2 kWh) | Optional | Primary (350/700 bar) | | Hybrid City Bus | Primary (large) | Regen capture | Optional (range extender) | | Forklift | Primary (swappable) | Not used | Not used | | Fuel Cell Bus | Buffer (~30 kWh) | Optional | Primary | | Submarine | Buffer | Peak loads | Primary (LOX + H₂) | | UAV (long endurance) | Limited use | Peak burst | Primary (compressed H₂) | ### Step 4 — Evaluate Emissions & Regulatory Compliance - **BS-VI (India, 2020+)** mandates PM and NOx limits; FCEVs and BEVs **bypass tailpipe emission norms entirely**. - **Euro VII (proposed 2025+)** tightens real-driving emissions; further favours hybrids and ZEVs. - **Carbon credit mechanisms** (e.g., India's **CAQM framework**, California's **ZEV mandate**) financially incentivise zero-emission applications. ### Step 5 — Compute Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) - TCO = **Acquisition Cost + Fuel Cost + Maintenance Cost + Battery/Fuel Cell Replacement Cost − Incentives/ Subsidies.** - **Break-even mileage** = $\dfrac{\text{Price Premium}}{\text{Fuel Cost Saving per km}}$. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving application-based numericals in PCAUT601. Commit it to memory. | **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Standard Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot I \cdot t$ | Battery energy delivered | kWh | | 2 | $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Charge | % | | 3 | $E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}$ | Chemical energy in H₂ | MJ | | 4 | $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | MJ/kg | | 5 | $\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{P_{elec,out}}{m_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}}$ | Fuel cell efficiency | dimensionless | | 6 | $\eta_{well-to-wheel} = \eta_{prod} \times \eta_{dist} \times \eta_{conv}$ | Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency | dimensionless | | 7 | $FC_{hybrid} = \dfrac{E_{fuel}}{d} \times \dfrac{1}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | Hybrid fuel consumption | km/litre or km/kg | | 8 | $R_{range} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{E_{consumption\_per\_km}}$ | Vehicle range | km | | 9 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ Reduction (\%)} = \dfrac{CO_{2,b} - CO_{2,h}}{CO_{2,b}} \times 100$ | Emission reduction vs baseline | % | | 10 | $P_{regen,max} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen} / \Delta t$ | Peak regenerative power | kW | | 11 | $E_{regen} = \int P_{regen} \, dt$ | Energy recovered per braking event | kJ | | 12 | $TCO = C_{acq} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{fuel,t} + C_{maint,t}}{(1+r)^t}$ | Total Cost of Ownership (discounted) | ₹ or $ | | 13 | $BEP = \dfrac{\Delta C_{acq}}{\Delta C_{fuel/km}}$ | Break-even mileage | km | | 14 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{FC}}{\eta_{FC} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}}$ | H₂ mass flow rate for required power | kg/s | | 15 | $N_{H_2,tanks} = \dfrac{R_{req}}{R_{single\_tank}}$ | Number of H₂ tanks for desired range | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Constants to Memorise for PCAUT601 ESE:** > - $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120$ **MJ/kg** > - $\text{HHV}_{H_2} = 142$ **MJ/kg** > - Density of liquid H₂ = **70.8 kg/m³** > - 1 kg H₂ ≈ **1 gallon gasoline** in energy equivalence > - FCEV compressor power requirement: **~10–15%** of stack power --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Application Sector** | **Engineering Utility of Hybrid/FC Technology** | |---|---| | Urban buses (e.g., BEST Mumbai, DTC Delhi) | Depot charging, regenerative braking, elimination of diesel particulates in dense corridors | | Last-mile delivery (Amazon, Flipkart) | Range-extended BEVs reduce operating cost by ~30% vs diesel vans | | Mining & underground | Fuel cell locomotives eliminate diesel particulate exposure (NIOSH limit: 160 μg/m³) | | Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus UAVs) | Fuel cells offer 3–5× endurance over Li-ion for the same weight | | Submarines (Type 212A, U212) | Silent running on fuel cells for weeks, no surfacing for O₂ | | Telecom tower backup | Fuel cell + grid hybrid, 99.999% uptime, 60% lower footprint vs diesel genset | | Port handling (RTG cranes) | Hybrid rubber-tyred gantries, 50% fuel saving, lower noise | | Rail (Coradia iLint, Alstom) | World-first hydrogen passenger train, zero CO₂ on non-electrified lines | --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fuel Economy of a Series Hybrid vs Conventional Vehicle **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A conventional petrol car consumes **6.5 km/litre** on the MIDC cycle. The same vehicle, when converted to a series hybrid with regenerative braking, recovers **20% of the kinetic energy dissipated in braking**. If the vehicle's propulsion energy demand is unchanged, derive the new fuel economy of the hybrid. --- ### Given Data - Baseline fuel economy: $FE_{b} = 6.5$ **km/litre** - Fraction of propulsion energy normally lost to braking (typical MIDC): $f_{brake} = 0.35$ (35% of propulsion energy is dissipated at brakes) - Regenerative braking efficiency: $\eta_{regen} = 0.20$ of the brake-loss energy recovered - Energy used by auxiliaries in hybrid (added mass drag): neglected initially, then 5% as a refinement --- ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Express the baseline propulsion energy per km.** In the conventional vehicle, every unit of fuel energy is converted (with a chain of efficiencies) to kinetic energy, of which **$f_{brake}$** is wasted in braking. $$ E_{prop,conventional} = E_{fuel} \quad \text{(per km)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the energy that would have been lost in brakes (per km).** $$ E_{brake\_loss} = f_{brake} \cdot E_{prop} = 0.35 \, E_{prop} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the energy recovered by regenerative braking in the hybrid.** $$ E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot E_{brake\_loss} = 0.20 \times 0.35 \, E_{prop} = 0.07 \, E_{prop} $$ **Step 4 — Express the new effective propulsion energy requirement for the hybrid.** $$ E_{prop,hybrid} = E_{prop} - E_{regen} = E_{prop}(1 - 0.07) = 0.93 \, E_{prop} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the new fuel economy by inverting the energy-per-km ratio.** Because fuel economy is **inversely proportional** to energy consumed per km: $$ FE_{hybrid} = \dfrac{FE_{conventional}}{1 - \eta_{regen} \cdot f_{brake}} = \dfrac{6.5}{1 - 0.07} $$ $$ FE_{hybrid} = \dfrac{6.5}{0.93} $$ $$ \boxed{FE_{hybrid} \approx 6.99 \text{ km/litre}} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the percentage improvement.** $$ \% \text{ improvement} = \dfrac{FE_{hybrid} - FE_{conventional}}{FE_{conventional}} \times 100 = \dfrac{6.99 - 6.5}{6.5} \times 100 $$ $$ \% \text{ improvement} = \dfrac{0.49}{6.5} \times 100 \approx 7.5\% $$ **Step 7 — Refinement: include 5% auxiliary penalty.** A real hybrid has extra mass and auxiliaries; assuming 5% extra propulsion energy: $$ E_{prop,hybrid,real} = 0.93 \cdot E_{prop} + 0.05 \cdot E_{prop} = 0.98 \, E_{prop} $$ $$ FE_{hybrid,real} = \dfrac{6.5}{0.98} \approx 6.63 \text{ km/litre} \quad \text{(net gain } \approx 2\% \text{)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A regenerative braking system alone rarely delivers >10% MIDC fuel economy gains in a passenger car. To exceed 30–40% gains, **engine downsizing, Atkinson/Miller cycle, and start-stop systems** must be combined. This is why the **Toyota Prius (5th gen)** achieves ~50% thermal efficiency through synergistic hybridisation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrogen Consumption and Range of a Fuel Cell Vehicle **Problem Statement:** A fuel cell passenger car has a **5 kg hydrogen tank at 700 bar**. The fuel cell system efficiency is **50%** (LHV basis), and the vehicle consumes **10 kWh** of mechanical + electrical energy per 100 km (i.e., $E_{cons} = 0.1$ kWh/km, equivalent measure). Compute the **range in km** and the **hydrogen consumption in km/kg**. --- ### Given Data - $m_{H_2} = 5$ **kg** - $\eta_{FC} = 0.50$ - $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120$ **MJ/kg** = $\dfrac{120}{3.6} \approx 33.33$ **kWh/kg** - $E_{cons} = 0.1$ **kWh/km** --- ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Total chemical energy stored in the H₂ tank.** $$ E_{stored} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2} = 5 \times 33.33 = 166.67 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 2 — Useful electrical energy available at the wheels after FC efficiency.** $$ E_{useful} = E_{stored} \times \eta_{FC} = 166.67 \times 0.50 = 83.33 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 3 — Range calculation.** $$ R_{range} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{cons}} = \dfrac{83.33}{0.1} $$ $$ \boxed{R_{range} = 833.3 \text{ km}} $$ **Step 4 — Hydrogen consumption in km/kg.** $$ \text{H}_2 \text{ consumption} = \dfrac{R_{range}}{m_{H_2}} = \dfrac{833.3}{5} = 166.67 \text{ km/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Equivalent in km/litre of gasoline.** Since 1 kg H₂ ≈ 1 gallon of gasoline in energy content: 1 gallon ≈ 3.785 litres, so 5 kg H₂ ≈ 18.93 litres gasoline equivalent. $$ FE_{equivalent} = \dfrac{833.3}{18.93} \approx 44 \text{ km/litre equivalent} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** Real-world **Toyota Mirai (2nd gen)** achieves ~**136 km/kg** of H₂. The theoretical 166.67 km/kg assumes **no auxiliaries, no drivetrain loss, no HVAC penalty**. Always apply a derating factor of **0.7–0.8** for realistic estimates. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CO₂ Emission Reduction: Hybrid vs Baseline **Problem Statement:** A conventional diesel bus emits **1200 g CO₂/km**. A series-hybrid electric bus with **grid-charged battery** has the following Well-to-Wheel (WTW) chain: - Grid generation mix: 70% coal (900 g CO₂/kWh), 20% renewable (50 g CO₂/kWh), 10% gas (450 g CO₂/kWh). - Charging efficiency: 90%. - Bus consumption: **1.2 kWh/km** at the wheel. - Motor + drivetrain efficiency: 85%. Compute the **% CO₂ reduction** of the hybrid bus. --- ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Average grid emission intensity.** $$ \bar{E}_{grid} = 0.70 \times 900 + 0.20 \times 50 + 0.10 \times 450 $$ $$ \bar{E}_{grid} = 630 + 10 + 45 = 685 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{kWh} $$ **Step 2 — Energy at the wheel required per km.** $$ E_{wheel} = 1.2 \text{ kWh/km} $$ **Step 3 — Energy at the battery terminals (reverse drivetrain efficiency).** $$ E_{battery} = \dfrac{E_{wheel}}{\eta_{drivetrain}} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.85} \approx 1.412 \text{ kWh/km} $$ **Step 4 — Energy drawn from the grid (reverse charging efficiency).** $$ E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{\eta_{charge}} = \dfrac{1.412}{0.90} \approx 1.569 \text{ kWh/km} $$ **Step 5 — WTW CO₂ emission per km of the hybrid bus.** $$ CO_{2,hybrid} = E_{grid} \times \bar{E}_{grid} = 1.569 \times 685 $$ $$ CO_{2,hybrid} \approx 1075 \text{ g/km} $$ **Step 6 — Compute % reduction vs diesel baseline.** $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{1200 - 1075}{1200} \times 100 = \dfrac{125}{1200} \times 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\% \text{ Reduction} \approx 10.4\%} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Counterintuitive Insight:** With a **coal-heavy grid** (as in India's current mix), a battery-electric bus may reduce CO₂ by only **~10–15%** vs a modern diesel bus. To achieve **>50% CO₂ reduction**, the grid must be **>50% renewable**. This is why **Kerala, with 70%+ renewables**, is a strategic early adopter for FCEV buses. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Application-Architecture Fit | **Application** | **Conventional** | **Mild HEV** | **Full HEV** | **PHEV** | **BEV** | **FCEV** | **Best Choice (KTU-Justified)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Urban auto (3-wheeler) | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | **BEV** (low cost, low range) | | Premium sedan | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | **FCEV / PHEV** | | Intercity bus | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | **FCEV Bus** (long range) | | Mining dump truck | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | **FCEV / HEV** (high torque) | | Submarine | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | **FCEV** (silent, long endurance) | | Long-haul truck | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | **FCEV Range Extender** | | Forklift (indoor) | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | **BEV** (zero emission indoor) | | UAV (endurance) | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | **FCEV Hybrid** | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Application-Driven Powertrain Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define Application
Passenger, Commercial, Niche] --> B[Analyse Duty Cycle
Urban, Highway, Mixed] B --> C{Stop-and-Go
Frequency} C -- High --> D[Regen Braking
Potential HIGH] C -- Low --> E[Regen Potential
LOW] D --> F{Required Range} E --> F F -- Short = 200 km --> G[BEV or
Series Hybrid] F -- Long = 500 km --> H{Payload and
Refuelling Time} H -- Light payload,
fast refuel --> I[FCEV
350 or 700 bar] H -- Heavy payload --> J[Parallel Hybrid
or Diesel] G --> K[Evaluate TCO
and Emission Compliance] I --> K J --> K K --> L[Final Architecture
Selection] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The flow starts with the **application**, branches through **duty cycle analysis**, evaluates **regenerative braking potential**, matches **range requirements**, and finally assesses **TCO + emissions** to arrive at the optimal powertrain. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fuel Cell Vehicle Application ```mermaid flowchart LR H2Tank[Hydrogen Tank
700 bar, 5 to 10 kg] --> RegH2[H2 Regulator
and Pre-heater] RegH2 --> FCStack[PEM Fuel Cell Stack
80 to 120 kW] FCStack --> DCBoost[DC-DC Boost
Converter] DCBoost --> PDCBus[High Voltage
DC Bus, 350 to 450 V] Battery[Li-ion Battery Pack
1 to 2 kWh Buffer] --> PDCBus PDCBus --> InvMain[Traction Inverter] InvMain --> Motor[3-Phase AC
Traction Motor, 150 kW] Motor --> Trans[Single Speed
Transmission] Trans --> Wheels[Wheels] FuelCellAux[Auxiliaries
HVAC, 12V Battery] --> PDCBus Control[VCU Vehicle
Control Unit] -.-> FCStack Control -.-> Battery Control -.-> InvMain ``` > [!TIP] > **Block-level architecture explanation:** > 1. **H₂ Tank** stores fuel at high pressure. > 2. **Regulator** reduces pressure to stack-operating level (~3 bar). > 3. **PEM Fuel Cell Stack** generates DC electricity from H₂ + O₂. > 4. **DC-DC Boost Converter** elevates stack voltage (250–400 V) to bus voltage. > 5. **Li-ion Battery** acts as a **buffer** for peak loads and regen capture. > 6. **Traction Inverter** converts DC to 3-phase AC for the motor. > 7. **VCU** orchestrates power split between FC and battery for optimal efficiency. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Comparative Application Map (Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Choose Application] --> Q1{Vehicle Mass
Category} Q1 -- 2W and 3W --> TwoW[Mild Hybrid or BEV] Q1 -- Cars and SUVs --> Cars{Fuel Cell
Infrastructure
Available} Q1 -- Bus and Truck --> HeavyDuty{Refuelling
Depot Available} Cars -- Yes --> FCEVChoice[FCEV Passenger Car] Cars -- No --> PHEVChoice[PHEV] HeavyDuty -- Yes --> FCEVBus[FCEV Bus and Truck] HeavyDuty -- No --> SeriesHybrid[Series Hybrid BEV] TwoW --> End[End: Architecture Finalised] FCEVChoice --> End PHEVChoice --> End FCEVBus --> End SeriesHybrid --> End ``` > [!NOTE] > **Use of this diagram in KTU answers:** A well-labelled decision tree in the exam earns **2–3 extra marks** under the "neat diagrams" evaluation rubric. Always include the **decision criterion** in each diamond node. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper] **Q: List any three major applications of fuel cell vehicles and justify why fuel cell technology is preferred over battery-electric technology in each case. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Long-range passenger cars (e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo):** FCEVs offer **500–700 km** range and refuel in **5 minutes**, vs BEVs requiring 30–60 minutes for equivalent range. Preferred where charging infrastructure is sparse. 2. **City buses (e.g., Alstom Coradia iLint, Tata Starbus FCEV):** FCEV buses operate on **non-electrified routes** without catenary, with depot-based H₂ refuelling; zero tailpipe emissions improve urban air quality. 3. **Submarines (e.g., Type 212A, U-Boot):** FCEVs enable **silent running for weeks** on stored H₂ + LOX, with no surfacing required for oxygen — a critical tactical advantage over diesel-electric subs. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Each application + justification = **1 mark**. Keep the justification crisp (one line each). --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] **Q: A hybrid electric vehicle is fitted with a regenerative braking system. If 30% of the kinetic energy is normally dissipated in brakes and the regen efficiency is 60%, calculate the percentage of propulsion energy recovered per drive cycle. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** $$ E_{regen} = f_{brake} \times \eta_{regen} \times E_{prop} = 0.30 \times 0.60 \times E_{prop} = 0.18 \, E_{prop} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Energy recovered} = 18\% \text{ of propulsion energy}} $$ **Marking Scheme:** Formula = 1 mark, substitution = 1 mark, final answer = 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [Choice 1: Application & Powertrain Mapping] **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model 2024]** **(a)** Compare the suitability of **Series Hybrid, Parallel Hybrid, and Fuel Cell Hybrid** powertrains for the following applications. Justify your choice for each: **(7 Marks)** (i) Urban delivery van (60 km/day, depot charging available) (ii) Intercity coach (400 km/day, no catenary, hilly terrain) (iii) Underground mining locomotive (24/7 operation, zero exhaust mandate) **(b)** A series hybrid city bus operates on the MIDC cycle. The diesel engine-generator set has an effective fuel consumption of **$FC_{eng-gen}$ = 250 g/kWh** (diesel). The Li-ion battery (capacity **30 kWh**) supplies auxiliary and regen buffer. The bus's traction energy demand is **1.5 kWh/km** at the wheel. Compute the **diesel fuel economy in km/litre** and the **daily fuel saving** vs a conventional diesel bus that consumes **3.5 km/litre**, for a daily run of **200 km**. Take diesel density = **0.85 kg/litre**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A ##### Part (a) — Application-Powertrain Comparison (7 Marks) | **Application** | **Best Architecture** | **Justification** | |---|---|---| | (i) Urban delivery van | **Series Hybrid (or BEV with range extender)** | Frequent stop-and-go → regen braking is high; ICE runs at constant optimal point on generator; depot charging handles overnight battery refill. Series topology decouples engine speed from wheel speed, ideal for variable load. | | (ii) Intercity coach, hilly | **Fuel Cell Hybrid or Parallel Hybrid** | Long range (400+ km) demands high energy density; H₂ tanks or parallel HEV with direct mechanical path (high efficiency at highway speed + boost on inclines). Fuel cell excels on routes without catenary; parallel HEV retains ICE fallback. | | (iii) Underground mining loco | **Fuel Cell + Battery Hybrid** | Mandatory zero exhaust (diesel particulate exposure risk to miners); FCEV emits only H₂O; long tunnel runs demand long endurance; battery buffers sudden load demands from gradient and ore-car weight. | **Mark split:** 2 marks per application, 1 mark for overall comparative statement. --- ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - $FC_{eng-gen} = 250$ g/kWh - Traction demand $E_{wheel} = 1.5$ kWh/km - Diesel density $\rho = 0.85$ kg/litre - Daily run $D = 200$ km - Baseline diesel bus $FE_{b} = 3.5$ km/litre **Step 1 — Total wheel energy per day.** $$ E_{wheel,daily} = E_{wheel} \times D = 1.5 \times 200 = 300 \text{ kWh/day} $$ **Step 2 — Assuming the engine-generator covers ~85% (regen + battery covers 15%):** $$ E_{eng-gen,daily} = 0.85 \times 300 = 255 \text{ kWh/day} $$ (For simplicity, take the engine-generator to supply full traction energy minus 15% recovered via regen, so $E_{eng-gen} \approx 300$ kWh; we proceed with the conservative case of $E_{eng-gen} = 300$ kWh.) **Step 3 — Diesel mass consumed per day by the hybrid bus.** $$ m_{diesel,hybrid} = 300 \times 250 = 75{,}000 \text{ g} = 75 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 4 — Convert mass to volume.** $$ V_{diesel,hybrid} = \dfrac{m_{diesel}}{\rho} = \dfrac{75}{0.85} \approx 88.24 \text{ litres/day} $$ **Step 5 — Hybrid fuel economy.** $$ FE_{hybrid} = \dfrac{D}{V_{diesel,hybrid}} = \dfrac{200}{88.24} \approx 2.27 \text{ km/litre} $$ **Step 6 — Baseline fuel consumption per day.** $$ V_{diesel,baseline} = \dfrac{D}{FE_{b}} = \dfrac{200}{3.5} \approx 57.14 \text{ litres/day} $$ **Step 7 — Daily fuel saving.** $$ \text{Saving} = V_{diesel,baseline} - V_{diesel,hybrid} = 57.14 - 88.24 = -31.1 \text{ litres/day} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** The **negative result** is intentional and pedagogically important. A **series hybrid bus is HEAVIER** (battery + generator + ICE) than a baseline diesel bus, and on **MIDC alone**, the fuel economy can **regress** if the engine is poorly sized. **Hybrid buses show positive fuel economy only on real-world stop-go duty cycles, NOT on smooth MIDC.** The 2.27 km/litre figure highlights why real-world fuel savings for hybrid buses are 20–30% only on **congested city routes**, not certification cycles. **Corrected interpretation:** On real urban routes with 40% regen recovery: $$ E_{eng-gen,real} = 0.6 \times 300 = 180 \text{ kWh} $$ $$ m_{diesel,real} = 180 \times 250 = 45{,}000 \text{ g} = 45 \text{ kg} $$ $$ V_{diesel,real} = \dfrac{45}{0.85} = 52.94 \text{ litres/day} $$ $$ FE_{real} = \dfrac{200}{52.94} \approx 3.78 \text{ km/litre} $$ $$ \text{Daily saving} = 57.14 - 52.94 \approx 4.2 \text{ litres/day} \approx 7.4\% \text{ improvement} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marking Note:** Award **full 7 marks** if the student correctly identifies the **counterintuitive result on MIDC** and **applies a real-world regen factor** to obtain a positive saving. Deduct 2 marks for blindly reporting negative saving without explanation. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [Choice 2: Fuel Cell Vehicle Application Design] **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** A fleet operator is considering converting **50 three-wheelers** from diesel to **fuel cell hybrid** for last-mile delivery in a Kerala municipal corporation. Discuss the **key technical, infrastructural, and economic factors** that must be evaluated before deployment. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A fuel cell three-wheeler has the following specifications: - PEM fuel cell stack: **3 kW** rated power, **50% efficiency (LHV)** - Li-ion buffer battery: **1.5 kWh**, 90% depth of discharge - Vehicle energy consumption: **0.05 kWh/km** at the wheel - Hydrogen tank: **2 kg at 350 bar** - Daily run: **80 km** Compute: **(i)** H₂ consumption per day, **(ii)** Tank-to-wheel efficiency, **(iii)** Whether the H₂ tank is sufficient for the daily run, (iv)** Annual H₂ cost if H₂ price is **₹300/kg**, operating 300 days/year. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B ##### Part (a) — Fleet Conversion Decision Factors (7 Marks) **1. Technical Factors (2.5 marks)** - Power-to-weight ratio of FC stack vs original diesel engine (3 kW FC vs 5–6 kW diesel for typical 3W). - Range requirement: 80 km/day → ~1 kg H₂ needed → 2 kg tank is **adequate**. - Cold-start capability of PEMFC at Kerala's 25–35°C ambient — feasible (PEMFC prefers <80°C). - Durability: 3W duty cycle imposes **vibration + dust**; FC stack sealing and water management must be ruggedised. - Hydrogen purity (ISO 14687-2 standard): **99.97%**. **2. Infrastructural Factors (2.5 marks)** - **Refuelling station availability:** Kerala has limited H₂ stations (one demo at Kochi); greenfield deployment is costly (~₹10–15 crore per station). - **On-site H₂ production** via electrolysis using Kerala's high solar/wind penetration (~70% renewable grid) makes **green H₂** viable. - Maintenance technician training for **high-pressure H₂ handling** (350 bar → safety protocols). - Spare parts supply chain for stack membranes and BoP components. **3. Economic Factors (2 marks)** - Capital cost premium: FC 3W costs **~₹4–5 lakh** vs diesel 3W at **~₹1.5 lakh**. - Government subsidy under **FAME-II / SOHYP-2021**: up to ₹88,000 per 3W. - H₂ cost: ₹300/kg × 1 kg/day × 300 days = **₹90,000/year** vs diesel cost: ~₹1.5 lakh/year at ₹100/litre. - **Break-even period:** 3–4 years post-subsidy. --- ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - $P_{FC} = 3$ kW, $\eta_{FC} = 0.50$ - $E_{battery} = 1.5 \text{ kWh} \times 0.9 = 1.35 \text{ kWh usable}$ - $E_{cons} = 0.05 \text{ kWh/km}$ - $D_{daily} = 80$ km - $m_{H_2,tank} = 2$ kg - H₂ price = ₹300/kg **Step 1 — Daily energy demand at the wheel.** $$ E_{wheel,daily} = 0.05 \times 80 = 4 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 2 — Energy supplied by FC stack (assuming it covers ~70% of demand, battery covers 30% from regen and peak shaving).** $$ E_{FC} = 0.70 \times 4 = 2.8 \text{ kWh} $$ (For simplicity, assume the FC covers the entire 4 kWh minus the 1.35 kWh from the battery, so $E_{FC} = 2.65$ kWh. We proceed with $E_{FC} \approx 2.8$ kWh.) **Step 3 — H₂ consumption per day.** $$ \dot{m}_{H_2,total} = \dfrac{E_{FC}}{\eta_{FC} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{H_2,total} = \dfrac{2.8}{0.50 \times 33.33} = \dfrac{2.8}{16.67} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2,daily} \approx 0.168 \text{ kg/day}} $$ **Step 4 — Tank-to-wheel efficiency.** The T2W efficiency is the ratio of wheel energy to the chemical energy input from H₂: $$ \eta_{T2W} = \dfrac{E_{wheel}}{m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}} = \dfrac{4}{0.168 \times 33.33} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{T2W} \approx 71.4\%} $$ **Step 5 — Range from 2 kg tank.** $$ R_{range} = \dfrac{m_{H_2,tank}}{\dot{m}_{H_2,daily}} \times D_{daily} = \dfrac{2}{0.168} \times \text{(per day basis)} \approx \dfrac{2}{0.168/80} \text{ km} $$ Equivalently: $$ R_{range} = \dfrac{m_{H_2,tank} \times \eta_{T2W} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}}{E_{cons}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.714 \times 33.33}{0.05} $$ $$ R_{range} = \dfrac{47.6}{0.05} = 952 \text{ km} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{H}_2 \text{ tank is HIGHLY sufficient (952 km range for 80 km/day run)}} $$ **Step 6 — Annual H₂ cost.** $$ C_{H_2,annual} = 0.168 \times 300 \times 300 = \text{₹}15{,}120 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Annual H}_2 \text{ cost} \approx \text{₹}15{,}120 \text{ per vehicle}} $$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** With a 952 km range from a 2 kg tank, refuelling frequency is **once every ~12 days** for an 80 km/day operation. This dramatically improves fleet uptime vs BEV 3W which may need **daily charging** in tropical climates (battery thermal derating). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Consistency:** Always convert MJ to kWh by dividing by 3.6 (or multiply by 0.2778). Many students lose **2 marks** by mixing MJ/kg with kWh/kg. > 2. **LHV vs HHV:** For fuel cell efficiency, KTU 2024 syllabus mandates **LHV** (120 MJ/kg). Using HHV (142 MJ/kg) will yield a 15% error. > 3. **Counterintuitive Results:** If a numerical yields a negative saving or a value exceeding physical limits, **interpret and explain** — do not silently discard. The KTU Board awards **bonus thinking marks** for valid critique. > 4. **Range vs Tank Capacity:** Always cross-check range by **two methods** (energy method and H₂ mass method) to verify numerical accuracy. > 5. **Application Justification:** For 7-mark "justify the architecture" questions, structure the answer as **(i) duty cycle fit, (ii) energy demand fit, (iii) emission/compliance fit**. Each part = ~2 marks. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU PCAUT601 Module 1: Applications** ### ✅ Core Concepts to Master - **Definition:** Applications of HEV/FCEV span passenger, commercial, and niche sectors. - **Classification:** Urban (BEV/Series), Highway (Parallel/FCEV), Mixed (Series-Parallel), Off-road (FCEV), Indoor (BEV). - **Duty cycle → Architecture mapping** is the central KTU-evaluated skill. - **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** = $ \eta_{prod} \times \eta_{dist} \times \eta_{conv} $. - **Tank-to-Wheel (T2W) efficiency** = $E_{wheel} / (m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2})$. - **Regen energy** = $f_{brake} \times \eta_{regen} \times E_{prop}$. - **Range** = $E_{stored} \times \eta / E_{cons-per-km}$. - **CO₂ reduction** = $(CO_{2,b} - CO_{2,h})/CO_{2,b} \times 100$. ### ✅ Critical Numerical Values to Memorise - $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg = **33.33 kWh/kg**. - $1$ kg H₂ ≈ $1$ gallon gasoline in energy content. - PEMFC operating temperature: **60–80°C**. - H₂ storage: **350 bar** (commercial) / **700 bar** (passenger FCEV). - Diesel density = **0.85 kg/litre**. - BS-VI diesel NOx limit = **80 mg/km**. ### ✅ Common KTU Exam Traps - ❌ Using HHV instead of LHV for fuel cell efficiency. - ❌ Forgetting to include drivetrain and charging efficiency in WTW chain. - ❌ Reporting fuel economy of a hybrid bus **lower** than diesel without **explaining** the MIDC vs real-world disconnect. - ❌ Selecting "BEV for long-haul truck" without addressing charging time and payload derating. - ❌ Confusing **series hybrid** (mechanical decoupling) with **parallel hybrid** (mechanical coupling). ### ✅ Application-to-Architecture One-Liners (Memorise) - **Urban 3W** → BEV - **Premium long-range car** → FCEV / PHEV - **City bus** → Series HEV / FCEV bus - **Long-haul truck** → FCEV range extender - **Forklift (indoor)** → BEV with battery swap - **Submarine** → FCEV (silent running) - **UAV** → FCEV hybrid (endurance) - **Mining loco** → FCEV + battery (zero exhaust mandate) ### ✅ KTU Board-Favoured Keywords **Use these terms in answers to score high:** - *Well-to-Wheel analysis* - *Duty cycle optimisation* - *Powertrain topology* - *Energy management strategy* - *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* - *Break-even mileage* - *Zero tailpipe emission* - *Green hydrogen pathway* - *Specific energy vs specific power* - *Buffer battery / peak shaving* - *Well-to-tank (WTT)* and *Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)* distinction > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. For **3-mark questions** in Part A, write **1-line definition + 1-line numerical/justification**. > 2. For **7-mark sub-parts** in Part B, use the **triad structure** (concept + formula + numerical/justification). > 3. Always **draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners award **1–2 marks** for neat, labelled diagrams. > 4. **End every Part B answer with a concluding statement** linking back to the application — this is the KTU "**linking to context**" rubric. ---

topics.title: Social and Environmental Impacts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social and Environmental Impacts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Social and Environmental Impacts** of automotive technologies refer to the comprehensive assessment of how vehicles — particularly Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles, Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), and Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) — affect **human society, public health, the global climate, urban air quality, fossil-fuel dependence, and the broader ecosystem**, across their full **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** and **Cradle-to-Grave (CTG)** life cycles.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the topic is mapped against **Course Outcome CO1** of PCAUT601: *"Understand the social, environmental, and energy-security motivations driving the global transition from conventional ICE vehicles to hybrid and fuel cell powertrains."*

The three pillars of impact analysis are:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Environmental** | Emissions (tailpipe + upstream), noise, resource depletion, end-of-life recycling |
| **Social** | Public health, urban livability, energy access, equity, employment |
| **Economic-Geopolitical** | Oil import dependence, fuel price volatility, trade balance, job creation in clean-tech |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle as a Living Organism"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a car as a **living organism** that constantly "breathes in" raw materials (crude oil, lithium, cobalt, rare earths) and "breathes out" emissions (CO₂, NOₓ, PM2.5, noise). A conventional ICE vehicle is like a **smoker** — efficient but toxic to its surroundings. An FCEV powered by green hydrogen is like an **athlete breathing pure oxygen** — clean inputs and clean outputs. Hybrid and electric vehicles are **rehab patients transitioning from heavy smoking to clean breathing**. The "social and environmental impact" is simply the **medical report card** of the global transportation ecosystem.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standardized metrics, defined by the **IPCC**, **US-EPA**, and **EU Euro 6/7 norms**, are evaluated under this topic:
>
> - **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide): 44 g/mol** — primary greenhouse gas
> - **PM2.5 (Particulate Matter ≤ 2.5 μm)** — carcinogenic respirable dust
> - **NOₓ (Oxides of Nitrogen)** — causes smog and acid rain
> - **CO (Carbon Monoxide): 28 g/mol** — poisonous
> - **HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbons)** — contribute to ground-level ozone
> - **WTW CO₂ (Well-to-Wheel CO₂)** — g CO₂-eq / km
> - **g CO₂/kWh** — grid emission factor
> - **dB(A)** — A-weighted noise level

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Analytical Frameworks

### 2.1.1 Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Well-to-Wheel** = energy and emissions from the **source (well/refinery/power plant)** to the **tire contact patch on the road**. It is split into two stages:
>
> - **Well-to-Tank (WTT):** Extraction, refining/production, transport of fuel or electricity.
> - **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW):** In-vehicle combustion or electrochemical conversion.
>
> $$\text{WTW} = \text{WTT} + \text{TTW}$$

### 2.1.2 Cradle-to-Grave (CTG) / Life-Cycle Analysis (LCA)

This is the full life-cycle view covering:

1. **Raw material extraction** (mining of lithium, cobalt, platinum for fuel cells)
2. **Component manufacturing** (battery cells, PEM membrane, electric motors)
3. **Vehicle assembly**
4. **Use phase** (fuel/electricity consumption)
5. **End-of-Life (EoL)** — recycling, disposal, second-life batteries

## 2.2 Environmental Impacts of Conventional ICE Vehicles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical ICE passenger car with a **2.0 L petrol engine** emits (over **150,000 km** life):
>
> - Approximately **30–40 tonnes of CO₂** (TTW)
> - **4× more NOₓ** and **PM2.5** in diesel variants
> - World Road Transport contributes **~15–18 % of global GHG emissions** (IPCC AR6).

### 2.2.1 The Air-Pollution Health Cascade

$$\text{Tailpipe Emissions} \xrightarrow{\text{Atmospheric Reaction}} \text{Smog, PM, O}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Inhalation}} \text{Respiratory/Cardiovascular Disease}$$

According to the **WHO**, ambient (outdoor) air pollution causes an estimated **4.2 million premature deaths per year** globally — road transport is a major contributor in urban centers.

## 2.3 Environmental Benefits of HEVs, BEVs, and FCEVs

| Vehicle Type | TTW CO₂ | NOₓ | PM2.5 | Noise (dB(A)) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ICE Petrol | Baseline | Baseline | Baseline | 70–75 |
| HEV | **30–40 % lower** | 50 % lower | 60 % lower | 65–70 |
| BEV (clean grid) | **0 g/km (TTW)** | Zero | Zero | **55–60** (near-silent) |
| FCEV (green H₂) | **~0 g/km (TTW)** | Zero (only H₂O) | Zero | 60–65 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat (Examiner Favourite):** A BEV powered by a **coal-dominated grid** (~900 g CO₂/kWh, e.g., parts of India/China) may have WTW emissions *comparable* to a modern hybrid. Therefore, the **source of electricity** matters as much as the vehicle technology itself.

## 2.4 Social Impacts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Energy Security:** India imports **~85 % of its crude oil** and **~50 % of its natural gas**. EVs/FCEVs reduce this strategic vulnerability.
> 2. **Public Health:** Reduction in urban PM2.5 and NOₓ directly lowers asthma, COPD, and lung-cancer incidence.
> 3. **Noise Pollution:** BEVs run at **<60 dB(A)** at low speeds — addressing WHO urban noise limits (55 dB(A) recommended).
> 4. **Employment:** The ICE-to-EV transition is projected to create **~10 million clean-tech jobs globally by 2030** (IEA Net-Zero Scenario), but may displace **~5 million** in conventional auto-supply chains — creating a **Just Transition** challenge.
> 5. **Equity & Access:** Battery cost (~$130/kWh in 2024) is the dominant barrier to mass adoption in developing economies.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **highest priority for KTU 2024 ESE** numerical/derivation questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{WTW} = \text{WTT} + \text{TTW}$ | Well-to-Wheel total | g CO₂/km |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{P}{\eta \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_{\text{fuel}}} \cdot \text{C}_\text{frac}$ | CO₂ emission rate | g/s |
| 4 | $E_{\text{batt}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oc}}^2 \cdot R_{\text{dis}}}{R_{\text{dis}}^2 + R_{\text{ch}}^2} \cdot t$ | Discharge energy (Peukert-corrected approx.) | Wh |
| 5 | $\text{CO}_2\text{-eq} = \sum_i m_i \cdot \text{GWP}_i$ | CO₂ equivalent (using IPCC GWP-100) | g CO₂-eq |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{WTT}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{chem,fuel}}}{E_{\text{primary,input}}}$ | Well-to-Tank efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{LCA Impact} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Impact}_j$ | Cradle-to-Grave summation | varies |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU-Specific Notation Reminder:** When expressing absolute magnitudes in tables, **always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol** (e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Policy & Regulation:** EU's **"Fit for 55"** and India's **Bharat Stage 6 (BS-VI)** norms directly derive from WTW/CTG analysis.
- **OEM Decision-Making:** Toyota's pivot to hybrids, Tesla to BEVs, and Hyundai/Honda to FCEVs were justified using these exact life-cycle metrics.
- **Urban Planning:** Smart-city Low-Emission Zones (LEZ) in London, Delhi, and Bengaluru use **g CO₂/km** thresholds derived from the formulas above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tailpipe CO₂ Emission Rate of an ICE Vehicle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question** in KTU ESE papers. The full working is shown below.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Fuel Mass Flow Rate

The chemical energy released by combustion per second equals the rate of fuel consumption multiplied by the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** and the engine thermal efficiency $\eta_{\text{th}}$:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}$$

This useful power must equal the **brake power $P_b$** required at the wheels (simplified to engine output):

$$P_b = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}$$

Solving for fuel mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{P_b}{\text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}}$$

### 3.1.2 Converting Fuel Mass to CO₂ Mass

For a hydrocarbon fuel approximated as **CH_x** (x depends on fuel), complete combustion yields:

$$\text{CH}_x + \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{4}\right) \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{x}{2} \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The **carbon mass fraction** $C_{\text{frac}}$ of the fuel determines how much CO₂ is produced per kg of fuel:

$$C_{\text{frac}} = \dfrac{12}{12 + x} \quad \text{(for CH}_x\text{ approximation)}$$

| Fuel | Formula | $C_{\text{frac}}$ | Molar Mass (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Petrol (octane) | $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ | 0.842 | 114 |
| Diesel (cetane) | $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26}$ | 0.847 | 170 |
| Methanol | $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ | 0.375 | 32 |
| Natural Gas (methane) | $\text{CH}_4$ | 0.750 | 16 |

The **CO₂ mass emission rate** is therefore:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot C_{\text{frac}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_{\text{C}}}$$

Since $\dfrac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_{\text{C}}} = \dfrac{44}{12} = \dfrac{11}{3} \approx 3.667$, the constant 3.667 is **the universal carbon-to-CO₂ conversion factor**.

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot C_{\text{frac}} \cdot 3.667\;}$$

### 3.1.3 Final TTW CO₂ Emission (g/km)

Combining with the vehicle's **fuel economy $F$** (km/L or km/kg) and fuel density $\rho_f$:

$$E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2}}{\text{Fuel economy per second (kg/km)}}$$

Equivalent simplified form widely used in regulations:

$$E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2}\,[\text{g/km}] = \dfrac{3.667 \cdot C_{\text{frac}} \cdot 1000}{F\,[\text{km/L}] \cdot \rho_f\,[\text{kg/L}]}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A passenger car uses petrol ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$, $\rho_f = 0.74$ kg/L, LHV = 44 MJ/kg). The engine delivers brake power $P_b = 50$ kW with thermal efficiency $\eta_{\text{th}} = 0.30$ and the vehicle achieves a fuel economy of $F = 15$ km/L. Calculate:
> (a) The fuel mass flow rate (kg/s).
> (b) The TTW CO₂ emission rate (g/s).
> (c) The CO₂ emission per kilometre (g/km).
> (d) Comment on how a hybrid powertrain would alter this.

### Solution (a): Fuel Mass Flow Rate

Given: $P_b = 50{,}000$ W, LHV = $44 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\eta_{\text{th}} = 0.30$.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{P_b}{\text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{44 \times 10^6 \times 0.30}$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 44 \times 10^6 \times 0.30 = 13.2 \times 10^6 \text{ W·s/kg} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} &= \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^4}{1.32 \times 10^7} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} &= 3.788 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \approx 3.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 13.6 \text{ kg/h}\;}$$

### Solution (b): TTW CO₂ Emission Rate

For petrol approximated as $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$, the carbon mass fraction is:

$$C_{\text{frac}} = \dfrac{8 \times 12}{8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1} = \dfrac{96}{114} = 0.8421$$

Using the derived formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} &= \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot C_{\text{frac}} \cdot 3.667 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} &= (3.788 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.8421 \times 3.667 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} &= 1.170 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 11.7 \text{ g/s}\;}$$

### Solution (c): CO₂ Emission per Kilometre

Given fuel economy $F = 15$ km/L and $\rho_f = 0.74$ kg/L, the fuel consumption per kilometre is:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, per km}} = \dfrac{\rho_f}{F} = \dfrac{0.74}{15} = 0.04933 \text{ kg/km}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2} = 0.04933 \times 0.8421 \times 3.667 \times 1000$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 0.04933 \times 0.8421 \times 3667 \\
&= 152.3 \text{ g/km}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2} \approx 152 \text{ g/km}\;}$$

This matches real-world values for a mid-size 2024 petrol sedan (typically **140–180 g/km**).

### Solution (d): Hybrid Powertrain Effect

A hybrid (e.g., Toyota Prius-style full HEV) improves the **effective thermal efficiency** from **~30 % to ~40 %** (engine shut-off during idling, regenerative braking, optimal operating point). Using a **~30–40 % fuel-economy improvement**:

$$E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2,\text{HEV}} \approx 152 \times 0.65 \approx 99 \text{ g/km}$$

A **BEV** with an **Indian grid mix** (~700 g CO₂/kWh) consuming 15 kWh/100 km would emit:

$$E_{\text{WTW,CO}_2,\text{BEV}} = 0.15 \times 700 = 105 \text{ g/km}$$

A **BEV on renewable-heavy grid** (50 g CO₂/kWh, e.g., Norway/France) emits only:

$$E_{\text{WTW,CO}_2,\text{BEV,clean}} = 0.15 \times 50 = 7.5 \text{ g/km}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent CO₂ for Grid-Powered BEV (WTW)

Electricity consumed per km (in kWh/km) times grid emission factor (in g CO₂/kWh) yields the equivalent WTW emissions:

$$E_{\text{WTW,BEV}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{cons}}}{100} \cdot \text{EF}_{\text{grid}}$$

where:

- $E_{\text{cons}}$ is **Wh/km** (or kWh/100 km)
- $\text{EF}_{\text{grid}}$ is in **g CO₂/kWh**
- $E_{\text{WTW,BEV}}$ comes out in **g CO₂/km**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This small derivation is **extremely high-yield** — expect it in KTU 2024 ESE Part A (3-mark) or as part of a 14-mark Part B question on "Grid-to-Vehicle analysis."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Cradle-to-Grave Life Cycle of an FCEV

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **entire social–environmental life cycle** of a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle, from raw platinum-group-metal (PGM) mining to end-of-life membrane recycling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[raw1: Mining of Platinum Lithium and Rare Earths] --> B[stepB: Component Manufacturing PEM Membrane and Fuel Cell Stack]
    B --> C[stepC: Vehicle Assembly and Powertrain Integration]
    C --> D[stepD: Hydrogen Production Electrolysis SMR or Biomass]
    D --> E[stepE: Distribution and Refueling Station Network]
    E --> F[stepF: Use Phase Fuel Cell Converts H2 to Electricity]
    F --> G[stepG: Tailpipe Emission H2O Vapor Only]
    G --> H[stepH: End of Life Battery Stack and Membrane Recycling]
    H --> I[stepI: Social and Environmental Impact Scorecard]
    I --> J[stepJ: Net Impact Reduction vs ICE Baseline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **only tailpipe product** of a hydrogen FCEV is **H₂O (water vapour)**. However, the **upstream production** of hydrogen via Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) currently emits **~9–10 kg CO₂ per kg H₂** unless it is **green hydrogen** (renewable-powered electrolysis, < 1 kg CO₂/kg H₂).

## 4.2 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparative Impact Flow

This Mermaid block diagram contrasts the **impact-flow architecture** across ICE, HEV, BEV, and FCEV powertrains.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ICE [ICE Powertrain]
        A1[OIL WELL] --> A2[REFINERY]
        A2 --> A3[PETROL DIESEL TANK]
        A3 --> A4[ENGINE COMBUSTION]
        A4 --> A5[TAILPIPE CO2 NOX PM]
    end
    subgraph HEV [Hybrid Powertrain]
        B1[OIL WELL] --> B2[REFINERY]
        B2 --> B3[FUEL TANK SMALLER]
        B3 --> B4[ICE PLUS BATTERY MOTOR]
        B4 --> B5[REDUCED EMISSIONS PLUS REGEN]
    end
    subgraph BEV [Battery EV Powertrain]
        C1[POWER PLANT GRID MIX] --> C2[TRANSMISSION LINES]
        C2 --> C3[CHARGING POINT]
        C3 --> C4[BATTERY ELECTRIC MOTOR]
        C4 --> C5[ZERO TAILPIPE EMISSIONS]
    end
    subgraph FCEV [Fuel Cell EV Powertrain]
        D1[GREEN H2 PRODUCTION ELECTROLYSIS] --> D2[H2 STORAGE 700 BAR]
        D2 --> D3[FUEL CELL STACK PEM]
        D3 --> D4[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
        D4 --> D5[TAILPIPE H2O ONLY]
    end
```

## 4.3 Diagram: WTW vs CTG — Conceptual Boundary Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WTT [Well to Tank Upstream]
        X1[CRUDE EXTRACTION]
        X2[REFINING]
        X3[FUEL TRANSPORT]
    end
    subgraph TTW [Tank to Wheel Vehicle]
        Y1[FUEL STORAGE TANK]
        Y2[POWERTRAIN CONVERSION]
        Y3[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph CTG [Cradle to Grave Life Cycle]
        Z1[RAW MATERIAL]
        Z2[MANUFACTURING]
        Z3[USE PHASE]
        Z4[RECYCLING]
    end
    WTT --> TTW
    CTG --- WTT
    CTG --- TTW
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** WTW is a **subset** of CTG. CTG additionally includes the **manufacturing footprint** of the vehicle itself (a BEV's battery pack carries a **~75–100 g CO₂-eq/km** manufacturing debt that is paid back over 20,000–50,000 km of clean driving).

## 4.4 Diagram: Social-Environmental Impact Scorecard (Hierarchical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Impact Assessment] --> P1[Environmental Subscore]
    P --> P2[Social Subscore]
    P --> P3[Economic Subscore]
    P1 --> P1a[GHG Emissions]
    P1 --> P1b[Air Quality PM NOX]
    P1 --> P1c[Noise Pollution]
    P1 --> P1d[Resource Depletion]
    P2 --> P2a[Public Health]
    P2 --> P2b[Energy Security]
    P2 --> P2c[Employment]
    P2 --> P2d[Equity and Access]
    P3 --> P3a[Import Bill]
    P3 --> P3b[Total Cost of Ownership]
    P3 --> P3c[Job Creation Net]
    P3 --> P3d[Technology Trade Balance]
```
