# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Define **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** analysis. Why is it considered more accurate than Tailpipe (TTW) alone for environmental assessment?

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):**
> Well-to-Wheel (WTW) analysis quantifies the total energy consumed and emissions produced from the **primary energy source** (crude oil well, solar farm, or hydrogen production plant) all the way to the **wheel interface** of the vehicle. It comprises **Well-to-Tank (WTT)** + **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)**. Unlike TTW alone, WTW captures **upstream emissions** such as oil extraction, refining, electricity generation, and hydrogen electrolysis. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** List **three social impacts** of transitioning from ICE vehicles to Electric Vehicles (BEVs).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Reduced urban air pollution**, leading to lower incidence of respiratory diseases (WHO estimates ~4.2 million annual premature deaths). *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Improved energy security** through reduced crude-oil import dependence (India imports ~85 % of its crude). *(1 mark)*
> 3. **New employment generation** in clean-tech sectors (battery manufacturing, charging infrastructure, software) offsetting some ICE supply-chain job losses. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one full question of 14 marks** per module, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both choices are provided below.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **Cradle-to-Grave (CTG)** life-cycle assessment of an electric vehicle, listing **five distinct stages** and the primary environmental impact at each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mid-size BEV consumes **16 kWh per 100 km**. Compute its **WTW CO₂ emission (g/km)** when charged from (i) India's grid mix (**720 g CO₂/kWh**), and (ii) a renewable-heavy grid (**50 g CO₂/kWh**). Comment on the social-environmental significance. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

The Cradle-to-Grave assessment of a BEV proceeds through the following five stages:

1. **Raw Material Extraction (1.5 marks):** Mining of lithium (Chile, Australia), cobalt (DRC), nickel (Indonesia), and graphite. Each kWh of battery carries **~150–200 kg CO₂-eq** in extraction and refining.

2. **Component Manufacturing (1.5 marks):** Battery cell production (cell formation, electrolyte filling) and electric motor (copper, rare-earth permanent magnets). PEM fuel cells additionally require **platinum** (5–10 g per stack).

3. **Vehicle Assembly (1 mark):** Body-in-white stamping, painting (VOC emissions), and final assembly. Estimated at **~6–8 tonnes CO₂-eq** per vehicle for the manufacturing phase.

4. **Use Phase (1.5 marks):** Electricity consumption governed by driving cycle. Grid carbon intensity dictates operational emissions. A 16 kWh/100 km BEV on a clean grid can operate at **<10 g CO₂/km**.

5. **End-of-Life (1.5 marks):** Battery recycling (hydrometallurgical, pyrometallurgical) and second-life applications (grid storage). EU regulations mandate **>90 % recyclability** by mass.

**Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $E_{\text{cons}} = 16$ kWh/100 km $= 0.16$ kWh/km.

**Case (i):** India grid mix $\text{EF} = 720$ g CO₂/kWh.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{WTW, BEV, India}} &= 0.16 \times 720 \\
&= 115.2 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{WTW, India}} \approx 115 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}\;}$$

**Case (ii):** Renewable-heavy grid $\text{EF} = 50$ g CO₂/kWh.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{WTW, BEV, Renewable}} &= 0.16 \times 50 \\
&= 8.0 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{WTW, Renewable}} \approx 8 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}\;}$$

**Comment (2 marks):** The Indian BEV's WTW CO₂ of 115 g/km is **comparable to a modern hybrid** (~100 g/km) but **~25 % lower than a petrol sedan** (~150 g/km). On a renewable grid, the BEV becomes **~95 % cleaner** than the same petrol car. The social-environmental significance is therefore **grid-dependent**: India must accelerate renewable energy deployment alongside EV adoption to realize the full environmental dividend. *(2 marks)*

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – 2024 ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental and social impacts of conventional ICE vehicles**, with reference to **greenhouse gas emissions, urban air quality, and energy security**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A diesel truck has $P_b = 250$ kW, $\eta_{\text{th}} = 0.38$, LHV of diesel = **42.5 MJ/kg**, $C_{\text{frac}} = 0.847$. Calculate (i) the fuel mass flow rate, (ii) the CO₂ emission rate in g/s, and (iii) the TTW CO₂ in g/km if the truck achieves 3.5 km/L with $\rho_f = 0.85$ kg/L. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Greenhouse Gas Emissions (2.5 marks):** A typical diesel truck emits **~800–1000 g CO₂/km** — about **5× a passenger car**. Globally, road transport contributes **~15–18 % of total GHG emissions** (IPCC AR6), and heavy-duty vehicles account for **~40 % of road-transport CO₂** despite being only ~5 % of vehicle population.

**Urban Air Quality (2.5 marks):** Diesel engines emit **PM2.5** (causing ~4.2 million premature deaths/year per WHO) and **NOₓ** (smog precursor, acid rain). Cities like Delhi, Beijing, and Mexico City routinely exceed WHO PM2.5 limits by **5–10×**, with road transport as the dominant source.

**Energy Security (2 marks):** The transport sector consumes **~28 % of global final energy**, almost entirely from petroleum. India imports **~85 %** of crude oil, creating a strategic vulnerability exposed during the 1973 and 1979 oil crises and the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict.

**Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Fuel mass flow rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} &= \dfrac{P_b}{\text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}} \\
&= \dfrac{250 \times 10^3}{42.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.38} \\
&= \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.615 \times 10^7} \\
&= 1.548 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \approx 0.0155 \text{ kg/s} = 55.7 \text{ kg/h}\;}$$

**(ii) CO₂ emission rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} &= \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot C_{\text{frac}} \cdot 3.667 \\
&= 1.548 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.847 \times 3.667 \\
&= 4.806 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 48.1 \text{ g/s}\;}$$

**(iii) TTW CO₂ in g/km:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel per km} &= \dfrac{\rho_f}{F} = \dfrac{0.85}{3.5} = 0.2429 \text{ kg/km} \\
E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2} &= 0.2429 \times 0.847 \times 3.667 \times 1000 \\
&= 754.0 \text{ g/km}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{TTW,CO}_2} \approx 754 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}\;}$$

This matches the **typical 700–900 g/km range** for heavy-duty diesel trucks — a strong motivation for **hydrogen FCEV** or **battery-electric heavy trucks** (Tesla Semi, BharatBenz e-trucks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students frequently forget to convert **MJ to kJ** or **kW to W** in fuel-flow calculations, leading to a **−3 to −5 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3.667 Factor:** The carbon-to-CO₂ molar mass ratio (44/12) is the **single most-missed constant** in this topic. Memorize it.
> 3. **Confusing WTW with CTG:** Many answers state that BEVs are "zero-emission." This is **incomplete** — they are **TTW zero-emission** but have **WTT/CTG emissions**.
> 4. **Ignoring Grid Mix:** A BEV on a coal grid is **not** a "zero-carbon" vehicle. Always specify the **EF_grid**.
> 5. **Formula Tables Without `\vert`:** Using bare pipes in markdown tables **breaks the KTU PDF export pipeline**. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin this up before ESE):**
>
> - **WTW = WTT + TTW**; CTG ⊃ WTW (broader, includes manufacturing & EoL).
> - **Universal CO₂ constant:** 3.667 (ratio of molar masses 44/12).
> - Carbon mass fraction $C_{\text{frac}}$: petrol ≈ 0.842, diesel ≈ 0.847, methane ≈ 0.750, methanol ≈ 0.375.
> - ICE: high TTW emissions, mature tech, oil-dependent.
> - **HEV:** 30–40 % lower CO₂, regenerative braking, no plug-in.
> - **BEV:** Zero TTW, **grid-dependent WTW**, battery manufacturing footprint ~75–100 g CO₂/km (paid back over 20,000–50,000 km).
> - **FCEV:** Zero TTW (only H₂O), but **green-hydrogen production is the key bottleneck** (SMR emits ~9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ vs. < 1 kg for green electrolysis).
> - **WHO 4.2 M** annual premature deaths from air pollution; **PM2.5** is the silent killer.
> - **Energy security:** India imports ~85 % crude → EV/FCEV transition is a **strategic national priority**.
> - **Just Transition:** ~10 M clean-tech jobs created vs. ~5 M ICE jobs displaced globally by 2030.
> - **Public Health Dividend:** Reduction in PM2.5 and NOₓ → direct healthcare cost savings.
> - **GWP-100 (Global Warming Potential, 100-year):** CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265, CO₂ = 1 (IPCC AR5).
> - **Euro 6/BS-VI** norms are the regulatory embodiment of these impact metrics.
> - **Always** compute **both TTW and WTT** in numericals — KTU examiners mark **both stages** if asked.
> - Remember the **g/km** unit convention: 1 ppm CO₂ ≈ **2.13 g/m³**; WTW CO₂ of a car in g/km ranges **8 (BEV-clean) to 1000 (heavy diesel)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hybrid Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hybrid Electric Drive-Train (HEDT):** A hybrid electric drive-train is an automotive propulsion system that combines **two or more distinct energy sources and/or energy converters** — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EMs)** — coupled through a mechanical/electrical transmission to deliver tractive power to the driving wheels. The term *"hybrid"* specifically refers to the **mixed use of two propulsion energy paths**: 1. **Thermal path** — chemical fuel (petrol/diesel/CNG) → ICE → mechanical power. 2. **Electrical path** — battery/ultracapacitor → inverter → electric motor → mechanical power. > [!NOTE] > **Hybrid Traction** is the engineering discipline of *coordinating and controlling* these two energy paths in real time to satisfy the vehicle's tractive power demand while minimizing fuel consumption, emissions, and energy storage stress. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > Imagine you are carrying a heavy suitcase up a hill. > - Your **legs** = the ICE (strong, steady, but slow to start and inefficient at low effort). > - Your **arms holding the handle from above** = the Electric Motor (instant response, strong at low speeds, but limited stamina). > - Your **brain** = the Hybrid Controller (decides *when* to use legs, arms, or both). > > When the suitcase is light → only arms (motor) work. > When the slope is steep → both legs and arms (ICE + Motor) push together. > When going downhill → arms *regenerate* by holding the suitcase back, charging a "muscle battery." This real-time co-operation of two energy sources — with energy recovery during deceleration — is the **basic concept of hybrid traction**. ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Symbols) | Symbol | Metric | Typical Range (Modern HEV) | |---|---|---| | **HF** | Hybridization Factor | **0.1 – 0.9** | | **SOC** | State of Charge (battery) | **0.3 – 0.8** | | **η_dis** | Battery discharge efficiency | **0.85 – 0.95** | | **η_chg** | Battery charge efficiency | **0.85 – 0.95** | | **η_ice** | ICE peak brake thermal efficiency | **0.35 – 0.42** | | **η_em** | Electric machine efficiency | **0.90 – 0.96** | | **η_regen** | Regenerative braking efficiency | **0.50 – 0.75** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary condition for a true Hybrid (KTU definition):** A vehicle is classified as a *hybrid* only if **at least two distinct energy storage devices** are present — one of which must be **electrical**. A pure battery EV is **NOT** a hybrid. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why is Hybridization Needed? (The Engineering Motivation) A conventional ICE vehicle suffers from three fundamental inefficiencies that hybrid traction directly addresses: 1. **Low efficiency at part-load:** A gasoline ICE delivers peak thermal efficiency only near its wide-open-throttle sweet spot (~2000–3000 rpm). During city driving, average efficiency drops below **15–20 %**. 2. **Idling losses:** Stopped at a traffic signal, the ICE burns fuel but produces no useful work. 3. **Braking losses:** Up to **30–60 %** of the traction energy supplied by the fuel is dissipated as heat in friction brakes during typical urban driving. > [!NOTE] > The **electric motor** solves all three: high efficiency across a wide speed-torque map, zero fuel use when stationary (motor off), and regenerative braking to recover kinetic energy. ### 2.2 The Three Foundational Architectures of Hybrid Traction | Configuration | Mechanical Coupling | Energy Path | Representative Vehicle | |---|---|---|---| | **Series Hybrid** | None — ICE *only* drives a generator | ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels | Chevrolet Volt (1st gen), Nissan e-Power | | **Parallel Hybrid** | Direct mechanical link (clutch/gears) between ICE and wheels | ICE → Wheels **AND** Motor → Wheels | Honda Civic Hybrid (1st gen) | | **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** | Planetary gear set + 2 motors | Both paths simultaneously | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape Hybrid | ### 2.3 Operating Modes of a Generic Hybrid Drive-Train A hybrid controller (often called the **Energy Management Strategy — EMS**) cycles between the following discrete modes: 1. **Pure Electric Mode:** ICE OFF; EM alone propels the vehicle (low-speed, low-demand). 2. **Pure ICE Mode:** EM OFF (or acting as generator); ICE alone drives the wheels (steady cruise). 3. **Hybrid (Boost) Mode:** Both ICE and EM deliver positive torque to the wheels (peak demand, hill climbing). 4. **ICE Charging Mode:** ICE drives both the wheels and the generator to charge the battery (low-demand steady driving). 5. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** ICE OFF, friction brakes supplemented; EM acts as a **generator** converting wheel kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery. 6. **Engine Start/Stop Mode:** ICE automatically shuts off at vehicle standstill and restarts on demand. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Hybrid Traction (Examination Ready) | # | Equation / Relationship | Variables | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{traction} = P_{ice} + P_{em} - P_{aux} - P_{loss}$ | Power at wheels | kW | Power balance at the drive-shaft | | 2 | $T_{wheel} = (T_{ice} \cdot \eta_{gear}) + (T_{em} \cdot \eta_{em})$ | Torque at wheels | N·m | Torque superposition (parallel hybrid) | | 3 | $\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{ice}}{i_{gear1}} = \dfrac{\omega_{em}}{i_{gear2}}$ | Angular speeds | rad/s | Speed coupling constraint | | 4 | $HF = \dfrac{P_{em}}{P_{em} + P_{ice}}$ | Hybridization Factor | dimensionless | Degree of electrification (0 = pure ICE, 1 = pure EV) | | 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ice} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{dis} \cdot \eta_{chg}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless | Cumulative efficiency of energy path | | 6 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{ice}}{\eta_{ice} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | Fuel mass-flow rate | kg/s | ICE fuel consumption | | 7 | $\Delta SOC = \dfrac{\int \left( P_{chg}\cdot\eta_{chg} - \dfrac{P_{dis}}{\eta_{dis}} \right) dt}{E_{batt}}$ | State-of-charge change | dimensionless | Battery energy bookkeeping | | 8 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m_{veh} \cdot \left( v_1^2 - v_2^2 \right) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Recovered kinetic energy | J | Regenerative braking energy | | 9 | $LHV_{gasoline} \approx 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value of gasoline | MJ/kg | **Standard metric** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU valuation, examiners award marks **only when equations are accompanied by a clearly defined meaning** of each variable. Memorising the equation alone without units or limits will fetch partial credit only. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Hybrid Torque Coupling Equation **Problem Statement:** Derive the wheel-torque expression for a parallel hybrid drive-train in which an ICE and an EM are mechanically coupled to the same final-drive shaft through separate gear ratios $i_1$ and $i_2$ respectively. **Given:** - ICE shaft speed: $\omega_{ice}$ - EM shaft speed: $\omega_{em}$ - Final drive ratio: $i_f$ - Wheel radius: $r_w$ - Wheel speed: $\omega_w$ **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply the speed coupling constraint.** The two machines rotate independently but drive a common shaft through fixed gear ratios. $$ \omega_w \cdot i_f = \omega_{ice} \cdot i_1 = \omega_{em} \cdot i_2 $$ **Step 2 — Express individual machine torques referred to the final-drive shaft.** The ICE torque, referred to the final-drive shaft, is amplified by the gear ratio $i_1$ (with efficiency loss $\eta_1$): $$ T_{ice,shaft} = T_{ice} \cdot i_1 \cdot \eta_1 $$ Similarly, the EM torque referred to the same shaft: $$ T_{em,shaft} = T_{em} \cdot i_2 \cdot \eta_2 $$ **Step 3 — Sum the torques at the final-drive shaft.** Because the two torque paths are mechanically parallel, torques **add directly** at the common node: $$ T_{shaft} = T_{ice} \cdot i_1 \cdot \eta_1 \;+\; T_{em} \cdot i_2 \cdot \eta_2 $$ **Step 4 — Reflect the shaft torque to the wheels.** $$ T_{wheel} = T_{shaft} \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_f $$ **Step 5 — Final combined expression.** $$ \boxed{\,T_{wheel} \;=\; \left( T_{ice} \cdot i_1 \cdot \eta_1 \;+\; T_{em} \cdot i_2 \cdot \eta_2 \right) \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_f\,} $$ **Interpretation:** Wheel torque is a **linear superposition** of ICE torque and EM torque, scaled by their respective gear ratios and efficiencies. This is the *fundamental mathematical identity* that distinguishes a parallel hybrid from a series hybrid (where ICE and wheels are never mechanically connected). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Hybridization Factor (HF) **Problem Statement:** Define and derive the dimensionless hybridization factor for an HEV. **Step 1 — Define the peak rated mechanical power of each propulsion source.** - $P_{ice}$: peak brake power of the internal combustion engine (kW). - $P_{em}$: peak mechanical output of the electric machine (kW). **Step 2 — Form the ratio of electrical power to the total peak propulsion power.** $$ HF \;=\; \dfrac{P_{em}}{P_{ice} + P_{em}} $$ **Step 3 — Boundary verification.** - If $P_{em} = 0$ (pure ICE vehicle) $\Rightarrow HF = 0$. - If $P_{ice} = 0$ (pure BEV — *technically not a hybrid by KTU definition*) $\Rightarrow HF = 1$. **Step 4 — KTU classification by HF value.** $$ HF < 0.05 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Micro-hybrid (start-stop only)} $$ $$ 0.05 \leq HF < 0.3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Mild hybrid} $$ $$ 0.3 \leq HF < 0.9 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Full hybrid} $$ $$ HF \geq 0.9 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Plug-in hybrid (PHEV)} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery **Step 1 — Kinetic energy of the moving vehicle before braking.** $$ E_{kinetic,1} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m_{veh} \cdot v_1^2 $$ **Step 2 — Kinetic energy after braking to speed $v_2$.** $$ E_{kinetic,2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m_{veh} \cdot v_2^2 $$ **Step 3 — Energy available for recovery (ideal, ignoring losses).** $$ E_{available} = E_{kinetic,1} - E_{kinetic,2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m_{veh} \cdot \left( v_1^2 - v_2^2 \right) $$ **Step 4 — Apply regenerative efficiency $\eta_{regen}$ to obtain actual stored energy.** $$ \boxed{\,E_{regen} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m_{veh} \cdot \left( v_1^2 - v_2^2 \right) \cdot \eta_{regen}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This recovered energy is the **single largest fuel-saving contributor** in urban drive-cycles (FTP-75, NEDC, WLTP-class 3). In KTU problems, $\eta_{regen}$ is typically taken in the range **0.5 – 0.75** unless specified otherwise. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Series vs Parallel vs Series-Parallel | Criterion | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid | Series-Parallel | |---|---|---|---| | Mechanical link ICE-Wheels | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes (via planetary)** | | ICE can directly propel wheels | No (always via generator) | Yes | Yes | | Number of EMs | 1 (large traction) | 1 (traction) | 2 (MG1 + MG2) | | Transmission complexity | Low | Medium | High (planetary + 2 MG) | | City-cycle fuel economy | **Excellent** | Good | **Excellent** | | Highway-cruise efficiency | Poor (double conversion) | Excellent | Excellent | | Typical HF | 0.5 – 0.8 | 0.1 – 0.4 | 0.3 – 0.6 | | Representative example | **Chevrolet Volt** | Honda Insight (1st) | **Toyota Prius** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Three Hybrid Configurations The following Mermaid diagram maps the *energy and mechanical coupling pathways* for the three foundational hybrid traction architectures. ```mermaid flowchart LR fuel[Fuel Tank]:::energy batt[HV Battery]:::energy ice[Internal Combustion Engine]:::machine gen[Generator / MG1]:::machine mot[Traction Motor / MG2]:::machine trans[Transmission and Final Drive]:::mech wheel[Driving Wheels]:::mech ctrl[Hybrid Control Unit ECU]:::ctrl subgraph SeriesPath[Series Hybrid Path] direction LR fuelS[Fuel] --> iceS[ICE] iceS -- mechanical --> genS[Generator] genS -- electrical --> battS[Battery] battS -- DC link --> motS[Traction Motor] motS -- mechanical --> wheelS[Wheels] end subgraph ParallelPath[Parallel Hybrid Path] direction LR fuelP[Fuel] --> iceP[ICE] battP[HV Battery] --> motP[Traction Motor] iceP -- clutch and gears --> transP[Common Transmission] motP -- gears --> transP transP --> wheelP[Wheels] end subgraph SplitPath[Series-Parallel Power-Split Hybrid] direction LR fuelX[Fuel] --> iceX[ICE] iceX --> planet[Planetary Gear Set] genX[Generator MG1] motX[Traction Motor MG2] battX[HV Battery] -- DC --> invX[Inverter] invX --> genX invX --> motX genX --> planet motX -- chain or gear --> wheelX[Wheels] planet -- ring gear --> wheelX end ctrl -. manages .-> ice ctrl -. manages .-> gen ctrl -. manages .-> mot ctrl -. monitors SOC .-> batt classDef energy fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,stroke-width:2px,color:#5a4500 classDef machine fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366 classDef mech fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px,color:#222222 classDef ctrl fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.2 Hybrid Operating Mode State Machine (Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Vehicle Ignition ON]) --> checkDemand{Traction
Power Demand P_dem} checkDemand -- "P_dem = 0
(Stop)" --> sstop[ICE Stop Mode
EM Idle] checkDemand -- "P_dem low
(P_dem <= P_em_max)" --> electric[Pure Electric Mode
ICE OFF, EM drives] checkDemand -- "P_dem medium
(P_ice_opt region)" --> icecruise[Pure ICE Mode
ICE drives wheels] checkDemand -- "P_dem high
(Peak demand)" --> boost[Hybrid Boost Mode
ICE + EM parallel] sstop --> checkDemand electric --> checkDemand icecruise --> checkDemand boost --> checkDemand icecruise -- "if SOC low
(SOC < SOC_min)" --> charge[ICE Charging Mode
ICE drives wheels + generator] charge --> checkDemand electric -- "if SOC low
(SOC < SOC_min)" --> icecruise boost -- "if SOC high
(SOC > SOC_max)" --> icecruise checkDemand -- "Brake pedal pressed" --> regen[Regenerative Braking Mode
EM as generator] regen -- "Brake released" --> checkDemand ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip (for KTU viva):** Always trace **three entities** on every hybrid block diagram — (1) the *fuel/energy arrow*, (2) the *mechanical shaft*, and (3) the *electrical cable*. If any two are missing on a path, that path is not contributing tractive power at that instant. ### 4.3 Energy Flow Block for a Parallel Hybrid (Text Schematic) ``` +----------------------+ +------------------------+ | Fuel Tank | | HV Battery Pack | | (Chemical Energy) | | (Electrochemical E) | +----------+-----------+ +-----------+------------+ | | v v +----------------------+ +------------------------+ | I C E | | Traction Motor (EM) | | (Thermal -> Mech) | | (Electrical -> Mech) | +----------+-----------+ +-----------+------------+ | | | Clutch C1 engaged | +---------------+----------------+ | v +----------------------+ | Common Transmission| | + Final Drive | +----------+-----------+ | v +----------------------+ | Driving Wheels | +----------------------+ | Regenerative Braking path: Wheels --> EM (now generator) --> Inverter --> Battery ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]** **Q1. Define a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV). List the main components of a hybrid electric drive-train.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A hybrid electric vehicle is an automobile that uses **two or more distinct energy sources** — typically a combination of an **internal combustion engine (ICE)** and one or more **electric machines** — to deliver tractive power to the wheels, with at least one energy storage device being **electrical** (battery or ultracapacitor). Main components of a hybrid electric drive-train: 1. **Internal Combustion Engine** — primary thermal source. 2. **Electric Motor/Generator** — secondary electric source. 3. **Energy Storage System** — high-voltage battery / ultracapacitor. 4. **Power Electronics** — inverter, DC-DC converter. 5. **Transmission / Coupling Device** — clutch, planetary gear set, or CVT. 6. **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU / ECU)** — supervisory controller. --- **Q2. State and briefly explain the hybridization factor (HF).** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The **Hybridization Factor (HF)** is a dimensionless metric that quantifies the *degree of electrification* of a hybrid drive-train: $$ HF = \dfrac{P_{em}}{P_{ice} + P_{em}} $$ where $P_{em}$ and $P_{ice}$ are the peak mechanical power ratings of the electric machine and the ICE respectively. - **HF = 0** → pure conventional ICE vehicle. - **HF between 0.05 and 0.3** → mild hybrid (e.g., start-stop + light assist). - **HF between 0.3 and 0.9** → full hybrid (e.g., Toyota Prius). - **HF ≥ 0.9** → plug-in hybrid (PHEV). It is used by KTU examiners to *classify* the architecture of an HEV numerically. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024 Scheme, Module 1]** > *(Answer ANY ONE full question from the following:)* --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the basic concept of hybrid traction in an automobile. Discuss its social and environmental necessity.** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Definition & Concept:** Hybrid traction is the coordinated use of **two distinct energy conversion paths** — a thermal path (fuel → ICE) and an electrical path (battery → EM) — to meet the instantaneous tractive power demand $P_{dem}(t)$ of a vehicle. The fundamental idea is to **operate each energy converter near its peak-efficiency operating point** by load-sharing them optimally in real time. The supervisory controller — the **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** — performs this load allocation based on driver demand, vehicle speed, and battery SOC. **2. Block Diagram (Parallel Hybrid):** ``` Fuel Tank ----> ICE ----+ | HV Battery --> Inverter --> EM ----+ | v Common Transmission | v Driving Wheels (Regen path: Wheels --> EM --> Battery) ``` **3. Social and Environmental Necessity:** - **Urban air quality:** CO, NOx, PM emissions from ICEs are major contributors to urban PM2.5 levels. WHO data links vehicular pollution to ~7 million premature deaths/year globally. - **CO₂ and climate:** Transport contributes ~24 % of global CO₂ emissions; hybrid traction can reduce fuel use by **20–40 %** in urban cycles. - **Oil security:** Reducing petroleum dependence for import-heavy economies (India imports >85 % of its crude). - **Noise pollution:** Pure-electric low-speed operation drastically reduces urban noise. **(b) With the help of a torque/speed diagram, describe the operating modes of a parallel hybrid electric vehicle.** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** A parallel HEV can operate in the following **six discrete modes**, governed by the HCU: | # | Mode | ICE State | EM State | When Used | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pure Electric | OFF | Motoring | Low-speed launch, SOC adequate | | 2 | Pure ICE | ON (drives) | OFF or idle | Highway cruise, SOC adequate | | 3 | Hybrid Boost | ON (drives) | Motoring (assist) | Hard acceleration, hill climb | | 4 | ICE Charging | ON (drives + genset) | Generating | Steady cruise, SOC low | | 5 | Regen Braking | OFF | Generating | Deceleration, braking | | 6 | Start/Stop | Auto OFF at idle | Idle | Traffic stops | **Torque-Speed Coordination:** - ICE operates in its **high-efficiency band** (typically 1500–3000 rpm, 60–80 % load). - EM fills the **torque gap** during transients where the ICE is inefficient. - During braking, the EM acts as a generator; the brake controller blends friction and regenerative torques. The combined torque available at the wheels is: $$ T_{wheel} = \left( T_{ice} \cdot i_1 \cdot \eta_1 + T_{em} \cdot i_2 \cdot \eta_2 \right) \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_f $$ --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a series-parallel (power-split) hybrid drive-train. How does the planetary gear set enable power-splitting?** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Architecture:** The series-parallel hybrid uses **two electric machines** (MG1 and MG2) plus an **ICE** mechanically coupled through a **planetary gear set (PGS)**. The PGS has three nodes: the sun gear, the planet carrier (connected to ICE), and the ring gear (connected to wheels via chain/gear). MG1 is mounted on the sun gear; MG2 is mounted coaxially with the ring gear. **Block Diagram:** ``` Fuel --> ICE --> Carrier (Planetary) | | Sun Ring | | MG1 MG2 (Gen) (Traction Motor) | | Inverter <-- HV Battery | Wheels ``` **2. Power-Split Mechanism:** The planetary gear set enforces a kinematic constraint among the three nodes: $$ \left(1 + k\right) \cdot \omega_{carrier} = \omega_{sun} + k \cdot \omega_{ring} $$ where $k$ = number of ring-gear teeth / number of sun-gear teeth. Because the **carrier speed is fixed by the ICE** and the **ring-gear speed is fixed by the wheels**, the sun-gear speed (and hence MG1's speed) is **automatically determined**. This means: - MG1 acts as a **continuously variable transmission (CVT)** — adjusting its torque splits the ICE power between a *mechanical path* (to wheels) and an *electrical path* (through MG1 → battery → MG2 → wheels). - The system behaves like a **series hybrid at low speeds** and a **parallel hybrid at high speeds**, transitioning smoothly — hence the name *power-split*. **(b) Derive the power-flow equation for a hybrid electric vehicle and explain the concept of regenerative braking energy.** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Power-Flow Derivation:** At any instant, the tractive power at the wheels is the algebraic sum of contributions from both energy paths minus losses: $$ P_{wheel} = \left( P_{ice} \cdot \eta_{ice,mech} \cdot \eta_{gear} \right) + \left( P_{batt} \cdot \eta_{dis} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{inv} \right) - P_{aux} $$ where $P_{aux}$ = power consumed by accessories (HVAC, lights, ECU). In regenerative mode (braking), $P_{batt}$ is negative (energy flows *into* the battery), and $P_{ice} = 0$ (engine off). Hence: $$ P_{wheel} = -P_{regen} \cdot \eta_{regen} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \dfrac{m_{veh}\cdot \left(v_1^2 - v_2^2\right)}{t_{brake}} \cdot \eta_{regen} $$ **2. Concept of Regenerative Braking Energy:** During conventional braking, kinetic energy $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ is dissipated as heat in friction pads. In a hybrid, the electric machine is **reversed electrically** to act as a generator, converting wheel kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery. For a 1500 kg vehicle decelerating from 60 km/h (16.67 m/s) to 0: $$ E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (16.67)^2 \approx 208.4 \text{ kJ} $$ At $\eta_{regen} = 0.65$, recovered energy = **135.5 kJ** — equivalent to ~3.5 g of gasoline, illustrating the cumulative fuel-saving potential over city cycles. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in "Hybrid Traction" questions:** > > 1. **Confusing series vs parallel topology:** A *very common* mistake is drawing a series hybrid where the ICE drives the wheels directly. In a series hybrid, the ICE is **mechanically decoupled from the wheels** — it only drives a generator. Lose up to **2 marks** for this. > > 2. **Forgetting units in formula sheets:** KTU award 1 mark per correctly cited equation *with units*. Submitting $P_{wheel} = P_{ice} + P_{em}$ without indicating kW will lose that mark. > > 3. **Ignoring efficiencies:** Writing $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ without $\eta_{regen}$ is treated as *theoretical maximum*, not *actual recovered energy*. Always multiply by $\eta_{regen}$. > > 4. **Wrong classification by HF:** Many students classify $HF = 0.5$ as "mild hybrid." It is **full hybrid** by the standard $0.3 \leq HF < 0.9$ band. Lose 1 mark. > > 5. **No block diagram on 7-mark questions:** A 7-mark question without a labelled block diagram loses at least **2 marks** in valuation — KTU examiners expect a schematic. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hybrid Electric Drive-Trains (Module 1)** - [x] **Hybrid Traction =** coordinated use of **two distinct energy paths** (thermal + electrical). - [x] A vehicle is a *true hybrid* only if **at least two distinct energy storage devices** exist, with one being **electrical**. - [x] Three core architectures: **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split)**. - [x] **Series Hybrid** → ICE drives generator → battery → motor → wheels. *No mechanical link* between ICE and wheels. - [x] **Parallel Hybrid** → ICE and EM *both* mechanically linked to wheels; torques **add**. - [x] **Power-Split** → Planetary gear set with **MG1 (sun)** and **MG2 (ring)**; ICE on carrier. Acts as series at low speed, parallel at high speed. - [x] **Hybridization Factor (HF) = $P_{em} / (P_{ice} + P_{em})$** — used to classify micro/mild/full/PHEV. - [x] **Operating Modes (6):** Pure Electric, Pure ICE, Hybrid Boost, ICE Charging, Regenerative Braking, Start-Stop. - [x] **Regenerative Braking Energy:** $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{veh} (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$. - [x] **Power balance equation:** $P_{wheel} = P_{ice}\eta_{ice,mech}\eta_{gear} + P_{batt}\eta_{dis}\eta_{em}\eta_{inv} - P_{aux}$. - [x] **Fuel consumption equation:** $\dot{m}_{fuel} = P_{ice} / (\eta_{ice} \cdot LHV_{fuel})$, where $LHV_{gasoline} \approx 44$ MJ/kg. - [x] **Standard efficiencies (memorise for KTU):** $\eta_{ice} \approx 0.35\text{–}0.42$, $\eta_{em} \approx 0.90\text{–}0.96$, $\eta_{regen} \approx 0.50\text{–}0.75$. - [x] **Always draw a labelled block diagram** on 7-mark hybrid architecture questions — it is a mandatory scoring element. - [x] **Toyota Prius =** canonical *power-split*; **Honda Insight (1st gen) =** canonical *parallel*; **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen) =** canonical *series*. - [x] **Social motivation:** CO₂ reduction (~24 % of global emissions from transport), urban air quality, oil import reduction.

topics.title: Introduction to Various Hybrid Drive-train Topologies (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Hybrid Drive-train Topologies (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Hybrid Drive-Train Topology

A **Hybrid Drive-Train Topology** refers to the *specific architectural arrangement* of the prime mover (Internal Combustion Engine - ICE), electric machines (Motor/Generator), energy storage system (Battery/Ultracapacitor), and the mechanical transmission that interconnects them to deliver tractive power to the driven wheels of a vehicle.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCAUT601), hybrid vehicle architectures are broadly classified by how the ICE and the electric motor(s) are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels and to each other. The three canonical topologies studied in Module 1 are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAUT601 / Module 1)**
> - **Series Hybrid:** ICE is *mechanically decoupled* from the wheels; it drives a generator only. The wheels are driven purely by the electric motor.
> - **Parallel Hybrid:** ICE and electric motor are *both mechanically coupled* to the wheels, either via a torque coupler or transmission; either or both can deliver tractive power.
> - **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid:** A planetary gear set mechanically couples ICE, generator, motor, and wheels, allowing the vehicle to operate in both series and parallel modes seamlessly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Chefs in a Kitchen"

Imagine a restaurant kitchen that must serve cooked food to customers (the wheels):

- **Series Hybrid → "The Sous-Chef Relay":** One chef (ICE) only prepares ingredients and passes them to a sous-chef (generator), who converts them into electricity. A different chef (electric motor) does the *actual cooking* (driving the wheels). The first chef never touches the stove directly.

- **Parallel Hybrid → "Two Chefs Cooking Together":** Two chefs (ICE and electric motor) stand side by side at the same stove. They can both stir the pot (deliver torque to the wheels) *simultaneously or independently*, depending on demand.

- **Series-Parallel Hybrid → "The Adaptive Kitchen with a Clutch and Conveyor":** A smart conveyor belt (planetary gear set) allows the kitchen to switch between the two modes. Sometimes the sous-chef relay works, sometimes both chefs cook together, depending on the order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Every B.Tech automobile engineering question on hybrid vehicles begins by asking the student to *identify* and *compare* these three topologies based on: (a) mechanical coupling path, (b) efficiency at low/high loads, and (c) typical real-world examples.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics Used at KTU

| Metric | Definition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** | Ratio of electric machine power to total peak powertrain power | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | Mass of fuel consumed per unit brake power per hour | **g/kWh** |
| **State of Charge (SOC)** | Available battery capacity relative to maximum | **%** (0–100) |
| **Regenerative Braking Efficiency (η_regen)** | Recovered kinetic energy / Total braking energy | **%** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Series Hybrid Drive-Train — Operational Logic

In a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE has *no mechanical path* to the wheels. Its sole function is to drive a generator (often called an Integrated Starter Generator — ISG). The generated electrical power feeds:
1. The Traction Motor (which drives the wheels via a fixed-ratio transmission/differential), and
2. The High-Voltage Battery Pack (which buffers energy).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**
- **ICE → Generator:** Mechanical power $P_{ICE-mech}$ is converted to electrical power $P_{gen,elec}$ with generator efficiency $\eta_{gen}$.
- **Generator → Battery/Motor:** Electrical power is split between charging the battery ($P_{bat,ch}$) and powering the traction motor ($P_{motor,elec}$).
- **Motor → Wheels:** The traction motor converts electrical power to mechanical wheel power $P_{wheel}$ with motor efficiency $\eta_{motor}$.
- **Decoupled Operation:** Because ICE speed is independent of wheel speed, ICE can always operate at its **Best Operating Line (BOL)** or **Optimal Efficiency Point (OEP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Series hybrids are ideal for *city buses and delivery vehicles* (stop-and-go traffic) where the ICE can be shut off at stops and run at constant speed to recharge the battery. **Example:** Chevrolet Volt (extended-range EV mode), Range Rover P400e (in EV mode), Volvo S60 T8 Twin Engine.

### 2.2 Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train — Operational Logic

In a **Parallel Hybrid**, the ICE and the electric traction motor are *both mechanically connected* to the driveline via a torque coupler (typically a clutch or planetary gear). Both can deliver torque to the wheels either independently or in combination.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**
- **Mode 1 — ICE only:** For high-speed cruising, the clutch to the motor is open (or the motor is idling), and ICE alone drives the wheels.
- **Mode 2 — Motor only:** For low-speed urban driving or reverse, ICE is off; the motor alone drives the wheels.
- **Mode 3 — Combined (Boost):** For maximum acceleration, both deliver torque simultaneously. Total wheel torque is the sum of individual torques.
- **Mode 4 — Regenerative Braking:** Motor acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy to electrical energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Parallel hybrids excel on *highway driving* where ICE efficiency is naturally high. **Example:** Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA — Integrated Motor Assist), Honda Insight (1st generation).

### 2.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Drive-Train — Operational Logic

This is the **most complex and commercially successful** topology, popularized by the Toyota Hybrid System (THS). A **planetary gear set (PGS)** acts as a continuously variable mechanical transmission (CVT equivalent) that splits ICE power between a path to the wheels and a path to the generator.

The planetary gear has three ports:
- **Sun gear (S)** — Connected to Motor/Generator 1 (MG1)
- **Planet carrier (C)** — Connected to ICE
- **Ring gear (R)** — Connected to Motor/Generator 2 (MG2) and the wheels (via final drive)

**Step-by-step operational logic:**
- **MG1** acts as both generator and motor. By controlling MG1's speed, the ICE speed can be *decoupled* from wheel speed (series mode behavior).
- **MG2** drives the wheels directly.
- The planetary gear naturally splits ICE power: a portion goes to wheels via the carrier-to-ring path (parallel mode behavior), and the rest goes through MG1 to MG2 to wheels (series mode behavior).
- **Result:** Seamless transition between series and parallel operation without a clutch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Series-Parallel hybrids combine the *low-speed efficiency of series* with the *high-speed efficiency of parallel*. **Example:** Toyota Prius (4th Gen), Toyota Camry Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wheel} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans} + P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{motor}$ | Wheel power balance (combined mode) | **kW** |
| 2 | $P_{gen,elec} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ | Generator electrical output (Series) | **kW** |
| 3 | $T_{wheel} = T_{ICE} + T_{motor}$ | Parallel mode torque addition | **Nm** |
| 4 | $T_{wheel} = T_{motor2}$ | Series mode — ICE contributes zero direct torque | **Nm** |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}}{\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}} = -\dfrac{N_s}{N_r}$ | Willis Equation — Planetary gear kinematics | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $DOH = \dfrac{P_{motor,max}}{P_{ICE,max} + P_{motor,max}}$ | Degree of Hybridization | 0–1 |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel,chem}}$ | Tank-to-Wheel efficiency | **%** |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{ \eta_{overall} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | **g/s** |
| 9 | $SOC_{new} = SOC_{old} + \dfrac{\int V_{bat} I_{bat}\, dt}{Q_{bat,max}}$ | Battery state-of-charge update | **%** |
| 10 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{kinetic,recovered}}{E_{kinetic,total}}$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet Usage Tip for KTU Exams:** Equation (5) — the Willis equation — is the *most asked* equation in KTU ESE questions on power-split hybrids. Memorize it with sign convention: sun and ring rotate in *opposite* directions relative to the carrier in a simple planetary set.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These topologies are not merely academic — they form the *decision-making backbone* for automotive OEMs:
- **Series:** Best for **heavy commercial vehicles** (Volvo 7900 Electric Hybrid Bus, Solaris Urbino Hybrid).
- **Parallel:** Best for **light-duty passenger cars** with limited battery budget (Honda IMA systems).
- **Series-Parallel:** Industry default for **mass-market hybrid sedans** (Toyota THS, Hyundai TMED — Transmission Mounted Electric Device).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Planetary Gear Kinematic Relation (Willis Equation)

The **Willis equation** is fundamental to KTU Module 1 questions on power-split hybrids. We derive it below step-by-step.

**Given:**
- A simple planetary gear set with sun gear teeth $N_s$, planet gear teeth $N_p$, and ring gear teeth $N_r$.
- All gears have the same module (pitch); hence tooth count is proportional to pitch radius.

**Step 1:** Define the pitch radii as $r_s$, $r_p$, $r_r$ (for sun, planet, ring respectively). Since $N \propto r$, we have:

$$\dfrac{N_s}{N_r} = \dfrac{r_s}{r_r}$$

**Step 2:** Write the kinematic constraint that the planet gear is *rolling without slipping* on both the sun and the ring simultaneously. The velocity of the planet gear's center equals the velocity of a point on the carrier:

$$v_{carrier} = \omega_{carrier} \cdot (r_s + r_p)$$

**Step 3:** Express the velocity of the same point using the sun gear's rolling contact:

$$v_{sun\ contact} = \omega_{sun} \cdot r_s + \omega_{carrier} \cdot (r_s + r_p) - \omega_{carrier} \cdot r_s$$

After simplification (the planet rotates at angular velocity $\omega_{p} = \omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier} \cdot \dfrac{r_s + r_p}{r_p}$), and substituting back:

$$\omega_{sun} \cdot r_s = \omega_{ring} \cdot r_r + \omega_{carrier} \cdot (r_s + r_r - r_r)$$

Wait, the cleaner textbook derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 4 (Clean Textbook Formulation):** Consider a planet gear rolling on the sun. The relative velocity at the sun-planet contact must be zero:

$$(\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}) \cdot r_s = -(\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}) \cdot r_r$$

The negative sign accounts for the planet's own spin being opposite relative to sun vs. ring rotations.

**Step 5:** Substitute $\dfrac{r_s}{r_r} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_r}$:

$$\dfrac{\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}}{\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}} = -\dfrac{N_s}{N_r}$$

This is the **Willis Equation** for the planetary gear set used in Toyota THS and similar systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Sign Significance:** The negative sign means that, relative to the carrier, the sun and ring rotate in *opposite directions*. For a typical THS ratio $\dfrac{N_s}{N_r} \approx 0.7$, this is the key tuning parameter.

### 3.2 Derivation of Overall Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency

For any hybrid topology, the tank-to-wheel efficiency $\eta_{t2w}$ is the ratio of useful mechanical energy delivered to the wheels to the chemical energy released from fuel.

**Step 1:** Define the ICE fuel energy input rate (using Lower Heating Value — LHV of gasoline $\approx$ **44 MJ/kg**):

$$P_{fuel,chem} = \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV$$

**Step 2:** ICE converts this to mechanical power with thermal efficiency $\eta_{ICE}$:

$$P_{ICE,mech} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot P_{fuel,chem}$$

**Step 3:** Apply transmission efficiency $\eta_{trans}$, motor efficiency $\eta_{motor}$, and generator efficiency $\eta_{gen}$ as appropriate. For a **combined parallel mode**:

$$P_{wheel} = P_{ICE,mech} \cdot \eta_{trans} + P_{motor,elec} \cdot \eta_{motor}$$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$\eta_{t2w} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel,chem}} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans} + \dfrac{P_{motor,elec}}{P_{fuel,chem}} \cdot \eta_{motor}$$

**Step 5:** Recognizing that $P_{motor,elec}$ also originates (in steady state) from fuel via the ICE-generator chain:

$$\eta_{t2w} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans} + \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot k$$

where $k$ is the fraction of fuel power routed through the electric path.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Topology Comparison

| Parameter | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid | Series-Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical ICE–Wheel Path** | None (decoupled) | Yes (via clutch) | Partial (via carrier→ring) |
| **Transmission Complexity** | Simple (1 fixed gear) | Moderate (multi-speed or CVT) | High (planetary + 2 MGs) |
| **ICE Operation Flexibility** | Highest (always at OEP) | Lowest (tied to wheel speed) | High (CVT-like decoupling) |
| **Low-Speed Efficiency** | High | Moderate | High |
| **High-Speed Efficiency** | Moderate | High | High |
| **Component Count** | 1 ICE + 1 Gen + 1 Motor + Battery | 1 ICE + 1 Motor + Battery | 1 ICE + 2 MGs + Battery |
| **Control Complexity** | Low | Moderate | Very High |
| **Cost** | High (large motor) | Lowest | Highest |
| **Best Application** | City buses, delivery vans | Compact cars, light trucks | Mid-size sedans, SUVs |
| **Real-World Example** | Chevrolet Volt (EREV) | Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA) | Toyota Prius (THS-II) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked for a "comparison," examiners expect *at least 4–5 of these rows* in a tabular format. Students who only write a paragraph *lose 2–3 marks* automatically.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Series Hybrid Drive-Train — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    FuelTankA[Fuel Tank] --> ICEA[Internal Combustion Engine]
    ICEA --> GenA[Generator]
    GenA --> PowerElectronicsA[Power Electronics Inverter]
    PowerElectronicsA --> BatteryA[HV Battery Pack]
    PowerElectronicsA --> TractionMotorA[Traction Motor]
    TractionMotorA --> TransmissionA[Fixed Ratio Transmission]
    TransmissionA --> WheelsA[Drive Wheels]
    BatteryA -.-> PowerElectronicsA
```

### 4.2 Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    FuelTankB[Fuel Tank] --> ICEB[Internal Combustion Engine]
    ICEB --> ClutchB[Clutch / Torque Coupler]
    ClutchB --> TransmissionB[Transmission]
    TransmissionB --> WheelsB[Drive Wheels]
    BatteryB[HV Battery Pack] --> PowerElectronicsB[Power Electronics]
    PowerElectronicsB --> TractionMotorB[Traction Motor]
    TractionMotorB --> TransmissionB
```

### 4.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Drive-Train — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    FuelTankC[Fuel Tank] --> ICEC[Internal Combustion Engine]
    ICEC --> CarrierC[Planetary Carrier Port]
    CarrierC --> PlanetaryC[Planetary Gear Set]
    PlanetaryC --> SunC[Sun Port MG1]
    PlanetaryC --> RingC[Ring Port]
    SunC --> MG1C[MG1 Motor Generator]
    RingC --> MG2C[MG2 Traction Motor]
    RingC --> FinalDriveC[Final Drive]
    FinalDriveC --> WheelsC[Drive Wheels]
    MG1C --> PowerElectronicsC[Power Electronics PCU]
    MG2C --> PowerElectronicsC
    PowerElectronicsC --> BatteryC[HV Battery Pack]
```

### 4.4 Combined Functional Architecture Flow (All Three Topologies)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Hybrid Vehicle Powertrain Selection] --> Q1{Is ICE mechanically connected to wheels?}
    Q1 -- No --> SeriesPath[Series Hybrid Topology]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Are ICE and Motor on same shaft with clutch coupling?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ParallelPath[Parallel Hybrid Topology]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is a planetary gear set used to split power?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SplitPath[Series Parallel Power Split Hybrid]
    SeriesPath --> UseCity[Best Use: Urban Stop and Go]
    ParallelPath --> UseHwy[Best Use: Highway Cruising]
    SplitPath --> UseMixed[Best Use: Mixed City and Highway]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the decision tree in Section 4.4 maps directly to the KTU Module 1 syllabus. If you remember this flowchart, you can answer any KTU "compare and contrast" question by tracing each topology through the decision branches.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "Degree of Hybridization (DOH)" for a hybrid vehicle. If a vehicle has an ICE rated at 80 kW and an electric motor rated at 40 kW, calculate the DOH.**

**Model Answer:**
DOH is the ratio of peak electrical machine power to the total peak powertrain power:

$$DOH = \dfrac{P_{motor,max}}{P_{ICE,max} + P_{motor,max}}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$DOH = \dfrac{40}{80 + 40} = \dfrac{40}{120} = 0.333 \text{ or } 33.3\%$$

This classifies the vehicle as a **mild hybrid** (DOH < 0.3 is micro, 0.3–0.5 is mild, > 0.5 is full hybrid). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between a Series and a Parallel hybrid drive-train with respect to mechanical coupling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical ICE–Wheel coupling | Decoupled (no direct path) | Direct (via clutch/coupling) |
| ICE function | Drives generator only | Drives wheels directly |
| Wheels driven by | Traction motor only | ICE and/or motor |
| Transmission | Simple, fixed ratio | Multi-speed or CVT |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series hybrid bus has the following specifications:
- ICE maximum power: 90 kW at 35% efficiency
- Generator efficiency: 92%
- Traction motor efficiency: 94%
- Fixed-ratio transmission efficiency: 96%
- Battery round-trip efficiency: 88%

Calculate the **overall tank-to-wheel efficiency** when the bus is driven in pure series mode (all power flows ICE → Generator → Motor → Wheels) and the **fuel consumption rate** if the wheels require 60 kW of mechanical power. Use LHV of diesel = 42.5 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
*(Architecture explanation, see Section 2.1 and Section 4.1 for the block diagram.)*

**Advantages of Series Hybrid:**
1. ICE always operates at its optimal efficiency point → lowest fuel consumption.
2. No conventional multi-speed transmission required → reduced mechanical complexity.
3. Simple driveline control — single traction motor.
4. ICE can be shut off during idling/stops → zero tailpipe emissions at standstill.
5. Smooth and quiet vehicle operation.

**Limitations of Series Hybrid:**
1. **Triple energy conversion** (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) → cumulative efficiency loss.
2. Requires **large traction motor** rated for peak vehicle power → high cost and weight.
3. Needs **large battery pack** to buffer energy → weight penalty.
4. Poor high-speed highway efficiency compared to parallel hybrid.

**(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for advantages, 2 for limitations)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate ICE mechanical output needed to produce 60 kW at the wheels.

$$P_{wheel} = P_{ICE,mech} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

$$60 = P_{ICE,mech} \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.94 \cdot 0.96$$

$$P_{ICE,mech} = \dfrac{60}{0.92 \cdot 0.94 \cdot 0.96} = \dfrac{60}{0.8297} = 72.31 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Compute overall tank-to-wheel efficiency.

$$\eta_{t2w} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

$$\eta_{t2w} = 0.35 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.94 \cdot 0.96 = 0.2904 \text{ or } 29.04\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute fuel chemical energy input rate.

$$P_{fuel,chem} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{t2w}} = \dfrac{60}{0.2904} = 206.61 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Compute fuel mass flow rate.

$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_{fuel,chem}}{LHV} = \dfrac{206.61 \text{ kJ/s}}{42500 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_f = 4.86 \text{ g/s} = 4.86 \times 3600 = 17.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\eta_{t2w} \approx 29.04\%$
- Fuel consumption rate $\approx 17.5 \text{ kg/h}$ **(7 marks — 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2, 1.5 for step 3, 1.5 for step 4)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid** architecture using a planetary gear set. Derive the **Willis equation** governing its kinematic relationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Toyota THS-style planetary gear set, the sun gear has 30 teeth and the ring gear has 78 teeth. If the ICE (on the carrier) runs at 2500 rpm and the wheels (on the ring) run at 1200 rpm, calculate the speed of MG1 (on the sun). State whether MG1 acts as a motor or a generator in this condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
*(See Section 3.1 for the full Willis equation derivation. The architecture explanation should reference Figure 4.3.)*

Key points to include:
- Three ports: Sun (MG1), Carrier (ICE), Ring (MG2 + Wheels).
- Power is split between a mechanical path (carrier → ring) and an electrical path (carrier → sun → MG1 → battery/MG2).
- Continuous variable transmission behavior without stepped gears.
- Toyota THS-II is the canonical example.

**(7 marks — 2 for derivation, 3 for architecture explanation, 2 for example)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $N_s = 30$, $N_r = 78$, $\omega_{carrier} = 2500$ rpm, $\omega_{ring} = 1200$ rpm.

**Willis Equation:**

$$\dfrac{\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}}{\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}} = -\dfrac{N_s}{N_r} = -\dfrac{30}{78} = -0.3846$$

**Step 1:** Compute numerator denominator values.

$$\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier} = 1200 - 2500 = -1300 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Willis:

$$\dfrac{\omega_{sun} - 2500}{-1300} = -0.3846$$

$$\omega_{sun} - 2500 = -1300 \cdot (-0.3846) = 500$$

$$\omega_{sun} = 500 + 2500 = 3000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3:** Determine MG1 mode.

Since $\omega_{sun} > \omega_{carrier}$ and the ICE is delivering power to the carrier, MG1 must be **absorbing mechanical power** (acting as a **generator**) to maintain its higher speed. This mechanical power is converted to electrical power and routed to MG2 or the battery.

**Final Answer:** $\omega_{sun} = 3000$ rpm; MG1 acts as a **generator** in this condition. **(7 marks — 2 for equation setup, 3 for arithmetic, 2 for mode interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Willis Equation:** The negative sign is *frequently* dropped by students. Lose 1–2 marks if you forget it.
> 2. **Confusing ICE Mechanical vs. Electrical Path:** In Series-Parallel, ICE power flows through *both* paths simultaneously. Students often wrongly say ICE power "goes through MG1 only" — that is incorrect.
> 3. **Forgetting Battery Round-Trip Efficiency:** In a real series hybrid design problem, neglecting $\eta_{bat}$ leads to over-estimation of range by ~10–12%.
> 4. **Units Mismatch in Fuel Consumption:** Always convert kW → kJ/s before dividing by LHV (MJ/kg). KTU examiners check this step strictly.
> 5. **Mislabelling Motor vs. Generator:** MG1 mode depends on *direction* of power flow, not just speed. Always check whether MG1 is absorbing or delivering mechanical power.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Topologies:** Series (decoupled), Parallel (coupled), Series-Parallel (planetary split).
- **Series** = best for *urban stop-and-go*; ICE always at optimum; large motor required.
- **Parallel** = best for *highway cruising*; smallest, cheapest motor; ICE tied to wheel speed.
- **Series-Parallel** = *industry default*; combines both advantages; most complex control.
- **Willis Equation** (most-asked formula):
$$\dfrac{\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}}{\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}} = -\dfrac{N_s}{N_r}$$
- **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** defines micro/mild/full hybrid classification.
- **Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency** = product of ICE × (Gen/Motor/Transmission) efficiencies for the chosen path.
- **Real-world examples to memorize:** Chevrolet Volt (Series), Honda Civic IMA (Parallel), Toyota Prius THS (Series-Parallel).
- **Negative sign in Willis Equation** is mandatory in KTU board solutions.
- **Battery SOC management** is a continuous control objective across all three topologies.
- **Regenerative braking** is possible in *all three* topologies but contributes most in series-parallel.
- **Cost ranking:** Series-Parallel > Series > Parallel.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-trains?

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-train Topologies** refers to the supervisory energy management and torque coordination strategy that dictates how mechanical and electrical power is apportioned, transferred, and regulated between two or more energy sources (typically an Internal Combustion Engine and one or more Electric Machines) through the chosen drive-train topology (Series, Parallel, or Series-Parallel) to meet the driver demanded propulsion power while optimizing efficiency, emissions, and battery state-of-charge.

In simpler terms, a hybrid vehicle has **two engines** — a petrol/diesel engine (called the *Internal Combustion Engine* or ICE) and an electric motor. They both can produce power, but they cannot both push the car at full force all the time. **Power Flow Control is the "brain"** that decides, at every single second, how much power should come from the engine, how much from the electric motor, whether the battery should be charged or discharged, and how to combine (or split) these powers to drive the wheels smoothly.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Two-Cook Kitchen

Imagine a restaurant kitchen where **Cook A** (the ICE) is very efficient at cooking large quantities of food slowly, and **Cook B** (the Electric Motor) is very quick at preparing small instant dishes but gets tired (battery drains) if overworked. The Head Chef (the **Hybrid Control Unit / Energy Management Strategy**) decides:
- During lunch rush (highway cruising) → Let Cook A handle it.
- During small orders (slow city traffic) → Let Cook B handle it.
- When both are needed (hard acceleration) → Both cooks collaborate.
- When there's leftover food (regenerative braking) → Store it back in the fridge (battery).

That Head Chef's logic IS the **Power Flow Control Strategy**.

## 1.2 Why is Power Flow Control Critical?

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Efficiency Optimization** | Keeps the ICE operating at its *Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)* sweet spot |
| **Emission Reduction** | Avoids inefficient ICE operation modes (cold start, idling, low load) |
| **Battery Protection** | Prevents over-charge/discharge of the High-Voltage (HV) battery pack |
| **Driveability** | Ensures seamless torque transitions between modes (no jerks) |
| **Range Extension** | Maximizes the utilization of stored electrical energy |

## 1.3 The Three Primary Drive-train Topologies (KTU Mandated)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Classification:** The syllabus categorizes hybrid drive-trains into **three principal topologies**. Power flow control strategies differ fundamentally for each, because the *physical path* of energy transmission changes.

1. **Series Hybrid Topology** — ICE and wheels are *not mechanically coupled*. ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels.
2. **Parallel Hybrid Topology** — ICE and Motor are *mechanically coupled* to the wheels through a torque adder (clutches/gears). Both can directly drive the wheels.
3. **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Topology** — Combines elements of both; uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** to mechanically split and electrically recombine power.

The bold engineering constants/metrics used throughout this module are:

- **State of Charge (SOC)**: typically maintained between **30%** and **80%** to maximize battery cycle life.
- **ICE Optimal Operating Point**: ~**2000–2500 RPM** at **mid-load** for typical gasoline engines.
- **Regenerative Braking Efficiency**: typically **60%–70%** of kinetic energy recovered.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Flow in Series Hybrid Topology

### Operating Principle

In a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**, there is **NO mechanical link** between the ICE and the driving wheels. The energy conversion chain is purely *electrical* between ICE and wheels.

**Energy Path:**
$$\text{ICE (Mechanical)} \longrightarrow \text{Generator (Electrical)} \longrightarrow \text{DC Bus} \longrightarrow \text{Traction Motor (Mechanical)} \longrightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

### Component Architecture
- **ICE** drives a **Generator (G)** exclusively.
- **High-Voltage Battery Pack** is connected to the DC bus through a **DC-DC Converter** (bidirectional).
- **Traction Motor (M)** drives the wheels through a **single-speed transmission (or fixed reduction)**.
- A **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** orchestrates the power split.

### Modes of Operation in Series HEV

| Mode | ICE State | Generator | Battery | Motor | Power Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure EV (Low Load)** | OFF | OFF | Discharging | ON | Battery → Motor → Wheels |
| **Pure ICE Drive (Steady Cruise)** | ON → Optimal Point | Charging | Charging or Float | ON | ICE → Gen → Battery & Motor → Wheels |
| **Peak Load (Hard Acceleration)** | ON (Max) | Generating | Discharging (assist) | ON (Max) | ICE + Battery → Motor → Wheels |
| **Regenerative Braking** | OFF | OFF | Charging | Acts as Generator | Wheels → Motor (as Gen) → Battery |
| **Battery Sustain Mode** | ON | Generating | Charging | ON | ICE → Gen → Battery (to maintain SOC) |
| **Standstill / Idle** | OFF | OFF | Float | OFF | No flow |

### Why Choose Series?
- **Simplicity**: No complex clutch or planetary gear synchronization.
- **ICE always runs at optimal point** → great for urban buses (e.g., **BYD K9**, **Hino Blue Ribbon**).
- **Disadvantage**: Two energy conversions (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) cause **~15–25% energy losses**.

## 2.2 Power Flow in Parallel Hybrid Topology

### Operating Principle

In a **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)**, both the **ICE and the Electric Motor are mechanically coupled** to the drive shaft through a **torque coupling device** (clutch, gear train, or chain).

**Energy Path:**
$$\text{ICE (Mech)} \oplus \text{Motor (Mech)} \xrightarrow{\text{Torque Coupler}} \text{Transmission} \longrightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

The key word here is **parallel mechanical paths** — both power sources can independently or simultaneously deliver torque to the wheels.

### Component Architecture
- **ICE** connected via **clutch C1** to the torque coupler.
- **Electric Motor (M)** connected via **clutch C2** to the same torque coupler.
- **Battery** provides electrical energy to M.
- A **5–8 speed automated manual transmission (AMT)** or CVT handles gear ratios.
- **VCU** controls clutch engagement timing and torque blending.

### Modes of Operation in Parallel HEV

| Mode | Clutch C1 | Clutch C2 | Power Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EV Mode (Pure Electric)** | Open | Engaged | Battery → Motor → Wheels |
| **ICE Mode (Pure Engine)** | Engaged | Open | ICE → Wheels |
| **Hybrid Mode (Combined)** | Engaged | Engaged | ICE + Motor → Wheels (torque additively summed) |
| **ICE + Battery Charging** | Engaged | Engaged | ICE → Wheels **and** ICE → Motor (as generator) → Battery |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Open | Engaged (motor as gen) | Wheels → Motor → Battery |
| **Power Boost (Overtaking)** | Engaged | Engaged (max torque) | ICE (max) + Motor (max) → Wheels |

### Mathematical Representation of Torque Blending

$$T_{\text{wheels}} = \eta_{\text{coupler}} \cdot \left[ T_{\text{ICE}} \cdot s_1 + T_{\text{motor}} \cdot s_2 \right]$$

Where:
- $s_1, s_2 \in \{0, 1\}$ are **clutch state variables** (0 = disengaged, 1 = engaged).
- $\eta_{\text{coupler}}$ is the mechanical efficiency of the torque coupler (typically **0.92–0.97**).

### Why Choose Parallel?
- **High efficiency during highway cruising** (single energy conversion).
- **Compact packaging** — both motors share the same transmission.
- **Disadvantage**: ICE cannot always operate at its optimal point (load-following duty cycle).

## 2.3 Power Flow in Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Topology

### Operating Principle

This is the **most sophisticated topology**, combining the *mechanical simplicity* of parallel drive with the *ICE-optimization* of series drive. The mechanical heart of this topology is the **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)**.

**Famous Real-World Example:** **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)** used in the **Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid**.

### The Planetary Gear Set — The Heart of Power-Split

A PGS has three nodes:
1. **Sun Gear (S)** — connected to **Generator (G)**.
2. **Planet Carrier (C)** — connected to **ICE**.
3. **Ring Gear (R)** — connected to **Motor (M)** and (through reduction gear) to the **Wheels**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Willis Equation (Kinematic Constraint of Planetary Gear Set):**
> $$\omega_S \cdot N_S + \omega_R \cdot N_R = \omega_C \cdot (N_S + N_R)$$
> Where $N_S$ and $N_R$ are the number of teeth on sun and ring gears respectively.

The **Generator controls $\omega_S$**, which means the Generator can **vary the ICE speed independently of the wheel speed** — this is the key to keeping the ICE at its optimal point.

### Modes of Operation

| Mode | ICE | Generator | Motor | Battery | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EV Start / Low Speed** | OFF | Free-spinning | Driving | Discharging | Pure electric |
| **Low-Speed Cruise** | ON | Generating (at optimal ICE speed) | Motor assist | Charging | Series-like behavior |
| **Normal Driving** | ON | Controlled (splits power) | Motor assist | Maintained | Power-split mode |
| **High-Speed Cruise** | ON | Near-zero generation | OFF or low | Float | Direct mechanical (parallel-like) |
| **Full Acceleration** | ON (Max) | Generating | ON (Max) | Discharging | Both motors combine |
| **Regenerative Braking** | OFF | Free | Acting as generator | Charging | Energy recovery |
| **Reverse** | OFF | — | Reverse rotation | Discharging | Motor direction reversed |

## 2.4 Universal Driver Demand Equation (KTU High-Yield)

Regardless of topology, the controller must satisfy the fundamental power balance:

$$P_{\text{demand}}(t) = \underbrace{P_{\text{ICE}}(t)}_{\text{Engine Power}} + \underbrace{P_{\text{Motor}}(t)}_{\text{Electric Power}} - \underbrace{P_{\text{aux}}(t)}_{\text{Auxiliaries (AC, lights)}}$$

Where:
$$P_{\text{demand}} = \frac{1}{\eta_t} \left[ M \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot v + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3 + M \cdot \delta \cdot v \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} \right]$$

- $M$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $f_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient (≈ **0.012** for passenger car)
- $\rho_a$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient
- $A$ = frontal area (m²)
- $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s)
- $\delta$ = rotational inertia factor (≈ 1.05)
- $\eta_t$ = drivetrain efficiency

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value/Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{\text{demand}}$ | Power demanded at wheels | kW |
| $P_{\text{ICE}}$ | Power from internal combustion engine | kW |
| $P_{\text{Motor}}$ | Electric motor power | kW |
| $P_{\text{Battery}}$ | Battery discharge/charge power | kW |
| $T_{\text{wheels}}$ | Total torque at wheels | N·m |
| $\omega_S, \omega_R, \omega_C$ | Sun, Ring, Carrier speeds | rad/s |
| $N_S, N_R$ | Sun, Ring gear teeth | unitless |
| $\eta_{\text{coupler}}$ | Coupling efficiency | 0.92 – 0.97 |
| $\eta_m, \eta_g$ | Motor, Generator efficiency | 0.90 – 0.95 |
| $s_1, s_2$ | Clutch state (0/1) | binary |
| $SOC$ | Battery state of charge | 0–100% |
| $\eta_{\text{regen}}$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | 60%–70% |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power flow control is what makes hybrids **sell in mass markets**. Without intelligent power flow:
- The ICE would always operate at whatever speed the driver demanded → **fuel waste**.
- The battery would be either over-charged or over-discharged → **battery death**.
- The driver would feel harsh jerks during mode transitions → **poor driveability**.

Industries using these strategies: **Toyota, Honda, Hyundai, BYD, Tesla (range extender mode), Ford, Volvo**.
