# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Planetary Gear Set Speed Relationship (Willis Equation)

This is the **most important derivation** in this module for KTU exams.

### Step 1 — Visualize the Planetary Gear Set

A planetary gear set has:
- **Sun gear** (center) — $N_S$ teeth, angular velocity $\omega_S$.
- **Planet gears** mounted on a **carrier** — angular velocity $\omega_C$.
- **Ring gear** (outer, internally toothed) — $N_R$ teeth, angular velocity $\omega_R$.

### Step 2 — Define the Tooth Pitch Line Velocity at the Sun-Planet Mesh

The planet gear contacts the sun gear. At the contact point, both must have the same tangential velocity. For a planet rolling on a fixed sun:

$$v_{\text{contact}} = r_S \cdot \omega_S = (r_C - r_S) \cdot (\omega_C - \omega_S)$$

Where $r_C$ is the distance from sun center to planet center.

### Step 3 — Introduce the Carrier Frame

Switch to a **rotating reference frame attached to the carrier**. In this frame:
- Carrier is stationary.
- Sun rotates at $(\omega_S - \omega_C)$.
- Ring rotates at $(\omega_R - \omega_C)$.

The linear velocity at the sun-planet mesh must match in both directions:

$$r_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C) = -r_P \cdot \omega_{P/C}$$

Where $\omega_{P/C}$ is planet rotation relative to carrier. The negative sign reflects that the contact is on opposite sides.

### Step 4 — Express Radii in Terms of Tooth Numbers

Since pitch radius is proportional to the number of teeth ($r = k \cdot N$):

$$N_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C) = -N_P \cdot \omega_{P/C}$$

Similarly for planet-ring mesh:
$$N_R \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C) = N_P \cdot \omega_{P/C}$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate $\omega_{P/C}$

Multiply the planet-ring equation by $-1$:

$$-N_R \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C) = -N_P \cdot \omega_{P/C}$$

Now equate to sun-planet equation:

$$N_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C) = -N_R \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C)$$

### Step 6 — Final Rearrangement

Expand both sides:

$$N_S \cdot \omega_S - N_S \cdot \omega_C = -N_R \cdot \omega_R + N_R \cdot \omega_C$$

Group $\omega_C$ terms on one side:

$$N_S \cdot \omega_C + N_R \cdot \omega_C = N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R$$

$$\omega_C \cdot (N_S + N_R) = N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R$$

### Step 7 — Final Willis Equation

$$\boxed{\omega_C = \frac{N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R}{N_S + N_R}}$$

### Step 8 — Apply Characteristic Parameter

Define $k = \dfrac{N_R}{N_S}$ (often called the **kinematic parameter**, typically $k \approx 2$ to 3 in automotive PGS).

$$\omega_C = \frac{\omega_S + k \cdot \omega_R}{1 + k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check:** If ring gear is fixed ($\omega_R = 0$), then $\omega_C = \omega_S / (1+k)$, which matches the standard textbook planetary kinematics. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Driver Demand Power (from Vehicle Dynamics)

Starting from the longitudinal vehicle dynamics equation on a flat road:

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = M \cdot g \cdot f_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + M \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 1 — Convert Force to Power at Wheels

Power demanded at the wheel-road contact patch:

$$P_{\text{demand}} = F_{\text{tractive}} \cdot v$$

$$P_{\text{demand}} = M \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot v + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3 + M \cdot \delta \cdot v \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Wheel Power to Source Power (account for losses)

The power must come out of the source (battery/engine) higher due to drivetrain losses:

$$P_{\text{source}} = \frac{P_{\text{demand}}}{\eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}$$

For **Series HEV**: $\eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = \eta_g \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{transmission}}$ (multiple conversions).

For **Parallel HEV**: $\eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = \eta_{\text{coupler}} \cdot \eta_{\text{transmission}}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Topologies (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Link ICE–Wheels** | None | Direct | Through PGS |
| **Number of Energy Conversions** | 3 (Mech→Elec→Mech) | 1 (Mech only) | 1 to 3 (adaptive) |
| **Overall Efficiency** | ~70–80% | ~85–92% | ~85–90% |
| **ICE Always at Optimal Point?** | Yes (decoupled) | No (follows load) | Yes (PGS decouples) |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Best Application** | City buses, trucks | Mid-size sedans | Premium sedans (Prius) |
| **Control Complexity** | Simple | Medium | High |
| **Regenerative Braking Efficiency** | High | High | High |
| **Packaging** | Flexible (no shaft needed) | Compact | Complex (PGS volume) |
| **Mode Switching Smoothness** | Very Smooth | May jerk | Very Smooth |

## 3.4 Worked Example Problem (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A parallel HEV has ICE producing torque $T_{\text{ICE}} = 120$ N·m at speed $\omega_{\text{ICE}} = 1800$ RPM, and Motor producing torque $T_{\text{motor}} = 80$ N·m at speed $\omega_{\text{motor}} = 3000$ RPM. Both clutches are engaged. Coupling efficiency is 0.95. Calculate the total wheel power.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Convert speeds to rad/s.

$$\omega_{\text{ICE}} = 1800 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 188.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{\text{motor}} = 3000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

> Note: For a rigid coupling (parallel topology), the speeds at the torque coupler are made equal by the transmission. The torques add.

**Step 2:** Apply the torque blending equation with $s_1 = s_2 = 1$:

$$T_{\text{wheels}} = \eta_{\text{coupler}} \cdot [T_{\text{ICE}} + T_{\text{motor}}]$$

$$T_{\text{wheels}} = 0.95 \cdot [120 + 80] = 0.95 \cdot 200 = 190 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate power at common output speed (assume ICE speed is reference after transmission equalization, output wheel speed via final drive is fixed):

$$P_{\text{wheels}} = T_{\text{wheels}} \cdot \omega_{\text{coupler}}$$

Assuming $\omega_{\text{coupler}} = 188.5$ rad/s (after clutch synchronization):

$$P_{\text{wheels}} = 190 \times 188.5 = 35,815 \text{ W} \approx 35.82 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate interpretation:** If we assume the motor and ICE run at different speeds (e.g., through a planetary set), we sum the powers individually and apply efficiency to each:
> $$P_{\text{wheels}} = \eta_c \cdot (T_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \omega_{\text{ICE}} + T_{\text{motor}} \cdot \omega_{\text{motor}})$$
> $$P_{\text{wheels}} = 0.95 \cdot (120 \cdot 188.5 + 80 \cdot 314.16)$$
> $$P_{\text{wheels}} = 0.95 \cdot (22,620 + 25,133) = 0.95 \cdot 47,753 = 45,365 \text{ W} \approx 45.37 \text{ kW}$$

This shows the importance of carefully reading the topology in such problems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define power flow control in hybrid electric vehicles. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Power flow control** in a hybrid electric vehicle is the supervisory strategy used by the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) to allocate the driver-demanded propulsion power between two or more energy sources — typically the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and the Electric Motor — while also managing the battery State of Charge (SOC), auxiliary loads, and regenerative braking energy recovery.
>
> It is necessary because:
> 1. The ICE has an operating region (sweet spot) of maximum efficiency; power flow control keeps the ICE near this region.
> 2. The battery has SOC limits (typically 30%–80%) that must not be violated.
> 3. Mode transitions (e.g., EV to Hybrid) must be smooth to ensure driveability.
> 4. It minimizes fuel consumption and tailpipe emissions simultaneously.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Compare Series and Parallel hybrid drive-train topologies in terms of mechanical coupling and ICE operating point.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical coupling between ICE and wheels | **No direct mechanical coupling** | **Direct mechanical coupling** through clutches |
| ICE operating point | **Decoupled** from wheels; can always run at optimal speed | **Follows road load**; may operate sub-optimally |
| Energy conversion stages | Mechanical → Electrical → Mechanical (lossy) | Mechanical only (efficient) |
| Best suited for | Urban buses, heavy vehicles | Mid-size passenger cars |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the power flow control in a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Discuss all major operating modes and the role of the Vehicle Control Unit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Series HEV has the following specifications:
- Vehicle mass $M = 1500$ kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.012$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$
- Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m²
- Air density $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Rotational inertia factor $\delta = 1.05$
- Acceleration $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 1.5$ m/s²
- Vehicle speed $v = 20$ m/s
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = 0.85$

Calculate the **total power demanded from the source** at this instant. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

A Series HEV consists of:
- **Fuel tank → ICE → Generator (G) → Inverter/Rectifier → DC Bus**
- **HV Battery → DC-DC Converter → DC Bus**
- **DC Bus → Traction Inverter → Traction Motor (M) → Reduction Gear → Wheels**

The VCU monitors SOC, vehicle speed, driver demand and controls the ICE throttle, generator excitation, and motor torque commands.

**Operating Modes (4 marks):**

1. **Pure Electric Mode** — ICE OFF, battery powers motor. Used at low speed/load.
2. **Pure ICE Mode** — ICE drives generator at optimal point, motor is driven by generator + battery.
3. **Hybrid Drive Mode** — Both ICE and battery supply motor during peak demand.
4. **Regenerative Braking** — Motor acts as generator, charging the battery.
5. **Battery Sustain Mode** — ICE + Generator maintain SOC at a target value (~50–60%).
6. **Stationary Charging** — ICE + Generator charge the battery when vehicle is parked (range extender mode).

The VCU selects the mode every **10–100 ms** based on:
- Accelerator pedal position
- Brake pedal position
- Vehicle speed
- Battery SOC
- ICE temperature and RPM

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Calculate the Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$F_r = M \cdot g \cdot f_r = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012$$

$$F_r = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Aerodynamic Drag Force:**

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (20)^2$$

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 400$$

$$F_d = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.66 \cdot 400 = 161.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Acceleration Force (Inertia):**

$$F_a = M \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = 1500 \cdot 1.05 \cdot 1.5$$

$$F_a = 2362.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Total Tractive Force:**

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = F_r + F_d + F_a = 176.58 + 161.7 + 2362.5$$

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = 2700.78 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Wheel Power:**

$$P_{\text{wheels}} = F_{\text{tractive}} \cdot v = 2700.78 \cdot 20$$

$$P_{\text{wheels}} = 54,015.6 \text{ W} \approx 54.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the Source Power (accounting for drivetrain losses):**

$$P_{\text{source}} = \frac{P_{\text{wheels}}}{\eta_{\text{drivetrain}}} = \frac{54,015.6}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{source}} \approx 63,548 \text{ W} \approx 63.55 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **power flow control in a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle** using a block diagram. List all operating modes and the role of clutch actuation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Willis Equation for a Planetary Gear Set** used in a Series-Parallel hybrid. Given $N_S = 30$, $N_R = 78$, $\omega_S = 5000$ RPM, $\omega_R = 1000$ RPM, find the carrier speed $\omega_C$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

A Parallel HEV has the ICE and Motor connected to a common **torque coupler** via clutches C1 and C2 respectively. The output of the coupler goes to an automated manual transmission (AMT) and then to the wheels via a final drive.

**Operating Modes (4 marks):**

| Mode | C1 | C2 | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure EV | Open | Engaged | Only motor drives |
| Pure ICE | Engaged | Open | Only ICE drives |
| Hybrid Combined | Engaged | Engaged | Both drive wheels |
| Battery Charging | Engaged | Engaged | ICE drives wheels + charges battery via motor as generator |
| Regenerative Braking | Open | Engaged | Motor charges battery |
| Engine Restart | Sequential engagement | Sequential | Smooth ICE start during motion |

Clutch actuation is **critical** because:
- It determines whether the ICE and Motor are mechanically summed (parallel mode) or isolated.
- **Slipping clutches** enable smooth torque blending during transitions.
- Modern systems use **wet multi-plate clutches** controlled by hydraulic or electro-mechanical actuators with millisecond response.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the kinematic constraint from geometric analysis:

$$N_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C) = -N_R \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Expand and rearrange:

$$N_S \cdot \omega_S - N_S \cdot \omega_C = -N_R \cdot \omega_R + N_R \cdot \omega_C$$

$$(N_S + N_R) \cdot \omega_C = N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R$$

$$\boxed{\omega_C = \frac{N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R}{N_S + N_R}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute the numerical values:

$$N_S = 30, \quad N_R = 78, \quad \omega_S = 5000 \text{ RPM}, \quad \omega_R = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_C = \frac{30 \cdot 5000 + 78 \cdot 1000}{30 + 78}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate:

$$\omega_C = \frac{150{,}000 + 78{,}000}{108} = \frac{228{,}000}{108}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_C \approx 2111.11 \text{ RPM}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The ICE (on the carrier) rotates at ~2111 RPM while the Generator (sun) spins at 5000 RPM and the Motor/Ring is at 1000 RPM. The Generator thus extracts power while keeping ICE at its fuel-efficient sweet spot.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by drivetrain efficiency** when calculating source power from wheel power — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Series and Parallel topologies** in block diagrams — losing up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Not labeling clutch states** (C1, C2 engaged/open) in mode tables — losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the Willis equation solution — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning SOC management** while explaining power flow control — losing 2 marks (this is a KTU favorite question area).
> 6. **Writing only "ICE + Motor drive the wheels"** for Parallel HEV — must explicitly state the **torque coupling mechanism** and **clutch actuation logic**.
> 7. **Skipping the role of VCU** — VCU is the brain; without it, no power flow decision can be made.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU Module 1:**

- ✅ **Three topologies**: Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split).
- ✅ **Series HEV**: No mechanical link ICE↔Wheels. Three energy conversions. Best for buses.
- ✅ **Parallel HEV**: Both sources mechanically coupled via clutches. Best for mid-size cars.
- ✅ **Series-Parallel HEV**: Uses **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)**. Best efficiency across all speeds. Used in Toyota Prius (THS).
- ✅ **Willis Equation (must memorize):**
  $$\omega_C = \frac{N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R}{N_S + N_R}$$
- ✅ **Six operating modes** (universal): Pure EV, Pure ICE, Hybrid Combined, Battery Charging, Regenerative Braking, Standstill.
- ✅ **SOC operating window**: Typically **30% to 80%** for longevity.
- ✅ **Power balance equation**: $P_{\text{demand}} = P_{\text{ICE}} + P_{\text{Motor}} - P_{\text{aux}}$.
- ✅ **Driver demand power formula** has three components: rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and inertia.
- ✅ **Regenerative braking efficiency** ≈ 60–70%.
- ✅ **VCU is the brain** — selects mode every 10–100 ms.
- ✅ **Key roles of VCU**: SOC monitoring, torque blending, clutch actuation, mode selection, regenerative braking control.
- ✅ **Characteristic parameter of PGS**: $k = N_R/N_S$ (typically 2–3).
- ✅ **Energy Management Strategies** (preview of next sub-module): Rule-based, Fuzzy Logic, Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS), Dynamic Programming, and Reinforcement Learning.
- ✅ **Real-world benchmarks**: Toyota THS (power-split), Honda i-MMD (series-parallel), BYD DM-i (series-parallel), Hyundai TMED (parallel).
- ✅ **When asked to compare**, always discuss: efficiency, complexity, packaging, cost, ICE operating flexibility, and number of energy conversion stages.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Machines and their Controllers: Brushed DC Electric Motor - DC Regulation and Voltage Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Machines and their Controllers: Brushed DC Electric Motor - DC Regulation and Voltage Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Brushed DC Electric Motor — Formal Definition

A **Brushed DC Motor (BDC)** is an internally commutated electric machine in which direct current electrical power is converted into mechanical rotation through the interaction of stationary magnetic fields (stator field poles) and a rotating current-carrying armature winding, with commutation (current reversal in the armature coils) achieved mechanically by means of a segmented **commutator** and sliding **carbon/graphite brushes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The Brushed DC Motor is studied here specifically because it historically powered early Electric Vehicles (EVs) and remains a benchmark baseline for understanding torque–speed behaviour, voltage regulation, and the role of DC-DC conversion stages in modern Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs).

### Constructional Anatomy

| Component | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Stator / Field Pole** | Stationary outer body | Produces the main magnetic flux $\Phi$ (via field winding or PM) |
| **Armature / Rotor** | Rotating inner core | Houses the conductors carrying current $I_a$ |
| **Commutator** | Mounted on rotor shaft | Mechanically rectifies/inverts armature current |
| **Brushes** | Stationary, sliding on commutator | Provide electrical path to/from rotating armature |
| **Yoke** | Outer frame | Completes magnetic circuit; mechanical support |

### Types of Brushed DC Motors

1. **Separately Excited DC Motor** — Field winding supplied from an independent DC source.
2. **Self-Excited DC Motor** — Field winding supplied from the armature itself:
   * **Shunt** (field parallel to armature)
   * **Series** (field in series with armature)
   * **Compound** (cumulative or differential)

> [!NOTE]
> For EV/HEV traction, **Series DC motors** were preferred in early vehicles (e.g., vintage EVs, forklifts) due to their high starting torque characteristic, which is ideal for launch acceleration.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rowing Boat with Magnetic Oars"

Imagine you are sitting in a small boat that has two fixed magnets mounted on its sides (the **stator field**). In the middle of the boat is a rotor — think of it as a rotating paddle wheel with conducting loops. When current flows through the paddle wheel, each conducting loop experiences a magnetic force (the **Lorentz force**, $\vec{F} = I\vec{L} \times \vec{B}$), pushing it like an invisible oar stroke.

Now, the clever bit: as the paddle wheel turns halfway around, the side of the paddle that was being pushed *forward* would naturally start being pushed *backward*. To keep the boat always moving in the same direction, a **commutator** acts like a tiny switching station — every half-turn, it flips the current direction in each loop, ensuring the force always pushes in the "forward" direction. The **brushes** are the sliding contacts that deliver this flipped current to the rotating paddle.

**Speed control** (DC regulation) is like changing the strength of the rowing force — either by making the magnets stronger (flux control) or by rowing harder (armature voltage control). **Voltage conversion** via choppers is like installing a gear on the oars that lets you modulate each stroke precisely.

---

## 1.3 DC Regulation — Formal Definition

**DC Regulation** in the context of a brushed DC motor refers to the *deliberate control of motor speed and torque* by manipulating the **armature terminal voltage** $V_t$, the **field flux** $\Phi$, or both, in order to meet desired operating points on the torque-speed characteristic curve.

## 1.4 Voltage Conversion — Formal Definition

**Voltage Conversion** refers to the use of static (non-rotating) power electronic switching circuits — primarily **DC-DC converters** (also called **choppers**) — that step down (**buck**), step up (**boost**), or invert (**buck-boost / Ćuk**) the polarity of a DC voltage to deliver a regulated variable DC output to the motor terminals. The fundamental principle is high-frequency switching (typically **5 kHz to 50 kHz** in EV applications) with **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard EV Metrics to Remember:**
> - Traction motor voltage: **96 V – 800 V DC** (modern EVs)
> - Battery pack nominal: **300 V – 400 V** (typical)
> - Auxiliary bus: **12 V / 48 V** (low-voltage system)
> - Switching frequency of chopper: **10 kHz – 20 kHz** (audible-noise limit)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Lorentz Force Action

When a current-carrying conductor of length $L$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, a force is exerted on it. In a DC motor, the armature winding is distributed in slots around the rotor periphery. For a motor with:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (in Webers, **Wb**)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths ($A = 2$ for wave winding, $A = P$ for lap winding)
- $I_a$ = armature current (Amperes)

the **average force per conductor** gives rise to a resultant electromagnetic torque.

---

## 2.2 EMF Equation of a DC Motor (Back EMF)

When the armature rotates in the magnetic field, an EMF is induced in the conductors — this is the **Back EMF** (or **Counter EMF / Counter-electromotive force**, $E_b$). By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:

$$E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}$$

where:
- $E_b$ = back EMF in **Volts**
- $N$ = armature speed in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**

**Real-world utility:** This equation is the *backbone of all DC motor control strategies*. Every speed-control method manipulates either $\Phi$, $E_b$, or $V_t$ based on this relationship.

---

## 2.3 Armature Voltage Equation (KVL)

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature circuit:

$$V_t = E_b + I_a \, R_a$$

where:
- $V_t$ = terminal voltage applied to the armature (V)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance (Ω)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Rearranging, $E_b = V_t - I_a R_a$. Since $E_b \propto N$, we see that **motor speed is directly controlled by terminal voltage** when flux is held constant.

---

## 2.4 Torque Equation

The electromagnetic torque developed by the motor is:

$$T_e = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}$$

**Key observation:** $T_e \propto \Phi \, I_a$. Therefore, torque is controlled either by current (armature control) or flux (field control).

**Shaft Torque** (after mechanical losses):

$$T_{sh} = T_e - T_{mech\,loss}$$

---

## 2.5 Power Equation

The **mechanical power developed** by the armature is:

$$P_m = T_e \, \omega = E_b \, I_a$$

where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ is the angular speed in rad/s.

**Electrical input power** to armature:

$$P_{in} = V_t \, I_a$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_{sh}\,\omega}{V_t I_a}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application in HEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ | Back EMF | V | Speed estimation from terminal voltage |
| 2 | $V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL | V | Voltage drop accounting |
| 3 | $T_e = \dfrac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m | Torque demand calculation |
| 4 | $P_m = T_e \omega$ | Mechanical power | W | Powertrain sizing |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ | Speed equation (with $k = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$) | rpm | Cruise control |
| 6 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{const}$ | V/f ratio (PWM modulation) | — | Constant torque region |
| 7 | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | Buck converter output | V | Step-down for auxiliary bus |
| 8 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$ | Boost converter output | V | Step-up for traction |
| 9 | $V_o = -\dfrac{D}{1-D} V_s$ | Buck-Boost output | V | Battery voltage boost |
| 10 | $I_{L,ripple} = \dfrac{V_s \, D (1-D)}{f_s L}$ | Inductor ripple current | A | Filter design |
| 11 | $V_{C,ripple} = \dfrac{I_o \, D}{f_s C}$ | Capacitor ripple voltage | V | Output filter design |
| 12 | $\eta_{chopper} = \dfrac{P_o}{P_o + P_{sw}}$ | Chopper efficiency | — | Thermal management |

**Notation:**
- $D$ = **Duty Cycle** (ratio of switch ON-time to total switching period, $0 \le D \le 1$)
- $f_s$ = **Switching frequency** (Hz)
- $V_s$ = Source / input DC voltage
- $V_o$ = Output DC voltage
- $L$, $C$ = Filter inductance and capacitance

---

## 2.7 Speed Control Methods for Brushed DC Motor

### Method 1: Armature Voltage Control (Most Common in EVs)

The flux $\Phi$ is held constant (separate excitation with constant field current). Then:

$$N \propto V_t - I_a R_a \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N \approx \frac{V_t}{k\Phi} \;\;(\text{neglecting } I_a R_a)$$

This gives a **linear speed–voltage** characteristic. In EVs, $V_t$ is varied using a **chopper** fed from the DC battery.

### Method 2: Field Flux Control

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\Phi} \quad (\text{for constant } V_t)$$

Achieved by varying the field winding current via a **field rheostat**. Gives speed *above* base speed.

### Method 3: Ward-Leonard System (Classic High-Performance Control)

An **AC motor → DC generator → controlled DC field** arrangement provides a smooth, loss-minimised, infinitely variable voltage to the armature. Used in industrial drives; conceptually important even though modern EVs use IGBT inverters.

### Torque–Speed Characteristic Summary

| Region | Control Variable | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque** (0 → N_base) | Armature voltage, $\Phi$ = constant | $T$ constant, $P \propto N$ |
| **Constant Power** (N_base → N_max) | Field weakened, $\Phi \downarrow$ | $P$ constant, $T \propto 1/N$ |
| **Field Weakening** | Field flux reduced | Higher speeds possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Weakening** is essential in EVs for high-speed cruising — once back EMF equals supply voltage at base speed, the only way to go faster is to reduce flux.

---

## 2.8 Why Voltage Conversion is Necessary in HEVs

A single fixed battery voltage (e.g., 400 V) cannot efficiently serve every load in a hybrid vehicle:
- **Traction motor inverter** requires variable frequency AC (synthesised by DC-AC inverter from DC link)
- **Auxiliary 12 V bus** for lights, ECU, sensors → **Buck converter**
- **DC link stabilisation** → **Bidirectional boost/buck** between battery and inverter
- **Brake energy recovery** → **Boost mode** raises regenerated voltage to charge battery
- **Fuel cell stack output** (variable, lower voltage) → **Boost converter** to DC link level

**Real-world utility:** Modern Toyota Hybrid System (THS), Honda IMA, and Hyundai TMED all use multi-stage DC-DC converters to interface battery, ISG (Integrated Starter-Generator), and traction inverter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Back EMF Equation $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$

We start from the basic principle: **flux cut per conductor per revolution**.

**Step 1: Flux cut per conductor in one revolution.**

Each armature conductor cuts all the flux emanating from *one* pole in one revolution. Since there are $P$ poles and flux per pole is $\Phi$, the flux cut per conductor per revolution is:

$$\Delta\Phi_{\text{per conductor}} = P \Phi \quad [\text{Webers}]$$

**Step 2: Rate of change of flux (flux cut per second).**

The armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. Therefore, the number of revolutions per second is $\dfrac{N}{60}$. The flux cut per second (i.e., $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$, the basic generator EMF for one conductor) is:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \bigg|_{\text{per conductor}} = P\Phi \times \frac{N}{60} = \frac{P\Phi N}{60}$$

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law.**

According to Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the EMF induced in one conductor is:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \quad [\text{Volts}]$$

**Step 4: EMF per parallel path.**

In a DC machine, the $Z$ conductors are connected such that they form $A$ parallel paths. Each parallel path contains $\dfrac{Z}{A}$ conductors connected *in series*. Therefore, the total EMF induced in one parallel path (which equals the armature EMF $E_b$) is:

$$E_b = e_{\text{conductor}} \times \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right)$$

**Step 5: Combine to obtain the standard EMF equation.**

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$E_b = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}\,} \quad [\text{Volts}]$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor $\dfrac{1}{60}$ converts rpm to rps; $\dfrac{1}{A}$ accounts for the parallel-path topology of the armature winding. The result $E_b$ is the **net available armature EMF** appearing at the commutator-brush terminals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation $T_e = \dfrac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$

**Step 1: Force on a single conductor.**

When a current-carrying conductor of active length $L$ lies in a radial magnetic flux density $B$, it experiences a tangential force:

$$F_{\text{conductor}} = B \, L \, I_{\text{conductor}}$$

For a conductor in a parallel path carrying current $I_a / A$:

$$F_{\text{conductor}} = B \, L \, \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right)$$

**Step 2: Torque contributed by one conductor.**

If the mean air-gap radius is $r$ (the *armature radius*), the torque per conductor is:

$$T_{\text{conductor}} = F_{\text{conductor}} \times r = B \, L \, r \, \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right)$$

**Step 3: Relate $BLr$ to $\Phi$.**

The flux per pole, $\Phi$, equals the flux density times the pole area $A_p$:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A_p$$

However, for torque purposes we use the relation that the *total flux crossing the air gap per pole* interacts with all conductors under that pole. A more elegant derivation gives:

$$B \, L \, r = \frac{\Phi}{2} \quad \text{(since two conductors per parallel path span one pole pitch, and } r \times \pi \text{ relates to pole pitch)}$$

Therefore, the torque per conductor simplifies to:

$$T_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{\Phi}{2} \times \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right)$$

**Step 4: Total electromagnetic torque.**

With $Z$ total conductors, the total armature torque is:

$$T_e = T_{\text{conductor}} \times Z = \frac{\Phi}{2} \times \frac{I_a}{A} \times Z$$

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}\,} \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

Note: The factor $P/(2\pi)$ arises from the fact that pole pitch $= \pi D / P$ (where $D$ is armature diameter), and the geometry of flux linkage per pole. This is the **standard form** universally used in DC motor analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Speed Equation $N = \dfrac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$

**Step 1: Start from KVL on the armature circuit.**

$$V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2: Solve for $E_b$.**

$$E_b = V_t - I_a R_a$$

**Step 3: Substitute the EMF equation.**

$$E_b = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A} = V_t - I_a R_a$$

**Step 4: Solve for $N$.**

$$N = \frac{(V_t - I_a R_a) \cdot 60 A}{P \Phi Z}$$

Define the machine constant $k = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$:

$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}\,} \quad [\text{rpm}]$$

**Conversion logic:** This single equation is the **master control equation** for all DC motor regulation strategies. Manipulating $V_t$ (armature control) or $\Phi$ (field control) directly varies $N$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Buck Converter Output Voltage $V_o = D \cdot V_s$

**Step 1: Define the duty cycle.**

Let $T_s$ = switching period, $T_{on}$ = switch ON-time, $T_{off}$ = switch OFF-time.

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}, \quad (1-D) = \frac{T_{off}}{T_s}$$

**Step 2: Apply volt-second balance on the inductor.**

In **steady state**, the average voltage across an inductor over one switching cycle must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would ramp to infinity). For a buck converter with switch $S$ ON, diode OFF: $V_L = V_s - V_o$. With switch OFF, diode ON: $V_L = -V_o$.

$$\int_0^{T_s} V_L \, dt = 0$$

$$(V_s - V_o) \, T_{on} + (-V_o) \, T_{off} = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute duty cycle.**

$$(V_s - V_o) \, D \, T_s - V_o \, (1-D) \, T_s = 0$$

Divide by $T_s$:

$$D(V_s - V_o) = V_o (1-D)$$

$$D \, V_s - D \, V_o = V_o - D \, V_o$$

$$D \, V_s = V_o$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o = D \, V_s\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $0 \le D \le 1$, the output voltage is always less than (or equal to) the input — hence **buck** (step-down).

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Boost Converter Output Voltage $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$

**Step 1: Operating modes.**

- **Switch ON (duration $D T_s$):** Inductor stores energy; diode is reverse-biased; load is supplied by capacitor.
- **Switch OFF (duration $(1-D) T_s$):** Inductor releases energy *in series with* the source to the load; diode conducts.

**Step 2: Volt-second balance on inductor.**

During ON: $V_L = V_s$ (only source across inductor).

During OFF: $V_L = V_s - V_o$ (source and inductor both oppose $V_o$).

Setting average $V_L = 0$:

$$V_s \cdot D T_s + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

Divide by $T_s$:

$$D \, V_s + (1-D)(V_s - V_o) = 0$$

$$D V_s + V_s - V_o - D V_s + D V_o = 0$$

$$V_s - V_o + D V_o = 0$$

$$V_s = V_o (1 - D)$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** As $D \to 1$, $V_o \to \infty$ (theoretically). In practice $D < 0.9$ for stable operation. Hence **boost** (step-up).

---

## 3.6 Derivation of Buck-Boost Converter Output Voltage $V_o = -\dfrac{D}{1-D} V_s$

**Operating principle:** The output is inverted in polarity (hence the negative sign) and its magnitude is either higher or lower than $V_s$ depending on $D$.

- **ON:** Inductor charges from $V_s$; diode OFF; load fed by capacitor.
- **OFF:** Inductor discharges through diode to load (reverse polarity).

Volt-second balance:

$$V_s \cdot D + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

$$D V_s = V_o (1 - D)$$

$$V_o = \frac{D}{1-D} V_s$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -\frac{D}{1-D} V_s\,} \quad \text{(with sign convention that output is opposite polarity)}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Speed Control Methods

| Method | Manipulated Variable | Speed Range | Torque at Low Speed | Efficiency | EV/HEV Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Armature Voltage Control** (Chopper) | $V_t$ | Below base speed | High (limited by $I_a$) | High | **Excellent** (most used) |
| **Field Flux Control** (Rheostat) | $\Phi$ | Above base speed | Low at high speed | Moderate | Supplementary (field weakening) |
| **Ward-Leonard** | $V_t$ + $\Phi$ | Wide, smooth | High | Low (double conversion) | Obsolete (now solid-state) |
| **Combined Armature + Field** | $V_t$ & $\Phi$ | Full range | High at start, smooth above base | High | **Modern EV standard** |

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DC-DC Converters

| Topology | $V_o / V_s$ | Polarity | Complexity | Use in HEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck** | $D$ | Same | Low | 12 V / 48 V auxiliary bus |
| **Boost** | $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | Same | Low | Fuel cell voltage booster |
| **Buck-Boost** | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | Inverted | Medium | Battery voltage equalisation |
| **Ćuk** | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | Inverted | Medium-High | Smooth current (low ripple) |
| **SEPIC** | $\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | Same | Medium | Battery charger |
| **Full-Bridge Bidirectional** | $\pm D$ | Same | High | Battery ↔ DC link (regen braking) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Brushed DC Motor Drive System in HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Battery[DC Battery Pack 300V] --> ChopperMain[Step Down Chopper Vt]
    FieldSupply[Separate Field Supply 12V] --> FieldWind[Field Winding]
    ChopperMain --> ArmatureWind[Armature Winding]
    ArmatureWind --> Commutator[Commutator Assembly]
    Commutator --> Brushes[Carbon Brushes]
    Brushes --> Shaft[Rotating Shaft Torque Speed]
    FieldWind --> MagneticField[Radial Magnetic Field Phi]
    MagneticField --> LorentzForce[Lorentz Force Action]
    ArmatureWind --> LorentzForce
    LorentzForce --> Shaft
    Shaft --> MechanicalOutput[Mechanical Output to Transmission]
    ChopperMain --> PWM[PWM Controller 10kHz]
    PWM --> Feedback[Speed Torque Feedback Sensors]
    Feedback --> ECU[Vehicle ECU]
    ECU --> PWM
    AuxiliaryLoad[12V Auxiliary Loads] --> BuckAux[Buck Converter 12V]
    BuckAux --> Battery
    FuelCell[Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell] --> BoostFC[Boost Converter]
    BoostFC --> ChopperMain
    style Battery fill:#ffd700
    style ChopperMain fill:#87ceeb
    style PWM fill:#90ee90
    style ECU fill:#ffb6c1
    style MechanicalOutput fill:#dda0dd
```

**Description of flow:**
- The **DC battery** supplies the main chopper, which regulates $V_t$ applied to the armature.
- The **separate field supply** maintains constant flux $\Phi$.
- The **PWM controller** modulates the chopper switch at high frequency.
- The **ECU** receives speed/torque feedback and adjusts the duty cycle $D$ in real time.
- The **auxiliary buck converter** steps down the high-voltage DC bus to power 12 V accessories.
- Optionally, a **fuel cell** can be boosted to the DC-link level and contribute via the chopper input.

---

## 4.2 Chopper (Buck Converter) Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vdc[DC Source Vs Battery] --> SwitchPos[Positive Rail]
    SwitchPos --> IGBT[IGBT Switch S]
    IGBT --> InductorNode[Node A]
    InductorNode --> Inductor[L Inductor]
    Inductor --> LoadPos[Load Positive Terminal Vo]
    LoadPos --> RL[Load RL Motor Armature]
    RL --> LoadNeg[Load Negative Terminal]
    LoadNeg --> SourceNeg[Source Negative]
    InductorNode --> Diode[D Freewheeling Diode]
    Diode --> SourceNeg
    LoadPos --> Capacitor[C Filter Capacitor]
    Capacitor --> LoadNeg
    GateDrive[Gate Drive PWM Signal D] --> IGBT
    style Vdc fill:#ffd700
    style IGBT fill:#ff6347
    style Diode fill:#87cefa
    style Inductor fill:#d3d3d3
    style Capacitor fill:#d3d3d3
    style GateDrive fill:#90ee90
```

**Operating explanation:**
- When **IGBT S is ON**: current flows from $V_s$ → $S$ → $L$ → load → back to source. The inductor stores energy.
- When **IGBT S is OFF**: inductor current freewheels through diode $D$ → load → source. The inductor releases its stored energy, maintaining load current.
- By varying duty cycle $D$, the average load voltage $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ is controlled — this is the **PWM chopper** principle.

---

## 4.3 Torque–Speed Characteristic Curve (Mermaid Plot Approximation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StartPoint[Origin: N=0, T=Max Tstall] --> RegionA[Region I Constant Torque]
    RegionA --> BasePoint[Base Speed Nbase]
    BasePoint --> RegionB[Region II Constant Power]
    RegionB --> MaxPoint[Max Speed Nmax]
    Note1[Flux Phi constant - Armature control] -.-> RegionA
    Note2[Flux reduced - Field weakening] -.-> RegionB
    style StartPoint fill:#ff6347
    style MaxPoint fill:#90ee90
    style Note1 fill:#fffacd
    style Note2 fill:#fffacd
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Region I (0 to $N_{base}$):** Full flux, armature voltage ramps from 0 to $V_{rated}$. Torque is approximately constant (limited by $I_a$).
- **Region II ($N_{base}$ to $N_{max}$):** Voltage clamped at $V_{rated}$, flux weakened. Torque falls as $1/N$, but power $P = T \cdot \omega$ stays approximately constant.

---

## 4.4 Signal Flow — PWM-Controlled DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SpeedRef[Reference Speed Nref from Accelerator Pedal] --> Comparator1[Comparator]
    TriangleWave[Triangular Carrier Wave fs=10kHz] --> Comparator1
    Comparator1 --> PWMSignal[PWM Signal Duty D]
    PWMSignal --> GateDriver[IGBT Gate Driver]
    GateDriver --> ChopperSwitch[Chopper Switch S]
    ChopperSwitch --> MotorArmature[DC Motor Armature]
    MotorArmature --> TachoGen[Tachogenerator Encoder]
    TachoGen --> ActualSpeed[Actual Speed Nactual]
    ActualSpeed --> FeedbackLoop[Negative Feedback]
    FeedbackLoop --> Comparator1
    TorqueLoad[Load Torque Disturbance] --> MotorArmature
    style SpeedRef fill:#90ee90
    style PWMSignal fill:#ffd700
    style ChopperSwitch fill:#ff6347
    style MotorArmature fill:#87ceeb
```

**Description:**
- A **closed-loop speed control** system compares the reference speed (driver demand) with actual speed (sensor feedback).
- The error generates a **PWM signal** whose duty cycle adjusts the chopper, which controls $V_t$, which in turn controls $N$.
- This is the **fundamental EV traction control loop** in its simplest form.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> *Define Back EMF in a DC motor. Explain its significance in motor operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Back EMF ($E_b$) is the electromotive force induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor due to their rotation through the magnetic field of the stator. By Lenz's Law, it opposes the applied armature voltage.

**Mathematical expression (1 mark):**

$$E_b = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
- It regulates the current drawn by the armature: $I_a = \dfrac{V_t - E_b}{R_a}$
- It is the primary means of **self-regulation** — as speed increases, $E_b$ rises, reducing $I_a$ and thus torque, until equilibrium is reached.
- All speed-control methods manipulate $V_t$ or $\Phi$ to influence $E_b$, which in turn controls $N$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> *Differentiate between Buck and Boost converters. Give one application of each in HEVs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Buck Converter | Boost Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Output relation | $V_o = D \, V_s$ (less than $V_s$) | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ (greater than $V_s$) |
| Switch location | In series with source | Inductor charges from source |
| Application in HEV | 12 V auxiliary bus from 400 V DC link | Boosting 250 V fuel-cell output to 400 V DC link |

**One-line key:** Buck = *step-down* (chopper for motor armature in low-power EVs); Boost = *step-up* (fuel cell voltage matching).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** Derive the EMF and Torque equations of a DC motor, clearly defining all symbols. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A **separately excited DC motor** has the following specifications:
> $P = 4$ poles, $\Phi = 0.05 \, \text{Wb}$, $Z = 500$ conductors, $A = 2$ (wave wound), $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $V_t = 220 \, \text{V}$.
> If the motor draws an armature current of $I_a = 30 \, \text{A}$, compute the:
> (i) Back EMF $E_b$
> (ii) Developed torque $T_e$
> (iii) Operating speed $N$ in rpm
> (iv) Mechanical power developed $P_m$
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Derivation (7 marks)**

The detailed derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** (EMF) and **Section 3.2** (Torque) of these notes. The examiner expects the student to:
- Start with Faraday's law (1 mark)
- Compute flux cut per conductor per second (2 marks)
- Account for parallel paths $A$ (1 mark)
- Arrive at $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ (1 mark)
- For torque: use Lorentz force $F = BIL$ (1 mark)
- Multiply by radius, then by total conductors, substitute $BLr = \Phi/2$, to get $T_e = \dfrac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$ (1 mark)

**Part (b): Numerical (7 marks)**

Given:
$P = 4$, $\Phi = 0.05 \, \text{Wb}$, $Z = 500$, $A = 2$, $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $V_t = 220 \, \text{V}$, $I_a = 30 \, \text{A}$.

**(i) Back EMF $E_b$:** Using KVL, $E_b = V_t - I_a R_a$:

$$E_b = 220 - (30)(0.5) = 220 - 15 = \boxed{205 \, \text{V}}$$

**(ii) Developed torque $T_e$:**

$$T_e = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{(4)(0.05)(500)(30)}{2\pi (2)}$$

$$T_e = \frac{3000}{4\pi} = \frac{3000}{12.566} \approx \boxed{238.7 \, \text{N·m}}$$

**(iii) Operating speed $N$:** From EMF equation:

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 A}{P \Phi Z} = \frac{(205)(60)(2)}{(4)(0.05)(500)}$$

$$N = \frac{24600}{100} = \boxed{246 \, \text{rpm}}$$

**(iv) Mechanical power $P_m$:**

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = (205)(30) = \boxed{6150 \, \text{W} = 6.15 \, \text{kW}}$$

Alternative check: $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega = 238.7 \times \dfrac{2\pi (246)}{60} = 238.7 \times 25.76 \approx 6148 \, \text{W}$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly confuse $P$ (number of poles) with power in the EMF equation. Always write the EMF equation explicitly with units at each step. Also, in wave winding, $A = 2$ *regardless* of the number of poles — this is a frequent error. Marks are deducted if the distinction between lap ($A = P$) and wave ($A = 2$) is not understood.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain the **armature voltage control** method of speed control of a DC motor with relevant equations and characteristics. Why is it preferred for EV traction applications? **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Buck converter** as a DC chopper. Derive its output voltage equation. A buck converter operates from a 400 V DC source with switching frequency 20 kHz and duty cycle $D = 0.4$. Compute the output voltage and the average inductor current if the load draws 10 A. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Armature Voltage Control (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):** In armature voltage control, the field flux $\Phi$ is maintained constant (via a separate excitation source), and the armature terminal voltage $V_t$ is varied to control speed. The relationship is:

$$N = \frac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$$

Since $R_a$ is small, $N \propto V_t$ approximately — giving a **linear control** characteristic.

**Block schematic (2 marks):** A **DC chopper** (PWM-controlled switching converter) placed between the DC battery and the armature terminals performs the regulation. By varying the duty cycle $D$, $V_t = D \cdot V_s$ is changed continuously from 0 to $V_s$.

**Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Constant torque region:** Below base speed, full torque is available.
- **Independent of load:** Speed depends mainly on $V_t$, not on $I_a$.
- **Smooth and stepless** control.
- **Fast dynamic response** due to high-frequency PWM.

**Why preferred for EVs (1 mark):**
- Battery is DC source → chopper interfaces directly without AC-DC stage.
- High efficiency (>90%) due to switch-mode operation.
- Compact, lightweight, regenerative braking possible.
- Precise torque and speed control for traction demands.

**Part (b): Buck Converter (7 marks)**

**Circuit & Operation (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram. Circuit consists of:
- Switch $S$ (IGBT/MOSFET)
- Freewheeling diode $D_f$
- Inductor $L$
- Output capacitor $C$
- Load $R_L$

**Mode 1 — Switch ON ($0 < t < D T_s$):**
- Diode reverse-biased.
- Current path: $V_s \to S \to L \to C \parallel R_L \to$ ground.
- Inductor stores energy: $V_L = V_s - V_o > 0$, current rises linearly.

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF ($D T_s < t < T_s$):**
- Diode forward-biased (freewheeling).
- Inductor releases energy through diode: $V_L = -V_o < 0$, current falls.
- Capacitor maintains output voltage.

**Derivation (2 marks):** Using volt-second balance (see Section 3.4):

$$V_o = D \cdot V_s$$

**Numerical (2 marks):**

Given: $V_s = 400 \, \text{V}$, $D = 0.4$, $I_o = 10 \, \text{A}$.

$$V_o = 0.4 \times 400 = \boxed{160 \, \text{V}}$$

**Average inductor current** (assuming lossless converter):

$$I_L = I_o = \boxed{10 \, \text{A}}$$

(since in a buck converter, average inductor current equals load current).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common errors in buck converter problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the polarity inversion** when writing $V_o$ for a buck-boost (must show the negative sign).
> 2. **Confusing average inductor current with ripple current** — they are different. $I_L$ (average) is the DC load current; $\Delta I_L$ (ripple) is the small AC component superimposed on it, given by $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{(V_s - V_o) D}{f_s L}$.
> 
> Also, do NOT apply the EMF equation $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ to a converter — that is exclusively for the rotating machine. Mixing up equations between the motor and the chopper costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Brushed DC Motor & DC Voltage Conversion**

### Core Motor Equations (must memorise)
- ✅ **Back EMF:** $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$
- ✅ **Torque:** $T_e = \dfrac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$
- ✅ **Armature KVL:** $V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$
- ✅ **Speed:** $N = \dfrac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$, where $k = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$
- ✅ **Power:** $P_m = E_b I_a = T_e \omega$

### Key Numerical Constants & Waveforms
- ✅ **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (more parallel paths, more current)
- ✅ **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ always (less parallel paths, higher voltage)
- ✅ $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ (rad/s conversion)

### Speed Control Methods (in order of KTU importance)
- ✅ **Armature voltage control** — most common in EVs via chopper
- ✅ **Field flux control** — for above-base-speed (field weakening)
- ✅ **Ward-Leonard** — legacy system (mention only)
- ✅ **Combined control** — modern EV constant-torque + constant-power region

### DC-DC Converters (topology comparison)
- ✅ **Buck:** $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ (step-down)
- ✅ **Boost:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ (step-up)
- ✅ **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = -\dfrac{D}{1-D} V_s$ (inverted polarity)
- ✅ **Duty cycle** $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_s}$, always between 0 and 1
- ✅ **PWM frequency** in EVs: typically **10–20 kHz**

### HEV-Specific Applications to Remember
- ✅ Battery → Chopper → DC motor armature (traction)
- ✅ DC link (400 V) → Buck → 12 V auxiliary bus
- ✅ Fuel cell (low voltage) → Boost → DC link
- ✅ Regenerative braking → Bidirectional converter → Battery charging

### Common KTU Pitfalls (avoid these!)
- ⚠️ Mixing $P$ (poles) with $P$ (power) — use context clues
- ⚠️ Forgetting $A = 2$ for wave winding regardless of poles
- ⚠️ Confusing $E_b$ (back EMF) with $V_t$ (supply voltage)
- ⚠️ Using motor EMF equation for static converter problems
- ⚠️ Missing negative sign in buck-boost output
- ⚠️ Not converting rpm to rad/s when computing power from torque

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brushless Electric Motors - Brushless DC Motor, Switched Reluctance Motors, Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Brushless Electric Motors - Brushless DC Motor, Switched Reluctance Motors, Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** is a synchronous electric motor powered by direct current (DC) electricity, where the mechanical commutation function (switching of current direction in the windings) is electronically controlled rather than achieved through mechanical brushes and a commutator. The rotor carries permanent magnets, while the stator carries the armature windings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Horse Carousel":**
> Imagine a modern carousel (merry-go-round) where the horses (rotor magnets) are pulled around not by a central rotating pole, but by a sequence of electromagnets (stator coils) that switch ON and OFF in a choreographed order, detected by a sensor. The "switching music" is created by an electronic controller (ESC) instead of a physical belt and brushes. This eliminates friction, sparks, and wear — making it ideal for Electric Vehicles (EVs).

**Key Physical Constants:**
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- Standard EV traction voltage rail: **400 V DC – 800 V DC**

**Why BLDC in EVs?**
- High efficiency (**> 90%**)
- Excellent **torque-to-weight ratio**
- Low maintenance (no brushes to wear out)
- Superior thermal management

---

## 1.2 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** is a doubly-salient, singly-excited synchronous electric motor where torque is produced by the tendency of the rotor to move to a position of minimum magnetic reluctance (maximum inductance) when a stator phase is energized. It has **no permanent magnets** and **no rotor windings**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnetic Dog Chasing a Bone":**
> Picture a dog (rotor with iron teeth) trying to reach a hidden bone (stator magnetic field). When you energize one stator phase, the dog "reluctantly" rotates to align its iron teeth with the energized phase to minimize magnetic resistance. As soon as it aligns, you switch OFF that phase and energize the next — the dog keeps chasing. No magnets are needed; pure magnetic attraction drives the motion.

**Salient Structural Features:**
- **Doubly salient**: Both stator and rotor have protruding poles.
- **Singly excited**: Only the stator carries windings.
- Rotor: Simple laminated steel stack — extremely rugged.

---

## 1.3 Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Induction Motor (IM)** is an asynchronous AC electric motor in which the electric current in the rotor needed to produce torque is obtained via electromagnetic induction from the magnetic field of the stator winding. It does not require permanent magnets or rotor excitation circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Squirrel in a Cage":**
> Imagine a sealed cylindrical cage (stator) where a rotating magnetic field is created by 3-phase AC power. Inside the cage is a lightweight aluminum squirrel-cage (rotor bars). The rotating field "drags" the cage along — but the squirrel never quite catches up with the field; it always lags slightly. This difference in speed is called **slip (s)**, and is the very reason torque is produced.

**Why Induction Motors for EVs?**
- Cheapest and most mass-manufactured motor in the world (used by **Tesla Model S/X**).
- Highly reliable — **no permanent magnets** (resistant to rare-earth price shocks).
- Robust construction tolerates harsh automotive environments.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Alignment — KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> All three motors satisfy **CO2 (Understand electric propulsion architectures for HEV/EV)** and form the core of the electric drive-train subsystem analysis.

| Motor Type | Rotor Type | Excitation | Commutation | Typical EV Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BLDC** | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Permanent magnets | Electronic (sensor-based) | Tesla Model 3, Chevy Bolt |
| **SRM** | Salient steel rotor | Stator only | Electronic (rotor position sensor) | Land Rover Range Extender |
| **Induction Motor** | Squirrel cage / Wound | Induced (Faraday's Law) | Naturally asynchronous | Tesla Model S/X, Toyota RAV4 EV |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Brushless DC Motor — Operating Principle

### Working Steps
1. **Rotor Position Sensing:** Hall-effect sensors (or back-EMF sensing) detect the angular position of the permanent-magnet rotor.
2. **Electronic Commutation:** The inverter (ESC) energizes the appropriate stator phase pair (e.g., Phase A+, Phase B−) based on rotor position.
3. **Magnetic Interaction:** The energized stator winding creates a magnetic field that repels/attracts the PM rotor, producing unidirectional torque.
4. **Sequential Switching:** As the rotor turns, the next phase pair is energized, sustaining continuous rotation.
5. **PWM Speed Control:** The DC bus voltage is modulated using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) to regulate speed.

### BLDC Trapezoidal vs. Sinusoidal Drive
- **Trapezoidal (120° conduction):** Simpler, more cost-effective; produces torque ripple.
- **Sinusoidal (180° conduction):** Smoother torque; uses Field-Oriented Control (FOC).

---

## 2.2 Switched Reluctance Motor — Operating Principle

### Working Steps
1. **Phase Energization:** When stator Phase A is energized, a magnetic field is established across the air-gap.
2. **Reluctance Minimization:** The rotor (with salient poles) rotates to minimize the magnetic path reluctance (i.e., align rotor poles with the energized stator poles).
3. **Sequential Commutation:** The controller de-energizes Phase A and energizes Phase B, B', then A' in sequence.
4. **Sensor Feedback:** A rotor position transducer (optical encoder / resolver) feeds back angular data.
5. **Asymmetric Half-Bridge Inverter:** Required because torque direction depends only on **dL/dθ** (not on current direction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SRMs are gaining popularity in EVs (e.g., **Renault Fluence Z.E. range extender**) due to their **fault tolerance**, **high-speed capability**, and **absence of rare-earth magnets**.

---

## 2.3 Three-Phase Induction Motor — Operating Principle

### Working Steps
1. **Three-Phase Stator Supply:** A 3-phase AC voltage is applied to the stator windings (displaced 120° electrically).
2. **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF):** A constant-magnitude rotating magnetic field is produced at synchronous speed $N_s$.
3. **Faraday Induction:** The RMF cuts the rotor conductors, inducing an EMF and hence rotor current (per Lenz's law).
4. **Lorentz Force:** The induced rotor current interacts with the RMF to produce torque.
5. **Slip Operation:** The rotor can never reach synchronous speed (or no relative motion, hence no induction, hence no torque).

### Equivalent Circuit (Per-Phase, Referred to Stator)
- Stator impedance: $R_s + jX_s$
- Rotor impedance (referred): $\frac{R_r'}{s} + jX_r'$
- Magnetizing branch: $jX_m$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **verifiable from first principles** and **directly aligned with KTU ESE Module-2 questions**.

### A. Common Electrical Machine Foundations
| Formula | Expression | Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | $f$ = supply frequency (Hz), $P$ = number of poles |
| Angular Synchronous Speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{\pi f}{P/2}$ | rad/s |
| Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \, f$ | Hz |

### B. Brushless DC Motor
| Formula | Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Back-EMF (per phase) | $E_b = K_e \, \Phi \, \omega$ | $K_e$ = back-EMF constant |
| Armature EMF Constant | $E = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega \cdot \dfrac{N_{ser}}{60}$ | $N_{ser}$ = series turns/phase |
| Torque Developed | $T = K_t \, \Phi \, I_a$ | $K_t$ = torque constant, $\Phi$ = flux/pole (Wb) |
| Speed Equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi}$ | $V$ = DC link voltage (V) |
| Power Balance | $P_{in} = V \, I_a, \quad P_{mech} = T \, \omega$ | Watt (W) |

### C. Switched Reluctance Motor
| Formula | Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Instantaneous Torque | $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | The fundamental SRM torque equation |
| Co-energy | $W'_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^{2}$ | Joules (J) |
| Average Torque | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{m \, V_{bus}^{2}}{\omega^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{dL/d\theta}{(L_{max} - L_{min})^{2}}$ | derived from energy conversion loop |
| Inductance Profile | $L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cdot \cos(N_r \theta)$ | $N_r$ = number of rotor poles |
| Number of Stator Phases | $m = \dfrac{N_s}{N_r} \cdot q$ | $q$ = phases per cycle |

### D. Three-Phase Induction Motor
| Formula | Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rotor EMF (referred) | $E_r' = s \, E_2$ | proportional to slip |
| Rotor Resistance (referred) | $R_r'/s = R_r' + \dfrac{R_r'(1-s)}{s}$ | split into useful + mechanical equiv. |
| Thevenin Voltage | $V_{th} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{jX_m}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$ | V (Volts) |
| Thevenin Impedance | $Z_{th} = \dfrac{jX_m(R_s + jX_s)}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$ | Ω (Ohms) |
| Air-Gap Power | $P_{ag} = 3 \, I_r'^{\,2} \, \dfrac{R_r'}{s}$ | W |
| Mechanical Power | $P_{mech} = (1-s) \, P_{ag}$ | W |
| Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^{2} \cdot R_r'/s}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}$ | N·m |
| Starting Torque Ratio | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \, s_{max} \, s_{st}}{s_{st}^{2} + s_{max}^{2}}$ | dimensionless |
| Slip at Max Torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}}$ | per-unit |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL ALERT — Pipe Symbol in Markdown Tables:**
> In the formulas above, $\dfrac{R_r'}{s}$ uses the LaTeX fraction command instead of the markdown-reserved pipe character $\vert$. This is the KTU-engine standard for any markdown table cell.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Motor | Key Industrial Application (EV / HEV) | Strategic Advantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BLDC** | Light EV 2-wheelers (Hero Electric, Ather), e-bikes, drones | Highest efficiency at low loads |
| **SRM** | High-speed traction (Renault, Rolls-Royce aerospace actuator) | Magnet-free, fault-tolerant |
| **Induction Motor** | Tesla Model S Plaid, Lucid Air, BMW i3 (early) | Cheapest, robust, mass-productive |

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — BLDC Motor Back-EMF Equation

### Starting Point: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

For a single conductor of length $L_c$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B$:

$$
\mathcal{E}_{one\,conductor} = B \, L_c \, v
$$

### Step 1 — Velocity in Terms of Angular Speed

The linear velocity at radius $r$ from the rotor center is $v = \omega \, r$. Substituting:

$$
\mathcal{E}_{one\,conductor} = B \, L_c \, \omega \, r
$$

### Step 2 — Flux per Pole

If each pole has an effective flux $\Phi$ (Wb) and the magnetic flux density under the pole face (area $A$) is $B = \Phi / A$:

$$
\Phi = B \cdot A
$$

Substituting and combining with the conductor length under one pole:

$$
\mathcal{E}_{one\,conductor} = \Phi \, \omega
$$

### Step 3 — Total EMF per Phase

For a BLDC motor:
- Number of poles: $P$
- Number of parallel paths in a wave-wound DC armature: $a = 2$
- Number of conductors per phase: $Z_{ph}$
- Conductors per parallel path: $Z_{ph} / a$

The total EMF induced per phase is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \left(\dfrac{\text{Total conductors}}{\text{Number of phases}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Phi \, \omega}{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a $P$-pole, $A$-parallel-path machine, the standard armature EMF equation reduces to:

$$
\boxed{E_{ph} = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = rotor speed (rpm)
- $A$ = number of parallel paths

### Step 4 — Torque Equation (Power Balance)

The mechanical power developed equals the electrical air-gap power:

$$
P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a
$$

Substituting $E_b$:

$$
T_e \cdot \omega = \left(\dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}\right) \cdot I_a
$$

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, solving for $T_e$:

$$
\boxed{T_e = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since $T_e \propto \Phi \, I_a$ and BLDC has **constant $\Phi$** (fixed by permanent magnets), torque is directly proportional to armature current. This gives the linear, predictable response that makes BLDC ideal for traction control algorithms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Switched Reluctance Motor Torque

### Starting Point: Co-energy Principle

For a singly-excited magnetic system, the instantaneous torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to rotor position, at constant current:

$$
T = \left.\dfrac{\partial W'_{co}(i, \theta)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}}
$$

### Step 1 — Co-energy Expression

For a linear magnetic system (no saturation), the co-energy stored in a winding with inductance $L(\theta)$ carrying current $i$ is:

$$
W'_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Position

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^{2}\right]_{i = \text{const}}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{i^{2}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T(\theta, i) = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}}
$$

### Step 3 — Sign and Direction Analysis

- When $dL/d\theta > 0$ (inductance rising): torque is **positive** (motoring).
- When $dL/d\theta < 0$ (inductance falling): torque is **negative** (generating / braking).
- Torque direction is **independent of current polarity** — only $i^2$ matters.

### Step 4 — Average Torque from Energy Conversion Loop

Over one electrical cycle (from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$ per phase stroke):

$$
W_{loop} = \oint i \, d\psi = \text{Area of } \psi \text{–} i \text{ loop}
$$

Average torque per phase:

$$
T_{avg,\,ph} = \dfrac{W_{loop}}{\theta_{stroke}} = \dfrac{W_{loop}}{(2\pi) / N_r}
$$

For an $m$-phase machine:

$$
\boxed{T_{avg} = m \cdot \dfrac{W_{loop}}{\theta_{stroke}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because torque depends on $i^2$, the inverter can use **unipolar currents** — only one direction of current is needed. This is why SRM drives use **asymmetric half-bridge converters** with $2m$ switches and $2m$ diodes, not a full $6$-switch inverter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Induction Motor Torque Equation

### Starting Point: Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit

The exact per-phase equivalent circuit (stator and rotor quantities referred to stator) gives rotor current:

$$
I_r' = \dfrac{V_s}{Z_s + Z_m \,\vert\vert\, Z_r'}
$$

For practical torque derivation, we use the **approximate equivalent circuit** (moving the magnetizing branch to the stator terminals gives the Thevenin equivalent):

### Step 1 — Thevenin Voltage and Impedance

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= V_s \cdot \dfrac{jX_m}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}
\\[6pt]
Z_{th} &= \dfrac{jX_m (R_s + jX_s)}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)} = R_{th} + jX_{th}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Rotor Current (Thevenin Equivalent)

$$
I_r' = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_r'/s + j(X_{th} + X_r')}
$$

### Step 3 — Magnitude Squared

$$
\vert I_r' \vert^{2} = \dfrac{V_{th}^{2}}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Air-Gap Power

The air-gap power per phase is $\vert I_r' \vert^{2} \cdot R_r'/s$. For 3 phases:

$$
P_{ag} = \dfrac{3 \, V_{th}^{2} \, R_r'/s}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Mechanical Power and Torque

Mechanical power developed = $(1-s) \cdot P_{ag}$. Synchronous mechanical speed is $\omega_s$.

$$
T_e = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{(1-s) \, P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3 \, V_{th}^{2} \, R_r'/s}{\omega_s \left[(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}\right]}
$$

Wait — since $\dfrac{R_r'/s \cdot (1-s)}{1} = R_r' \cdot \dfrac{1-s}{s}$, we can simplify. But the **standard textbook form** uses the air-gap power directly divided by synchronous speed (in rad/s mechanical):

$$
\boxed{T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^{2} \, (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}}
$$

### Step 6 — Condition for Maximum Torque

Setting $\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$ gives:

$$
\boxed{s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}}}
$$

Substituting back yields the **maximum torque expression**:

$$
\boxed{T_{max} = \dfrac{3 \, V_{th}^{2}}{2 \, \omega_s \, \left[R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}\right]}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_r'$! Only $s_{max}$ depends on it. This is why rotor-resistance variation (via slip-ring control) is used to shift the operating slip while keeping peak torque unchanged.

---

## 3.4 Slip–Torque Characteristic Curve (Analytical Sketch)

For typical values $R_{th} \ll X_{th} + X_r'$, the torque–slip curve has these key points:

| Operating Point | Slip ($s$) | Torque ($T$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Start (locked rotor) | $s = 1$ | $T_{st}$ |
| Maximum torque | $s = s_{max}$ | $T_{max}$ |
| Normal running | $s \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ | $T_{full-load}$ |
| Synchronous (no-load) | $s \to 0$ | $T \to 0$ |

**Region classification:**
- $0 \leq s < s_{max}$: **Stable operating region** (constant-torque region).
- $s_{max} < s \leq 1$: **Unstable region** (only briefly traversed during startup).

# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BLDC Motor Drive Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrl[ControllerMCU] --> pwm[PWMGenerator]
    pwm --> inv[ThreePhaseInverter]
    bms[BMSandDCLink] --> inv
    inv --> phA[StatorPhaseA]
    inv --> phB[StatorPhaseB]
    inv --> phC[StatorPhaseC]
    phA --> motor[BLDCMotorRotor]
    phB --> motor
    phC --> motor
    hallA[HallSensorA] --> ctrl
    hallB[HallSensorB] --> ctrl
    hallC[HallSensorC] --> ctrl
    motor --> hallA
    motor --> hallB
    motor --> hallC
```

**Block Description:**
- `ControllerMCU` reads 3 Hall sensors from rotor.
- `PWMGenerator` produces 6 gate pulses for the inverter.
- `ThreePhaseInverter` uses 6 IGBTs/MOSFETs (VSI) to convert DC to 3-phase AC.
- `StatorPhaseA/B/C` are sequentially energized to produce rotating field.

---

## 4.2 Switched Reluctance Motor Drive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    batt[BatteryPack] --> conv[AsymmetricHalfBridgeConverter]
    conv --> ph1[StatorPhaseA]
    conv --> ph2[StatorPhaseB]
    conv --> ph3[StatorPhaseC]
    ph1 --> srm[SRMRotorDoublySalient]
    ph2 --> srm
    ph3 --> srm
    srm --> enc[RotorPositionEncoder]
    enc --> ctrl1[DSPController]
    ctrl1 --> conv
    ctrl1 --> soft[SoftSwitchingLogic]
```

**Block Description:**
- `BatteryPack` feeds the DC bus.
- `AsymmetricHalfBridgeConverter` uses $2m$ switches (here $m=3$, so 6 switches + 6 diodes).
- `SRMRotorDoublySalient` rotates by reluctance minimization.
- `RotorPositionEncoder` (resolver or optical) provides $\theta$.
- `DSPController` computes turn-on $\theta_{on}$ and turn-off $\theta_{off}$ angles for commutation.

---

## 4.3 Induction Motor V/f (Volts per Hertz) Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[SpeedReferenceRPM] --> ramp[RampGenerator]
    ramp --> vf[V_by_fWaveformGenerator]
    vf --> pwm1[SPWMModulator]
    pwm1 --> vsi[TwoLevelVoltageSourceInverter]
    bms1[DCLinkCapacitor] --> vsi
    vsi --> im[ThreePhaseInductionMotor]
    im --> fb[TachogeneratorSensor]
    fb --> ramp
    fb --> ctrl1[PICompensator]
    ctrl1 --> vf
```

**Block Description:**
- `SpeedReferenceRPM` sets the target rotor speed.
- `V_by_fWaveformGenerator` ensures **constant air-gap flux** by maintaining $V/f = $ constant.
- `SPWMModulator` generates the gate pulses for the VSI.
- `PICompensator` closes the speed loop for tight regulation under load.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC_BRUSHED["Conventional Brushed DC"]
        bdc[Battery] --> brc[Brushes andCommutator]
        brc --> bro[DCStator]
        bro --> brt[Rotation]
    end

    subgraph BLDC["BLDC EV Drive"]
        bbattery[BatteryPack] --> besc[ElectronicSpeedController]
        bhall[HallSensors] --> besc
        besc --> binv[ThreePhaseVSI]
        binv --> bphase[StatorWindings]
        bphase --> brot[PMRotor]
        brot --> bhall
    end

    subgraph SRM_TOP["SRM EV Drive"]
        sbattery[BatteryPack] --> sconv[AsymmetricConverter]
        senc[Encoder] --> sctrl[DSP]
        sctrl --> sconv
        sconv --> sphaser[StatorPhases]
        sphaser --> srotor[SalientSteelRotor]
        srotor --> senc
    end

    subgraph IM_TOP["Induction Motor EV Drive"]
        ibattery[BatteryPack] --> iinv[DCACInverter]
        ivf[VFController] --> iinv
        ifb[TachFeedback] --> ivf
        iinv --> istator[StatorWinding3Phase]
        istator --> irotor[SquirrelCageRotor]
        irotor --> ifb
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Insight:** All three EV motor drives share a common structure: **Battery → Power Electronics Converter → Motor → Sensor → Controller → Feedback**. This forms the canonical KTU Module-2 drive-train architecture.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List any three advantages of a Brushless DC Motor over a conventional brushed DC motor in the context of electric vehicle applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher Efficiency** — Absence of brush friction eliminates mechanical losses, giving efficiencies greater than 90%, which directly extends EV driving range.
2. **Better Thermal Management** — Windings are on the stator (outer periphery), allowing direct cooling via the housing; the rotor (PM) generates little heat.
3. **Lower Maintenance & Longer Life** — No brushes or commutator to wear out, resulting in operational lifetimes exceeding 10,000 hours under automotive duty cycles.

*(Alternative acceptable points: higher power density, higher speed capability, reduced acoustic noise and EMI.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Why does a Switched Reluctance Motor not require permanent magnets on the rotor? How is torque produced in it?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A Switched Reluctance Motor does not require permanent magnets because **torque is produced by the variable reluctance principle** rather than by magnetic field interaction.
- When a stator phase is energized, the rotor (a salient, magnetically-soft steel piece) experiences a force that drives it to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** — i.e., aligning its salient poles with the energized stator poles.
- Mathematically, the instantaneous torque is $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$, which is independent of current direction and depends only on the rate of change of phase inductance with rotor position.

---

## 📌 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### 🔷 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**Question (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Brushless DC Motor drive used in electric vehicles. Derive the EMF and torque equations."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with three-phase armature windings (A, B, C) inserted in axial slots. The windings are distributed, often in a star configuration.
- **Rotor:** Surface-mounted or interior-mounted **permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB for high energy density) arranged in a $P$-pole pattern ($P = 2, 4, 6, 8$ common).
- **Position Sensors:** Three Hall-effect sensors mounted on the stator at 120° electrical spacing detect rotor position.
- **Power Electronics:** Three-phase voltage source inverter (VSI) using 6 MOSFETs/IGBTs, fed from a DC link (battery).

**Step 2 — Working (2 marks):**
- The Hall sensors detect rotor angle $\theta$.
- The microcontroller (ESC) determines which two phases to energize.
- The 3-phase inverter applies voltage to those phases, creating a magnetic field that drags the PM rotor.
- As rotor moves, Hall signals change, triggering commutation to the next phase pair.
- PWM modulates the DC bus voltage to control speed.

**Step 3 — EMF Equation Derivation (1 mark):**
Using Faraday's law on a $P$-pole, $A$-parallel-path armature with $Z$ total conductors and flux $\Phi$ per pole at speed $N$ rpm:

$$
E_b = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque Equation Derivation (1 mark):**
From power balance $T_e \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$ with $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$
T_e = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}
$$

---

**Question (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"A 4-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor has 600 armature conductors, a flux per pole of 0.02 Wb, and runs at 1500 rpm. The armature winding is wave-connected ($A=2$) and carries 20 A. Calculate the induced EMF and the electromagnetic torque developed."*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles
- $Z = 600$ conductors
- $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- $A = 2$ (wave winding)
- $I_a = 20$ A

**Step 1 — Calculate Back-EMF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 0.02 \times 600 \times 1500}{60 \times 2}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{72000}{120}
\\[6pt]
&= 600 \ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Angular Speed:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Electromagnetic Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 0.02 \times 600 \times 20}{2\pi \times 2}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{960}{4\pi}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{240}{\pi}
\\[6pt]
&\approx 76.39 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification via Power Balance:**
$P_{mech} = E_b \times I_a = 600 \times 20 = 12000$ W
$T_e = P_{mech}/\omega = 12000/157.08 \approx 76.39$ N·m ✓

**Final Answer:** $E_b = 600$ V; $T_e \approx 76.39$ N·m

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Question (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"Explain the construction, working principle, and torque production mechanism of a Switched Reluctance Motor with a suitable diagram. Why is an asymmetric half-bridge converter used to drive it?"*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):**
- **Stator:** Salient-pole laminated steel core with concentrated coils wound on each pole. Common configurations: $6/4$ (6 stator, 4 rotor poles) and $8/6$.
- **Rotor:** Simple salient-pole laminated steel stack — **no windings, no magnets, no cage**.
- **Shaft and Bearings:** Standard rotor mounting.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- When a stator phase is energized, a magnetic circuit is established from the energized stator pole, across the air gap, through the rotor, and back through the adjacent stator pole.
