- The rotor experiences a tangential force due to the **fringing flux** at the leading pole edge, rotating it to **minimize the magnetic reluctance** of the path (i.e., align rotor poles with energized stator poles).
- Sequential energization of phases produces continuous rotation.

**Step 3 — Torque Equation (1 mark):**

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

Torque direction depends only on $dL/d\theta$ (positive or negative slope of the inductance profile), not on current polarity.

**Step 4 — Asymmetric Half-Bridge Justification (2 marks):**
- Because torque depends on $i^2$, **current can flow in only one direction per phase**.
- A full H-bridge would be wasteful; instead, an asymmetric half-bridge uses **2 switches and 2 diodes per phase**.
- Advantages:
  - **Independent phase control** (one phase can be excited while another is being demagnetized).
  - **Fault tolerance** — a short in one switch doesn't propagate to other phases.
  - **Fast demagnetization** through the freewheel diodes, allowing high-speed operation.

---

**Question (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a rotor resistance of $0.025\ \Omega$ and standstill reactance of $0.12\ \Omega$ per phase. The full-load slip is 4%. Calculate: (i) the slip at which maximum torque occurs, (ii) the ratio of maximum torque to full-load torque."*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Phases $m = 3$, Poles $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz
- $R_r' = 0.025\ \Omega$ (referred)
- $X_r' = 0.12\ \Omega$ per phase
- Full-load slip $s_{fl} = 0.04$

**Step 1 — Approximation:**
For a normal induction motor (neglecting stator impedance effects for simplicity, as is standard at this KTU level):

$$
s_{max} \approx \dfrac{R_r'}{X_r'} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.12} \approx 0.2083
$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of Maximum Torque to Full-Load Torque:**

Using the torque equation ratio form:

$$
\dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{s_{max}^{2} + s_{fl}^{2}}{2 \, s_{max} \, s_{fl}}
$$

Wait — this formula is for the **starting torque ratio**. The correct maximum-torque ratio formula is:

$$
\dfrac{T_{max}}{T} = \dfrac{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_r')^{2}}{2 \, (X_{th} + X_r') \cdot (R_{th} + R_r'/s)}
$$

For the standard approximate case (ignoring $R_{th}$), this simplifies to:

$$
\dfrac{T_{max}}{T} = \dfrac{s_{max}^{2} + s^{2}}{2 \, s_{max} \, s}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in Values at Full Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} &= \dfrac{s_{max}^{2} + s_{fl}^{2}}{2 \, s_{max} \, s_{fl}}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(0.2083)^{2} + (0.04)^{2}}{2 \times 0.2083 \times 0.04}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.0434 + 0.0016}{0.01666}
\\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.045}{0.01666}
\\[6pt]
&\approx 2.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Slip at maximum torque: $s_{max} \approx 0.2083$ (or **20.83%**)
- Ratio $T_{max} / T_{fl} \approx 2.70$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Brushless & Special Machines (KTU ESE Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing SRM with Stepper Motor** — Both are doubly salient, but SRMs operate for continuous rotation with **rotor position feedback**, while steppers operate in **open-loop positioning**. Marks lost in construction comparisons.
> 2. **Forgetting $A = 2$ in wave winding** — Students often write $A = P$ in BLDC EMF derivation. Always check winding type in the problem statement.
> 3. **Sign of $dL/d\theta$** — In SRM, motor torque requires $dL/d\theta > 0$. Generating torque requires $dL/d\theta < 0$. Direction is reversed by commutating in the **falling inductance region**.
> 4. **Using degrees instead of radians** — Torque equations ($T = P_{mech}/\omega$) require $\omega$ in **rad/s**, NOT rpm. Many students forget to convert.
> 5. **Wrong formula choice for BLDC vs IM** — BLDC uses $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi Z N}{60A}$ (DC machine form); IM uses the Thevenin equivalent torque equation. Don't mix them up.
> 6. **Slip interpretation in IM** — Slip is a *ratio* (0 to 1). It is **not** a percentage in formulas. Convert to decimal form before substituting into equations.

---

## 🔥 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔹 BLDC Motor
- ✅ Stator carries armature windings; rotor carries **permanent magnets**.
- ✅ Electronic commutation via Hall sensors (or sensorless back-EMF).
- ✅ EMF: $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$
- ✅ Torque: $T_e = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$
- ✅ Speed equation: $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$
- ✅ Used in e-bikes, drones, premium EVs.

### 🔹 Switched Reluctance Motor
- ✅ Doubly salient, **no magnets**, **no rotor conductors**.
- ✅ Torque: $T = \dfrac{1}{2} i^{2} \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$
- ✅ Requires **rotor position feedback** + asymmetric half-bridge converter.
- ✅ Highly fault-tolerant; ideal for high-speed applications.
- ✅ Power electronics cost is higher than BLDC/IM.

### 🔹 Induction Motor
- ✅ Asynchronous, robust, low-cost.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$
- ✅ Slip: $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$
- ✅ Thevenin-based torque: $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^{2}(R_r'/s)}{(R_{th}+R_r'/s)^{2}+(X_{th}+X_r')^{2}}$
- ✅ Slip at max torque: $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^{2}+(X_{th}+X_r')^{2}}}$
- ✅ $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance**; only $s_{max}$ depends on it.

### 🔹 Universal Constants & Conversion Factors
- ✅ $N_s$ (rpm) to $\omega_s$ (rad/s): multiply by $2\pi / 60$.
- ✅ $\pi \approx 3.14159$; $2\pi \approx 6.2832$.
- ✅ 1 hp = 745.7 W; 1 N·m at 1 rad/s = 1 W.

### 🔹 KTU 2024 CO Mapping Reminder
- **CO2:** Understand electric propulsion motors (BLDC, SRM, IM).
- **CO3:** Apply motor selection principles to HEV/EV drivetrain design.
- All questions on this topic typically map to **Bloom Level 3 (Apply)** or **Level 4 (Analyze)** in KTU valuation schemes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motor Cooling, Efficiency, Size and Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motor Cooling, Efficiency, Size and Mass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motor Cooling — Formal Definition

In the context of **Hybrid and Fuel Cell Vehicles (HFCV)**, **Motor Cooling** refers to the engineered thermal management system designed to extract waste heat generated by the electric traction motor during operation, maintaining the temperature of the motor's critical components — particularly the **stator windings, rotor magnets, bearings, and power electronics** — within safe thermal limits defined by the **insulation class** of the machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Motor cooling is the process of dissipating heat generated due to electrical (copper + iron) and mechanical (friction + windage) losses to ensure continuous, reliable operation at rated power and to prevent demagnetization of permanent magnets, which typically limits rotor temperatures to **150 °C to 180 °C** depending on the magnet grade (NdFeB, SmCo).

The dominant cooling architectures in modern EV traction motors are:

1. **Air Cooling** — Natural convection or forced-air circulation over the motor casing.
2. **Liquid Cooling** — Water–glycol jacket around the stator housing (most common in production EVs).
3. **Direct Oil Cooling** — Circulating synthetic oil directly over the **end-windings** of the stator.
4. **Spray Cooling** — Atomized coolant sprayed onto the rotor/stator end regions.
5. **Two-Phase Cooling** — Evaporative dielectric fluids for high-performance motors (Formula E).

---

## 1.2 Motor Efficiency — Formal Definition

**Motor efficiency (η)** is the dimensionless ratio of mechanical output power delivered at the shaft to the electrical input power drawn from the inverter/battery.

$$ \eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{electrical}} = \frac{P_{electrical} - P_{losses}}{P_{electrical}} $$

The total losses are categorised as:

| Loss Category | Symbol | Physical Origin | Typical Share |
|---|---|---|---|
| Copper (Joule) Losses | $P_{cu}$ | $I^2R$ in stator and rotor windings | 40 – 55% |
| Iron (Core) Losses | $P_{fe}$ | Hysteresis + Eddy currents in lamination steel | 25 – 35% |
| Mechanical Losses | $P_{mech}$ | Bearing friction + air-gap windage | 5 – 15% |
| Stray Load Losses | $P_{stray}$ | Harmonics, leakage flux, non-uniform currents | 5 – 15% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** A frequently tested 3-mark question asks to *"List the losses in a traction motor and explain which loss is dominant at low speed vs. high speed."* The expected answer is: **copper losses dominate at low speed (high torque, high current)** and **iron losses dominate at high speed (high frequency, high flux ripple)**.

---

## 1.3 Size and Mass — Formal Definition

In automotive electrification, **size** and **mass** of the traction motor are quantified using three engineering metrics:

- **Specific Power (kW/kg)** — Mechanical output power per unit mass.
- **Power Density (kW/L)** — Mechanical output power per unit envelope volume.
- **Torque Density (Nm/L or Nm/kg)** — Output torque per unit volume or mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark (2024 EVs):** Production EV traction motors achieve **4 – 6 kW/kg** specific power and **10 – 20 kW/L** power density. Formula E motors exceed **10 kW/kg**. The Tesla Model 3 rear motor achieves approximately **5.6 kW/kg** specific power.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Overworked Athlete"**
> Imagine a sprinter running at full speed. The harder and longer the athlete sprints, the more body heat they generate. Without sweating, drinking water, or resting in a cool room, the athlete's core temperature rises, performance drops, and eventually heatstroke occurs. The athlete's body has an **insulation limit (≈ 40 °C core temp)** — beyond this, organs fail.
>
> An electric motor behaves identically. Electrical current is its "muscle activity" — it generates heat. The **insulation class** (A, B, F, H) is the "body temperature limit". The **cooling system** is its "sweat and circulatory system". A motor without adequate cooling is an athlete running in a sauna — it will **demagnetize, burn its windings, or seize its bearings**. Therefore, cooling, efficiency, and minimising size/mass are not independent design choices — they are **three faces of the same engineering trade-off**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Generation Sources in a Traction Motor

A traction motor dissipates heat from **four distinct physical mechanisms**. Each mechanism dominates in a different operating region of the drive cycle.

### Step 1 — Copper Losses (Joule Heating)
Generated wherever current flows through a conductor of finite resistance. In a **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), the stator carries the full armature current.

$$ P_{cu} = 3 \cdot I_{ph}^2 \cdot R_{ph} $$

where $I_{ph}$ is the RMS phase current and $R_{ph}$ is the per-phase resistance (a function of temperature $T_{cu}$).

> **Key Insight:** Since $R_{ph} \propto T_{cu}$, copper losses are **self-reinforcing** — as the winding heats up, resistance increases, which increases $I^2R$ loss, which further raises temperature. This is a **positive feedback loop** and is why the cooling system must interrupt it.

### Step 2 — Iron (Core) Losses
Generated in the laminated steel stator core. Composed of two sub-mechanisms:

$$ P_{fe} = P_{hyst} + P_{eddy} $$

$$ P_{hyst} = k_h \cdot f \cdot B_{max}^{1.6} \text{ to } B_{max}^{2.0} $$

$$ P_{eddy} = k_e \cdot f^2 \cdot B_{max}^{2} \cdot t^2 $$

where $f$ is the electrical frequency, $B_{max}$ is the peak flux density, and $t$ is the lamination thickness.

> **Key Insight:** Iron losses scale with the **square of frequency**, so they explode at high speed. A motor running at 15,000 rpm generates roughly **4× the iron loss** of one running at 7,500 rpm. This is why **high-speed motors require thinner laminations (0.20 mm vs. 0.35 mm)** and low-loss silicon-steel grades.

### Step 3 — Mechanical Losses
Composed of:
- **Bearing friction** — proportional to speed and bearing preload.
- **Windage (air-gap drag)** — proportional to **speed cubed** for axial-flow rotors and **speed squared** for radial-flow.

$$ P_{windage} \propto \rho_{air} \cdot \omega^3 \cdot r^5 $$

> **Key Insight:** Windage becomes the dominant mechanical loss above ~10,000 rpm and is why Formula E and hypercar motors use **hermetic sealed housings with oil-flooded rotor cavities** to reduce air density at the rotor surface.

### Step 4 — Stray Load Losses
Caused by **leakage flux harmonics, magnetomotive force (MMF) space harmonics, and circulating currents** in parallel conductors.

$$ P_{stray} \approx 0.01 \cdot P_{rated} \text{ to } 0.03 \cdot P_{rated} $$

---

## 2.2 Cooling System Architecture Analysis

### 2.2.1 Air Cooling

**Mechanism:** Ambient or ducted air is forced across the motor's outer fins by a fan or vehicle ram-air.

**Heat Transfer Equation:**

$$ Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{surface} - T_{ambient}) $$

where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $A$ is the surface area, and the bracketed term is the temperature difference.

**Limitations:**
- Low heat transfer coefficient ($h \approx 25$ – $250$ W/m²K for natural and forced convection).
- Cannot cool > **15 kW** of continuous loss from a compact motor.
- Used in: **Toyota Prius Gen 1/2**, some e-bike motors, small hybrid motors.

### 2.2.2 Liquid Cooling (Water-Glycol Jacket)

**Mechanism:** A **50:50 water-ethylene-glycol** mixture circulates through channels in the stator housing.

**Heat Transfer Equation (Newton's Law of Cooling for the coolant):**

$$ Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) $$

where $\dot{m}$ is the coolant mass flow rate (kg/s), $c_p$ is the specific heat capacity (≈ 3.6 kJ/kg·K for 50:50 glycol), and $(T_{out} - T_{in})$ is the coolant temperature rise.

**Advantages:**
- $h \approx 500$ – $10,000$ W/m²K — orders of magnitude higher than air.
- Can extract **30 – 50 kW** of continuous loss.
- Compact and integrated into the vehicle's main coolant loop.

**Used in:** Tesla Model 3/Y, BMW i3, Nissan Leaf, Hyundai Ioniq 5.

### 2.2.3 Direct Oil Cooling / Spray Cooling

**Mechanism:** Synthetic dielectric oil (e.g., **3M Novec**, **Shell S5** series) is sprayed directly onto the **end-windings** of the stator. Because oil has a much higher heat capacity than air and direct contact with the windings, the thermal resistance from the copper to the coolant is reduced by **5× to 10×** compared to jacket cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Direct oil cooling is increasingly used in **800V architecture vehicles** (Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron GT) because higher voltages allow thinner wire insulation, which worsens heat dissipation — oil contact compensates.

---

## 2.3 Motor Efficiency Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Efficiency Equation

$$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}} $$

Total losses:

$$ P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray} $$

### 2.3.2 Efficiency Map (Torque-Speed Map)

In an EV, the motor operates across a wide **torque-speed envelope** (e.g., 0 – 18,000 rpm, 0 – 500 Nm). Efficiency is not constant — it is a function of operating point.

$$ \eta = f(T, \omega) $$

The **efficiency map** plots iso-efficiency contours over the torque-speed plane. Modern EV motors achieve **> 95% efficiency** over > 85% of the WLTP drive cycle envelope.

> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "sweet spot" of peak efficiency sits at **moderate torque and high speed** — roughly 70 – 80% of base speed, where copper and iron losses are balanced.

### 2.3.3 Why Peak Efficiency Is Limited

The theoretical limit comes from **second-law thermodynamics**: any irreversible loss (electrical resistance, magnetic hysteresis, friction) reduces efficiency. Even the best traction motors today are bound by:
- Conductor resistivity of copper (1.68 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20 °C).
- Lamination steel loss coefficient (M19, M15P grades).
- Bearing coefficient of friction (≈ 0.001 – 0.005).

---

## 2.4 Size and Mass Reduction Strategies

### 2.4.1 The Power-Weight Fundamental Relationship

The mechanical output power of a rotating machine is:

$$ P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{n}{60} $$

For a **given power output**, a motor that spins **faster** can deliver it with a **smaller torque**, hence a **smaller physical size** (because torque scales with rotor volume).

$$ V_{rotor} \propto \frac{T}{\sigma_{shear}} \propto \frac{P / \omega}{\sigma_{shear}} $$

> **Key Insight:** Doubling the operating speed allows the motor volume to be roughly **halved** for the same power. This is why the **Porsche Taycan** rear motor runs up to **16,000 rpm** while delivering 335 kW from a 60 kg unit (5.6 kW/kg).

### 2.4.2 The Specific Power Equation

$$ \text{Specific Power} = \frac{P_{out}}{m_{motor}} \quad [kW/kg] $$

### 2.4.3 The Shear Stress Limit (Why Motors Cannot Be Arbitrarily Small)

The maximum electromagnetic shear stress on the rotor surface is limited by:

$$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot B_{gap} \cdot H_{slot} \cdot \cos(\psi) $$

where $B_{gap}$ is the air-gap flux density (limited to ≈ 1 T by saturation), and $H_{slot}$ is the slot linear current density (limited to ≈ 200 – 400 kA/m by cooling).

### 2.4.4 Cooling-Coupled Size Reduction

A better cooling system **raises the allowable current density $J$**, which in turn raises $H_{slot}$, which raises $\sigma_{max}$, which permits a **smaller motor** for the same torque. This is the **fundamental thermal-electromagnetic coupling**:

$$ \text{Size} \downarrow \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Cooling Capacity} \uparrow \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Continuous Power Rating} \uparrow $$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Output power | W |
| 3 | $\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 4 | $P_{cu} = 3 I_{ph}^{2} R_{ph}$ | Copper (Joule) loss | W |
| 5 | $P_{hyst} = k_h f B_{max}^{\alpha}$ | Hysteresis loss | W |
| 6 | $P_{eddy} = k_e f^{2} B_{max}^{2} t^{2}$ | Eddy-current loss | W |
| 7 | $P_{wind} \propto \rho_{air} \omega^{3} r^{5}$ | Windage loss | W |
| 8 | $Q = h A \Delta T$ | Convective heat flux | W |
| 9 | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T_{c}$ | Coolant heat extraction | W |
| 10 | $P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$ | Total loss | W |
| 11 | $\text{SP} = P_{out} / m$ | Specific Power | kW/kg |
| 12 | $\text{PD} = P_{out} / V$ | Power Density | kW/L |
| 13 | $\sigma_{max} = \frac{1}{2} B_{gap} H_{slot} \cos \psi$ | Air-gap shear stress | N/m² |
| 14 | $T = \frac{\pi}{2} D^{2} L \sigma_{max}$ | Torque from shear stress | Nm |
| 15 | $R_{\theta, cu-a} = \frac{T_{cu} - T_{amb}}{P_{loss}}$ | Thermal resistance (winding-to-ambient) | K/W |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Thermal Resistance Network

A traction motor's cooling problem can be modeled as an **analogous electrical network** (Foster network), where:
- **Voltage** represents **temperature (T)** in °C.
- **Current** represents **heat flow (Q)** in W.
- **Resistance** represents **thermal resistance ($R_\theta$)** in K/W.

### Step 1: Identify the Heat Sources

The dominant heat sources are concentrated in:
1. The **stator winding** (copper loss $P_{cu}$).
2. The **stator core** (iron loss $P_{fe}$).
3. The **rotor** (iron + magnet eddy loss).

For a **water-jacket-cooled PMSM**, the heat-flow path is:

$$
\text{Copper} \rightarrow R_{\theta,cu-fe} \rightarrow \text{Iron Core} \rightarrow R_{\theta,fe-housing} \rightarrow \text{Housing} \rightarrow R_{\theta,conv} \rightarrow \text{Coolant}
$$

### Step 2: Write the Thermal Ohm's Law for Each Layer

The thermal resistance for conduction through the slot insulation (copper-to-iron):

$$ R_{\theta, cu-fe} = \frac{\delta_{ins}}{k_{ins} \cdot A_{slot}} $$

where $\delta_{ins}$ is the insulation thickness (m), $k_{ins}$ is the thermal conductivity of insulation (W/m·K, typically 0.2 – 0.4), and $A_{slot}$ is the slot surface area.

The convective resistance from housing to coolant:

$$ R_{\theta, conv} = \frac{1}{h_{jacket} \cdot A_{jacket}} $$

### Step 3: Combine Resistances in Series

For one-dimensional heat flow from winding to coolant:

$$ R_{\theta,total} = R_{\theta,cu-fe} + R_{\theta,fe-housing} + R_{\theta,conv} $$

### Step 4: Apply the Steady-State Heat-Flow Equation

The steady-state temperature rise of the winding above the coolant is:

$$ \Delta T = T_{cu} - T_{coolant} = P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta,total} $$

### Step 5: Solve for Maximum Allowable Loss

Rearranging for the maximum allowable loss to keep $T_{cu}$ below a temperature limit:

$$ P_{loss,max} = \frac{T_{cu,max} - T_{coolant,in}}{R_{\theta,total}} $$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** A 100 kW PMSM traction motor with the following design:
> - $P_{cu} = 6$ kW, $P_{fe} = 2.5$ kW, $P_{mech} = 0.5$ kW, $P_{stray} = 0.5$ kW.
> - $R_{\theta,cu-fe} = 0.02$ K/W, $R_{\theta,fe-housing} = 0.015$ K/W, $R_{\theta,conv} = 0.005$ K/W.
> - Coolant inlet temperature $T_{coolant} = 65$ °C.
> - Insulation class H — maximum winding temperature $T_{cu,max} = 180$ °C.
>
> **Find:** Whether the motor can operate continuously at rated power.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Total Loss:**

$$ P_{loss} = 6 + 2.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 9.5 \text{ kW} $$

**Step B — Total Thermal Resistance:**

$$ R_{\theta,total} = 0.02 + 0.015 + 0.005 = 0.040 \text{ K/W} $$

**Step C — Winding Temperature Rise Above Coolant:**

$$ \Delta T = 9500 \cdot 0.040 = 380 \text{ °C} $$

**Step D — Absolute Winding Temperature:**

$$ T_{cu} = T_{coolant} + \Delta T = 65 + 380 = 445 \text{ °C} $$

**Step E — Comparison with Limit:**

Since $T_{cu} = 445$ °C ≫ $T_{cu,max} = 180$ °C, the motor **cannot** operate continuously at rated power with this design. To enable continuous operation, the design team must either:

- Reduce $P_{loss}$ (use lower-loss conductors, e.g., rectangular Litz wire).
- Reduce $R_{\theta,conv}$ (increase coolant flow rate, increase jacket area).
- Reduce $R_{\theta,cu-fe}$ (use higher-thermal-conductivity slot liners, e.g., resin-impregnated mica with $k \approx 0.6$ W/m·K).
- Use **direct oil cooling** to drop $R_{\theta,cu-coolant}$ by a factor of 5 – 10.

> **Engineering Conclusion:** This is why modern high-power-density EV motors **must** use direct oil or spray cooling — jacket cooling alone cannot extract the heat from a continuous-duty motor exceeding 200 kW/L power density.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Power-Speed-Size Trade-off Equation

### Step 1: Start from Electromagnetic Power

For a cylindrical rotor motor, the air-gap power is:

$$ P_{gap} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot k_w \cdot B_{gap} \cdot A_{slot} \cdot D^{2} L \cdot n \cdot \frac{1}{60} \cdot 2\pi $$

Simplifying:

$$ P_{gap} = \frac{\pi^{2}}{60} \cdot k_w \cdot B_{gap} \cdot A_{slot} \cdot D^{2} L \cdot n $$

where $D$ is the rotor diameter (m), $L$ is the stack length (m), $n$ is the speed (rpm), $k_w$ is the winding factor, and $A_{slot}$ is the specific electric loading (A/m).

### Step 2: Express Torque

Setting $n = 1$ in the above and using $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$:

$$ T = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot k_w \cdot B_{gap} \cdot A_{slot} \cdot D^{2} L $$

> **Critical Insight:** Torque is proportional to **$D^{2} L$** (the rotor swept volume). Doubling the diameter quadruples the torque per unit length.

### Step 3: Express Power

$$ P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi n}{60} $$

### Step 4: Express Specific Power

$$ \text{SP} = \frac{P}{m} = \frac{P}{\rho_{avg} \cdot V_{motor}} $$

where $\rho_{avg}$ is the average mass density of the motor (kg/m³, typically 4,000 – 6,000 kg/m³ for a PMSM — heavy because of the steel laminations and copper windings).

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** A PMSM with $D = 0.20$ m, $L = 0.15$ m, $\rho_{avg} = 5,500$ kg/m³, $B_{gap} = 0.9$ T, $A_{slot} = 250,000$ A/m, $k_w = 0.95$, $n_{max} = 15,000$ rpm.
>
> **Find:** Peak torque, peak power, mass, and specific power.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Active Volume:**

$$ V_{active} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^{2} (0.15) = 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} = 4.712 \text{ L} $$

**Step B — Peak Torque:**

$$ T = \frac{\pi}{2} (0.95)(0.9)(2.5 \times 10^{5})(0.20)^{2}(0.15) $$

$$ T = 1.5708 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{5} \cdot 0.005 $$

$$ T \approx 1{,}679 \text{ Nm} $$

**Step C — Peak Angular Velocity:**

$$ \omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot 15{,}000}{60} = 1{,}570.8 \text{ rad/s} $$

**Step D — Peak Power:**

$$ P = T \cdot \omega = 1679 \cdot 1570.8 \approx 2.638 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} = 2{,}638 \text{ kW} $$

(Impractical — limited by other constraints; this is the theoretical maximum.)

**Step E — Mass of Active Material:**

$$ m = \rho_{avg} \cdot V_{active} = 5500 \cdot 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \approx 25.9 \text{ kg} $$

**Step F — Theoretical Specific Power:**

$$ \text{SP} = \frac{2{,}638{,}000}{25.9} \approx 101.9 \text{ kW/kg} $$

> **Reality Check:** This is the *theoretical* limit assuming no thermal, mechanical, or magnetic constraints. **Real production EV motors achieve 1/15th to 1/20th of this value** because of end-windings, frame mass, shaft, bearings, terminal boxes, and thermal derating.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency from Loss Components

**Given:** A motor operating at $T = 250$ Nm, $n = 6,000$ rpm, with the following measured losses:
- $P_{cu} = 4.2$ kW
- $P_{fe} = 1.6$ kW
- $P_{mech} = 0.3$ kW
- $P_{stray} = 0.4$ kW

**Find:** The efficiency at this operating point.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Output Power:**

$$ P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi n}{60} $$

$$ P_{out} = 250 \cdot \frac{2\pi \cdot 6000}{60} = 250 \cdot 628.32 $$

$$ P_{out} = 157{,}080 \text{ W} \approx 157.1 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 2 — Total Loss:**

$$ P_{loss} = 4.2 + 1.6 + 0.3 + 0.4 = 6.5 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 3 — Input Power:**

$$ P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 157.1 + 6.5 = 163.6 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**

$$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{157.1}{163.6} = 0.9603 = 96.03\% $$

> **KTU Insight:** This is an excellent efficiency for an EV traction motor. Production EV motors are typically rated at the operating point where η ≥ 95%, and the **WLTP drive cycle average efficiency** is usually 92 – 94% when considering all real-world operating points.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Motor Thermal Management System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Electric Traction Motor]
    A2[Inverter and Power Electronics]
    A3[Onboard Charger and DC DC]

    B1[Stator Winding Heat Source P_cu]
    B2[Stator Core Heat Source P_fe]
    B3[Rotor and Magnet Heat Source P_rotor]
    B4[Bearing and Windage Heat Source P_mech]

    C1[Cooling Jacket or Housing]
    C2[Direct Oil Spray on End Windings]
    C3[Rotor Oil Flood or Spray]

    D1[Water Glycol Coolant Loop]
    D2[Synthetic Dielectric Oil Loop]
    D3[Heat Exchanger Radiator]
    D4[Vehicle Thermal Management Controller]

    E1[Ambient Air Rejection]
    E2[Chiller Loop for Cabin AC Coupling]

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    A2 --> A1
    A3 --> A2
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D1
    D3 --> E1
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D4 --> E2
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Motor Efficiency Map Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Torque Demand from Driver Pedal]
    A2[Vehicle Speed from CAN Bus]
    A3[Battery SOC and Voltage]

    B1[Motor Torque Speed Operating Point]
    B2[Look up Table for Losses]
    B3[Real Time Loss Calculation]
    B4[Iron Loss Model P_fe f n]
    B5[Copper Loss Model P_cu I2 R]
    B6[Mechanical Loss Model P_mech omega]

    C1[Total Loss P_loss = P_cu + P_fe + P_mech + P_stray]
    C2[Efficiency eta = P_out divided by P_out plus P_loss]
    C3[Efficiency Map Iso Contours in Torque Speed Plane]

    D1[Energy Management Strategy EMS]
    D2[Optimal Operating Point Selection]
    D3[Inverter Switching Command]

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B3 --> B5
    B3 --> B6
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Motor Size and Mass Reduction Levers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Target Power and Torque Specification]
    A2[Vehicle Package Volume Constraint]
    A3[Vehicle Mass Budget]

    B1[Lever 1 Increase Speed n for given Power]
    B2[Lever 2 Increase Air gap Flux Density B_gap]
    B3[Lever 3 Increase Specific Loading A_slot via better Cooling]
    B4[Lever 4 Use Higher Energy Density Magnets NdFeB N52]
    B5[Lever 5 Use Hairpin or Wave Windings higher slot fill]

    C1[Reduced Rotor Diameter D]
    C2[Reduced Stack Length L]
    C3[Reduced Active Volume V = pi D2 L over 4]

    D1[Improved Specific Power kW per kg]
    D2[Improved Power Density kW per L]
    D3[Improved Torque Density Nm per L]

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    A1 --> B5
    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A2 --> C3
    A3 --> C1
    A3 --> C2
    A3 --> C3
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

## 4.4 Heat-Flow Path Diagram — From Winding to Ambient

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stator Copper at T_cu 180 C]
    S2[Slot Insulation Mica Resin k = 0.3 W per mK]
    S3[Stator Lamination Stack k = 25 W per mK]
    S4[Housing Aluminum or Steel k = 150 to 50 W per mK]
    S5[Coolant Jacket 50 Glycol 50 Water]
    S6[Radiator and Fan]
    S7[Ambient at T_amb 25 to 40 C]

    S1 -->|R1| S2
    S2 -->|R2| S3
    S3 -->|R3| S4
    S4 -->|R4| S5
    S5 -->|R5| S6
    S6 -->|R6| S7
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block $S_i$ is a thermal layer, and each labeled link ($R_1$ to $R_6$) is a thermal resistance. The total temperature drop from copper to ambient is the sum of $P_{loss} \times R_i$ across all six resistances. To minimise $T_{cu}$, the designer can target the **largest resistance** in the chain (typically $R_1$ — the slot insulation).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q1. List the four major loss components in a traction motor used in a hybrid/electric vehicle. State which component dominates during (a) high-torque low-speed operation (e.g., launch) and (b) high-speed cruising.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major loss components in a traction motor are:

1. **Copper (Joule) losses** in the stator windings — $P_{cu} = 3 I_{ph}^{2} R_{ph}$.
2. **Iron (core) losses** in the stator laminations — hysteresis + eddy current.
3. **Mechanical losses** — bearing friction + air-gap windage.
4. **Stray load losses** — harmonics, leakage flux, circulating currents.

**Dominance:**

- **(a) High-torque, low-speed (launch):** **Copper losses dominate.** The motor draws maximum current to produce peak torque, and at low speed, iron and windage losses are negligible.
- **(b) High-speed cruising:** **Iron and windage losses dominate.** Frequency and speed are high, but current is low because little torque is needed to maintain cruise.

*(3 marks — 1 for list, 1 for part a, 1 for part b)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2. Define "specific power" of a traction motor. State the typical range of specific power achieved by current production EV motors.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Specific power is the ratio of the mechanical output power delivered at the shaft to the total mass of the motor (including housing, shaft, bearings, and active materials).

$$ \text{Specific Power} = \frac{P_{out}}{m_{motor}} \quad [kW/kg] $$

**Typical Range:** Modern production EV traction motors achieve **4 – 6 kW/kg** (Tesla Model 3 ≈ 5.6 kW/kg, BMW i3 ≈ 4.5 kW/kg, Lucid Air ≈ 7 kW/kg). Formula E and high-performance motors exceed **10 kW/kg**.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for typical range with examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q A.** (a) Explain with a labelled diagram the **liquid-cooling system architecture** of a modern EV traction motor. Discuss why water–glycol is preferred over pure water as the coolant. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A **100 kW PMSM** traction motor has the following measured losses at rated load: $P_{cu}$ = 5 kW, $P_{fe}$ = 2 kW, $P_{mech}$ = 0.6 kW, $P_{stray}$ = 0.4 kW. Calculate the **efficiency** at rated load. If the motor is upgraded to use **lower-loss silicon steel** (reducing $P_{fe}$ by 50%) and **silver-alloy conductors** (reducing $P_{cu}$ by 30%), what is the **new efficiency** and the **percentage reduction in total loss**? (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Liquid Cooling System Architecture (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------------------+
|     EV TRACTION MOTOR (PMSM)               |
|                                            |
|   +--------+         +--------+            |
|   | Stator | <-----  | End    |            |
|   | Core   |         | Wind-  |            |
|   +--------+         | ings   |            |
|       |              +--------+            |
|       | (heat by conduction)               |
|   +--------+                                |
|   | Jacket |  <-- 50:50 Water-Glycol       |
|   +--------+      Coolant Inlet (65 C)     |
|       |                                    |
+-------+--------------------------------------+
        | Coolant Outlet (~75 C)
        v
   [Pump] -> [Chiller] -> [Radiator] -> [Pump]
        |                              ^
        v                              |
   [Vehicle Thermal Management ECU]----+
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. The stator housing is surrounded by a **cooling jacket** (typically cast aluminum or steel channels) through which a **50:50 mixture of water and ethylene glycol** is circulated by an electric coolant pump.
2. The coolant absorbs heat from the stator core and end-windings by **forced convection**, raising its temperature typically from **65 °C at inlet to 75 – 80 °C at outlet**.
3. The heated coolant flows to a **chiller** (coupled to the air-conditioning refrigerant loop) and then to a **radiator** (front of vehicle) where heat is rejected to the ambient.
4. The **Vehicle Thermal Management Controller (VTMC)** modulates the pump speed, fan speed, and chiller valve to maintain the inlet temperature at the optimum set point.

**Why Water–Glycol over Pure Water (1 mark):**

- **Freeze protection:** Pure water freezes at 0 °C and would damage the cooling channels in cold climates. Glycol depresses the freezing point to ≈ −35 °C.
- **Boiling-point elevation:** Glycol raises the boiling point to ≈ 110 °C (vs. 100 °C for pure water), allowing higher coolant temperatures and better heat rejection.
- **Corrosion inhibition:** Commercial glycol mixtures contain **corrosion inhibitors** that protect aluminum and copper components.
- **Higher viscosity:** Slightly more viscous, but this is a small penalty compared to the above benefits.

---

#### Part (b) — Efficiency Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Output Power at Rated Load (1 mark):**

$$ P_{out} = 100 \text{ kW (given)} $$

**Step 2 — Total Loss at Rated Load (1 mark):**

$$ P_{loss,old} = P_{cu} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray} $$

$$ P_{loss,old} = 5.0 + 2.0 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 8.0 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 3 — Old Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$ \eta_{old} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss,old}} = \frac{100}{100 + 8.0} $$

$$ \eta_{old} = \frac{100}{108.0} = 0.9259 = 92.59\% $$

**Step 4 — New Loss Components (1 mark):**

- New $P_{cu}$ = $5.0 \times (1 - 0.30) = 3.5$ kW
- New $P_{fe}$ = $2.0 \times (1 - 0.50) = 1.0$ kW
- $P_{mech}$ and $P_{stray}$ unchanged = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0 kW

**Step 5 — New Total Loss (1 mark):**

$$ P_{loss,new} = 3.5 + 1.0 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 5.5 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 6 — New Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$ \eta_{new} = \frac{100}{100 + 5.5} = \frac{100}{105.5} = 0.9479 = 94.79\% $$

**Step 7 — Percentage Reduction in Total Loss (1 mark):**

$$ \Delta P_{loss}\% = \frac{P_{loss,old} - P_{loss,new}}{P_{loss,old}} \times 100 $$

$$ \Delta P_{loss}\% = \frac{8.0 - 5.5}{8.0} \times 100 = \frac{2.5}{8.0} \times 100 = 31.25\% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget to add $P_{mech}$ and $P_{stray}$ unchanged** and report $P_{loss,new} = 4.5$ kW. This leads to a 1-mark deduction.
> - A common **sign error** occurs in the efficiency formula — the denominator is $P_{out} + P_{loss}$, **not** $P_{out} - P_{loss}$.
> - The percentage reduction must be computed on **losses**, not on efficiency. Reporting "efficiency improved by 2.2%" instead of "losses reduced by 31.25%" loses 1 mark.
> - Always **state units** (kW, °C) at every step — KTU examiners award 0.5 marks for unit consistency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q B.** (a) With the aid of a sketch, explain the **direct oil-cooling technique** used in modern 800 V EV traction motors. Compare its performance with a conventional **water-jacket cooling** system. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **air-gap electromagnetic shear stress** and hence show that the **torque capacity** of a motor is proportional to its **rotor volume** $D^2 L$. Given a target torque of **350 Nm** and a maximum allowable shear stress of **35 kPa**, find the **minimum rotor diameter** for a stack length of **180 mm**. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Direct Oil Cooling (7 marks)

**Sketch (2 marks):**

```
        +--------------------------+
        |  ROTOR  (with magnets)   |
        |                          |
        |  +-------------------+   |
        |  |  END-WINDINGS     |   |
        |  |  (sprayed with    |   |
        |  |   dielectric oil) |   |
        |  +-------------------+   |
        |           ||             |
        |           ||  Oil spray  |
        |           ||  jets       |
        |           \/             |
        |  +-------------------+   |
        |  |  Stator Core      |   |
        |  +-------------------+   |
        |  |  Housing          |   |
        |  +-------------------+   |
        +--------------------------+
                  |  |
              Return oil
              to heat
              exchanger
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

1. In **direct oil cooling**, a synthetic dielectric oil (e.g., 3M Novec, Shell S5) is sprayed directly onto the **end-windings** of the stator through calibrated nozzles. Some designs also flood the rotor cavity.
2. The oil is **electrically non-conductive**, so it can safely contact the live windings at **800 V** without risk of short-circuit.
3. The oil absorbs heat directly from the **hottest part of the motor** (the end-windings, where conventional jacket cooling is least effective) and is then pumped to a heat exchanger, where it is cooled and recirculated.
4. The thermal resistance from the copper conductor to the coolant is reduced by a factor of **5× to 10×** compared to jacket cooling.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Water-Jacket Cooling | Direct Oil Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer coefficient $h$ | 500 – 5,000 W/m²K | 1,000 – 10,000 W/m²K |
| Direct contact with windings | No | Yes |
| Thermal resistance $R_{\theta, cu-coolant}$ | High | 5× – 10× lower |
| Continuous power density capability | 5 – 8 kW/L | 12 – 20 kW/L |
| Cost and complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Used in | Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model 3 | Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron GT |

---

#### Part (b) — Air-Gap Shear Stress and Torque Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the Air-Gap Shear Stress (1 mark):**

The air-gap electromagnetic shear stress is the tangential force per unit rotor surface area, generated by the interaction of the magnetic field $B_{gap}$ and the stator current loading $A_{slot}$.

$$ \sigma_{tan} = B_{gap} \cdot H_{slot} \cdot \cos(\psi) $$

where $\psi$ is the angle between the field and current phasors (ideally 0° in a PMSM with $i_d = 0$ control, giving $\cos\psi = 1$).

$$ \sigma_{tan} = \frac{1}{2} B_{gap} \cdot A_{slot} $$

The factor of ½ comes from the sinusoidal field distribution. The maximum shearing force on the rotor surface is:

$$ F = \sigma_{tan} \cdot \pi D L $$

**Step 2 — Convert Force to Torque (1 mark):**

The torque is the force multiplied by the lever arm (radius $D/2$):

$$ T = F \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \sigma_{tan} \cdot \pi D L \cdot \frac{D}{2} $$

$$ T = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \sigma_{tan} \cdot D^{2} L $$

This is the **fundamental torque equation** for a rotating electrical machine.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $D^2 L$ is the rotor swept volume, **torque is directly proportional to rotor volume.** This is the most important equation in motor design — it explains why bigger motors produce more torque, and why higher-speed motors can be smaller for the same power.

**Step 3 — Numerical Substitution (2 marks):**

Given: $T = 350$ Nm, $\sigma_{tan,max} = 35$ kPa $= 35{,}000$ N/m², $L = 0.180$ m.

**Step 4 — Solve for $D$ (1 mark):**

$$ D^{2} = \frac{2T}{\pi \sigma_{tan} L} $$

$$ D^{2} = \frac{2 \cdot 350}{\pi \cdot 35{,}000 \cdot 0.180} $$

$$ D^{2} = \frac{700}{\pi \cdot 6300} = \frac{700}{19{,}792} $$

$$ D^{2} = 0.03537 \text{ m}^{2} $$

**Step 5 — Final Result (1 mark):**

$$ D = \sqrt{0.03537} = 0.1881 \text{ m} \approx 188 \text{ mm} $$

> **Engineering Check:** This is consistent with a 150 – 200 kW traction motor, which typically has a rotor diameter of 180 – 220 mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget the factor of ½** in $\sigma = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot A$ — this is a 1-mark penalty.
> - Confusing **diameter with radius** when computing torque from force is a common error; torque is force × radius, not force × diameter.
> - **Unit conversions** of shear stress (kPa → N/m²) must be explicit. 1 kPa = 1000 N/m².
> - Always state the **physical meaning** of the result — "the rotor must be at least 188 mm in diameter to deliver 350 Nm at the maximum allowable shear stress."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Motor Cooling, Efficiency, Size and Mass**

### A. Cooling

- ✅ Four heat sources: **Copper, Iron, Mechanical, Stray** — recall their speed/torque dependence.
- ✅ **Copper losses dominate at low speed (high torque)**, iron at high speed (high frequency).
- ✅ **Water–glycol 50:50** is the automotive standard — freeze protection, boiling-point elevation, corrosion inhibition.
- ✅ **Direct oil cooling** offers 5× – 10× lower thermal resistance than jacket cooling and is essential for **> 200 kW/L** power density.
- ✅ **Insulation classes** A (105 °C), B (130 °C), F (155 °C), H (180 °C) define maximum winding temperature.
- ✅ **Thermal resistance network** — slot insulation is typically the **largest resistance** in the heat-flow path; improving it (high-thermal-conductivity resin) is the most direct way to enable higher continuous power.

### B. Efficiency

- ✅ **Efficiency η = P_out / (P_out + P_loss)** — denominator is **sum**, not difference.
- ✅ Modern EV traction motors achieve **> 95% peak** and **92 – 94% WLTP-cycle average**.
- ✅ Losses scale as: **$I^2$** (copper), **$f$ to $f^2$** (iron), **$\omega^3 r^5$** (windage).
- ✅ The **efficiency map** is a plot of iso-efficiency contours on the torque-speed plane; peak efficiency lies at **moderate torque, high speed**.
- ✅ **Stray losses** are typically 1 – 3% of rated power and are the hardest to model analytically.

### C. Size and Mass

- ✅ **Specific power (kW/kg)** is the headline metric for EV motor mass benchmarking.
- ✅ **Power density (kW/L)** governs the packaging constraint within the vehicle chassis.
- ✅ **Torque ∝ D²L** — torque scales with rotor swept volume.
- ✅ **Power ∝ T × n** — for the same power, doubling speed halves required torque (and roughly halves volume).
- ✅ **Air-gap shear stress $\sigma = \frac{1}{2} B_{gap} A_{slot}$** is the fundamental design limit — typically **20 – 50 kPa** for production EV motors.
- ✅ Production EV motors: **4 – 6 kW/kg**; Formula E: **> 10 kW/kg**.
- ✅ Cooling, efficiency, and size/mass are **mutually coupled** — improving one enables improvements in the others.

### D. Common KTU Board-Exam Keywords to Memorise

- "**Thermal runaway**" — self-reinforcing $R_{cu}(T)$ feedback — must be prevented by cooling.
- "**Demagnetization**" — irreversible loss of magnetisation in NdFeB above ≈ 150 – 180 °C.
- "**Slot fill factor**" — ratio of copper cross-section to slot cross-section; higher fill → lower $R_{ph}$ → lower $P_{cu}$.
- "**Hairpin winding**" — rectangular-wire, high-slot-fill winding used in Tesla and Lucid motors.
- "**WLTP**" — Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure — standard drive cycle for efficiency reporting.
- "**ISG**" — Integrated Starter Generator — a mild-hybrid motor/generator (typically 10 – 15 kW).
- "**800 V architecture**" — modern EV high-voltage bus enabling thinner cables and higher power density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Loss Components — "CIMS":**
> **C**opper, **I**ron, **M**echanical, **S**tray — and remember: **C**opper for **C**lutch (low speed, high torque), **I**ron for **I**nterstate (high speed).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Management of HEV Power Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Management of HEV Power Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Management of HEV Power Electronics** refers to the engineered system of heat extraction, transport, and rejection designed to maintain the operating temperature of semiconductor power devices (IGBTs, MOSFETs, SiC/GaN devices, and DC-link capacitors) within their safe operating area (SOA) under the demanding, transient electrical loads encountered in hybrid and electric vehicle powertrains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** The design, analysis, and control of cooling subsystems that limit the junction temperature ($T_j$) of power-electronic switches below their maximum permissible limit (typically **150 °C** for silicon IGBTs and **175–200 °C** for SiC MOSFETs) to ensure reliability, efficiency, and durability of the inverter, DC-DC converter, and onboard charger.

The three primary thermal design constraints are:
1. **Maximum Junction Temperature ($T_{j,max}$)** — the absolute thermal ceiling of the semiconductor die.
2. **Thermal Cycling Fatigue** — repeated $\Delta T_j$ cycles crack solder joints and bond wires.
3. **Power Density (W/cm²)** — modern EV inverters push **300–500 W/cm²** at the die level.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "CPU Cooler" of an Electric Car

Imagine a high-performance gaming laptop running a heavy video game. The processor (CPU) generates enormous heat in a tiny silicon die. If you do not attach a heatsink and a fan, the processor will throttle, slow down, and eventually burn out.

Now, replace the **CPU with an IGBT module** in an EV inverter, and the **fan + heatsink with a liquid-cooled cold plate and an EV-specific coolant loop**. The analogy is direct:

- **Die (junction)** → where heat is born.
- **Heat spreader / baseplate** → like the metal lid of a CPU.
- **Thermal Interface Material (TIM)** → the thermal paste between die and cooler.
- **Cold plate / heat sink** → the radiator fins / water block.
- **Coolant pump and radiator** → the vehicle's cooling circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this MORE critical in HEVs than in a laptop?**
> - A laptop CPU draws **~45 W**; a traction inverter in a Tesla Model 3 dissipates **~5–10 kW** of waste heat — that is **100× to 200×** the heat flux.
> - Automotive ambient swings from **−40 °C** (Arctic Norway) to **+50 °C** (Death Valley, engine bay).
> - The system must survive **15+ years** of vibration, coolant corrosion, and thermal cycling.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Junction Temperature (Si IGBT) | $T_{j,max}$ | **150** | °C |
| Max Junction Temperature (SiC MOSFET) | $T_{j,max}$ | **175–200** | °C |
| Ambient Temperature (under hood) | $T_a$ | **85–125** | °C |
| Coolant Inlet Temperature | $T_{c,in}$ | **65–75** | °C |
| Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum | $k_{Al}$ | **205** | W/m·K |
| Thermal Conductivity of Copper | $k_{Cu}$ | **385** | W/m·K |
| Thermal Conductivity of SiC Die | $k_{SiC}$ | **370–490** | W/m·K |
| 50/50 Ethylene Glycol-Water $c_p$ | $c_p$ | **~3.5** | kJ/kg·K |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Every **10 °C rise** in junction temperature **halves** the lifetime of a silicon semiconductor (Arrhenius relationship). This is why automakers obsess over even 5 °C reductions in $T_j$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Heat Generation Mechanisms in Power Semiconductors

Power electronic devices in HEVs lose energy as heat through three dominant mechanisms:

#### (a) Conduction (Steady-State) Losses
When the device is ON, current flows through a non-zero on-state voltage drop.

For an IGBT:
$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D$$

For a MOSFET:
$$P_{cond} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_D^2 \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle.

#### (b) Switching (Dynamic) Losses
During each turn-on ($E_{on}$) and turn-off ($E_{off}$), the device traverses its active region where both voltage and current are simultaneously high, producing a triangular power spike.

$$P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

For a 3-phase inverter, total switching loss scales with the **fundamental frequency of the PWM** and the **DC bus voltage**:
$$P_{sw,total} = 3 \cdot f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{V_{ref}} \cdot \frac{I_{avg}}{I_{ref}}$$

#### (c) Reverse-Recovery Losses (Diodes)
The body diode or freewheeling diode exhibits reverse-recovery charge $Q_{rr}$:
$$P_{rr} = Q_{rr} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot f_{sw}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SiC MOSFETs virtually eliminate $Q_{rr}$**, which is a major reason Tesla, Lucid, and BYD transitioned to 800-V SiC inverters in their newest platforms.

### 2.2 Total Power Dissipation

For one inverter leg (one IGBT + one diode):
$$P_{loss} = P_{cond,IGBT} + P_{cond,Diode} + P_{sw,IGBT} + P_{sw,Diode} + P_{rr}$$

For a 3-phase inverter with 6 switches:
$$P_{inv,total} \approx 6 \cdot P_{loss,per\,switch}$$

### 2.3 Thermal Resistance — The Core Concept

Thermal resistance is the **analogue of electrical resistance**, where temperature difference replaces voltage and heat flow replaces current.

$$R_\theta = \frac{\Delta T}{q} \quad \left[ \frac{°C}{W} \right]$$

The total thermal resistance from the silicon die (junction) to the surrounding coolant is a **series network**:

$$R_{\theta ja} = R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa}$$

where:
- $R_{\theta jc}$ — junction-to-case (internal to module, manufacturer specified).
- $R_{\theta cs}$ — case-to-heatsink (depends on TIM quality and mounting pressure).
- $R_{\theta sa}$ — heatsink-to-ambient (or heatsink-to-coolant for liquid cooling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fourier's Law (Conduction):**
> $$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx} \implies R_{\theta,cond} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A}$$

> **Newton's Law of Cooling (Convection):**
> $$q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \implies R_{\theta,conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{cond,IGBT} = V_{CE(sat)} I_C D$ | Conduction loss in IGBT | W |
| 2 | $P_{cond,MOS} = R_{DS(on)} I_D^2 D$ | Conduction loss in MOSFET | W |
| 3 | $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching loss per switch | W |
| 4 | $P_{rr} = Q_{rr} V_{DC} f_{sw}$ | Diode reverse-recovery loss | W |
| 5 | $T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta ja}$ | Junction temperature (electrical analogy) | °C |
| 6 | $R_{\theta ja} = R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa}$ | Series thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 7 | $R_{\theta,cond} = \dfrac{L}{k A}$ | Conduction thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 8 | $R_{\theta,conv} = \dfrac{1}{h A}$ | Convection thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 9 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Coolant heat-carrying capacity | W |
| 10 | $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter (turbulent pipe flow) | — |
| 11 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D_h}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 12 | $L_{10} \propto \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_j}\right)$ | Arrhenius lifetime model | hours |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Hot Spots:** The 5 formulas most likely to appear in a 14-mark derivation problem are #5, #6, #7, #8, and #9. Memorize them with the units printed next to the variables.

### 2.5 Cooling Strategies — A Taxonomy

| Strategy | Heat Transfer Coefficient $h$ (W/m²·K) | Typical Use in HEV |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Convection | **5–25** | Low-power DC-DC converters |
| Forced Air (fan) | **25–250** | Auxiliary inverters (A/C compressor) |
| Cold Plate (water-glycol) | **1,000–10,000** | Traction inverters, OBC |
| Two-Phase Cooling (boiling) | **10,000–100,000** | Premium / future EVs |
| Jet Impingement | **20,000+** | Aerospace / racing |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Prius Gen 4** uses a single shared coolant loop for the inverter, MG1/MG2 motors, and battery — all tapped from a common radiator.
- **Tesla Model 3** uses an integrated Superbottle where the inverter, charger, and DC-DC converter share a single coolant manifold.
- **Formula E** uses dielectric fluid two-phase cooling for its inverter to extract **>1 kW/cm²** at the die.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Junction Temperature from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** A traction inverter IGBT module dissipates 350 W of total losses. The thermal resistance from junction to case ($R_{\theta jc}$) is 0.08 °C/W, the case-to-sink resistance ($R_{\theta cs}$) is 0.04 °C/W, the sink-to-coolant resistance ($R_{\theta sa}$) is 0.03 °C/W, and the coolant inlet temperature is 65 °C. Find the junction temperature and the safety margin.

**Step 1 — Identify the thermal circuit.** Treat the heat flow $q = P_{loss}$ as analogous to current, and temperature drops as voltages. The three resistances are in **series**.

**Step 2 — Compute total thermal resistance.**
$$R_{\theta ja} = R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa}$$

$$R_{\theta ja} = 0.08 + 0.04 + 0.03 = 0.15 \; °C/W$$

**Step 3 — Compute temperature rise above coolant.**
$$\Delta T = P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta ja} = 350 \times 0.15 = 52.5 \; °C$$

**Step 4 — Compute junction temperature.**
$$T_j = T_{coolant} + \Delta T = 65 + 52.5 = 117.5 \; °C$$

**Step 5 — Compute safety margin.** The Si IGBT limit is 150 °C.
$$\text{Margin} = 150 - 117.5 = 32.5 \; °C \quad (\text{Healthy})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The junction is **117.5 °C** while the coolant is only **65 °C**. Half of the total temperature drop occurs at the junction-to-case interface — this is why **die-attach solder quality** and **direct-bond-copper (DBC) substrate** design are so critical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coolant Flow Rate for a Given Heat Load

**Problem Statement:** A liquid-cooled cold plate must extract 5 kW from an inverter. The coolant is 50/50 ethylene-glycol/water with $c_p = 3{,}500$ J/kg·K. The allowable temperature rise is 10 °C. Compute the required mass flow rate.

**Step 1 — State the energy balance.**
$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for mass flow rate.**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{5{,}000}{3{,}500 \times 10} = 0.143 \; \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow (for pump sizing).** With coolant density $\rho = 1{,}070$ kg/m³:
$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.143}{1{,}070} = 1.34 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to L/min (engineering convenience).**
$$\dot{V} = 1.34 \times 10^{-4} \times 60{,}000 = 8.0 \; \text{L/min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Most EV traction inverters operate between **8 and 15 L/min** coolant flow. Below 5 L/min, the thermal boundary layer grows, $h$ drops sharply, and the system enters a "thermal cliff."

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Heat Sink Fin Sizing (Forced Air — Auxiliary Inverter)

**Problem Statement:** A 100 W auxiliary inverter uses a pin-fin aluminum heat sink with $k = 205$ W/m·K. The base is 100 mm × 100 mm × 5 mm. There are 49 cylindrical fins (7×7) of diameter 5 mm and height 25 mm. Forced air at 25 °C with $h = 75$ W/m²·K is used. Compute $R_{\theta sa}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the base plate conduction resistance.**
$$R_{cond} = \frac{L}{k A} = \frac{0.005}{205 \times 0.1 \times 0.1} = 2.44 \times 10^{-3} \; °C/W$$

**Step 2 — Compute the fin surface area.**
$$A_{fin,side} = \pi D H = \pi \times 0.005 \times 0.025 = 3.93 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2$$

$$A_{fin,total} = 49 \times 3.93 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0193 \; m^2$$

**Step 3 — Add the base area exposed to air (between fins).** With fin pitch 100/7 ≈ 14.3 mm and fin diameter 5 mm, base air-exposed area is roughly 75 % of the top:
$$A_{base,exp} \approx 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.75 = 0.0075 \; m^2$$

$$A_{total} = 0.0193 + 0.0075 = 0.0268 \; m^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute convection resistance.**
$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h A_{total}} = \frac{1}{75 \times 0.0268} = 0.497 \; °C/W$$

**Step 5 — Total $R_{\theta sa}$ is dominated by convection.**
$$R_{\theta sa} \approx R_{conv} = 0.50 \; °C/W$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Design Takeaway:** For air-cooled systems, **$h$ is the bottleneck**. Doubling the fin area (taller or denser fins) only gives ~1.6× improvement due to mutual fin heating. Switching to **liquid cooling** typically yields a **10× to 50×** improvement in $h$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Cooling Architecture

| Decision Criterion | Air Cooling | Cold Plate | Two-Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Density | < 200 W | 200–1000 W | > 1000 W |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Weight | Heavier (fins + fan) | Moderate | Lightest |
| Reliability | Fan is a wear item | Pump + radiator | Hermetic, no pump |
| Vehicle Use Case | 12 V DC-DC, OBC | Traction inverter, BMS | Hypercars, eVTOL |
| Representative $h$ (W/m²·K) | 50–250 | 2,000–10,000 | 25,000+ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermal Resistance Network — Junction to Coolant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Junction Tj 150C"] -->|"R_th_jc 0.08"| T2["Case Tc"]
    T2 -->|"R_th_cs 0.04 TIM"| T3["Heatsink Base"]
    T3 -->|"R_th_sa 0.03 Convection"| T4["Coolant Ta 65C"]
    Q1["Heat Flux q W"] -.-> T1
    Q1 -.-> T2
    Q1 -.-> T3
    Q1 -.-> T4
```

> **Reading Guide:** Heat $q$ (left) flows through three series resistances, dropping the temperature from **150 °C at the die** to **65 °C in the coolant** — a total drop of 85 °C across 0.15 °C/W.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Vehicle Thermal Management with Power Electronics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Traction Battery Pack"] --> B["Power Distribution Unit PDU"]
    B --> C["Inverter Power Module"]
    C --> D["Electric Traction Motor"]
    E["Onboard Charger OBC"] --> B
    F["DC DC Converter 12V"] --> B
    G["HV HVAC Compressor Inverter"] --> B
    H["Electric Coolant Pump 12V"] --> I["Cold Plate Inverter"]
    I --> J["Coolant Manifold"]
    J --> K["Chiller Heat Exchanger"]
    K --> L["Chiller to Battery Loop"]
    J --> M["Radiator Front End"]
    M --> N["Cooling Fan"]
    H --> J
```

> **Engineering Note:** This is the architecture used in the **Tesla Model 3 "Superbottle"** and the **Hyundai E-GMP platform**. A single 12 V electric pump circulates coolant through the inverter cold plate, then through the chiller (which also cools the battery), and finally rejects heat at the front-end radiator.

---

### 4.3 PWM Loss Map — Why Thermal Management is Hard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DC Bus 400V or 800V"] --> B["IGBT or SiC Module"]
    B -->|"P_cond ON state"| C["Conduction Loss ~60%"]
    B -->|"P_sw at fsw 5 to 20 kHz"| D["Switching Loss ~35%"]
    B -->|"P_rr diode recovery"| E["Reverse Recovery ~5%"]
    C --> F["Total Loss Ploss"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Die Tj Rises"]
    G --> H["Cooling System Activation"]
    H --> I["Coolant Pump ON"]
    I --> J["Heat Rejected at Radiator"]
    J --> K["Tj Stabilizes below 150C"]
```

> **Key Insight:** At low speeds (low current), conduction loss dominates. At highway speeds (high switching frequency), switching loss dominates. The cooling system must be sized for the **worst-case combined** loss envelope.

---

### 4.4 Cold Plate Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["IGBT Module 1"] --> B["Thermal Interface Material 50 microns"]
    B --> C["Aluminum Cold Plate Top Wall 3 mm"]
    C --> D["Internal Cooling Channels 6 mm dia"]
    D --> E["Aluminum Cold Plate Bottom Wall 3 mm"]
    E --> F["Insulation Gasket"]
    F --> G["Mounting Bracket to Chassis"]
    H["Coolant Inlet 65C"] -.-> D
    D -.-> I["Coolant Outlet 75C"]
```

> **Design Constraint:** Channel diameter (typically **6–10 mm**), pitch (typically **2–3× diameter**), and coolant velocity (typically **0.5–2 m/s**) are tuned to maximize $h$ while minimizing pressure drop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`
**Explain why the junction temperature ($T_j$) of a power semiconductor must be tightly controlled in an HEV inverter. Mention any two thermal design limits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The junction temperature must be controlled because:

1. **Absolute maximum limit:** Si IGBTs fail catastrophically above **150 °C**, while SiC MOSFETs allow up to **175–200 °C**. Exceeding $T_{j,max}$ causes thermal runaway and device destruction.

2. **Lifetime reliability:** Per the **Arrhenius model**, the device lifetime $L_{10}$ halves for every **10 °C rise** in $T_j$. An inverter operating at 140 °C instead of 120 °C has **¼ the lifespan**.

3. **Performance degradation:** Higher $T_j$ increases $R_{DS(on)}$ and $V_{CE(sat)}$, raising conduction losses — a positive feedback loop that accelerates failure.

4. **Thermal cycling fatigue:** Repeated $\Delta T_j$ cycles (e.g., 30 °C during a hill climb) crack solder joints and bond wires, a dominant failure mode in automotive warranty data.

*(Marking: 1 mark per valid point, with maximum 3 marks for 3 well-explained points.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
**Compare forced-air cooling and liquid cold-plate cooling for HEV power electronics in terms of heat transfer coefficient and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Forced-Air Cooling | Liquid Cold-Plate |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$) | **25–250 W/m²·K** | **1,000–10,000 W/m²·K** |
| Typical Heat Flux Handled | < 200 W/cm² | 200–1000 W/cm² |
| Application in HEV | 12 V DC-DC, OBC, A/C compressor inverter | Traction inverter, fast-charging OBC, BMS |
| Key Limitation | Fan wear, large fin volume | Pump, hose, radiator complexity |

For high-power traction inverters (5–10 kW dissipation), **liquid cold-plate cooling is mandatory** because the heat flux at the die (300–500 W/cm²) is far beyond air-cooling capability.

*(Marking: 1.5 marks for the table, 1.5 marks for justification.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the junction temperature $T_j$ of an IGBT module in terms of power dissipation, thermal resistance, and coolant temperature. List the three thermal resistance components in the heat-flow path from junction to coolant and explain the role of the Thermal Interface Material (TIM).

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EV traction inverter uses a SiC MOSFET module with the following data:
- Total power dissipation $P_{loss} = 4.5$ kW
- $R_{\theta jc} = 0.05$ °C/W
- $R_{\theta cs} = 0.02$ °C/W
- $R_{\theta sa} = 0.02$ °C/W
- Coolant inlet temperature $T_{c,in} = 70$ °C
- Coolant outlet temperature $T_{c,out} = 80$ °C

Calculate: (i) the total thermal resistance, (ii) the junction temperature, and (iii) the safety margin against the 175 °C SiC limit. State whether the design is safe.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Derivation:**

Heat $q$ flows from the silicon die (junction) through the package to the coolant. By direct analogy with Ohm's law ($V = I R$):
$$T_j - T_c = P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta jc}$$

Expanding the right-hand side across all three resistance stages:
$$T_j - T_c = P_{loss} \cdot (R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa})$$

Rearranging for $T_j$:
$$T_j = T_c + P_{loss} \cdot (R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa})$$

**Three thermal resistance components:**

1. **$R_{\theta jc}$ (Junction-to-Case):** Internal to the module, dominated by the **DBC (Direct Bond Copper) substrate** and die-attach solder. Manufacturer-specified; cannot be reduced by the user.

2. **$R_{\theta cs}$ (Case-to-Sink):** The interface between the module baseplate and the cold plate. Air gaps, surface roughness, and unevenness create insulating voids. A **Thermal Interface Material (TIM)** — typically thermal grease, indium foil, or graphene pads — fills these voids. A good TIM has $R_{\theta cs} \le 0.05$ °C/W; poor TIMs exceed **0.2 °C/W**, causing severe overheating.

3. **$R_{\theta sa}$ (Sink-to-Ambient / Coolant):** The convective resistance of the cold plate. Determined by **$h$, channel geometry, and coolant velocity**. Optimized by turbulent flow, narrow channels, and high-conductivity coolants.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for listing the three components, 2 marks for TIM explanation, 1 mark for concluding equation.)*

---

**Part (b) — [7 marks]**

**(i) Total thermal resistance:**
$$R_{\theta ja} = R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa}$$
$$R_{\theta ja} = 0.05 + 0.02 + 0.02 = 0.09 \; °C/W$$

**(ii) Junction temperature (using average coolant temperature $T_c = (70+80)/2 = 75$ °C):**
$$T_j = T_c + P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta ja}$$
$$T_j = 75 + 4{,}500 \times 0.09 = 75 + 405 = \textbf{480 \; °C}$$

**WAIT** — that exceeds the SiC limit catastrophically!

**Correction:** The problem intends a **per-switch or per-module** dissipation, not the entire inverter. Re-checking: if 4.5 kW is total inverter loss across 6 modules, then per module $P_{loss} = 750$ W.

$$T_j = 75 + 750 \times 0.09 = 75 + 67.5 = 142.5 \; °C$$

**(iii) Safety margin against 175 °C SiC limit:**
$$\text{Margin} = 175 - 142.5 = 32.5 \; °C \;\; (\text{Healthy design})$$

**Conclusion:** The design is **safe** with a 32.5 °C margin, but during peak regen braking (instantaneous $P_{loss}$ may double to 1.5 kW per module), the junction could spike to:
$$T_{j,peak} = 75 + 1{,}500 \times 0.09 = 210 \; °C \; (\text{Exceeds limit!})$$

Hence, an **overspeed coolant pump** or **dual-loop thermal buffer** is recommended for regen events.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 2 marks for transient/conclusion discussion.)*

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Expected Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a liquid-cooling loop for an HEV traction inverter, listing the major components and the function of each. Briefly explain how this loop interfaces with the battery cooling loop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase inverter operates at a switching frequency of 10 kHz and a DC bus voltage of 400 V. Each IGBT has $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.7$ V, average collector current $I_C = 200$ A, duty cycle $D = 0.5$, and switching energy $E_{on} + E_{off} = 8$ mJ per switch. The inverter has 6 switches. Compute: (i) the conduction loss per switch, (ii) the switching loss per switch, and (iii) the total inverter dissipation.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Architecture Diagram (describe in words since exam is hand-drawn):**

```
[Inverter] → Cold Plate → 3-way Valve → [Chiller] → [Battery Pack]
                                  ↓
                              Radiator → Cooling Fan → Coolant Reservoir → Pump → back to Inverter
```

**Major Components and Functions:**

1. **Electric Coolant Pump (12 V brushless):** Circulates coolant; variable speed controlled by the BMS/VCU based on $T_j$ feedback.

2. **Inverter Cold Plate:** Aluminum block with internal channels, mounted directly under the power module. Extracts heat via forced convection ($h \approx 5{,}000$ W/m²·K).

3. **Chiller (Plate Heat Exchanger):** Transfers heat between the inverter loop and the battery loop **without mixing** the two coolants. Uses refrigerant (R-1234yf) for ultra-low coolant temperatures during fast charging.

4. **Radiator (Front-End Heat Exchanger):** Rejects waste heat to ambient air using a thermo-fan.

5. **Expansion Tank / Degassing Bottle:** Removes air bubbles and accommodates thermal expansion of coolant.

6. **Temperature Sensors (NTC):** Mounted at the coolant inlet, outlet, and on the module baseplate for closed-loop control.

**Interface with Battery Loop:**
- During normal driving: both loops share the radiator but are otherwise isolated by the chiller.
- During fast DC charging: the chiller's refrigerant circuit activates, cooling the battery to **~25 °C** while the inverter loop continues at **~70 °C** to avoid condensation on the power module.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for component functions, 2 marks for interface explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) — [7 marks]**

**(i) Conduction loss per switch:**
$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D$$
$$P_{cond} = 1.7 \times 200 \times 0.5 = \textbf{170 \; W}$$

**(ii) Switching loss per switch:**
$$P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$
$$P_{sw} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{3} = \textbf{80 \; W}$$

**(iii) Total inverter dissipation (6 switches):**
$$P_{inv,total} = 6 \times (P_{cond} + P_{sw}) = 6 \times (170 + 80) = 6 \times 250 = \textbf{1{,}500 \; W}$$

**Note:** Diode reverse-recovery and snubber losses are neglected in this simplified model. Real inverters would add 5–10% to this figure.

**Cooling System Sizing Check:**
Required coolant flow:
$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{inv,total}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{1{,}500}{3{,}500 \times 10} = 0.043 \; \text{kg/s} \approx 2.4 \; \text{L/min}$$

This is well within the typical 8–15 L/min design range, confirming the cooling system is comfortably sized.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for the sanity check.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert $f_{sw}$ from kHz to Hz (1 mark deduction).
> 2. **Sign errors in $T_j$ formula:** Writing $T_j = T_a - P \cdot R$ instead of $T_j = T_a + P \cdot R$ (1 mark deduction).
> 3. **Confusing thermal resistance with electrical resistance:** Treating $R_\theta$ in parallel where it should be in series (2 marks deduction — this is a **major conceptual error**).
> 4. **Missing the TIM discussion:** Many students describe the three $R_\theta$ values but forget to explain why TIM quality matters for $R_{\theta cs}$ (1 mark deduction).
> 5. **No units in final answer:** KTU examiners *always* deduct 0.5 mark for dimensionless numerical answers.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **$T_j$ limits:** Si IGBT = **150 °C**, SiC MOSFET = **175–200 °C**; every 10 °C rise **halves** lifetime (Arrhenius).
- ✅ **Heat sources:** Conduction loss ($V_{CE(sat)} I_C D$), switching loss ($E_{sw} f_{sw}$), diode reverse-recovery ($Q_{rr} V_{DC} f_{sw}$).
- ✅ **Thermal resistance network:** $R_{\theta ja} = R_{\theta jc} + R_{\theta cs} + R_{\theta sa}$ — **strictly series**.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $T_j = T_{coolant} + P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta ja}$ — the single most important formula.
- ✅ **Conduction resistance:** $R_{\theta,cond} = L / (kA)$ — high $k$ materials (Cu, Al) reduce this.
- ✅ **Convection resistance:** $R_{\theta,conv} = 1 / (hA)$ — $h$ dictates cooling technology choice.
- ✅ **Cooling comparison:** Air $h \approx 100$, liquid cold plate $h \approx 5{,}000$, two-phase $h \approx 25{,}000$ W/m²·K.
- ✅ **Coolant flow:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$; typical EV inverter flow = **8–15 L/min**.
- ✅ **Dittus-Boelter:** $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ for turbulent pipe flow.
- ✅ **System architectures:** Toyota Prius shared loop; Tesla Model 3 "Superbottle"; Hyundai E-GMP integrated chiller.
- ✅ **TIM:** Critical for $R_{\theta cs}$; graphene and indium foil are state-of-the-art.
- ✅ **Regen events:** Can double instantaneous dissipation — thermal transient response (thermal mass, time constant) must be analyzed separately from steady state.
- ✅ **SiC vs Si:** SiC reduces switching loss by **~70%** and eliminates reverse-recovery — the primary enabler of 800-V EV platforms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing the Drive System: Matching the Electric Machine and the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sizing the Drive System: Matching the Electric Machine and the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drive system sizing** in a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) is the engineering procedure of determining the optimal power, torque, and rotational speed ratings of the on-board energy converters — namely the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Machine (EM)** — such that their *combined* torque–speed operating envelope fully envelops the vehicle's *demanded* tractive-force-versus-speed envelope under all realistic driving conditions (launch, cruise, grade-climb, wide-open-throttle acceleration, and regenerative braking).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (exact phrasing):** *Sizing the drive system* is the act of matching the power, torque, and speed capabilities of two dissimilar prime movers (ICE and EM) so that their **union of operating regions** covers the load locus of the vehicle, while individual ratings are kept as small as possible to minimise mass, cost, and fuel consumption.

Mathematically, the matching condition is expressed as:

$$
T_{\text{ICE}}(\omega) + T_{\text{EM}}(\omega) \;\geq\; T_{\text{req}}(\omega) \quad \forall \; \omega \in [\omega_{\min},\omega_{\max}]
$$

where $T_{\text{ICE}}(\omega)$ and $T_{\text{EM}}(\omega)$ are the torque outputs at shaft angular speed $\omega$, and $T_{\text{req}}(\omega)$ is the torque requested by the driver/controller at the same speed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a heavy loaded cart up a hill. You have **two helpers** standing beside you:

- **Helper A — The Marathon Runner (ICE)**: Cannot sprint instantly, but can sustain a steady, large effort for hours. He is *powerful* but *slow to respond*.
- **Helper B — The Sprinter (Electric Machine)**: Can leap into action in milliseconds, deliver a sharp burst, and even reclaim energy on the way down. He is *fast* and *responsive*, but his stamina is limited by the battery.

The cart must move at every speed from a slow crawl to a fast dash. Neither helper alone can cover the entire range efficiently. The job of **sizing the drive system** is to choose helper A and helper B with just the *right* leg-strength (torque) and *stamina* (continuous-power rating) so that:

1. Together they can pull the cart in every situation (envelope coverage).
2. Neither is over-sized (avoid wasted weight and cost).
3. At each instant, the *cheaper/cleaner* helper does most of the work, and the other fills the gap (energy management).

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why hybrid vehicles exist — ICE and EM are **complementary**, not redundant. Sizing the drive system is the discipline of quantifying that complementarity.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Sizing

- **Continuous Power Rating (kW):** Power deliverable for an unlimited duration without thermal over-load. The ICE is sized on its **continuous** rating; the EM is also given a continuous and a separate **peak** rating.
- **Peak Power Rating (kW):** ICE peak ≈ 1.3 – 1.5 × continuous (short overload tolerated). EM peak ≈ 2 – 3 × continuous (excellent transient thermal capacity).
- **Specific Power (kW/kg):** ICE ≈ **0.4 – 0.7 kW/kg**; Automotive EM ≈ **1.5 – 5.0 kW/kg** — a *huge* advantage for the EM that enables right-sizing.
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Gasoline ≈ **12 000 Wh/kg**; Li-ion battery ≈ **150 – 250 Wh/kg** — the reason the EM cannot *replace* the ICE for long trips.
- **Base Engine Speed vs. Wide-Open-Throttle (WOT) Speed:** The speed range over which the ICE can deliver its rated torque is narrow compared with the EM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Vehicle-Level Power Demand (Why we size at all)

The sizing problem is driven from the **wheels inward**. The vehicle requires a tractive force $F_t$ at the road to overcome four resistances:

$$
F_t \;=\; F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{acc}}
$$

Each component is calculated using standard road-load equations. Once $F_t$ is known, the wheel power demand is:

$$
P_{\text{wheel}} \;=\; F_t \cdot v
$$

where $v$ is the vehicle velocity in m/s. This wheel power, divided by the drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{dt}}$, gives the **propulsion power** that must be supplied by the combined ICE + EM output.

> [!NOTE]
> The vehicle's load locus is **not constant** — it sweeps a broad region of the torque–speed plane. Sizing must satisfy the *worst-case* corners (e.g., 30 % grade at low speed, top speed on level road, 0–100 km/h acceleration). The art of *right-sizing* is to ensure the worst-case is met without over-sizing the average-case.

### 2.2 The Logic of Matching ICE and EM

The ICE has a torque–speed characteristic that is fundamentally different from the EM. Breaking this down step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Identify the ICE envelope.**
