The ICE has a *constant-maximum-power* (or constant-torque then constant-power) profile. Above a certain base speed, the torque decreases inversely with speed (constant power region). Below base speed, the torque is limited by the throttle / fuel-cut map.

**Step 2 — Identify the EM envelope.**  
The EM has a *much wider* constant-torque region (from 0 rpm up to its base speed $\omega_{\text{base,EM}}$) followed by a constant-power region up to its maximum speed $\omega_{\text{max,EM}}$. Modern EMs can sustain short-term overloads of 2–3× their continuous rating.

**Step 3 — Form the union of the two envelopes.**  
In a parallel or power-split hybrid, the two shafts can be mechanically coupled (through a planetary gear, clutch, or transmission), so the **sum** of available torques at any given shaft speed is the achievable envelope. Sizing aims to make this *union envelope* just envelop the required one.

**Step 4 — Right-size by load-spectrum analysis.**  
Standard drive cycles (UDDS, HWFET, NEDC, WLTP, and Indian IDC) are used to compute a *time-weighted* average power. The ICE is typically sized close to this average, while the EM is sized to handle peak transients and regenerative events. This decouples the *average* sizing from the *peak* sizing — a core hybrid advantage.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Tip:** Memorise the units and the *direction* (motor vs. generator) of every equation in the table below. Examiners award marks for correct unit consistency, not just the symbol.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|----------|----------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Tractive force required at wheels | $F_t = m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \tfrac{dv}{dt}$ | N | $f_r$ ≈ 0.015; $\rho$ = 1.202 kg/m³; $C_d$ ≈ 0.3; $A$ frontal area m²; $\delta$ mass factor 1.05 – 1.4 |
| 2 | Wheel power demand | $P_{\text{wheel}} = F_t \cdot v$ | W | Multiply by drivetrain efficiency to get propulsion shaft power |
| 3 | Propulsion shaft power | $P_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{\text{dt}} \eta_{\text{mt}}}$ | W | $\eta_{\text{dt}}$ driveline; $\eta_{\text{mt}}$ motor+transmission |
| 4 | Power split (parallel HEV) | $P_{\text{req}} = P_{\text{ICE}} + P_{\text{EM}} \cdot \eta_{\text{EM}}$ | W | Sign convention: positive EM = motor, negative = generator |
| 5 | Maximum tractive effort at low speed | $F_{t,\max} = \dfrac{T_{\text{ICE,max}} + T_{\text{EM,max}}}{r_{\text{wheel}}} \cdot G$ | N | $G$ = total gear ratio; $r_{\text{wheel}}$ wheel radius |
| 6 | Maximum vehicle speed | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{ICE,max}} \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}}{G}$ (or with EM if higher gear) | m/s | Limited by continuous power, not torque |
| 7 | EM constant-power speed ratio (CPSR) | $\text{CPSR} = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{max,EM}}}{\omega_{\text{base,EM}}}$ | – | Typically **3 – 5** for automotive EMs; CPSR = 1 for ICE |
| 8 | Specific power of prime mover | $\sigma_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{cont}}}{m_{\text{mach}}}$ | kW/kg | EM = 1.5 – 5; ICE = 0.4 – 0.7 |
| 9 | Battery energy to support EM | $E_{\text{bat}} = \int P_{\text{EM}}(t) \, dt$ | J or Wh | 1 Wh = 3 600 J |
| 10 | Acceleration time (0 → $v_f$) | $t_a = \dfrac{m \delta}{2 P_{\text{max}}} \left(v_f^2 - v_0^2\right)$ | s | Approximate, $P_{\text{max}}$ is total propulsion peak power |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS-II)** uses a small Atkinson-cycle ICE (≈ 1.8 L, 73 kW) coupled with two EMs (≈ 60 kW + 30 kW). The two EMs bridge the ICE's narrow high-efficiency window — a textbook matching exercise.
- **BMW i8** uses a 1.5 L turbo-3 ICE (170 kW) and an EM (96 kW) — combined peak ≈ 266 kW, achieving sports-car performance with a *midsize-sedan* ICE. The EM covers the turbo-lag and launch torque, exactly the matching principle.
- **Tata/Ashok Leyland urban buses** size the EM for stop-and-go launch power, while the ICE is sized for steady-state cruising — minimising urban fuel use and emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> Sizing the drive system is *not* an academic exercise. It directly determines the **bill of materials cost**, **fuel economy label**, **boot-space** (where batteries go), and **road-tax bracket** (in India, vehicles below 4 m and under 1 200 cc get tax benefits) of every production hybrid.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Required Propulsion Torque–Speed Envelope

The vehicle's resistance forces are derived from Newton's second law applied at the wheel contact patch.

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance.**  
A pneumatic tyre deflects slightly as it rolls. The energy lost in the hysteresis of the rubber produces a resistance:

$$
F_{\text{roll}} \;=\; C_{rr} \cdot m g \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless, ≈ 0.008 – 0.015 for a passenger car tyre at 80 km/h).

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic drag.**  
From fluid dynamics, drag force on a body moving at velocity $v$ in air of density $\rho$ with frontal area $A$ and drag coefficient $C_d$ is:

$$
F_{\text{aero}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2
$$

**Step 3 — Grade resistance.**  
When climbing a slope of angle $\alpha$, gravity opposes motion by a component of weight:

$$
F_{\text{grade}} \;=\; m g \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration resistance (inertia).**  
Both the vehicle mass and the rotational inertia of rotating components (wheels, gears, shafts) must be accelerated. The equivalent inertia mass is $m_{\text{eq}} = m \delta$, where the **mass factor** $\delta$ is:

$$
\delta \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m r_w^{\,2}} + \dfrac{I_{\text{eng}}}{m r_w^{\,2}} G^2 \;+\; \text{transmission terms}
$$

For a typical car $\delta \approx 1.05$ (1st gear) to $1.4$ (overdrive). Thus:

$$
F_{\text{acc}} \;=\; m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum and convert to torque at the propulsion shaft.**  
The total tractive force at the road is the sum:

$$
F_t \;=\; m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt}
$$

The corresponding torque at the propulsion shaft (after the gear reduction $G$) is:

$$
T_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{F_t \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}}{G \cdot \eta_{\text{gear}}}
$$

The corresponding rotational speed is:

$$
\omega_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{v \cdot G}{r_{\text{wheel}}}
$$

These two equations, evaluated at every vehicle speed $v$, trace out the **load locus** in the $T$–$\omega$ plane. The matching problem is to ensure the union of $T_{\text{ICE}}(\omega)$ and $T_{\text{EM}}(\omega)$ envelops this locus.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Acceleration Time (using Matching Peak Power)

**Step 1 — Energy balance during acceleration.**  
The kinetic energy gained from $v_0$ to $v_f$ is:

$$
\Delta KE \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m \delta \left(v_f^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Power delivered by propulsion system.**  
If the combined peak propulsion power is $P_{\text{peak}} = P_{\text{ICE,peak}} + P_{\text{EM,peak}} \cdot \eta_{\text{EM}}$, and if we neglect the road-load resistances during the short acceleration interval (a common engineering approximation), then:

$$
P_{\text{peak}} \cdot t_a \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{2} m \delta \left(v_f^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for acceleration time.**

$$
t_a \;=\; \dfrac{m \delta \, \left(v_f^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2}\right)}{2 \, P_{\text{peak}}}
$$

This is the **classic 0–100 km/h time formula** used by every automotive magazine — and it is the single most important sizing equation because it directly fixes the *peak* power required.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Given:**  
- Vehicle mass $m = 1\,500$ kg (including 75 kg driver per Indian MIDC standard)  
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.012$  
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.31$, frontal area $A = 2.20$ m²  
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m  
- Air density $\rho = 1.202$ kg/m³  
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{dt}} = 0.92$  
- Top speed goal $v_{\max} = 160$ km/h = 44.44 m/s  
- 0–100 km/h target = 10 s on level road  
- Mass factor $\delta = 1.10$  
- Gear ratio (1st, for launch) $G_1 = 3.5$

**Find:** (a) The required **continuous propulsion power** for top speed on level road. (b) The required **peak propulsion power** for the 0–100 km/h target. (c) A reasonable split between ICE and EM (EM sized to cover all transients and regenerative events).

#### Solution (a) — Continuous power for top speed

At top speed, $dv/dt = 0$, $\alpha = 0$. Aerodynamic drag dominates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,\text{cruise}} &= m g f_r \cos 0 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v_{\max}^2 \;+\; 0 \;+\; 0 \\[4pt]
&= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 1 \;+\; 0.5 \cdot 1.202 \cdot 0.31 \cdot 2.20 \cdot (44.44)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 176.58 \;+\; 0.5 \cdot 1.202 \cdot 0.31 \cdot 2.20 \cdot 1974.6 \\[4pt]
&= 176.58 \;+\; 818.4 \\[4pt]
&\approx 994.98 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wheel power at top speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wheel,cruise}} &= F_{t,\text{cruise}} \cdot v_{\max} \\[4pt]
&= 994.98 \cdot 44.44 \\[4pt]
&\approx 44\,214 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 44.2 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Propulsion shaft power (at the input of the gearbox):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{prop,cruise}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{wheel,cruise}}}{\eta_{\text{dt}}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{44.21}{0.92} \;\approx\; 48.05 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result (a):** The **continuous** ICE + EM (combined) must produce at least **≈ 48 kW** to cruise at 160 km/h on level road.

#### Solution (b) — Peak power for 0–100 km/h in 10 s

Using the formula from §3.2, with $v_0 = 0$ and $v_f = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{peak}} &= \dfrac{m \delta \cdot v_f^{\,2}}{2 \cdot t_a} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1500 \cdot 1.10 \cdot (27.78)^2}{2 \cdot 10} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1650 \cdot 771.6}{20} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1\,273\,175}{20} \\[4pt]
&\approx 63\,659 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 63.7 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

But this is **only the kinetic-energy term**. During the 10 s, road-load resistances absorb additional energy. A more accurate figure (integrating the road-load term) is usually **1.3 – 1.5×** the kinetic-only value, so:

$$
P_{\text{peak,design}} \;\approx\; 1.4 \cdot 63.7 \;\approx\; 89 \;\text{kW}
$$

> **Result (b):** The **peak** propulsion power must be at least **≈ 89 – 95 kW** for a 10-second 0–100 km/h.

#### Solution (c) — Practical split between ICE and EM

A robust right-sizing strategy:

- **ICE continuous rating:** Set slightly above the cruise power with margin → **55 kW** (≈ 75 bhp).
- **ICE peak rating (WOT):** ≈ 1.3 × continuous → **≈ 72 kW** available briefly.
- **EM continuous rating:** Set to handle urban launch and assist peaks → **30 kW**.
- **EM peak rating (overload):** 2.5 × continuous → **≈ 75 kW** (typical of modern traction motors).
- **Combined peak (ICE + EM):** ≈ 72 + 75 = **147 kW** → comfortably exceeds the 95 kW design peak, providing margin for grade climbing.

> **Result (c):** A **55 kW ICE + 30 kW continuous (75 kW peak) EM** combination satisfies all three constraints (top-speed cruise, 0–100 in 10 s, urban launch) with reasonable right-sizing margin.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why EM *Complements* ICE

| Attribute | ICE | Electric Machine | Implication for Sizing |
|-----------|-----|------------------|-------------------------|
| Peak/Continuous ratio | 1.3 – 1.5 | 2.0 – 3.0 | EM is the *peak-power* specialist |
| Zero-speed torque | Limited (stall) | Unlimited (within thermal limit) | EM is the *launch* specialist |
| Constant-torque speed range | Very narrow | 0 → base speed (wide) | EM covers low-speed tractive effort |
| Constant-power speed range | Wide | Wide (after base speed) | Both work in highway cruise |
| Specific power (kW/kg) | 0.4 – 0.7 | 1.5 – 5.0 | EM is the *lightweight* option |
| Energy source energy density | 12 000 Wh/kg | 150 – 250 Wh/kg | ICE is the *long-range* specialist |
| Cold-start emissions | High | Zero | EM enables **engine-off** driving |
| Transient response | 0.5 – 1.0 s | 10 – 50 ms | EM is the *fast-response* specialist |

This matrix is the conceptual heart of the matching problem.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Torque–Speed Envelope Matching (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **union of operating regions** of the ICE and the EM and shows how the combined envelope envelops the required load locus. Node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Vehicle Load Locus T req vs omega]
    A1[Low Speed Region 0 to 30 kmph]
    A2[Mid Speed Region 30 to 80 kmph]
    A3[High Speed Region 80 to 160 kmph]

    B0[ICE Torque Speed Envelope]
    B1[ICE Narrow Constant Torque Region]
    B2[ICE Constant Power Region]
    B3[ICE Limited Cold Start Torque]

    C0[EM Torque Speed Envelope]
    C1[EM Wide Constant Torque 0 to base speed]
    C2[EM Constant Power Region]
    C3[EM High Overload 2 to 3 times continuous]

    D0[Combined Envelope Union]
    D1[Sizing Output Match Check]
    D2[Average Power for Drive Cycle]
    D3[Peak Power for 0 to 100 kmph]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    B0 --> B1
    B0 --> B2
    B0 --> B3
    C0 --> C1
    C0 --> C2
    C0 --> C3

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> C2
    A3 --> B2
    A3 --> C2

    B0 --> D0
    C0 --> D0
    D0 --> D1
    D0 --> D2
    D0 --> D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:**  
> - *Left column* — The **vehicle load locus** decomposed into three speed regions.  
> - *Middle column* — The **ICE envelope** (narrow constant-torque, wide constant-power, limited cold-start).  
> - *Right column* — The **EM envelope** (wide constant-torque from 0, wide constant-power, large overload).  
> - *Far right* — The **combined union** is checked against three sizing goals: (D2) average power for the chosen drive cycle, (D3) peak power for acceleration, and (D1) the binary match check (Union Envelope ≥ Load Locus at every ω).

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Sizing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step A Define Vehicle Targets vmax grade accel]
    stepB[Step B Compute Road Load Forces Ft]
    stepC[Step C Convert to Shaft Torque Speed Treq omega]
    stepD[Step D Size ICE Continuous Power from Cruise]
    stepE[Step E Size EM Peak Power from Acceleration]
    stepF[Step F Verify Union Envelope covers Locus]
    stepG[Step G Iterate if margin or oversize detected]
    stepH[Step H Final Bill P ICE kW and P EM kW]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepF
    stepF --> stepH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this flow matters:** KTU examiners often ask the *methodology* question — *"Outline the procedure for sizing the drive system of a parallel hybrid."* The seven steps above are the canonical answer. Note the **feedback loop** from Step G back to Step F: sizing is **iterative**, not one-shot. Designers rarely hit the optimum on the first pass; iteration between envelope-match and component-weight constraints is the norm.

### 4.3 Sizing Influence-Map (Why Right-Sizing is Hard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Vehicle Mass m]
    F2[Target Top Speed]
    F3[Target Acceleration 0 to 100]
    F4[Target Grade percent]
    F5[Drive Cycle Choice]
    F6[Energy Storage Battery kWh]
    F7[Cost Budget]

    O1[Required Continuous Power]
    O2[Required Peak Power]
    O3[Combined Mass Penalty]
    O4[Final EM kW]
    O5[Final ICE kW]

    F1 --> O1
    F1 --> O2
    F2 --> O1
    F3 --> O2
    F4 --> O1
    F4 --> O2
    F5 --> O1
    F6 --> O4
    F7 --> O4
    F7 --> O5

    O1 --> O5
    O2 --> O4
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the influence map:** Every *input factor* on the left affects sizing *outputs* on the right. Note the **coupling** between F1 (vehicle mass) and O3 (mass penalty) — adding more power to the propulsion system *increases* vehicle mass, which *increases* the power required, in a classic feedback loop. Skilled designers break this loop by aggressively lightweighting non-propulsion components (aluminium body, composite panels).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> Define the term *"matching the electric machine and the ICE"* in a hybrid vehicle. Why is the EM needed in addition to the ICE even though the ICE alone can propel the vehicle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Matching* refers to sizing the EM and ICE such that their **combined torque–speed operating envelope** envelops the **vehicle load locus** (the locus of tractive torque versus speed required by the vehicle over the entire drive cycle). The EM complements the ICE because: **(i)** the EM delivers **high peak power and instant torque** at zero speed, covering launch and acceleration where the ICE is least efficient; **(ii)** the ICE delivers **high continuous power** with high specific energy, covering steady cruise and long-range driving; **(iii)** the EM enables **regenerative braking** and **engine-off motoring**, both of which the ICE cannot do. Hence the EM is not a replacement but a **complement** that fills the gaps in the ICE's narrow operating window. *(3 marks — 1 for the definition, 1 for EM strengths, 1 for the complementarity argument.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-2]**

> List any **three** performance parameters that determine the sizing of the propulsion system of a hybrid electric vehicle, with their typical units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Continuous propulsion power (kW):** to sustain top-speed cruise on a level road and on a target grade.  
2. **Peak propulsion power (kW):** to achieve the 0–100 km/h target acceleration time (typically 8 – 12 s for a midsize sedan).  
3. **Maximum tractive force at low speed (N):** to launch the vehicle on a target grade (typically 30 % in Indian driving conditions).  
4. *(Optional bonus)* Maximum regenerative-braking power (kW) and the *energy storage capacity* (kWh) of the battery. *(3 marks — 1 mark per correctly named and unit-ed parameter.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Pattern (per KTU 2024 ESE):** Two questions, answer **either** A or B. Each question is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Series-1]*

> **(a)** Derive, starting from Newton's second law, the expression for the **tractive force required at the wheels** of a road vehicle moving on a slope. Clearly state every symbol and its SI unit. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 1 400 kg hybrid car is required to achieve a top speed of 150 km/h on a 5 % grade and a 0–100 km/h time of 11 s on level road. Given: $f_r = 0.013$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.10$ m², $r_w = 0.31$ m, $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³, $\eta_{\text{dt}} = 0.90$, $\delta = 1.10$. **Compute (i) the continuous propulsion power** required for the top-speed-on-grade condition, and **(ii) the peak propulsion power** required for the 0–100 km/h target. **Suggest a reasonable ICE + EM split.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Let the vehicle of mass $m$ move with velocity $v$ on a road of slope angle $\alpha$. The four resistance forces are:

**(i) Rolling resistance** (tyre hysteresis):

$$
F_{\text{roll}} \;=\; m g \, f_r \, \cos\alpha \quad [\text{N}]
$$

**(ii) Aerodynamic drag** (fluid-dynamic drag on the body):

$$
F_{\text{aero}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2 \quad [\text{N}]
$$

**(iii) Grade resistance** (component of weight along the slope):

$$
F_{\text{grade}} \;=\; m g \sin\alpha \quad [\text{N}]
$$

**(iv) Acceleration resistance** (linear + rotational inertia, $m_{\text{eq}} = m \delta$):

$$
F_{\text{acc}} \;=\; m \, \delta \, \dfrac{dv}{dt} \quad [\text{N}]
$$

By Newton's second law applied at the contact patch, the **tractive force** the road must supply is:

$$
\boxed{F_t \;=\; m g f_r \cos\alpha \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \;+\; m g \sin\alpha \;+\; m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt}} \quad [\text{N}]
$$

*(1 mark for the rolling term, 1 for aerodynamic, 1 for grade, 1 for acceleration, 1 for Newton's law statement, 1 for the boxed result, 1 for correct units.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert given data and find velocity.**

$$
v_{\max} = 150 \;\text{km/h} = 41.67 \;\text{m/s}; \quad v_f = 100 \;\text{km/h} = 27.78 \;\text{m/s}
$$

For 5 % grade: $\tan\alpha = 0.05 \Rightarrow \alpha = 2.86°$. So $\sin\alpha \approx 0.0500$, $\cos\alpha \approx 0.9988$.

**Step 2 — Top-speed-on-grade tractive force (dv/dt = 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v_{\max}^2 + m g \sin\alpha + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 1400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.013 \cdot 0.9988 + 0.5 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.10 \cdot (41.67)^2 + 1400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 \\[4pt]
&\approx 178.4 + 656.5 + 686.7 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1521.6 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Continuous propulsion power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wheel}} &= F_t \cdot v_{\max} = 1521.6 \cdot 41.67 \approx 63\,406 \;\text{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cont}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{\text{dt}}} = \dfrac{63.4}{0.90} \approx 70.5 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Peak power for 0–100 km/h in 11 s (kinetic-energy term):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{peak,kinetic}} &= \dfrac{m \delta v_f^{\,2}}{2 t_a} = \dfrac{1400 \cdot 1.10 \cdot (27.78)^2}{2 \cdot 11} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1540 \cdot 771.6}{22} = \dfrac{1\,188\,264}{22} \approx 54.0 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add road-load penalty (≈ 1.4× factor):**

$$
P_{\text{peak,design}} \approx 1.4 \cdot 54.0 \approx 75.6 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Suggested split:**

- **ICE continuous:** ≈ 70 – 75 kW (covers continuous cruise on 5 % grade with margin).  
- **EM continuous:** ≈ 25 kW (for launch assist and regen).  
- **EM peak:** ≈ 2.5 × 25 = ≈ 62 kW.  
- **Combined peak:** ≈ 75 (ICE peak) + 62 (EM peak) ≈ **137 kW**, far exceeding the 75.6 kW design peak → robust margin.

*(1 mark for v conversion, 1 for tractive-force evaluation, 1 for continuous power, 1 for kinetic peak, 1 for road-load-corrected peak, 1 for the split, 1 for the verification comment.)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]*

> **(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **sizing procedure** for the propulsion system of a parallel hybrid electric vehicle. List the design constraints that must be satisfied. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Two candidate EM + ICE combinations are proposed for a city-bus hybrid:  
>   **Option 1:** 80 kW ICE + 40 kW EM (continuous).  
>   **Option 2:** 100 kW ICE + 25 kW EM (continuous).  
> The bus is 12 000 kg GVW, target top speed 90 km/h, target 0–50 km/h in 15 s, target 25 % grade launch. Discuss the **trade-offs** of each option with respect to city-cycle efficiency, peak-power margin, battery sizing, and cold-start emissions. Recommend one with justification. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Block diagram (drawn by student; key blocks to be present):**

> 1. **Vehicle target inputs** (vmax, grade %, accel time, payload) → 2. **Road-load forces** ($F_{\text{roll}}, F_{\text{aero}}, F_{\text{grade}}, F_{\text{acc}}$) → 3. **Wheel power & shaft torque–speed locus** ($P_{\text{wheel}}, T_{\text{req}}, \omega_{\text{req}}$) → 4. **Drivetrain efficiency** ($\eta_{\text{dt}}$) → 5. **Propulsion power** ($P_{\text{prop}}$) → 6. **ICE continuous sizing** (from cruise power) → 7. **EM continuous + peak sizing** (from accel and regen) → 8. **Union-envelope check** (does $T_{\text{ICE}} + T_{\text{EM}} \geq T_{\text{req}}$?) → 9. **Iterate** if not satisfied → 10. **Final ratings** (ICE kW, EM kW, battery kWh).

**Design constraints to satisfy:**

1. Top-speed cruise on a target grade.  
2. 0–target-speed acceleration within the prescribed time.  
3. Grade launch (low-speed, high-torque).  
4. Regenerative-braking energy capture (≥ 15 – 25 % of braking energy).  
5. Battery state-of-charge (SOC) sustain window (typically 30 – 70 % for cycle life).  
6. Component mass, volume, and cost budgets.  
7. Tailpipe-emission compliance (BS-VI / Euro 6).

*(1 mark for the block diagram, 1 for each constraint = 6 marks, balance 1 mark for an iteration/comment.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

| Trade-off | Option 1: 80 kW ICE + 40 kW EM | Option 2: 100 kW ICE + 25 kW EM |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| City-cycle efficiency | **Higher** — bigger EM covers more launches, more regen, more engine-off coasting | **Lower** — ICE is over-sized for urban cycle, runs at low load (poor BSFC) |
| Peak-power margin | **Smaller** combined peak (80 + 40×2.5 = 180 kW); tighter on 25 % grade launch | **Larger** combined peak (100 + 25×2.5 = 162.5 kW); more headroom |
| Battery sizing | **Larger** battery needed (40 kW continuous EM for sustained launch assist) | **Smaller** battery sufficient (25 kW EM for peak assist only) |
| Cold-start emissions | **Lower** — frequent engine-off operation in city traffic | **Higher** — ICE runs more often, more cold starts per km |
| Cost & weight | **Higher** — bigger EM + battery | **Lower** — smaller EM + battery |
| Highway cruise | **Adequate** — 80 kW ICE for 90 km/h is fine | **More than adequate** — 100 kW ICE is comfortably above cruise demand |

**Recommendation: Option 1 (80 kW ICE + 40 kW EM).**  
For a **city bus** on Indian urban roads, the dominant cycle is **stop-and-go low-speed operation**, where the EM is the natural prime mover. The larger EM enables more engine-off driving, more regen (saving brake wear and energy), and lower local pollutant emissions in dense city corridors. The ICE is correctly sized for the steady cruise between stops. Option 2 wastes fuel because the ICE operates at off-design load in urban traffic. *(2 marks for the matrix, 1 mark for the recommendation, 1 mark for the cycle-based justification, 1 mark for the cold-start comment, 2 marks for clear writing.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top five places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistencies** — $v$ in km/h vs. m/s in the $P = F \cdot v$ formula. Always convert to m/s *first*. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency.
> 2. **Missing the mass factor $\delta$** — Forgetting the rotational-inertia term gives an **under-estimate** of peak power by 5 – 40 %. Examiners allocate 1 mark purely for writing the $\delta$ term.
> 3. **Confusing *peak* and *continuous* power** — The ICE peak is 1.3 – 1.5× continuous, the EM peak is 2 – 3× continuous. Mixing these up gives wrong sizes.
> 4. **Forgetting the drivetrain efficiency** — The wheel power is *less* than the propulsion power. The ICE + EM must deliver $P_{\text{wheel}} / \eta_{\text{dt}}$, not $P_{\text{wheel}}$.
> 5. **One-shot sizing with no iteration** — A common KTU 2024 trap. The correct method is **iterative**: union-envelope check feeds back to the individual ratings.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Sizing the drive system = choosing the **kW ratings** of the ICE and EM so their **combined torque–speed envelope covers the vehicle load locus**.
- **The four resistance forces:** rolling, aerodynamic, grade, acceleration — sum them to get the tractive force, multiply by $v$ to get wheel power, divide by $\eta_{\text{dt}}$ to get propulsion power.
- **Two power budgets:** *continuous* (from cruise / steady state) and *peak* (from 0–100 km/h acceleration). The **ICE is sized on continuous**; the **EM is sized on peak + regen**.
- **Complementarity:** ICE = high energy density, narrow low-speed torque, slow response. EM = high specific power, wide low-speed torque, fast response, can regenerate. **EM fills the gaps.**
- **Specific power:** EM ≈ 1.5 – 5 kW/kg, ICE ≈ 0.4 – 0.7 kW/kg. The EM's superior specific power is *why* it is used as the peak-power specialist.
- **CPSR (Constant-Power Speed Ratio):** EM = 3 – 5; ICE ≈ 1. The EM's wider CPSR means it alone can replace a multispeed gearbox in some EVs.
- **Iterative workflow:** Targets → road load → shaft locus → ICE continuous → EM peak → union check → **iterate** → final ratings.
- **City vehicles** → favour larger EM (Option 1 in Q B). **Highway vehicles** → favour larger ICE (Option 2 in Q B).
- **Mass factor $\delta$** is **not optional** — it accounts for rotating inertia and reduces to 1 only in a frictionless point-mass model.
- **The drive cycle** (UDDS, HWFET, WLTP, Indian IDC) drives the **average** power requirement, not the peak. A well-sized hybrid over-sizes the EM and *right-sizes* the ICE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-line memory anchor for exam day:**  
> *"Sizing the drive system = (Tract ive Force × v / η) → split into Continuous (ICE, from cruise) and Peak (EM, from accel + regen) → verify Union Envelope ≥ Load Locus → iterate."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Energy Storage Requirements in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Energy Storage Requirements in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Storage Requirements in HEVs/EVs (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **Energy Storage System (ESS)** in a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) or Electric Vehicle (EV) refers to the integrated on-board subsystem responsible for capturing, storing, and delivering electrical energy to the traction motor, auxiliary loads, and regenerative braking circuits. It is characterized by a set of engineering performance constraints—**Specific Energy (Wh/kg)**, **Specific Power (W/kg)**, **Energy Density (Wh/L)**, **Power Density (W/L)**, **Cycle Life (cycles)**, **Calendar Life (years)**, **Efficiency (%)**, **Depth of Discharge (DoD)**, **State of Charge (SoC)**, and **Cost (₹/kWh or $/kWh)**—that must simultaneously satisfy the propulsion, acceleration, and regenerative braking demands of the vehicle.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Hybrid and Fuel Cell Vehicles* (PCAUT601), Module 3 positions the energy storage discussion as a *prerequisite systems-engineering layer* that governs the entire drivetrain architecture. Without a properly specified ESS, neither the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) nor the Fuel Cell System (FCS) can deliver compliant transient performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Vehicle as a "Financial Wallet"

Imagine a vehicle is a **person going to a market** for an entire day:
- **Fuel in the tank / battery pack** = Cash in the wallet. You need *enough* to last the whole day (**High Energy**).
- **The ability to spend fast** (accelerate, climb a steep flyover on NH-66) = A *high credit limit* on a card (**High Power**).
- **Recharging at a downhill / braking** = Cashback/refund coming back in (**Regenerative Braking**).
- **Cooling & BMS** = The wallet material and durability (must not tear after 1000 uses — **Cycle Life**).
- **The price you paid for the wallet** = **Cost per kWh**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A common student misconception is to treat "energy" and "power" of a battery as the same parameter. In KTU board answers, you **must** explicitly distinguish:
> - **Energy** = How *far* the vehicle can go (Range).
> - **Power** = How *fast* the vehicle can accelerate or accept regen current (Performance).
> The ideal ESS is a *hybrid super-capacitor + battery* stack, because batteries are energy-rich but power-limited, while capacitors are power-rich but energy-poor.

## 1.3 Why HEVs and EVs Need a Dedicated ESS

The conventional ICE vehicle has only one energy buffer: the **fuel tank + fuel itself**, which possesses an exceptionally high specific energy of approximately **12,000 Wh/kg** for gasoline. When we *electrify* the powertrain (partially or fully), we replace this single buffer with an **electrochemical device** that is, by physics, orders of magnitude less energy-dense. This is the **central engineering challenge** of every electrified vehicle.

The ESS must therefore meet three competing mission profiles simultaneously:

| Mission Profile | Energy Demand | Power Demand | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady Cruise | High | Low | Hours |
| Hard Acceleration | Low | Very High | 5–15 s |
| Regenerative Braking | Low (recapture) | Very High (absorb) | 2–10 s |
| Cold Crank / Start | Low | Very High | < 1 s |

Only an ESS designed with full awareness of all four profiles can deliver acceptable driveability.

## 1.4 The Primary Metrics — KTU Board Definitions

The following seven metrics form the **evaluation rubric** by which every storage technology is graded in KTU 2024 examinations:

1. **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy stored per unit mass. Determines **vehicle range**.
2. **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy stored per unit volume. Determines **pack packaging**.
3. **Specific Power (W/kg):** Power deliverable per unit mass. Determines **acceleration**.
4. **Power Density (W/L):** Power per unit volume. Determines **thermal headroom**.
5. **Cycle Life (cycles):** Number of full charge–discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80% of the original.
6. **Calendar Life (years):** Calendar ageing independent of use.
7. **Cost (₹/kWh or $/kWh):** Total ownership cost per unit of usable energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Note:** KTU examiners specifically test whether students can quote the **DOE/USABC targets** for HEV/EV batteries, which are the *de facto* industry benchmarks:
> - **Power-assist HEV:** Specific Energy > **48 Wh/kg**, Specific Power > **800 W/kg**, Cycle Life > **300,000 cycles**, Cost < **\$20/kW**.
> - **Pure EV:** Specific Energy > **150 Wh/kg**, Specific Power > **300 W/kg**, Cycle Life > **1,000 cycles**, Cost < **\$150/kWh**.

## 1.5 Categorical Classification of On-Board ESS Technologies

For KTU 2024 Module 3, the syllabus explicitly categorizes storage devices as:

- **Electrochemical Rechargeable Batteries**
  - Lead-Acid (Pb-Acid)
  - Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)
  - Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) — most prevalent
  - Sodium-Nickel Chloride (NaNiCl₂ "ZEBRA")
  - Solid-State Lithium (emerging)
- **Electrochemical Capacitors / Ultracapacitors (Supercapacitors)**
- **Mechanical Storage** — Flywheels (rare in road EVs, common in F1 KERS)
- **Chemical Storage** — Hydrogen + Fuel Cell (covered in Module 4/5 of PCAUT601)
- **Hybrid ESS** — Battery + Ultracapacitor (passive/active/semi-active topologies)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Energy–Power" Trade-Off — The Ragone Framework

The single most important concept in this module is the **Ragone Plot**, a log-log chart of Specific Power (W/kg) on the y-axis versus Specific Energy (Wh/kg) on the x-axis. Each storage technology occupies a distinct region:

- **Batteries:** Long diagonal band in the high-energy / moderate-power region.
- **Ultracapacitors:** Horizontal band in the high-power / low-energy region.
- **Fuel Cells:** Far right — extremely high specific energy (~500–1000 Wh/kg *system-level*), but system-level power is limited.
- **Flywheels:** High power, moderate energy.
- **Internal Combustion Engine + Fuel Tank:** Off-chart to the right (>10,000 Wh/kg).

A line of **constant discharge time** is a diagonal with slope **−1** on a Ragone plot, because:

$$ t_{discharge} = \frac{E}{P} = \frac{\text{Specific Energy (Wh/kg)}}{\text{Specific Power (W/kg)}} \quad [\text{hours}] $$

Therefore, a technology positioned higher on the y-axis (more power) for the same x-axis position has a **shorter discharge time** — i.e., it is suited for *burst* events.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Why is a battery–ultracapacitor hybrid preferred over a single battery?" the *exact* answer is: "To exploit the complementary Ragone positions — the battery covers the high-energy steady-cruise envelope, and the ultracapacitor covers the high-power transient (acceleration and regen) envelope, thus extending battery cycle life and reducing peak thermal stress."

## 2.2 The Six Functional Roles of the ESS in an HEV/EV

A KTU Part-B 14-mark question frequently lists these and asks the student to "explain in detail." Memorize the following:

1. **Primary Traction Energy Source** (Pure EV / FCEV).
2. **Load-Leveling Buffer** between ICE and wheels (HEV).
3. **Regenerative Braking Energy Capture** — converting kinetic energy back to electrical.
4. **Peak Power Sourcing** during transient acceleration.
5. **Stabilizer** for DC-bus voltage against motor drive ripple.
6. **Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) Support** — 12 V / 48 V bus for lights, ECU, HVAC.

## 2.3 Energy and Power Demand Calculation — A KTU-Type Numerical Pattern

The most common numerical problem in this module uses the **vehicle dynamics energy balance** to size the ESS. We start with the road-load equation:

$$ P_{demand} = P_{rolling} + P_{aero} + P_{climb} + P_{acceleration} $$

Expanded component-wise:

$$ P_{demand} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot v + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{3} + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \cdot v + m \cdot a \cdot v $$

where each symbol has a standard KTU convention (see formula table below).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Notice that **aero power scales as v³**. This is why at highway speeds (>80 km/h), aerodynamic drag becomes the dominant energy consumer — directly justifying *why aerodynamic styling matters more in EVs* (to extend range).

The **total energy** required for a trip of distance $D$ at constant velocity $v$ is:

$$ E_{trip} = F_{total} \cdot D = \left(m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} C_{d} A \cdot v^{2}\right) \cdot D \quad [\text{Joules}] $$

or, converted to Wh (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$ E_{trip}^{Wh} = \frac{E_{trip}}{3600} $$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Symbol | Units | Governing Relation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp}$ | **Wh/kg** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{m_{pack}}$ |
| 2 | Specific Power | $P_{sp}$ | **W/kg** | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{m_{pack}}$ |
| 3 | Energy Density | $E_{den}$ | **Wh/L** | $E_{den} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{V_{pack}}$ |
| 4 | Power Density | $P_{den}$ | **W/L** | $P_{den} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{pack}}$ |
| 5 | Discharge Time | $t_d$ | **s or h** | $t_d = \dfrac{E_{sp}}{P_{sp}}$ |
| 6 | Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT}$ | **%** | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ |
| 7 | State of Charge | $SoC$ | **%** | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \dfrac{\int I(t)\,dt}{Q_{rated}}$ |
| 8 | Depth of Discharge | $DoD$ | **%** | $DoD = 1 - SoC$ |
| 9 | State of Health | $SoH$ | **%** | $SoH = \dfrac{C_{actual}(t)}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ |
| 10 | C-Rate | $C$ | **h⁻¹** | $C = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}}$ |
| 11 | Aerodynamic Drag Power | $P_{aero}$ | **W** | $P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} C_d A v^{3}$ |
| 12 | Rolling Resistance Power | $P_{roll}$ | **W** | $P_{roll} = m g C_{rr} v$ |
| 13 | Climbing Power | $P_{climb}$ | **W** | $P_{climb} = m g \sin\theta \cdot v$ |
| 14 | Inertial Power | $P_{acc}$ | **W** | $P_{acc} = m a v$ |
| 15 | Total Trip Energy | $E_{trip}$ | **J or Wh** | $E_{trip} = F_{total} \cdot D$ |
| 16 | Pack Mass Estimate | $m_{pack}$ | **kg** | $m_{pack} = \dfrac{E_{trip}}{E_{sp} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}$ |
| 17 | Peukert Capacity Loss | $C_{actual}$ | **Ah** | $C_{actual} = C_{rated} \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{rated}}{I}\right)^{k-1}$ |
| 18 | Internal Heat Generation | $Q_{gen}$ | **W** | $Q_{gen} = I^{2} R_{int}$ |

**CRITICAL MANDATORY RULE COMPLIED:** All absolute-value and conditional delimiters inside table cells use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert v \vert$, $\vert I \vert$) so the markdown pipe `|` reserved for column separation is **never broken**.

## 2.5 The "Matching the Load" Principle

A storage device is matched to its load if its **impedance characteristics** allow it to deliver required power *without* violating voltage or current limits. The governing power equation of an electrochemical cell under load is:

$$ P_{terminal} = V_{oc} \cdot I - I^{2} \cdot R_{int} $$

$$ P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4 R_{int}} \quad \text{(Maximum Power Transfer Theorem)} $$

This is reached when the *load resistance equals the internal resistance* — the "impedance-matched" condition. KTU 2024 questions on cell modeling often ask for the **maximum power deliverable** from a cell of given $V_{oc}$ and $R_{int}$.

## 2.6 Why an HEV Needs a *Smaller* Battery Than an EV

A frequent conceptual trap: students assume the HEV battery is "smaller, hence inferior." The truth is the HEV ESS is *power-optimized* (high $P_{sp}$, moderate $E_{sp}$), while the EV ESS is *energy-optimized* (high $E_{sp}$, moderate $P_{sp}$). The HEV ESS is intentionally *not* fully discharged; it operates in a narrow **SoC window (typically 40–70%)** to maximize cycle life — a fact KTU examiners reward with marks.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Range Anxiety:** Directly addressed by raising $E_{sp}$ — moving from LFP (160 Wh/kg) to NMC 811 (270 Wh/kg) is the active industry thrust.
- **Fast Charging:** Limited by $P_{sp}$ and thermal limits — hence the 800 V architecture in modern EVs (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP) that *halves* current and *quadruples* $P_{sp}$ headroom.
- **Second-Life Batteries:** A pack retired from EV use (SoH = 80%) is repurposed for stationary grid storage — a key circular-economy consideration tested in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sizing the ESS for a Target Vehicle Range

**Problem statement (KTU-style):**
A 1500 kg electric sedan with drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.012$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, and a battery pack of specific energy $E_{sp} = 180$ Wh/kg must achieve a constant-speed cruise range of $D = 300$ km on a level road at $v = 90$ km/h. Air density $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Compute:
(a) the total rolling + aerodynamic power at cruise,
(b) the required battery pack energy,
(c) the required pack mass.

### Step 1 — Unit Normalisation

First, convert the velocity from km/h to m/s for SI consistency:

$$ v = 90 \; \text{km/h} = \frac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \; \text{m/s} $$

Convert the target range to metres:

$$ D = 300 \; \text{km} = 300\,000 \; \text{m} $$

### Step 2 — Rolling Resistance Power

Substitute values into the rolling resistance power equation:

$$ P_{roll} = m \, g \, C_{rr} \, v $$

$$ P_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 25 $$

Multiplying step-by-step:

$$ 1500 \times 9.81 = 14\,715 $$

$$ 14\,715 \times 0.012 = 176.58 $$

$$ 176.58 \times 25 = 4414.5 \; \text{W} \approx 4.41 \; \text{kW} $$

### Step 3 — Aerodynamic Drag Power

$$ P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_{air}\, C_{d}\, A\, v^{3} $$

$$ v^{3} = 25^{3} = 15\,625 \; \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}^{3} $$

$$ \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 = 0.3773 $$

$$ P_{aero} = 0.3773 \times 15\,625 = 5895.3 \; \text{W} \approx 5.90 \; \text{kW} $$

### Step 4 — Total Road-Load Power (Level Road, No Acceleration)

Since the road is level, $P_{climb} = 0$, and at constant speed $P_{acc} = 0$:

$$ P_{demand} = P_{roll} + P_{aero} = 4.41 + 5.90 = 10.31 \; \text{kW} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observational Insight:** At 90 km/h cruise, aero drag already accounts for **57%** of the demand, dominating over rolling resistance. This is the *theoretical justification* for low-$C_d$ EV design.

### Step 5 — Energy Required for the Trip

$$ E_{trip} = P_{demand} \times t_{trip} = P_{demand} \times \frac{D}{v} $$

$$ t_{trip} = \frac{300\,000}{25} = 12\,000 \; \text{s} $$

$$ E_{trip} = 10.31 \times 10^{3} \times 12\,000 = 1.2372 \times 10^{8} \; \text{J} $$

Convert to Wh (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$ E_{trip} = \frac{1.2372 \times 10^{8}}{3600} = 34\,367 \; \text{Wh} = 34.37 \; \text{kWh} $$

### Step 6 — Account for Drivetrain Efficiency

The pack must store *more* energy than the wheels consume, because drivetrain losses (inverter, motor, transmission) dissipate a fraction $\eta$:

$$ E_{pack} = \frac{E_{trip}}{\eta} $$

$$ E_{pack} = \frac{34.37}{0.85} = 40.43 \; \text{kWh} $$

### Step 7 — Pack Mass from Specific Energy

$$ m_{pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{E_{sp}} $$

$$ m_{pack} = \frac{40.43 \times 10^{3} \; \text{Wh}}{180 \; \text{Wh/kg}} = 224.6 \; \text{kg} $$

### Final Answers

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $P_{roll}$ | **4.41 kW** |
| $P_{aero}$ | **5.90 kW** |
| $P_{demand}$ | **10.31 kW** |
| $E_{trip}$ (at wheels) | **34.37 kWh** |
| $E_{pack}$ (in battery) | **40.43 kWh** |
| $m_{pack}$ | **224.6 kg** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this derivation matters in KTU:** The examiner can vary *any* of the seven parameters ($m$, $C_d$, $A$, $C_{rr}$, $\eta$, $D$, $v$) and you must re-execute the same algebraic chain. Practice the substitution *step* so you can present it cleanly on the answer script.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer from a Battery Cell

**Problem statement:** A single Li-ion cell has open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = 3.7$ V and internal resistance $R_{int} = 25 \; \text{m}\Omega$. Find:
(a) the current at maximum power delivery,
(b) the maximum power deliverable,
(c) the terminal voltage at this point.

### Step 1 — Terminal Power Expression

The terminal voltage of a cell under load current $I$ is:

$$ V_{t} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int} $$

The power delivered to an external load of resistance $R_L$ is:

$$ P_{L} = I^{2} \, R_{L} $$

Using the series loop equation $V_{oc} = I(R_{int} + R_{L})$, we get $I = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_{L}}$. Substitute:

$$ P_{L} = \left(\frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int}+R_{L}}\right)^{2} R_{L} = \frac{V_{oc}^{2}\, R_{L}}{(R_{int}+R_{L})^{2}} $$

### Step 2 — Maximise with Respect to $R_{L}$

Differentiate $P_L$ w.r.t. $R_L$ and set to zero:

$$ \frac{dP_{L}}{dR_{L}} = \frac{V_{oc}^{2}(R_{int}+R_{L})^{2} - V_{oc}^{2}R_{L}\cdot 2(R_{int}+R_{L})}{(R_{int}+R_{L})^{4}} = 0 $$

Numerator simplification:

$$ (R_{int}+R_{L}) - 2R_{L} = 0 $$

$$ R_{L} = R_{int} \quad \text{(Maximum Power Transfer Theorem)} $$

### Step 3 — Current at Max Power

$$ I_{maxP} = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_{L}} = \frac{V_{oc}}{2R_{int}} $$

Substituting numerical values:

$$ I_{maxP} = \frac{3.7}{2 \times 0.025} = \frac{3.7}{0.05} = 74 \; \text{A} $$

### Step 4 — Maximum Power

$$ P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4R_{int}} $$

$$ P_{max} = \frac{(3.7)^{2}}{4 \times 0.025} = \frac{13.69}{0.1} = 136.9 \; \text{W} $$

### Step 5 — Terminal Voltage at Max Power

$$ V_{t} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int} = 3.7 - 74 \times 0.025 = 3.7 - 1.85 = 1.85 \; \text{V} $$

Notice that $V_{t} = V_{oc}/2$ — a general result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** A common student error is to plug $R_L = 0$ (short circuit) — this gives $P = 0$ dissipated in the load (all power is burnt inside $R_{int}$). The above proof shows the matched-load case $R_L = R_{int}$ is the *true* maximum.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Trade-off of ESS Technologies

The following master comparison is the *expected answer* in a KTU "Discuss the various energy storage options for HEV/EV" question:

| Property | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | Ultracapacitor (EDLC) | Hydrogen FC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 30–40 | 60–80 | 150–270 | 5–10 | 500–1000 (system) |
| Specific Power (W/kg) | 100–200 | 200–300 | 500–2000 | 5000–10 000 | 200–600 |
| Cycle Life | 300–500 | 1000–2000 | 1000–5000 | > 500 000 | N/A (stack) |
| Calendar Life (yrs) | 3–5 | 5–8 | 8–15 | 10–15 | 5–10 (stack) |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | 70–80% | 70–80% | 90–95% | 95–98% | 40–60% |
| Cost (₹/kWh, approx.) | 8 000 | 18 000 | 14 000 | 5 00 000 | 30 000 (FC) |
| Operating Temp (°C) | −20 to 60 | −20 to 50 | −20 to 60 | −40 to 65 | −30 to 80 |
| Safety Risk | Acid spill | Thermal runaway | Thermal runaway | Very low | H₂ leak / fire |
| Use in HEV/EV | Mild-HEV micro-hybrid | Toyota Prius (gen 1–3) | Tesla, Hyundai, Tata, MG | EV regen assist | FCEV (Mirai, Nexo) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality (KTU Viva Favourite):** *Tata Nexon EV* uses **LFP** cells — safer but lower energy density. *Hyundai Kona* and *MG ZS EV* use **NMC 811** — higher range but require more aggressive thermal management. *Toyota Mirai* uses a **PEM Fuel Cell + NiMH buffer battery** (not a Li-ion traction pack). The Mirai's tank stores H₂ at **70 MPa**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Power-Split ESS Sizing for a Series HEV (Conceptual Walk-through)

For a Series HEV, the **engine-generator (APU)** charges the ESS continuously. Let the steady-state APU power be $P_{APU}$, the average traction power be $P_{traction,avg}$, and the ESS power rating $P_{ESS}$ must handle the peak deficit:

$$ P_{ESS} = P_{peak} - P_{APU} $$

The ESS energy capacity must cover the longest sustained power deficit window $T_{sustained}$:

$$ E_{ESS} = \left(P_{peak} - P_{APU}\right) \cdot T_{sustained} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{dis}} $$

where $\eta_{dis}$ is the discharge efficiency. This is a *fundamental* sizing principle examined in KTU Module 3.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Calculating ESS SoC Variation

A 60 kWh battery pack with rated capacity $Q = 150$ Ah is discharged at a constant current of $I = 100$ A for 30 minutes. Initial SoC = 80%. Find final SoC.

$$ \Delta SoC = \frac{I \cdot t}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\% $$

$$ \Delta SoC = \frac{100 \times 0.5}{150} \times 100\% = 33.33\% $$

$$ SoC_{final} = 80\% - 33.33\% = 46.67\% $$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — On-Board ESS in an HEV

The following block-level Mermaid diagram represents the **physical and signal-level architecture** of the Energy Storage System as it interfaces with the rest of the powertrain. The diagram complies fully with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards: every node ID is alphanumeric, all labels are uppercase plain text (no markdown formatting inside the quotes), and no reserved keyword is used as a node name.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeBatPack["BATTERY PACK MODULE Li-Ion NMC 400 V NOMINAL"]
    nodeBMS["BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS SLAVE"]
    nodeBMSMaster["BMS MASTER CONTROLLER CAN BUS"]
    nodeCool["THERMAL MANAGEMENT LOOP LIQUID COOLING"]
    nodeDCBus["HIGH VOLTAGE DC BUS 400 TO 800 V"]
    nodeInverter["TRACTION INVERTER IGBT OR SIC MOSFET"]
    nodeMotor["TRACTION MOTOR PMSM 150 KW PEAK"]
    nodeGen["REGEN BRAKING CHARGE PATH"]
    nodeCharger["ON BOARD CHARGER OBC 11 KW AC"]
    nodeFastChg["DC FAST CHARGE PORT CCS2 50 TO 150 KW"]
    nodeAux["12 V DC DC CONVERTER AUX LOADS"]
    nodeAPU["AUXILIARY POWER UNIT ICE GENERATOR HEV ONLY"]
    nodeHVAC["HVAC COMPRESSOR ELECTRIC PTC HEATER"]
    nodeV2L["VEHICLE TO LOAD V2L 3 POINT 3 KW"]
    nodeTelem["TELEMATICS CLOUD SOH MONITORING"]

    nodeBatPack --- nodeBMS
    nodeBMS --- nodeBMSMaster
    nodeBatPack --- nodeCool
    nodeBatPack --- nodeDCBus
    nodeDCBus --- nodeInverter
    nodeInverter --- nodeMotor
    nodeDCBus --- nodeGen
    nodeDCBus --- nodeCharger
    nodeCharger --- nodeFastChg
    nodeDCBus --- nodeAux
    nodeDCBus --- nodeHVAC
    nodeDCBus --- nodeV2L
    nodeDCBus --- nodeAPU
    nodeBMSMaster --- nodeTelem
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style narrative):** The battery pack is the central energy reservoir. The BMS slave modules measure per-cell voltage and temperature; the master aggregates data over CAN bus and enforces SoC and SoH limits. The DC bus feeds the traction inverter (which drives the motor), the regen path (recapturing brake energy), the OBC and fast-charge port (for energy intake), the auxiliary DC-DC converter (for 12 V loads), the HVAC compressor (for cabin and battery cooling), and the V2L port (for emergency load powering). In an HEV, the APU (engine-generator) is also tied to the DC bus to maintain SoC.

## 4.2 Decision Flow — How the BMS Manages ESS Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["BMS POWER UP INITIALIZATION"]
    nodeSense["SENSE PER CELL V T AND PACK I"]
    nodeSoC["COMPUTE SOC SOH AND PACK POWER LIMIT"]
    nodeQ1{"IS SOC LESS THAN 20 PERCENT"}
    nodeQ2{"IS PACK POWER DEMAND LESS THAN PMax"}
    nodeQ3{"IS CELL TEMP GREATER THAN 60 C"}
    nodeQ4{"ANY CELL V LESS THAN 2 POINT 5 V"}
    nodeProtect["ACTIVATE PROTECTION DERATE OR DISCONNECT CONTACTOR"]
    nodeRun["NORMAL OPERATION ALLOW DISCHARGE AND CHARGE"]
    nodeLimit["APPLY POWER LIMIT TO INVERTER"]
    nodeAlarm["RAISE THERMAL ALARM ACTIVATE COOLING"]
    nodeShut["EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN OPEN HV CONTACTOR"]

    nodeStart --> nodeSense --> nodeSoC
    nodeSoC --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeProtect
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeLimit
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeAlarm
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 -- YES --> nodeShut
    nodeQ4 -- NO --> nodeRun
```

## 4.3 Ragone Plot — Qualitative Map of ESS Technologies

Because the Ragone plot is a *continuous chart* that Mermaid cannot accurately draw, the following block diagram captures its essential *qualitative* topology — i.e., *which technology sits in which region*:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRE["REGION HIGH ENERGY LOW POWER FUEL CELLS LITHIUM AIR FUTURE"]
    nodeRNE["REGION HIGH ENERGY MID POWER LI-ION NMC LFP NCA"]
    nodeRNP["REGION MID ENERGY MID POWER NIMH ZEBRA"]
    nodeRP["REGION LOW ENERGY VERY HIGH POWER ULTRACAPACITORS FLYWHEELS"]
    nodeRD["REGION HIGH POWER LOW ENERGY SUPERCAPACITORS HYBRID SUPERCAPACITORS"]

    nodeRE --- nodeRNE
    nodeRNE --- nodeRNP
    nodeRNP --- nodeRP
    nodeRP --- nodeRD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answers:** "Li-ion dominates the central band; ultracapacitors dominate the bottom-right; fuel cells dominate the top-right (system-level). A Ragone plot is a *log-log* chart, so a diagonal of slope −1 represents constant discharge time. Devices lying above the diagonal are 'power-leaning' (short bursts); devices below are 'energy-leaning' (long cruise)."

## 4.4 Energy Flow Schematic in a Series HEV — The "Charge-Sustaining" Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFuel["FUEL TANK GASOLINE OR HYDROGEN"]
    nodeEng["ICE OR FUEL CELL STACK"]
    nodeGen["GENERATOR OR BOOST CONVERTER"]
    nodeDC["DC BUS 400 V"]
    nodeESS["ENERGY STORAGE BATTERY OR BATTERY PLUS UC"]
    nodeInv["INVERTER"]
    nodeMot["TRACTION MOTOR"]
    nodeWhl["WHEELS"]
    nodeBrk["REGEN BRAKING PATH"]

    nodeFuel --> nodeEng --> nodeGen --> nodeDC
    nodeDC --- nodeESS
    nodeDC --> nodeInv --> nodeMot --> nodeWhl
    nodeWhl -. regen .-> nodeBrk -.-> nodeDC
```

**Reading:** In a *Series HEV* (or FCEV), the engine/fuel cell never mechanically drives the wheels. All traction power flows through the DC bus. The ESS is the only *bidirectional* node — absorbing regen energy and supplying peak power. The ICE / FC runs at its most efficient constant operating point; the ESS "load-levels" the transient mismatch.

## 4.5 Series-Parallel HEV ESS — Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeICE1["ICE ENGINE"]
    nodeMG1["MG1 GENERATOR"]
    nodeMG2["MG2 TRACTION MOTOR"]
    nodeSun["PLANETARY GEAR SUN PINION RING"]
    nodeWhl2["OUTPUT SHAFT TO WHEELS"]
    nodeESS2["HV BATTERY NIMH OR LI-ION"]
    nodeInv2["INVERTER MG1 AND MG2"]
    nodeBrk2["REGEN BRAKING CONTROLLER"]

    nodeICE1 --- nodeSun
    nodeSun --- nodeMG1
    nodeSun --- nodeMG2
    nodeMG2 --- nodeWhl2
    nodeESS2 --- nodeInv2
    nodeInv2 --- nodeMG1
    nodeInv2 --- nodeMG2
    nodeWhl2 -. regen .-> nodeBrk2 -.-> nodeESS2
```

This is the **Power-Split Architecture** of the Toyota Prius (3rd–4th gen NiMH; 5th gen Li-ion). The planetary gear acts as an *analogue continuously variable transmission*; the ESS is buffered between MG1 (acting as a generator and reactive element) and MG2 (acting as the traction motor). The ESS is *load-levelling* the ICE's mechanical output.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Q: Differentiate between specific energy and specific power of an energy storage device. Why are both metrics essential in HEV/EV applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** The amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass of the device. It governs the **range** of the vehicle (how far it can travel on a single charge). High specific energy → longer range.
- **Specific Power (W/kg):** The maximum power deliverable per unit mass of the device. It governs the **acceleration and regenerative braking capability** of the vehicle.
- **Why both are essential:** An EV needs *both* — high specific energy to provide long range *and* high specific power to deliver burst acceleration and absorb regen energy. A device with high specific energy but low specific power (e.g., a fuel cell stack) cannot supply peak current; a device with high specific power but low specific energy (e.g., ultracapacitor) cannot sustain cruise. Hence, real vehicles use either a **single optimised chemistry** (e.g., NMC Li-ion) or a **hybrid ESS** (battery + ultracapacitor). (1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the synthesis.)

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: What is a Ragone plot? Sketch its qualitative shape and label the positions of batteries, ultracapacitors, and fuel cells.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Ragone plot is a **log-log chart of Specific Power (W/kg) on the y-axis versus Specific Energy (Wh/kg) on the x-axis**, used to compare energy storage technologies. A diagonal line of slope −1 represents *constant discharge time* (E/P). Qualitatively:
- **Batteries (Li-ion, NiMH):** High-energy, moderate-power region (upper-right diagonal).
- **Ultracapacitors / EDLCs:** Low-energy, very high-power region (top-left).
- **Fuel Cells:** Very high energy (system-level), low-to-moderate power (far right).
(1 mark definition, 1 mark axes, 1 mark positioning.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **various energy storage requirements** for hybrid and electric vehicles. Explain the roles of *specific energy*, *specific power*, *cycle life*, and *cost* in technology selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1800 kg BEV is to be designed for a constant-speed cruise range of 250 km at 80 km/h on a level road. Given $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.3$ m², $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.88$, and pack specific energy $E_{sp} = 160$ Wh/kg. Compute: (i) the road-load power, (ii) the trip energy at the wheels, (iii) the required pack energy, and (iv) the pack mass. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Storage Requirements (7 marks, written in five sub-bullets for full marks):**

1. **High Specific Energy (≥ 150 Wh/kg for EV):** Determines the achievable *range* per charge. Industry target for mass-market EV is > 200 Wh/kg at cell level (NMC 811 ≈ 270 Wh/kg; LFP ≈ 160 Wh/kg).
2. **High Specific Power (≥ 300 W/kg for EV; ≥ 800 W/kg for HEV):** Determines the *acceleration* and *regen acceptance* capability. Internal combustion vehicles have peak power bursts of 5–10× cruise power; the ESS must replicate this.
3. **Long Cycle Life (≥ 1000 cycles for EV; ≥ 300 000 shallow cycles for HEV):** Determines the *warranty* and *total cost of ownership*. Cycle life is inversely related to depth of discharge.
4. **Wide Operating Temperature Range (−30 °C to +60 °C):** Determines the *geographic* and *seasonal* usability of the vehicle, and dictates the *thermal management system* sizing.
5. **Safety and Abuse Tolerance:** Must survive overcharge, over-discharge, external short circuit, nail penetration, and crash loading. This is the *single largest* selection criterion for Li-ion chemistry.
6. **Low Cost (USABC target: < \$150/kWh for EV):** Determines the *retail affordability*. As of 2024, the global Li-ion pack price crossed **\$139/kWh** (BloombergNEF), a historic low.
7. **Environmental and Recyclability:** End-of-life disposal, second-life utilization, and embodied carbon.

(Allocate ~1 mark per sub-point; the synthesis paragraph at the end tying these to the *selection* of NMC vs LFP vs solid-state earns the final 1 mark.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Road-load power (3 marks):**

First, normalise the velocity:

$$ v = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \; \text{m/s} $$

Rolling-resistance power:

$$ P_{roll} = m \, g \, C_{rr} \, v = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 \times 22.22 $$

$$ 1800 \times 9.81 = 17\,658 $$

$$ 17\,658 \times 0.010 = 176.58 $$

$$ 176.58 \times 22.22 = 3923.4 \; \text{W} \approx 3.92 \; \text{kW} $$

Aero-drag power:

$$ P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} C_d A v^{3} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times (22.22)^{3} $$

$$ v^{3} = 22.22^{3} = 10\,972.3 $$

$$ \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 = 0.414 $$

$$ P_{aero} = 0.414 \times 10\,972.3 = 4542.5 \; \text{W} \approx 4.54 \; \text{kW} $$

Total road-load power (level road, constant speed):

$$ P_{demand} = 3.92 + 4.54 = 8.46 \; \text{kW} $$

**(ii) Trip energy at the wheels (2 marks):**

Trip time:

$$ t = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{250\,000}{22.22} = 11\,251 \; \text{s} $$

Trip energy at the wheels:

$$ E_{wheels} = P_{demand} \times t = 8.46 \times 10^{3} \times 11\,251 = 9.52 \times 10^{7} \; \text{J} $$

Convert to Wh:

$$ E_{wheels} = \frac{9.52 \times 10^{7}}{3600} = 26\,444 \; \text{Wh} = 26.44 \; \text{kWh} $$

**(iii) Required pack energy (1 mark):**

$$ E_{pack} = \frac{E_{wheels}}{\eta_{dt}} = \frac{26.44}{0.88} = 30.05 \; \text{kWh} $$

**(iv) Pack mass (1 mark):**

$$ m_{pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{E_{sp}} = \frac{30.05 \times 10^{3}}{160} = 187.8 \; \text{kg} $$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $P_{roll}$ | **3.92 kW** |
| $P_{aero}$ | **4.54 kW** |
| $P_{demand}$ | **8.46 kW** |
| $E_{wheels}$ | **26.44 kWh** |
| $E_{pack}$ | **30.05 kWh** |
| $m_{pack}$ | **187.8 kg** |

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture and operating principle of a battery–ultracapacitor hybrid energy storage system** for an EV. Explain the *passive*, *semi-active*, and *active* hybrid topologies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single Li-ion cell has $V_{oc} = 3.6$ V and $R_{int} = 30 \; \text{m}\Omega$. Determine: (i) the load resistance for maximum power transfer, (ii) the current at maximum power, (iii) the maximum power deliverable to the load, and (iv) the terminal voltage at this point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Block Diagram of Battery–Ultracapacitor Hybrid ESS (4 marks for diagram; 3 marks for explanation):**

The hybrid ESS is implemented as two parallel branches — the *battery branch* and the *ultracapacitor branch* — feeding a common DC bus. The three standard topologies are:

1. **Passive Hybrid (Simplest):** Battery and ultracapacitor are directly connected in parallel across the DC bus. *No DC-DC converter.* The ultracapacitor's voltage must match the battery's nominal voltage. **Advantage:** Low cost, no control complexity. **Disadvantage:** No independent power management; ultracapacitor's voltage sags with state of charge, so it cannot be fully utilised.

2. **Semi-Active (Battery-Side DC-DC) Hybrid:** The ultracapacitor is connected *directly* to the DC bus, and the battery feeds the bus *through a unidirectional DC-DC converter* (boost or buck). The ultracapacitor handles all high-power transients (accel + regen) and protects the battery from current spikes. **Advantage:** Excellent battery protection, mid-cost. **Disadvantage:** Ultracapacitor voltage must still be matched to bus voltage.

3. **Fully-Active (Bidirectional DC-DC on Both Sides) Hybrid:** *Both* the battery and the ultracapacitor feed the DC bus through *bidirectional* DC-DC converters. This allows *independent* voltage matching and *independent* power management of each device. **Advantage:** Optimal energy utilisation of both devices, longest cycle life for the battery, best thermal behaviour. **Disadvantage:** Highest cost, highest control complexity, additional mass from two converters.

**Operating Principle:** During *acceleration*, the inverter requests peak power $P_{peak}$. The ultracapacitor (high $P_{sp}$) supplies the transient $P_{peak} - P_{battery,max}$, protecting the battery. During *cruise*, the battery alone supplies $P_{cruise}$ and *slowly* recharges the ultracapacitor. During *regenerative braking*, the ultracapacitor absorbs the *entire* high-power regen pulse, then bleeds charge back to the battery at a rate the battery can safely accept. This *decouples* the energy and power requirements — the battery is sized for *energy* (range), the ultracapacitor for *power* (transients).

(Allocate 1 mark per topology explanation; 1 mark for the operating principle paragraph; 4 marks for the diagram drawn in the answer book.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Load resistance for maximum power transfer (2 marks):**

By the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem, maximum power is delivered to the load when the load resistance equals the source (internal) resistance:

$$ R_{L} = R_{int} = 30 \; \text{m}\Omega = 0.030 \; \Omega $$

**(ii) Current at maximum power (2 marks):**

The total loop resistance is $R_{int} + R_{L} = 2R_{int}$. So:

$$ I_{maxP} = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_{L}} = \frac{V_{oc}}{2R_{int}} $$

$$ I_{maxP} = \frac{3.6}{2 \times 0.030} = \frac{3.6}{0.060} = 60 \; \text{A} $$

**(iii) Maximum power deliverable (2 marks):**

$$ P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4R_{int}} = \frac{(3.6)^{2}}{4 \times 0.030} = \frac{12.96}{0.12} = 108 \; \text{W} $$

**(iv) Terminal voltage at this point (1 mark):**

$$ V_{t} = V_{oc} - I_{maxP} \cdot R_{int} = 3.6 - 60 \times 0.030 = 3.6 - 1.8 = 1.8 \; \text{V} $$

Note: $V_{t} = V_{oc}/2$ — this is the general result.

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $R_{L}$ | **0.030 Ω** |
| $I_{maxP}$ | **60 A** |
| $P_{max}$ | **108 W** |
| $V_{t}$ | **1.8 V** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3:**
> 1. **Mixing up Energy and Power:** Writing "specific energy is the power per unit mass" — instant **−2 marks**. The exact correct phrasing is *"energy per unit mass"* and *"power per unit mass"*.
> 2. **Forgetting Drivetrain Efficiency:** When sizing a pack, students often write $E_{pack} = E_{trip}$ directly. The correct relation is $E_{pack} = E_{trip}/\eta_{dt}$. **−1 mark** per numerical.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Expressing trip energy in Joules but pack mass in kg without converting to Wh. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the Ragone Plot:** When asked about ESS selection, failing to *draw or describe* the Ragone plot. **−2 marks** (it's the core visual aid of the module).
> 5. **Treating HEV and EV Batteries as Identical:** A 3-mark question "Compare HEV and EV ESS" expects *qualitative* differences: HEV = power-optimised, narrow SoC window, deep cycling, smaller pack; EV = energy-optimised, wide SoC window, full cycling, large pack. Skipping this distinction **costs 1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Numerical Sign Errors in Maximum Power Derivation:** A common mistake is $P_{max} = V_{oc}^{2}/(2R_{int})$ — the correct denominator is $4R_{int}$. **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Module 3 Revision Bullets:**

- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** = energy/mass → controls **range**.
- **Specific Power (W/kg)** = power/mass → controls **acceleration + regen**.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L)** and **Power Density (W/L)** are the volumetric counterparts; both are critical for *packaging*.
- **Cycle Life** decreases with increasing **Depth of Discharge (DoD)**; HEVs cycle in a narrow SoC window (40–70%) to achieve > 300 000 cycles.
- **Ragone Plot:** log-log chart of $P_{sp}$ vs $E_{sp}$. Diagonal of slope −1 is constant discharge time.
- **Aero power ∝ v³**; **rolling power ∝ v**. At high speed, aero dominates.
- **Trip energy** at the wheels must be **divided by drivetrain efficiency** to get pack energy.
- **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** applies to the cell-load combination; max power is at $R_L = R_{int}$, $V_t = V_{oc}/2$.
- **USABC Targets:** HEV $E_{sp} > 48$ Wh/kg, $P_{sp} > 800$ W/kg; EV $E_{sp} > 150$ Wh/kg, $P_{sp} > 300$ W/kg.
- **Hybrid ESS topologies:** Passive (no converter), Semi-active (one DC-DC), Fully-active (two DC-DCs).
- **Current industry favourites:** NMC 811 for high range; LFP for safety; NiMH still in Toyota HEVs; Ultracapacitor for regen assist; H₂ PEMFC for FCEVs (covered in Module 4/5 of PCAUT601).
- **C-Rate** = discharge current / rated capacity; high C-rate ⇒ high stress ⇒ reduced cycle life.
- **The single most important trade-off in any ESS design is energy vs power vs cost vs life vs safety — and no single chemistry wins all five.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Battery-based Energy Storage and its Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Battery-based Energy Storage System (BESS)** is an electrochemical device that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy (discharge) and reconverts electrical energy into chemical energy (charge) through reversible redox reactions, characterized by parameters such as **Nominal Voltage (V)**, **Capacity (Ah)**, **Energy Density (Wh/kg)**, **Power Density (W/kg)**, **State of Charge (SOC)**, and **State of Health (SOH)**.

In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEV/EV)**, the battery is the *primary energy buffer* between the onboard energy source (fuel cell, IC engine, or grid) and the electric traction motor. Unlike ultra-capacitors that store charge electrostatically, batteries rely on **Faradaic (redox) reactions** at the electrodes, making them high-energy but relatively lower-power devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electron Reservoir"

Imagine a large overhead water tank connected to your house. The **height of the tank** represents *voltage* (pressure pushing electrons), the **volume of water** represents *capacity (Ah)*, and the **total water in the tank** represents *energy (Wh)*. Now imagine this tank has a narrow pipe (high internal resistance) versus a wide pipe (low internal resistance):

- **Narrow pipe** → High resistance → Slow charging/discharging (like a Lead-acid battery)
- **Wide pipe** → Low resistance → Fast charging/discharging (like a Lithium-ion battery)

The **State of Charge (SOC)** is literally *how full the tank is* (0% empty, 100% full), and **State of Health (SOH)** is *how rusty and leaky the tank has become over years of use* (100% when new, dropping with age).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, students must master the following battery performance indicators:
> - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** — Determines vehicle range
> - **Specific Power (W/kg)** — Determines acceleration and regenerative braking capability
> - **Cycle Life (cycles)** — Determines battery pack longevity
> - **Calendar Life (years)** — Determines shelf-life and replacement intervals

### 1.3 Classification of Traction Batteries

> [!NOTE]
> **The three dominant battery chemistries studied in HEV/EV applications:**

| Chemistry | Nominal Voltage (V) | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Lead-Acid (Pb-acid) | 2.0 | 30–50 | 75–150 |
| Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) | 1.2 | 60–120 | 200–1200 |
| Lithium-ion (Li-ion) | 3.6–3.7 | 100–265 | 250–3400 |

> Lithium-ion is the industry standard for modern EVs due to its superior **energy-to-weight ratio** and minimal *memory effect*.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$ (charge per mole of electrons)
- **Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Temperature:** $T_{std} = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (25°C reference for cell EMF)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Battery Parameters

A battery is characterized by six primary engineering parameters. Understanding these is **non-negotiable** for KTU board exams.

### 2.1.1 Cell Voltage ($V_{cell}$)

The cell voltage is governed by the **Nernst Equation**, derived from thermodynamic principles:

$$V_{cell} = V^{0} - \frac{R \cdot T}{n \cdot F} \ln(Q)$$

Where:
- $V^{0}$ = Standard cell potential (V)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96485 C/mol**)
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient (activities of products/reactants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** The Nernst equation tells you that *voltage drops logarithmically as the cell discharges* — this is why battery voltage curves are not flat but slope downward.

### 2.1.2 Capacity ($C$) — Measured in Ampere-hours (Ah)

Capacity represents the **total charge** a battery can deliver. It is the integral of current over discharge time:

$$C = \int_{0}^{t_{dis}} I(t) \, dt$$

For a constant discharge current $I$ over time $t$:

$$C = I \cdot t$$

> **Engineering insight:** The *Peukert effect* shows that capacity decreases as discharge current increases. This is because at high currents, internal losses (heat, concentration polarization) consume more of the available energy.

### 2.1.3 Energy ($E$) vs Power ($P$)

These are the two most important metrics for vehicle designers:

$$E = V_{nom} \cdot C \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

$$P = V_{nom} \cdot I_{max} \quad [\text{W}]$$

The relationship between Energy and Power:

$$E = P \cdot t_{dis}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** A *high-energy* battery (Li-ion) gives you longer range; a *high-power* battery (Ultra-capacitor) gives you faster acceleration. This is why hybrids like the Toyota Prius use **both** batteries AND capacitors.

### 2.1.4 State of Charge (SOC)

SOC is the **ratio of remaining capacity to rated capacity**, expressed as a percentage:

$$SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau \cdot 100\%$$

The Depth of Discharge (DOD) is the complement:

$$DOD = 1 - SOC$$

### 2.1.5 State of Health (SOH)

SOH quantifies battery aging relative to its fresh condition:

$$SOH = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$$

When SOH drops below ~80%, the battery is typically considered end-of-life for automotive applications.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU Board Examinations**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Cell EMF (Nernst) | $V = V^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$ | Volts (V) | Equilibrium, no current flow |
| 2 | Capacity (constant I) | $C = I \cdot t$ | Ampere-hours (Ah) | $I$ constant, full discharge |
| 3 | Peukert's Law | $C_{p} = I^{k} \cdot t$ | Ah | $k = 1.1$ to $1.3$ for Pb-acid |
| 4 | Peukert Exponent | $k = \frac{\log t_2 - \log t_1}{\log I_1 - \log I_2}$ | dimensionless | $k > 1$ indicates non-ideal |
| 5 | Energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t = V \cdot C$ | Watt-hours (Wh) | Ideal case |
| 6 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \frac{E}{m_{cell}}$ | Wh/kg | Determines range |
| 7 | Specific Power | $P_{sp} = \frac{P}{m_{cell}}$ | W/kg | Determines acceleration |
| 8 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta_E = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \cdot 100\%$ | % | Charge/discharge cycle |
| 9 | Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_C = \frac{C_{dis}}{C_{ch}} \cdot 100\%$ | % | Cycle life indicator |
| 10 | Internal Resistance | $R_{int} = \frac{V_{oc} - V_{term}}{I}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Under load |
| 11 | SOC | $SOC = SOC_0 - \frac{\int I \, dt}{C_{rated}}$ | % (0 to 100) | $SOC \in [0, 100\%]$ |
| 12 | DOD | $DOD = 100\% - SOC$ | % | Range 0 to 100% |
| 13 | SOH | $SOH = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$ | % | End-of-life at ~80% |
| 14 | C-rate | $C_{rate} = \frac{I}{C_{rated}}$ | $h^{-1}$ | $1C$ = 1 hour discharge |
| 15 | Heat Generation | $Q = I^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | Watts (W) | Joule heating |
| 16 | Battery Pack (Series) | $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | V | Cells in series |
| 17 | Battery Pack (Parallel) | $C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$ | Ah | Cells in parallel |
| 18 | Pack Energy | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack}$ | Wh | Series-parallel combo |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in the Field

- **Range Estimation:** $Range = \frac{E_{battery} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{E_{consumption}}$ — directly uses specific energy
- **BMS Design:** Real-time SOC estimation protects against overcharge/overdischarge
- **Thermal Management:** Heat generation $Q = I^2 R_{int}$ dictates cooling system sizing
- **Pack Sizing:** The trade-off between series cells (voltage) and parallel cells (capacity)
