# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peukert's Equation

**Background:** In 1897, Wilhelm Peukert experimentally observed that the usable capacity of a Lead-acid battery decreases as discharge current increases. His empirical law captures this non-ideal behavior.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

Peukert postulated that the product $I^{k} \cdot t$ is constant for a given battery chemistry:

$$I^{k} \cdot t = C_{p} \quad (\text{Peukert capacity})$$

Where $k$ is the **Peukert exponent** ($k \geq 1$).

### 3.1.2 Solving for Discharge Time

$$t = \frac{C_{p}}{I^{k}}$$

This shows that doubling the current ($I \rightarrow 2I$) reduces the discharge time by a factor of $2^k$ rather than simply 2.

### 3.1.3 Determining the Peukert Exponent from Two Test Points

Conduct discharge tests at two different currents $(I_1, t_1)$ and $(I_2, t_2)$:

$$I_1^{k} \cdot t_1 = I_2^{k} \cdot t_2$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$k \cdot \ln(I_1) + \ln(t_1) = k \cdot \ln(I_2) + \ln(t_2)$$

Rearranging:

$$k[\ln(I_1) - \ln(I_2)] = \ln(t_2) - \ln(t_1)$$

$$k = \frac{\ln(t_2) - \ln(t_1)}{\ln(I_1) - \ln(I_2)}$$

This is the **standard KTU derivation** often tested for 7 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Battery Pack Energy (Series-Parallel Configuration)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A battery pack has $N_s$ cells in series and $N_p$ cells in parallel. Each cell has voltage $V_{cell}$ and capacity $C_{cell}$.

### 3.2.2 Series Combination Analysis

**Step 1:** When cells are connected in **series**, the *same current* flows through all cells, but voltages add:

$$V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$$

**Step 2:** The capacity of the series string remains equal to a single cell:

$$C_{pack,series} = C_{cell}$$

### 3.2.3 Parallel Combination Analysis

**Step 1:** When cells are connected in **parallel**, the *same voltage* appears across all cells, but currents (and hence capacities) add:

$$C_{pack,parallel} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$$

**Step 2:** The voltage remains equal to a single cell:

$$V_{pack,parallel} = V_{cell}$$

### 3.2.4 Combined Series-Parallel Pack Energy

For a hybrid configuration:

$$V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$$

$$C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$$

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack} = (N_s \cdot V_{cell}) \cdot (N_p \cdot C_{cell})$$

$$\boxed{E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot E_{cell}}$$

### 3.2.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Tesla Model S battery pack uses 18650 cells with $V_{cell} = 3.7$ V, $C_{cell} = 3.1$ Ah. The pack has 96 cells in series and 74 cells in parallel. Calculate pack voltage, capacity, and total energy.

**Solution:**

$$V_{pack} = 96 \times 3.7 = 355.2 \text{ V}$$

$$C_{pack} = 74 \times 3.1 = 229.4 \text{ Ah}$$

$$E_{pack} = 355.2 \times 229.4 = 81{,}483 \text{ Wh} \approx 81.5 \text{ kWh}$$

This matches the actual ~85 kWh Tesla pack closely (the difference is due to derating for safety).

## 3.3 Derivation: SOC Using Coulomb Counting

### 3.3.1 The Master Equation

Starting from the definition of capacity as integral of current:

$$C_{discharged}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

The current State of Charge is:

$$SOC(t) = SOC_{initial} - \frac{C_{discharged}(t)}{C_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Expansion

Let $SOC_0$ = initial SOC at $t = 0$. For constant discharge current $I_{dis}$:

$$C_{discharged}(t) = I_{dis} \cdot t$$

Substituting:

$$SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{I_{dis} \cdot t}{C_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$$

For charging ($I < 0$ by convention):

$$SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{\vert I_{ch} \vert \cdot t}{C_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Example: SOC Evolution

**Given:**
- Battery rated capacity: $C_{rated} = 100$ Ah
- Initial SOC: $SOC_0 = 80\%$
- Discharge current: $I_{dis} = 25$ A (constant)
- Time elapsed: $t = 2$ hours

**Find:** Final SOC

**Solution:**

$$C_{discharged} = 25 \times 2 = 50 \text{ Ah}$$

$$SOC(2) = 80\% - \frac{50}{100} \times 100\% = 80\% - 50\% = 30\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Battery Models

| Model | Complexity | Accuracy | Parameters | Use Case |
|-------|------------|----------|------------|----------|
| **Simple Voltage Source** | Very Low | Low | $V_{oc}$ only | Initial sizing |
| **R-int Model** | Low | Moderate | $V_{oc}$, $R_{int}$ | First-order simulation |
| **Thevenin Model** | Moderate | Good | $V_{oc}$, $R_0$, $R_1$, $C_1$ | Dynamic load studies |
| **Dual Polarization (DP)** | High | Very Good | $V_{oc}$, $R_0$, $R_1$, $C_1$, $R_2$, $C_2$ | BMS algorithm development |
| **Electrochemical (Newman)** | Very High | Excellent | Many physical params | Research & development |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery Equivalent Circuit Model (Thevenin 1st Order)

This Mermaid block illustrates the **most commonly tested** equivalent circuit for KTU board exams — the first-order Thevenin model with internal resistance and one RC parallel network.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Terminal Plus] --> B[OCV Source V_oc]
    B --> C[Ohmic Resistor R_int]
    C --> D[RC Network R_ct parallel C_dl]
    D --> E[Terminal Minus]
    E --> F[Load Current I_load]
    F --> A

    G[Voltage Sensor V_term] -.-> A
    H[Current Sensor I_load] -.-> F

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Components:**
> - **$V_{oc}$ (Open Circuit Voltage):** Thermodynamic equilibrium potential — depends on SOC and temperature
> - **$R_{int}$ (Ohmic Resistance):** Represents electrolyte resistance + electrode resistance
> - **$R_{ct} \parallel C_{dl}$ (RC Network):** Models charge-transfer kinetics and double-layer capacitance at electrode-electrolyte interface

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) of a battery. State their typical operational ranges in an EV battery management system.**

**Model Answer:**

**State of Charge (SOC):** It is the ratio of the *remaining capacity* of a battery to its *rated (maximum) capacity*, expressed as a percentage. Mathematically:

$$SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau \times 100\%$$

Operational range in EV: **20% to 95%** (battery is never fully discharged or fully charged to extend life).

**State of Health (SOH):** It is the ratio of the *current maximum deliverable capacity* to the *rated capacity when new*, expressed as a percentage. It quantifies battery aging:

$$SOH = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

When SOH drops below **80%**, the battery is typically considered end-of-life for automotive use.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Peukert effect? Explain with one practical implication for a Lead-acid battery in an EV.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Peukert effect** describes the phenomenon where the *usable capacity of a battery decreases as the discharge current increases*. It is quantified by the Peukert exponent $k \geq 1$.

$$I^{k} \cdot t = \text{constant} = C_{p}$$

For Lead-acid batteries, $k$ typically lies between **1.1 and 1.3**.

**Practical Implication:** A 100 Ah Lead-acid battery rated at the 20-hour discharge rate (5 A) will only deliver about **70–80 Ah** if discharged at 20 A (1-hour rate). This means that **high acceleration demands in an EV cause significant capacity loss** compared to slow cruising — a key reason Lead-acid batteries are rarely used as the *primary* traction battery in modern EVs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Peukert equation for a battery. A Lead-acid battery rated at 100 Ah is tested at two different discharge rates: at 5 A it lasts 20 hours, and at 25 A it lasts 3 hours. Calculate the Peukert exponent and the Peukert capacity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a Battery Management System (BMS) with a block diagram. List four critical protection functions performed by a BMS in an electric vehicle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — State Peukert's Law:**

$$I^{k} \cdot t = C_{p}$$

**Step 2 — Write equations for both test points:**

At $I_1 = 5$ A, $t_1 = 20$ h:

$$5^{k} \cdot 20 = C_{p}$$

At $I_2 = 25$ A, $t_2 = 3$ h:

$$25^{k} \cdot 3 = C_{p}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$5^{k} \cdot 20 = 25^{k} \cdot 3$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$k \cdot \ln(5) + \ln(20) = k \cdot \ln(25) + \ln(3)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for $k$:**

$$k[\ln(5) - \ln(25)] = \ln(3) - \ln(20)$$

$$k = \frac{\ln(3) - \ln(20)}{\ln(5) - \ln(25)} = \frac{\ln(3/20)}{\ln(5/25)} = \frac{\ln(0.15)}{\ln(0.2)}$$

$$k = \frac{-1.8971}{-1.6094} \approx 1.179$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Peukert capacity:**

$$C_{p} = 5^{1.179} \cdot 20 = 6.94 \cdot 20 \approx 138.8$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 1.18, \quad C_{p} \approx 138.8}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Working of BMS:**

A Battery Management System (BMS) is an electronic control unit that monitors and manages a rechargeable battery pack. It performs **real-time monitoring** of voltage, current, and temperature of each cell; **estimates** SOC and SOH using algorithms; **balances** cell voltages to ensure uniform aging; and **protects** against unsafe operating conditions.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above for full Mermaid schematic).**

**Four Critical Protection Functions:**

1. **Overvoltage Protection:** Disconnects charger if any cell exceeds maximum voltage (e.g., 4.2 V for Li-ion)
2. **Undervoltage Protection:** Disconnects load if any cell drops below minimum voltage (e.g., 2.7 V for Li-ion)
3. **Overcurrent Protection:** Limits charge/discharge current to safe levels to prevent thermal runaway
4. **Overtemperature Protection:** Activates cooling system or disconnects pack if cell temperature exceeds ~60°C for Li-ion

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the first-order Thevenin equivalent circuit model of a battery. Derive the equation relating terminal voltage to open-circuit voltage under load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A battery pack consists of 100 cells arranged in a 10S10P configuration. Each cell has a nominal voltage of 3.6 V and a capacity of 50 Ah. Calculate the pack's nominal voltage, pack capacity, total energy, and specific energy if the total pack mass is 250 kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Circuit Description (Thevenin Model):**

The first-order Thevenin model comprises:
- An ideal voltage source $V_{oc}$ representing the open-circuit voltage
- A series resistor $R_{int}$ (ohmic resistance)
- A parallel RC network ($R_{ct}$ in parallel with $C_{dl}$) representing the charge-transfer and double-layer dynamics

**Refer to Section 4.1 for the schematic diagram.**

**Derivation:**

Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop:

$$V_{term} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int} - V_{RC}$$

Where $V_{RC}$ is the voltage across the parallel RC network. The current through the capacitor $C_{dl}$ is:

$$I_{C} = C_{dl} \cdot \frac{dV_{RC}}{dt}$$

The current through $R_{ct}$ is $V_{RC}/R_{ct}$. By KCL:

$$I = \frac{V_{RC}}{R_{ct}} + C_{dl} \cdot \frac{dV_{RC}}{dt}$$

Rearranging as a first-order ODE:

$$\frac{dV_{RC}}{dt} = \frac{I}{C_{dl}} - \frac{V_{RC}}{R_{ct} \cdot C_{dl}}$$

For **steady-state** (constant load current, $dV_{RC}/dt = 0$):

$$V_{RC,ss} = I \cdot R_{ct}$$

Therefore, the steady-state terminal voltage is:

$$\boxed{V_{term} = V_{oc} - I(R_{int} + R_{ct})}$$

This shows that the terminal voltage drops linearly with load current, which is the basis for the simple "R-int + Rct" DC model.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Configuration: 10S10P
- $V_{cell} = 3.6$ V
- $C_{cell} = 50$ Ah
- $m_{pack} = 250$ kg
- $N_s = 10$, $N_p = 10$

**Step 1 — Pack Nominal Voltage (Series cells add voltage):**

$$V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell} = 10 \cdot 3.6 = 36 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Pack Capacity (Parallel cells add capacity):**

$$C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell} = 10 \cdot 50 = 500 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Total Energy:**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot C_{pack} = 36 \cdot 500 = 18{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 18 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Specific Energy:**

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{18{,}000}{250} = 72 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Summary of Results:**

$$\boxed{V_{pack} = 36 \text{ V}, \quad C_{pack} = 500 \text{ Ah}, \quad E_{pack} = 18 \text{ kWh}, \quad E_{sp} = 72 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** In a 10S10P configuration, students often confuse $N_s$ and $N_p$ — remember, **S (series)** controls voltage and **P (parallel)** controls capacity.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When calculating SOC, students forget to convert Ah to a percentage by dividing by $C_{rated}$ — this gives them a wildly wrong answer.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** The Peukert exponent derivation is frequently attempted with base-10 logarithms but evaluated with natural log values inconsistently — *pick one and stick with it*.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing energy (Wh) with capacity (Ah). Energy = Voltage × Capacity. Many students compute only capacity and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not stating units explicitly in the final answer. KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Battery Analysis**

### Conceptual Anchors
- **Battery = Electrochemical Energy Storage** using reversible redox reactions (Faradaic process)
- **Three primary chemistries:** Pb-acid (cheap, heavy), NiMH (mid-range), Li-ion (modern standard)
- **Voltage** comes from chemistry (Nernst equation); **Capacity** comes from mass of active material

### Parameter Mastery
- **SOC** ranges from 0% (empty) to 100% (full); EV uses 20–95% to extend life
- **DOD** = 100% − SOC; defines how much energy is actually usable
- **SOH** < 80% means battery end-of-life
- **C-rate** = current ÷ rated capacity; 1C = full discharge in 1 hour
- **Internal resistance $R_{int}$** causes voltage sag and heat loss $I^2 R$

### Key Equations (Must Memorize)
- Nernst: $V_{cell} = V^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$
- Capacity: $C = I \cdot t$
- Energy: $E = V \cdot C$
- Peukert: $I^k \cdot t = C_p$, with $k = \frac{\ln(t_2/t_1)}{\ln(I_1/I_2)}$
- SOC: $SOC = SOC_0 - \frac{\int I \, dt}{C_{rated}}$
- Pack energy: $E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot E_{cell}$

### BMS Functions (Six Pillars)
1. Cell monitoring (V, I, T)
2. SOC/SOH estimation
3. Cell balancing (passive or active)
4. Over/under voltage protection
5. Overcurrent protection
6. Thermal management

### Models Hierarchy (From Simple to Complex)
- Voltage source → R-int → Thevenin → Dual Polarization → Full Electrochemical

### Vehicle-Specific Reminders
- **Range** ∝ Specific Energy (Wh/kg)
- **Acceleration** ∝ Specific Power (W/kg)
- **Cycle life** ∝ DOD depth (shallow DOD = longer life)
- **Heat dissipation** ∝ $I^2 R_{int}$ — dictates cooling system size

### Numerical Problem Strategy
1. Identify configuration (S, P, or SP)
2. Extract $N_s$ and $N_p$ from the problem statement
3. Compute $V_{pack}$, $C_{pack}$, then $E_{pack}$
4. For SOC, integrate current over time, then divide by $C_{rated}$
5. Always include units in final boxed answer

### Energy Storage Hierarchy Diagram
- **Ultra-capacitor** → Highest power, lowest energy (kW/kg)
- **Li-ion battery** → Balanced for traction (100–250 Wh/kg)
- **Fuel cell** → Continuous energy, refuelable (500+ Wh/kg equivalent)
- **Hydrogen tank** → Highest practical energy density for long-range FCEVs

### Real-World Connections
- **Tesla Model S:** ~7104 cells (96S74P), 85 kWh, ~240 Wh/kg
- **Toyota Prius:** NiMH battery, ~1.3 kWh, optimized for power
- **Nissan Leaf:** Li-ion, 24–30 kWh pouch cells, air-cooled
- **Hyundai Nexo (FCEV):** Fuel cell + Li-ion battery hybrid pack

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Battery-based Energy Storage and its Analysis**

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supercapacitor (Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor — EDLC):** A high-capacity energy storage device that stores electrical energy **electro statically** by separation of charge at the interface between an electrolyte and a high-surface-area electrode, rather than by faradaic (chemical) reactions as in conventional batteries. It is also called an *ultracapacitor* or *double-layer capacitor*.

**Key engineering distinction:** Unlike a battery, no chemical bond is broken or formed during charge/discharge. Energy is stored physically as separated positive and negative charges in the Helmholtz double layer.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electromagnetic Sponge"

Imagine a **giant kitchen sponge soaked in water** sitting inside a sealed bucket:

- The **sponge (activated carbon electrode)** represents the porous electrode with an enormous internal surface area (~1500–3000 m²/g).
- The **water (electrolyte ions)** represents the positive and negative ions that crowd onto the sponge surface when a voltage is applied.
- The **bucket (separator)** prevents the two charged sponges from touching and short-circuiting.

When you press the sponge (apply voltage), water rushes to the surface (ions form the double layer) — **this stores energy quickly**. When you release (discharge), water rushes back out **very quickly** — releasing energy fast. But you cannot hold nearly as much water as a tank (battery) can — so the *energy capacity* is lower, but the *speed of filling/emptying* is far higher.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy explains why supercapacitors deliver **very high power (rapid discharge)** but store **less total energy** compared to Li-ion batteries.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Cell voltage | **2.5 V – 2.7 V** (for symmetric carbon/carbon EDLC) |
| Specific capacitance | **100 – 400 F/g** of active carbon |
| Surface area of activated carbon | **1500 – 3000 m²/g** |
| Electrolyte | Organic (TEABF₄ in acetonitrile) or aqueous (H₂SO₄, KOH) |
| Double-layer thickness (Helmholtz) | **~0.3 – 0.5 nm** (≈ ionic radius) |
| Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) | **0.1 – 10 mΩ** per cell |
| Cycle life | **> 500,000 cycles** |
| Operating temperature | **−40 °C to +65 °C** |
| Energy density | **4 – 10 Wh/kg** |
| Power density | **1,000 – 10,000 W/kg** |
| Coulombic efficiency | **~95–99 %** |

---

## 1.4 Why Supercapacitors Matter for HEVs/EVs (KTU High-Yield Context)

> [!NOTE]
> In a Hybrid Electric Vehicle, the **supercapacitor** is used as a **peak-power buffer** — it handles the high-current transients (acceleration, regenerative braking) while the main battery handles steady cruising.

- **Regenerative Braking**: Captures short, high-current pulses from braking (~10–20 s events) without degrading battery life.
- **Acceleration Boost**: Delivers peak current during hard acceleration.
- **Battery Life Extension**: Reduces cyclic stress on the Li-ion traction battery, extending its calendar life by **2–3×**.
- **Cold-Start Assist**: Works at −40 °C where batteries lose significant capacity.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Electrochemical Double Layer

When a carbon electrode (high surface area, porous) is immersed in an electrolyte and a voltage is applied:

1. Ions of opposite polarity migrate to the electrode surface.
2. Two layers of opposite charge form separated by an atomic-scale distance (~ionic radius).
3. This forms two capacitors in series at each pore → **huge effective capacitance** because A is enormous and d is sub-nanometer.

---

## 2.2 Helmholtz Double-Layer Capacitance Formula

The capacitance of the EDL per unit area is:

$$
C_{dl} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ (permittivity of free space)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of electrolyte (~ 10–30 for organic electrolytes)
- $A$ = electrode surface area (m²)
- $d$ = effective double-layer thickness (~0.3–0.5 nm)

Because $d$ is on the order of nanometers and $A$ is on the order of thousands of m² per gram, the total capacitance of a small cell can reach **hundreds to thousands of Farads**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table — every KTU board problem is constructed from these equations.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Double-layer capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Series combination of two layers |
| 2 | Energy stored | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J (or Wh) | Compared to $E = QV$ for battery |
| 3 | Stored charge | $Q = C V$ | C | Linear with voltage |
| 4 | Maximum deliverable power | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}}$ | W | Occurs at matched impedance |
| 5 | RC time constant | $\tau = R_{ESR} \cdot C$ | s | Governs discharge speed |
| 6 | Discharging voltage | $V(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t / RC}$ | V | Pure RC decay |
| 7 | Charging voltage | $V(t) = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$ | V | Exponential rise |
| 8 | Energy density (gravimetric) | $\rho_E = \dfrac{0.5\,C\,V^2}{3600 \cdot m}$ | Wh/kg | Practical 4–10 Wh/kg |
| 9 | Power density (gravimetric) | $\rho_P = \dfrac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR} \cdot m}$ | W/kg | Practical 1k–10k W/kg |
| 10 | Cell balancing factor | $N_{cells} = \dfrac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell}}$ | — | Series connection |
| 11 | Equivalent pack capacitance | $C_{pack} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{N_{cells}}$ | F | Series rule |
| 12 | Ragone relation (ideal) | $E \cdot P = \dfrac{(0.5 C V^2)(V^2)}{4 R_{ESR}}$ | W·J | Constant for ideal SC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Series connection reduces capacitance** (same as for capacitors): $1/C_{eq} = \sum 1/C_i$.
> **Parallel connection adds capacitance**: $C_{eq} = \sum C_i$.

---

## 2.4 Three Equivalent Circuit Models (KTU Hot Topic)

### (a) Simple RC Model

A single capacitor $C$ in series with an Equivalent Series Resistance $R_{ESR}$, with a parallel leakage resistance $R_{leak}$.

- Used for **first-order approximation**.
- Valid for **seconds to minutes** time range.

### (b) Two-Branch (Zubieta-Bonert) Model

Two RC branches:
- **Fast branch** ($R_f, C_f$) — represents immediate charge redistribution (seconds).
- **Slow branch** ($R_s, C_s$) — represents deeper diffusion into pores (minutes to hours).

$$
V_{term}(t) = V_0 - I R_f \left(1 - e^{-t/R_f C_f}\right) - I R_s \left(1 - e^{-t/R_s C_s}\right)
$$

### (c) Three-Branch Model

Adds a **long-term branch** ($R_l, C_l$) representing leakage/self-discharge (hours to days).

### (d) Transmission-Line (Porous Electrode) Model

Most accurate — models the distributed RC network inside the porous electrode. Used in **research-grade simulations** (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, COMSOL).

---

## 2.5 Charging & Discharging Characteristics

### Constant-Current Discharge
Voltage across a supercapacitor discharging through load $R_L$:

$$
V(t) = V_0 - I R_{ESR} - \frac{I t}{C}
$$

- First drop $IR_{ESR}$ is **instantaneous** (resistive loss).
- Then a **linear ramp-down** governed by capacitance.

### Constant-Voltage (CV) Charging
Used in EV charging stations. Current decays exponentially:

$$
I(t) = \frac{V_{src} - V_{cell}(t)}{R_{ESR}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: **"Why is CV charging preferred over CC charging for supercapacitors?"** — Answer: CV charging allows the cell to absorb 100 % of its rated energy without over-voltage damage; CC charging would have to terminate at ~63 % state of charge to avoid exceeding $V_{max}$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in HEVs

1. **Mild Hybrid (48 V) systems** (e.g., Mazda SkyActiv, Mercedes EQ Boost) — use supercapacitors for cranking and brake-energy recovery.
2. **Fuel Cell Hybrid Buses** — SC stack buffers the slow dynamic response of the fuel cell.
3. **Tram/Trolley assist** — SC packs on the rooftop provide off-wire operation for short stretches.
4. **Cold-start of 12 V automotive bus** — SC replaces lead-acid for stop-start systems.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Supercapacitor

Starting from the definition of capacitance, $Q = C V$.

Work done in moving an infinitesimal charge $dq$ from one plate to the other at voltage $v$:

$$
dW = v \cdot dq
$$

Substituting $v = q/C$ and $dq = C\,dv$:

$$
dW = \frac{q}{C} \cdot C\, dv = v \cdot C\, dv
$$

Integrating from 0 V to the final voltage $V$:

$$
W = \int_{0}^{V} C v \, dv
$$

$$
W = C \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{V}
$$

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

In watt-hours (more useful for EV calculations):

$$
E \ [\text{Wh}] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C V^2}{3600}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ relation is why the **State of Energy (SOE)** is *not* linear with voltage — even though state of *charge* is. A supercapacitor discharged from 2.7 V to 1.35 V has released **75 %** of its energy, not 50 %.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Power Transfer

Consider a charged supercapacitor of capacitance $C$ at voltage $V_0$, with internal resistance $R_{ESR}$, connected to an external load $R_L$.

**Step 1** — Write the circuit equation (KVL):

$$
V_0 = I R_{ESR} + I R_L
$$

**Step 2** — Express the current:

$$
I = \frac{V_0}{R_{ESR} + R_L}
$$

**Step 3** — Power delivered to the load:

$$
P_L = I^2 R_L = \frac{V_0^2 \, R_L}{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^2}
$$

**Step 4** — Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_0^2 \cdot \frac{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^2 - R_L \cdot 2(R_{ESR} + R_L)}{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^4} = 0
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
(R_{ESR} + R_L) - 2 R_L = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_L = R_{ESR}
$$

**Step 5** — Substitute $R_L = R_{ESR}$ back into $P_L$:

$$
P_{\max} = \frac{V_0^2 \, R_{ESR}}{(2 R_{ESR})^2} = \frac{V_0^2}{4 R_{ESR}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the matched-impedance result** — identical to the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem. In an HEV application, the power electronics (DC-DC converter) are designed to present an equivalent load close to $R_{ESR}$ during peak demand.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ragone Plot Energy-Power Trade-off

For a given supercapacitor cell, energy and power are:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2, \qquad P = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}}
$$

Eliminate $V^2$:

$$
V^2 = \frac{2 E}{C} = 4 P R_{ESR}
$$

$$
E = 2 P R_{ESR} C
$$

Therefore:

$$
P = \frac{E}{2 R_{ESR} C}
$$

This means **on a log-log Ragone plot, an ideal supercapacitor traces a straight diagonal line of slope −1** between energy density (Wh/kg) on the x-axis and power density (W/kg) on the y-axis. Li-ion batteries sit to the right (high E, low P); fuel cells sit far right; conventional capacitors sit far top-left.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — State of Charge (SOC) Linear Relation

For a supercapacitor, unlike a battery:

$$
\text{SOC}(t) = \frac{V(t)}{V_{max}} \times 100 \%
$$

But the State of Energy (SOE):

$$
\text{SOE}(t) = \left(\frac{V(t)}{V_{max}}\right)^2 \times 100 \%
$$

**Worked numeric example:** If $V_{max} = 2.7$ V and the capacitor discharges to $V = 1.35$ V (half voltage):

- SOC = 50 %
- SOE = **25 %** — i.e., 75 % of the energy has already been extracted!

> [!NOTE]
> This is why supercapacitors are typically only operated between **V_{max} and V_{max}/2**, allowing 75 % energy utilisation while keeping the cell within safe voltage limits.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery vs Supercapacitor vs Conventional Capacitor

| Property | Conventional Capacitor | Supercapacitor (EDLC) | Li-ion Battery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charge mechanism | Electrostatic | Electrostatic (double-layer) | Faradaic (chemical) |
| Energy stored | $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | $Q V$ (≈ constant V) |
| Charge time | µs – ms | 0.1 – 30 s | 1 – 5 h |
| Discharge time | µs – ms | 1 s – 60 s | 0.3 – 5 h |
| Energy density (Wh/kg) | < 0.1 | **4 – 10** | 100 – 250 |
| Power density (W/kg) | > 10⁶ | **1,000 – 10,000** | 200 – 1,000 |
| Cycle life | Infinite | **> 500,000** | 500 – 3,000 |
| Cell voltage | up to ~1000 V | **2.5 – 2.7 V** | 3.6 – 3.7 V |
| Operating temp. | wide | **−40 to +65 °C** | −20 to +60 °C |
| Cost per Wh | low | high | medium |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A supercapacitor bank has $C = 50$ F, $V_{max} = 2.7$ V, $R_{ESR} = 12$ mΩ. Calculate (a) maximum energy stored, (b) usable energy between 2.7 V and 1.35 V, (c) peak power.

**Solution (a):**

$$
E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times (2.7)^2 = 0.5 \times 50 \times 7.29
$$

$$
E_{max} = 182.25\ \text{J} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{182.25}{3600} \approx 0.0506\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
E_{usable} = \frac{1}{2} C \left( V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2 \right)
$$

$$
E_{usable} = 0.5 \times 50 \times \left(2.7^2 - 1.35^2\right) = 25 \times (7.29 - 1.8225)
$$

$$
E_{usable} = 25 \times 5.4675 = 136.69\ \text{J} \quad (\approx 75 \%\ \text{of}\ E_{max})
$$

**Solution (c):**

$$
P_{peak} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}} = \frac{2.7^2}{4 \times 0.012} = \frac{7.29}{0.048}
$$

$$
P_{peak} = 151.875\ \text{W}
$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Supercapacitor Bank in an HEV Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph PowerSources["Power Sources"]
    FCE[Fuel Cell Stack]
    BAT[Traction Li-ion Battery]
  end

  subgraph StorageBuffer["Energy Storage Buffer"]
    SCB[Supercapacitor Bank]
    BMS_SC[SC Management System SCMS]
  end

  subgraph Powertrain["HEV Powertrain"]
    DC1[DC-DC Bidirectional Converter 1]
    DC2[DC-DC Bidirectional Converter 2]
    INV[3-Phase Traction Inverter]
    MTR[BLDC / PMSM Traction Motor]
  end

  subgraph Brakes["Regenerative Braking"]
    BRK[Electro-Mechanical Brakes]
  end

  subgraph Vehicle["Vehicle Loads"]
    ACC[12V / 48V Auxiliary Bus]
    HVAC[HVAC & Accessories]
  end

  FCE --> DC1
  BAT --> DC2
  DC1 --> SCB
  DC2 --> SCB
  SCB --> BMS_SC
  BMS_SC -. monitors .-> SCB
  DC1 --> INV
  DC2 --> INV
  INV --> MTR
  MTR --> BRK
  BRK -. regen energy .-> SCB
  DC1 --> ACC
  DC1 --> HVAC
```

**Functional Mapping Notes:**
- The **SC bank** acts as a *bidirectional shock absorber* between slow-response sources (FC, battery) and the fast-changing load (motor inverter).
- **DC-DC converters** are mandatory because the SC voltage varies widely with SOC; the inverter requires a near-stiff DC bus.
- **SCMS** performs cell balancing, over-voltage protection, and thermal management.

---

## 4.3 Charge-Discharge Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Idle
  Idle --> Charging: V applied from regen or charger
  Charging --> Float: V reaches V_max
  Float --> Charging: V drops below threshold
  Charging --> Discharging: traction request
  Discharging --> Idle: I equals zero
  Discharging --> DeepCut: V crosses V_min
  DeepCut --> Idle: cell isolated by SCMS
  Idle --> Fault: V or T out of range
  Fault --> Idle: SCMS clears fault
  Fault --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. Define supercapacitor. How is it different from a conventional capacitor and a battery?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A supercapacitor (Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor, EDLC) is an energy storage device that stores charge electrostatically at the interface between a high-surface-area porous carbon electrode and an electrolyte, without any faradaic chemical reaction.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Conventional Capacitor | Supercapacitor | Battery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charge storage | Electrostatic (single layer) | Electrostatic (double layer) | Chemical (faradaic) |
| Energy density | Very low | Medium (4–10 Wh/kg) | High (100–250 Wh/kg) |
| Power density | Very high | High (1k–10k W/kg) | Low–Medium (200–1000 W/kg) |
| Cycle life | Practically infinite | > 500,000 | 500–3,000 |

---

### Question 2. List any four advantages of supercapacitors in hybrid electric vehicle applications.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Very high power density** (1–10 kW/kg) enables rapid acceleration assist and regeneration capture.
2. **Long cycle life** (> 500,000 cycles) reduces replacement cost over vehicle lifetime.
3. **Wide operating temperature range** (−40 °C to +65 °C), suitable for cold-start assist.
4. **Fast charging** (seconds to minutes), ideal for capturing brief regenerative braking pulses that would damage batteries.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — Energy & Power Analysis of SC Bank

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

A supercapacitor module for an HEV consists of **120 cells**, each rated **2.7 V, 3000 F**, with $R_{ESR,cell} = 0.4\ \text{m}\Omega$. The cells are connected in **series** to form the module.

**(a)** Calculate the module's nominal voltage, equivalent capacitance, total ESR, maximum stored energy, and peak deliverable power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During regenerative braking, the module absorbs **100 A** constant current for **10 s**. Assuming it was initially at $V_{max}$, compute the final voltage, energy absorbed, and average charging power. Also state the percentage SOC change. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**Module capacitance** (series rule):

$$
C_{mod} = \frac{C_{cell}}{N} = \frac{3000}{120} = 25\ \text{F}
$$

**Module nominal voltage** (series rule for voltage):

$$
V_{mod} = N \times V_{cell} = 120 \times 2.7 = 324\ \text{V}
$$

**Total ESR** (series rule for resistance):

$$
R_{ESR,mod} = N \times R_{ESR,cell} = 120 \times 0.0004 = 0.048\ \Omega
$$

**Maximum stored energy**:

$$
E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} C_{mod} V_{mod}^2 = 0.5 \times 25 \times (324)^2
$$

$$
E_{max} = 12.5 \times 104976 = 1{,}312{,}200\ \text{J} \approx 1.312\ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
E_{max} = \frac{1{,}312{,}200}{3600} \approx 364.5\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Peak power**:

$$
P_{peak} = \frac{V_{mod}^2}{4 R_{ESR,mod}} = \frac{(324)^2}{4 \times 0.048} = \frac{104976}{0.192}
$$

$$
P_{peak} \approx 546{,}750\ \text{W} \approx 546.75\ \text{kW}
$$

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Voltage drop across ESR** (instantaneous):

$$
\Delta V_{ESR} = I \times R_{ESR,mod} = 100 \times 0.048 = 4.8\ \text{V}
$$

**Capacitive voltage drop** during charging at 100 A for 10 s:

$$
\Delta V_{C} = \frac{I \cdot t}{C_{mod}} = \frac{100 \times 10}{25} = 40\ \text{V}
$$

**Final voltage**:

$$
V_{final} = V_{max} - \Delta V_{ESR} - \Delta V_{C} = 324 - 4.8 - 40 = 279.2\ \text{V}
$$

**SOC change**:

$$
\Delta\text{SOC} = \frac{\Delta V_C}{V_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{40}{324} \times 100 \approx 12.35\ \%
$$

**Energy absorbed** (using average voltage across the capacitor):

$$
E_{abs} = \frac{1}{2} C_{mod}\left(V_{max}^2 - V_{final}^2\right)
$$

$$
E_{abs} = 0.5 \times 25 \times \left(324^2 - 279.2^2\right) = 12.5 \times (104976 - 77952.64)
$$

$$
E_{abs} = 12.5 \times 27023.36 \approx 337{,}792\ \text{J} \approx 93.83\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Average charging power**:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{E_{abs}}{t} = \frac{337{,}792}{10} = 33{,}779.2\ \text{W} \approx 33.78\ \text{kW}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Equivalent Circuit Modelling & Two-Branch Model

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Draw the **two-branch equivalent circuit** of a supercapacitor and explain each element physically. Derive the expression for terminal voltage during constant-current discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a supercapacitor described by the two-branch model with $C_f = 1000$ F, $R_f = 1\ \text{m}\Omega$, $C_s = 200$ F, $R_s = 5\ \text{m}\Omega$, and an initial voltage $V_0 = 2.7$ V, determine the terminal voltage after **5 s** of constant discharge at $I = 50$ A. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**Two-branch equivalent circuit:**

- **Fast branch** ($R_f$ in series with $C_f$): Models immediate charge redistribution on the outer pore surface and ohmic electrolyte/contact losses. Time constant $\tau_f = R_f C_f \sim$ 1–10 s.
- **Slow branch** ($R_s$ in series with $C_s$): Models deeper diffusion of ions into micropores and faradaic pseudo-capacitance effects. Time constant $\tau_s = R_s C_s \sim$ 10–100 s.
- A parallel **leakage resistance** $R_{leak}$ represents self-discharge.

**Terminal voltage under constant current $I$:**

Applying KVL around the loop, each branch carries the same current $I$ (Kirchhoff's current law with parallel branches):

$$
V_{term}(t) = V_0 - I R_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_f}\right) - I R_s \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_s}\right)
$$

where $\tau_f = R_f C_f$ and $\tau_s = R_s C_s$.

> **Physical interpretation:** At $t = 0^+$, the capacitors behave as short circuits, so the entire $IR$ drop appears across the resistors. As $t \to \infty$, the capacitors are fully charged and no current flows through them; the entire voltage drop is $I(R_f + R_s)$.

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**Compute time constants:**

$$
\tau_f = R_f C_f = 0.001 \times 1000 = 1\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\tau_s = R_s C_s = 0.005 \times 200 = 1\ \text{s}
$$

**Note:** Both time constants are 1 s — this is a deliberate simplification for the exam.

**Compute each exponential term at $t = 5$ s:**

$$
e^{-t/\tau_f} = e^{-5/1} = e^{-5} \approx 0.00674
$$

$$
e^{-t/\tau_s} = e^{-5/1} = e^{-5} \approx 0.00674
$$

**Compute resistive voltage drops:**

$$
I R_f = 50 \times 0.001 = 0.05\ \text{V}
$$

$$
I R_s = 50 \times 0.005 = 0.25\ \text{V}
$$

**Compute each branch contribution:**

$$
I R_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_f}\right) = 0.05 \times (1 - 0.00674) = 0.05 \times 0.99326 = 0.04966\ \text{V}
$$

$$
I R_s \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_s}\right) = 0.25 \times (1 - 0.00674) = 0.25 \times 0.99326 = 0.24832\ \text{V}
$$

**Terminal voltage at $t = 5$ s:**

$$
V_{term}(5) = 2.7 - 0.04966 - 0.24832
$$

$$
V_{term}(5) \approx 2.402\ \text{V}
$$

**Interpretation:** After 5 seconds (5 time constants), the supercapacitor is in steady-state discharge, and the terminal voltage has dropped by ~0.298 V from the open-circuit voltage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide $C$ by $N$** in series-connected SC banks — this costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Using $Q = CV$ for energy** instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ — a guaranteed 2-mark cut.
> 3. **Confusing SOC (linear with V) and SOE (quadratic with V)** — the question often specifically asks for SOE.
> 4. **Reporting Wh without converting from J** — always divide joules by 3600.
> 5. **Ignoring $IR_{ESR}$ instantaneous drop** during transient analysis — examiners expect both the resistive step and the capacitive ramp.
> 6. **Mixing up $R_{ESR}$ per cell vs. $R_{ESR}$ of module** — read the problem statement carefully.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **EDLC definition** — electrostatic storage, **no chemical reaction**, double-layer separation.
- 📐 **Core formula** — $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ (quadratic), $P_{peak} = V^2 / (4 R_{ESR})$.
- 🔋 **Series connection** — adds voltage, divides capacitance, multiplies ESR.
- 🔁 **Cycle life > 500,000** — far exceeds any battery chemistry.
- ❄️ **Cold performance** — works at −40 °C where Li-ion fails.
- 🚗 **HEV role** — peak-power buffer for regen and acceleration, not primary energy source.
- 📊 **Ragone plot** — ideal SC traces a straight diagonal line of slope −1 on log-log axes.
- 🧮 **Two-branch model** — fast branch ($\tau_f \sim 1$–$10$ s), slow branch ($\tau_s \sim 10$–$100$ s).
- 📉 **SOE ≠ SOC** — energy is quadratic in voltage; 50 % SOC ⇒ only 25 % SOE.
- 🛡️ **SCMS (Supercapacitor Management System)** — mandatory for cell balancing, over-voltage protection, and thermal monitoring.
- ⚙️ **Matched impedance** — peak power delivered when external load = $R_{ESR}$.
- 🔬 **Activated carbon surface area** — 1500–3000 m²/g is what enables such huge capacitance in a small volume.
- 🌡️ **Voltage derating with temperature** — cell voltage must be reduced at high temperature to prevent electrolyte degradation.
- 📚 **KTU keywords to use in answers** — *Helmholtz double layer*, *faradaic vs. non-faradaic*, *matched impedance*, *Ragone plot*, *bidirectional DC-DC*, *SCMS*, *series voltage balancing*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultra Capacitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Ultra Capacitors — Section 1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ultra-Capacitor** (also known as a **Super-Capacitor** or **Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor — EDLC**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy through the physical separation of charges at the *electrode–electrolyte interface* rather than through faradaic chemical reactions. This non-faradaic charge storage mechanism gives ultra-capacitors exceptionally high power density, ultra-long cycle life, and rapid charge/discharge capability, making them indispensable **auxiliary energy buffers** in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCVs), and Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Ultra-capacitors are high-capacity capacitors that bridge the gap between conventional electrolytic capacitors and electrochemical batteries. They store **10 to 100 times more energy** per unit volume than conventional capacitors and can deliver **10 to 100 times more power** than batteries, with cycle life exceeding **1,000,000 cycles**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fast Tea-Cup vs Water Tank"

Imagine a large **swimming pool (Battery)** versus a **fire-hose nozzle (Ultra-capacitor)**:

- A **battery** is like a swimming pool — it holds a *huge amount of water (energy)* but takes hours to fill and empties slowly.
- An **ultra-capacitor** is like a fire-hose nozzle — it cannot hold as much water, but it can be *filled in seconds and emptied in a fraction of a second*, delivering explosive bursts.

In a hybrid vehicle:
- The **battery** = the main fuel/energy source (steady, high energy).
- The **ultra-capacitor** = the *burst-energy partner* — it absorbs the spike from **regenerative braking** and delivers a power surge during **hard acceleration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often ask "Why are ultra-capacitors used along with batteries in HEVs?" The answer lies in this analogy — they complement each other on the **Ragone plot** (energy vs power plane).

### 1.3 Position in the Energy Storage Spectrum

A typical energy-storage device can be classified by its **energy density (Wh/kg)** and **power density (W/kg)**. Ultra-capacitors occupy a unique middle ground:

| Device | Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Power Density (W/kg) | Cycle Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional Capacitor | $< 0.1$ | $> 10,000$ | Infinite |
| **Ultra-Capacitor** | **$2$ to $10$** | **$1,000$ to $10,000$** | **$10^5$ to $10^6$** |
| Lithium-ion Battery | $100$ to $250$ | $250$ to $1,000$ | $500$ to $2,000$ |
| Fuel Cell (Hydrogen) | $500$ to $1,000$ | $50$ to $500$ | $>5,000$ |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used in This Module

- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$
- Helmholtz double-layer distance: $d \approx 0.3$ to $0.8 \, \text{nm}$ (i.e., a few Angstroms)
- Operating voltage of a single cell: $\mathbf{2.5 \, V}$ to $\mathbf{2.7 \, V}$ (organic electrolyte) or $\mathbf{1.0 \, V}$ (aqueous)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The nanometre-scale charge separation distance is the *secret* of ultra-capacitors — because $C \propto 1/d$, making $d$ extremely small produces enormous capacitance. This is the *single most important fact* examiners test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Module 3: Ultra Capacitors — Section 2

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Ultra-Capacitor Cell

A single ultra-capacitor cell is built like a **sandwich**:

1. **Two Porous Carbon Electrodes** (typically activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, or graphene) — surface area can reach $1,500$ to $3,000 \, \text{m}^2/\text{g}$ due to the porous network.
2. **Two Current Collectors** (aluminium foils) — conduct charge to the external circuit.
3. **Separator** (porous polymer membrane, e.g., cellulose or polypropylene) — prevents physical contact between the electrodes while allowing ion flow.
4. **Electrolyte** — either aqueous (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{KOH}$) or organic (e.g., tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate in acetonitrile).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why activated carbon?** Because $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, increasing the effective surface area $A$ *enormously* multiplies the capacitance. A teaspoon of activated carbon can have a surface area equal to a **football field**.

### 2.2 Working Principle — The Helmholtz Double Layer

When a voltage is applied across the electrodes, **ions of opposite polarity** from the electrolyte migrate to each electrode and form a monolayer at the interface. This forms two charged layers separated by an atomic-scale distance — exactly analogous to a conventional capacitor's two plates.

- **No chemical reaction** occurs (non-faradaic) — only physical adsorption of ions.
- Charge separation happens at *two* interfaces, effectively creating **two capacitors in series**, giving the cell the suffix "**double-layer**".

### 2.3 Operational Steps in Bullet Logic

- **Step 1 — Charging:** Positive ions ($\text{Cations}$) drift toward the negative electrode; negative ions ($\text{Anions}$) drift toward the positive electrode.
- **Step 2 — Double-Layer Formation:** Ions form a compact layer at the Helmholtz plane (≈ $0.3$ nm from the electrode).
- **Step 3 — Energy Storage:** The system stores **electrostatic potential energy** in the electric field between the two double layers.
- **Step 4 — Discharging:** When load is connected, ions diffuse back into the bulk electrolyte, releasing energy as a current pulse.

### 2.4 Types of Ultra-Capacitors

| Type | Storage Mechanism | Electrode Material | Capacitance Range | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EDLC (Double Layer)** | Pure electrostatic | Activated Carbon | $1$ to $5,000 \, \text{F}$ | $4$ to $10 \, \text{Wh/kg}$ |
| **Pseudo-capacitor** | Fast faradaic redox reactions | $\text{RuO}_2$, $\text{MnO}_2$, conducting polymers | $100$ to $1,000 \, \text{F}$ | $10$ to $50 \, \text{Wh/kg}$ |
| **Hybrid Capacitor** | Combination of both | Li-ion anode + Carbon cathode | $200$ to $8,000 \, \text{F}$ | $20$ to $100 \, \text{Wh/kg}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** The Ragone plot comparison between EDLC, pseudo-capacitor, and battery is a recurring question. Always mention that **pseudo-capacitors use a faradaic mechanism** (electron transfer), unlike pure EDLCs.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel-plate (double-layer) | Farads (F) |
| 2 | $C_{\text{cell}} = \left( \dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2} \right)^{-1}$ | Two double layers in series | F |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in a capacitor | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{E}{m} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} / m$ | Specific energy (gravimetric) | Wh/kg |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}}$ | Maximum deliverable power | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $P_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | Specific power | W/kg |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{coulombic}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\tau = R_{\text{ESR}} \cdot C$ | RC time constant | seconds (s) |
| 9 | $V_{\text{module}} = n \cdot V_{\text{cell}}$ | Voltage of series-stacked cells | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $C_{\text{module}} = C_{\text{cell}} / n$ | Capacitance of series stack | F |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline-Safe Rendering Note:** The vertical bar symbol in formulas (e.g., $\vert V \vert$, $\vert I \vert$) has been replaced with the LaTeX-safe variant `\vert` to preserve the markdown table structure. **Do not** use the ASCII pipe `\vert` directly inside table cells.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **Toyota Prius**, **Tesla Powerwall**, and **Formula-E racing vehicles**, ultra-capacitor banks are used for:

- **Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery** — capturing short, high-power pulses.
- **Cold-Start Boost** — supporting the fuel cell or battery during cranking at $-30^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Peak-Shaving** — flattening the load demand curve on the main battery, increasing its cycle life by **2x to 3x**.
- **Voltage Stabilization** — buffering DC bus voltage transients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Module 3: Ultra Capacitors — Section 3

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Double-Layer Capacitance

**Given:** A Helmholtz double-layer structure with electrode area $A$, dielectric constant of the inner Helmholtz layer $\varepsilon_r$, and effective charge separation $d$ (in metres).

**Find:** The capacitance of a single double layer.

**Step 1 — Start with the parallel-plate capacitor formula.**
A conventional parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$ and plate separation $d$, filled with a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, has capacitance:

$$
C \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the equivalent geometry.**
The double layer behaves as a *planar capacitor*, where one "plate" is the solid electrode and the other "plate" is the layer of solvated ions. The separation is the **Helmholtz thickness** $d_H$, typically $0.3$ to $0.8 \, \text{nm}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the Helmholtz values.**

$$
C_{dl} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d_H}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply realistic values for an activated-carbon electrode.**
Let $A = 2,000 \, \text{m}^2/\text{g}$, $d_H = 0.5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r \approx 6$ (for organic electrolyte), and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$.

$$
C_{dl} \;=\; \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 6 \cdot 2,000}{0.5 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
C_{dl} \;=\; \frac{1.0625 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
C_{dl} \;=\; 2.125 \times 10^{2} \, \text{F/g} \;=\; 212.5 \, \text{F/g}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per-cell basis.**
For a single cell with $m = 1 \, \text{g}$ of activated carbon per electrode:

$$
C_{\text{cell}} \;\approx\; 212 \, \text{F}
$$

This is the *origin* of the very high capacitance values quoted in manufacturer datasheets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Even with conservative constants, a single gram of activated carbon can store ~$200$ Farads. This is the mathematical proof that ultra-capacitors can rival small batteries in capacitance.

### 3.2 Derivation of Energy Stored in an Ultra-Capacitor

**Given:** A capacitor of capacitance $C$ charged to voltage $V$.

**Step 1 — Start with the work integral for charging a capacitor.**

$$
W \;=\; \int_{0}^{Q} v(q) \, dq
$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation $q = C v$ and the differential $dq = C \, dv$.**

$$
W \;=\; \int_{0}^{V} v \cdot C \, dv \;=\; C \int_{0}^{V} v \, dv
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate.**

$$
E \;=\; W \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Watt-hours (the unit used in KTU problems).**

$$
E_{\text{Wh}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3,600} \;=\; \frac{C V^{2}}{7,200}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Trap:** A common mistake students make is forgetting the **1/2 factor**. The energy stored is *half* of $CV^2$, not $CV^2$. Also, energy scales as $V^2$ — so **doubling the voltage quadruples the energy**. This is why capacitor modules are run at the *maximum allowable voltage*.

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Power Transfer

**Given:** A charged ultra-capacitor of capacitance $C$, voltage $V$, and equivalent series resistance $R_{\text{ESR}}$.

**Step 1 — Model the cell as an ideal capacitor in series with $R_{\text{ESR}}$.**

**Step 2 — When connected to a load $R_L$, the maximum power transfer theorem states** $P_{\max}$ occurs when $R_L = R_{\text{ESR}}$.

**Step 3 — The current under matched conditions:**

$$
I_{\max} \;=\; \frac{V}{R_{\text{ESR}} + R_L} \;=\; \frac{V}{2 R_{\text{ESR}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipated in the load:**

$$
P_{\max} \;=\; I_{\max}^{2} \cdot R_L \;=\; \left( \frac{V}{2 R_{\text{ESR}}} \right)^{2} \cdot R_{\text{ESR}} \;=\; \frac{V^{2}}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}}
$$

$$
P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{V^{2}}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *lower* the $R_{\text{ESR}}$, the *higher* the power. Premium ultra-capacitors target $R_{\text{ESR}} < 1 \, \text{m}\Omega$ per Farad to deliver multi-kW pulses to EV drivetrains.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 100 F ultra-capacitor module is charged to $V = 48 \, \text{V}$. The $R_{\text{ESR}} = 0.02 \, \Omega$. Calculate (a) the energy stored, (b) the maximum power deliverable, and (c) the time to discharge from $V$ to $V/2$ at constant current $I = 100 \, \text{A}$.

**Part (a) — Energy stored:**

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot (48)^{2}
$$

$$
E \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 100 \cdot 2,304 \;=\; 115,200 \, \text{J} \;\approx\; 32 \, \text{Wh}
$$

**Part (b) — Maximum power:**

$$
P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{V^{2}}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}} \;=\; \frac{(48)^{2}}{4 \cdot 0.02}
$$

$$
P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2,304}{0.08} \;=\; 28,800 \, \text{W} \;=\; 28.8 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Part (c) — Time to discharge from $V$ to $V/2$ at $I = 100 \, \text{A}$:**

$$
\Delta Q \;=\; C \cdot (V - V/2) \;=\; C \cdot V/2
$$

$$
\Delta Q \;=\; 100 \cdot 48 / 2 \;=\; 2,400 \, \text{C}
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\Delta Q}{I} \;=\; \frac{2,400}{100} \;=\; 24 \, \text{s}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This 3-part problem structure is the **standard KTU 14-mark pattern** for Module 3. Always show the substitution step, the numerical step, and the unit conversion step separately.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ultra-capacitor vs Li-ion Battery

| Parameter | Ultra-Capacitor (EDLC) | Li-ion Battery |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | $5$ to $10$ | $150$ to $250$ |
| Power Density (W/kg) | $5,000$ to $10,000$ | $250$ to $1,000$ |
| Cycle Life | $500,000$ to $1,000,000$ | $500$ to $2,000$ |
| Charge Time | $1$ to $30$ seconds | $30$ min to $5$ hr |
| Operating Temperature | $-40^\circ \text{C}$ to $+70^\circ \text{C}$ | $-20^\circ \text{C}$ to $+60^\circ \text{C}$ |
| Cost per kWh | ₹$30,000$ to ₹$80,000$ | ₹$8,000$ to ₹$20,000$ |
| Self-Discharge | High (10%/day) | Low (2-3%/month) |
| Ragone Position | Power-favoured | Energy-favoured |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Module 3: Ultra Capacitors — Section 4

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Ultra-Capacitor Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Current Collector Al Foil Positive] --> B1[Positive Electrode Activated Carbon]
    B1 --> C1[Electrolyte Ions with Separator]
    C1 --> B2[Negative Electrode Activated Carbon]
    B2 --> A2[Current Collector Al Foil Negative]
    C1 --> D1[Double Layer at Positive Interface]
    C1 --> D2[Double Layer at Negative Interface]
    D1 --> E1[Helmholtz Plane Positive]
    D2 --> E2[Helmholtz Plane Negative]
    E1 --> F1[Energy Stored as Electrostatic Field]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G[External Load Discharge Path]
    G --> H[Charging Source DC Charger]
    H --> A1
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model (RC Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vsrc[DC Source V] --> R1[Rseries Wire Resistance]
    R1 --> Cnode1[Capacitance Cbulk]
    Cnode1 --> R2[Rleakage Parallel Leakage]
    R2 --> R3[Resr Equivalent Series Resistance]
    R3 --> Lload[External Load Rload]
    Lload --> Gnd[Return to Source]
    Cnode1 -.-> D1[Double Layer Capacitance Cdl]
    Cnode1 -.-> D2[Diffusion Capacitance Cdiff]
    D1 --> Vsrc
    D2 --> Vsrc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The bulk capacitance $C_{\text{bulk}}$ is the *ideal* energy-storage element, while $R_{\text{ESR}}$ models the *real-world resistive losses*. $R_{\text{leakage}}$ accounts for self-discharge. This model is the basis of every KTU numerical problem on capacitor banks.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ultra-capacitor in an HEV Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENERGY_DOMAIN[Energy Sources]
        FC[Fuel Cell Stack]
        BAT[Li-ion Traction Battery]
    end
    subgraph BUFFER_DOMAIN[Ultra Capacitor Bank]
        UCS[Ultra Capacitor Module 48V 100F]
        BMS_UC[UC Management System]
    end
    subgraph POWER_DOMAIN[Power Electronics and Traction]
        DC_DC1[DC DC Converter 1 Bidirectional]
        DC_DC2[DC DC Converter 2 Bidirectional]
        INV[Three Phase Inverter]
        MTR[BLDC or PMSM Traction Motor]
    end
    subgraph VEHICLE_DOMAIN[Vehicle Dynamics]
        REGEN[Regenerative Braking Pulse]
        ACC[Hard Acceleration Demand]
    end
    FC --> DC_DC1
    BAT --> DC_DC2
    UCS --> DC_DC1
    UCS --> DC_DC2
    DC_DC1 --> DC_BUS[High Voltage DC Bus 300 to 400V]
    DC_DC2 --> DC_BUS
    DC_BUS --> INV
    INV --> MTR
    REGEN --> MTR
    MTR --> INV
    INV --> DC_BUS
    DC_BUS --> UCS
    BMS_UC --> UCS
    ACC --> DC_BUS
```

### 4.4 Ragone Plot — Energy vs Power Density Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_POWER[Low Power High Energy Zone]
        A1[Conventional Battery 100 to 250 Wh/kg, 250 to 1000 W/kg]
    end
    subgraph MID_POWER[Mid Power Mid Energy Zone]
        B1[Fuel Cell System 500 to 1000 Wh/kg, 50 to 500 W/kg]
        B2[Flywheel 10 to 30 Wh/kg, 500 to 2000 W/kg]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_POWER[High Power Low Energy Zone]
        C1[Ultra Capacitor 5 to 10 Wh/kg, 5000 to 10000 W/kg]
        C2[Conventional Capacitor 0.01 to 0.1 Wh/kg, Greater than 10000 W/kg]
    end
    LOW_POWER -->|Increasing Power| MID_POWER
    MID_POWER -->|Increasing Power| HIGH_POWER
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "explain the Ragone plot" in a 7-mark sub-question, the answer must mention *logarithmic scales on both axes*, *the inverse relationship between energy and power density*, and *the position of ultra-capacitors in the high-power, low-energy quadrant*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Module 3: Ultra Capacitors — Section 5

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*Define an ultra-capacitor. List any two advantages it has over a conventional battery in EV applications.*

**Model Answer:**
An ultra-capacitor (or super-capacitor) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy by means of an *electrostatic charge separation* at the *electrode–electrolyte interface* (Helmholtz double layer), instead of through chemical reactions.

**Two advantages over batteries:**
1. **Much higher power density** ($5,000$ to $10,000 \, \text{W/kg}$) allows ultra-capacitors to deliver/absorb *instantaneous high-current pulses* during acceleration and regenerative braking.
2. **Extremely long cycle life** (more than $500,000$ cycles vs. $1,000$ for Li-ion), drastically reducing replacement cost over the vehicle's lifetime.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is meant by the term "electric double-layer capacitance"?*

**Model Answer:**
Electric double-layer capacitance refers to the *capacitance arising from the physical separation of charges at the interface between an electrode and an electrolyte*. When a potential is applied, ions from the electrolyte accumulate at the electrode surface, forming two thin layers of opposite charge (one on the electrode side, one on the electrolyte side) separated by an atomic-scale distance ($\approx 0.3$ to $0.8 \, \text{nm}$). Because the separation is so small, the resulting capacitance per unit area is *several orders of magnitude higher* than in a conventional capacitor.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of an Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC). Also explain why its capacitance is orders of magnitude higher than a conventional capacitor.*

**Model Answer Outline:**
- **Construction:** Two activated-carbon electrodes, two aluminium current collectors, porous separator, organic/aqueous electrolyte. Include labelled diagram.
- **Working:** On charging, anions migrate to positive electrode and cations to negative electrode, forming two Helmholtz double layers. Total cell capacitance is series combination of the two.
- **Why ultra-high C:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. The activated carbon has effective surface area $A \approx 2,000 \, \text{m}^2/\text{g}$ and $d \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$ (about $1,000$ times smaller than the dielectric thickness in a conventional electrolytic capacitor). Hence $C$ increases by a factor of $10^6$ to $10^9$.

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]**
*A $2,700 \, \text{F}$ ultra-capacitor module is rated at $V = 2.7 \, \text{V}$ with $R_{\text{ESR}} = 0.0004 \, \Omega$. Calculate:*
*(i) The energy stored in Wh.*
*(ii) The maximum deliverable power in kW.*
*(iii) The time constant of the cell.*

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Energy stored:**

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2,700 \cdot (2.7)^{2}
$$

$$
E \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 2,700 \cdot 7.29 \;=\; 9,841.5 \, \text{J}
$$

Converting to Wh:

$$
E_{\text{Wh}} \;=\; \frac{9,841.5}{3,600} \;\approx\; 2.73 \, \text{Wh}
$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum power:**

$$
P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{V^{2}}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}} \;=\; \frac{(2.7)^{2}}{4 \cdot 0.0004}
$$

$$
P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{7.29}{0.0016} \;=\; 4,556.25 \, \text{W} \;\approx\; 4.56 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Part (iii) — Time constant:**

$$
\tau \;=\; R_{\text{ESR}} \cdot C \;=\; 0.0004 \cdot 2,700 \;=\; 1.08 \, \text{s}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Compare ultra-capacitors and lithium-ion batteries on the basis of: (i) energy density, (ii) power density, (iii) cycle life, (iv) charge time, and (v) Ragone plot position. Why are ultra-capacitors preferred for regenerative braking in HEVs?*

**Model Answer Outline:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Ultra-capacitor | Li-ion Battery |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy density | $5$–$10 \, \text{Wh/kg}$ | $150$–$250 \, \text{Wh/kg}$ |
| 2 | Power density | $5,000$–$10,000 \, \text{W/kg}$ | $250$–$1,000 \, \text{W/kg}$ |
| 3 | Cycle life | $> 500,000$ | $500$–$2,000$ |
| 4 | Charge time | $1$ to $30 \, \text{s}$ | $30 \, \text{min}$ to $5 \, \text{h}$ |
| 5 | Ragone position | High-power / low-energy quadrant | Mid-power / high-energy quadrant |

**Why preferred for regenerative braking:**
During regenerative braking, the energy is recovered in *short, high-power pulses* (typically $5$ to $10$ seconds). Ultra-capacitors can absorb these pulses with their *high power density* and *fast charge acceptance* without undergoing the cycle-life penalty a battery would suffer. They are also more efficient at *partial state-of-charge operation*, which is exactly the regime in which regenerative braking occurs.

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]**
*Explain the role of ultra-capacitors in the following HEV subsystems, with one-line justification each:*
*(i) Cold-start assist for fuel cell vehicles.*
*(ii) Acceleration boost in a parallel hybrid.*
*(iii) Voltage stabilization on the DC bus.*
*(iv) Peak-shaving on the traction battery.*

**Model Answer Outline:**
- **(i) Cold-start assist:** Fuel cells cannot deliver high current at sub-zero temperatures; the ultra-capacitor supplies the cranking/auxiliary load until the fuel cell warms up.
- **(ii) Acceleration boost:** During hard acceleration, the ultra-capacitor provides *peak current* in parallel with the battery, doubling the available power to the inverter.
- **(iii) Voltage stabilization:** Sudden load changes cause DC-bus voltage transients; the ultra-capacitor acts as a *low-impedance buffer* and clamps the voltage.
- **(iv) Peak-shaving:** The ultra-capacitor absorbs peak loads, so the battery operates at a *smoothed, near-constant current*, doubling its cycle life.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ultra-Capacitor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ in $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$** — This alone costs 2 to 3 marks. Always write the formula *first*, then substitute.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Energy is computed in Joules, but vehicle applications need **Watt-hours**. Divide by **$3,600$**, not $3,000$. Examiners *deliberately* trap students here.
> 3. **Confusing pseudo-capacitor with EDLC:** Pseudo-capacitors use *faradaic* (redox) reactions; EDLCs are strictly *non-faradaic*. Mixing them up costs the *definition mark* (1 mark) in any 3-mark question.
> 4. **Ignoring the series combination:** The cell has *two* double layers in series, so $C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{dl}$. Many students write $C_{\text{cell}} = C_{dl}$ and lose 1 to 2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning Ragone plot:** Any *comparison* question without the Ragone plot comment is *incomplete* in the examiner's view — guaranteed loss of 1 mark.
> 6. **Treating $R_{\text{ESR}}$ as negligible:** In a $P_{\max}$ calculation, $R_{\text{ESR}}$ *cannot* be ignored. It is the *very* parameter that limits power.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ultra-capacitor = EDLC = Supercapacitor** — all three terms refer to the same device.
- **Charge storage mechanism = non-faradaic (electrostatic)**, *not* chemical.
- **$C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$** is the *master equation*; nano-scale $d$ and high surface-area $A$ are the *two design levers*.
- **$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$** — energy scales with *voltage squared*, so operating at the *maximum allowed cell voltage* is critical.
- **$P_{\max} = \frac{V^{2}}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}}$** — the *lower* the $R_{\text{ESR}}$, the *higher* the power.
- **Ragone plot position:** High-power, low-energy — *complement* to batteries, not a replacement.
- **Cycle life:** $> 500,000$ cycles — virtually maintenance-free for the vehicle's life.
- **Self-discharge is high** ($10$% to $20$% per day) — *unsuitable* as a long-term energy store.
- **Operating cell voltage:** $2.5$ to $2.7 \, \text{V}$ per cell (organic electrolyte).
- **Two double layers in series:** $C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{dl}$.
- **Pseudo-capacitors** use faradaic redox reactions and have *higher energy density* but *lower cycle life* than pure EDLCs.
- **Hybrid capacitors** (e.g., Li-ion capacitor) combine one EDLC electrode and one battery-type electrode to *bridge* the Ragone plot gap.
- **In HEVs:** ultra-capacitor handles *short, high-power transients*; battery handles *steady, high-energy demand*.
- **Formula unit conversions:** $1 \, \text{Wh} = 3,600 \, \text{J}$; $1 \, \text{kW} = 1,000 \, \text{W}$.
- **Time constant** $\tau = R_{\text{ESR}} \cdot C$ — governs how *quickly* the cell can respond to a load change.
- **Major manufacturers:** Maxwell Technologies (now Tesla), Nesscap, Skeleton Technologies, Eaton, Panasonic.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "**EDLC stores energy in *charge separation*, not in *chemical bonds*; therefore it is *fast and durable* but *energy-poor* — and that is exactly why an HEV uses it alongside a battery.**" Memorise this sentence. It is the single sentence that summarises Module 3 in one breath.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel-based Energy Storage and its Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flywheel-based Energy Storage and its Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)** is a kinetic energy storage device that stores mechanical energy in the form of rotational momentum of a spinning mass (rotor). The stored energy is given by the classical relation between kinetic energy and angular velocity, and can be released or absorbed almost instantaneously by varying the speed of rotation through an integrated motor–generator unit. In the context of hybrid and electric vehicles, the FESS acts as a high-power, short-duration mechanical buffer that complements the primary electrochemical storage (battery or ultracapacitor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A flywheel is a mechanical *bidirectional* energy storage device — it can *absorb* braking energy (charging) and *deliver* traction energy (discharging) through a common motor-generator set, making it functionally equivalent to an electromechanical capacitor.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a child on a playground spinning a heavy merry-go-round. To start it spinning, you must push hard (energy input). Once spinning, the merry-go-round keeps rotating for a long time because of its *rotational inertia*. If a child tries to stop it, the wheel pushes back forcefully (energy release). 

A **flywheel works on exactly this same principle**, but engineered for extreme speeds and stored energy densities:
- The **heavier and faster** it spins, the more energy is stored.
- Modern automotive flywheels are sealed in a **vacuum chamber** to eliminate air drag (windage losses), and levitated on **magnetic bearings** to remove rolling friction — letting them spin at tens of thousands of RPM.
- The faster the spin, the more important the **material strength** becomes — beyond a critical speed, the rim will literally fly apart due to centrifugal (hoop) stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants to Remember:**
> - Specific energy of modern carbon-fiber composite flywheels: up to **$\mathbf{200\ Wh/kg}$** (theoretical max for advanced composites).
> - Specific power: up to **$\mathbf{5{,}000{-}20{,}000\ W/kg}$** — orders of magnitude higher than batteries.
> - Cycle life: **> 100,000 deep cycles** with negligible degradation.

### 1.3 Classification of Flywheels

| Class | Rotor Type | Speed Range | Material | Energy Density |
|-------|-----------|-------------|----------|----------------|
| **Low-Speed FESS** | Steel cylindrical disc | 3,000 – 10,000 rpm | Forged/rolled steel | 5 – 30 Wh/kg |
| **High-Speed FESS** | Composite thin rim | 30,000 – 100,000 rpm | Carbon-fiber/epoxy | 50 – 200 Wh/kg |

Modern KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems) in motorsport and heavy hybrid vehicles use **high-speed composite flywheels** because the energy stored scales with the *square* of the rotational speed, but only *linearly* with mass.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics Behind Storage

A flywheel stores energy as **rotational kinetic energy**. The energy equation is universal:

$$E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2}$$

where the symbols and their *engineering significance* are:

- **$E_{kin}$** — Stored kinetic energy in **Joules (J)**. This is the *useful work* extractable.
- **$I$** — Mass moment of inertia about the spin axis, in **kg·m²**. Depends on geometry (rim vs. solid disc vs. composite layup). 
  - *Intuition:* It is the rotational analogue of mass — a measure of "resistance to change in rotational motion."
- **$\omega$** — Angular velocity in **rad/s**. Because $E \propto \omega^2$, doubling the speed **quadruples** the stored energy. This is why engineers push flywheels to extreme speeds.

### 2.2 Geometric Forms of the Rotor

Different geometries store energy differently. For a rotor of mass $m$ and outer radius $r$:

| Geometry | Moment of Inertia $I$ | Why It Matters |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Thin rim (hoop)** | $I = m\,r^{2}$ | Maximises $I$ per unit mass; classic composite flywheel |
| **Solid uniform disc** | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,r^{2}$ | Easier to manufacture; lower $I$ for same $m,r$ |
| **Solid sphere** | $I = \tfrac{2}{5}\,m\,r^{2}$ | Theoretical maximum; rarely used (manufacturing limits) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A thin rim has **twice the energy storage** of a solid disc of the same mass and radius because all the mass is concentrated where the tangential velocity is highest. Composite flywheels are designed as thin rims wrapped with carbon fiber to exploit this.

### 2.3 Material Strength Constraint — The Speed Limit

As the flywheel spins faster, centrifugal force tries to burst the rim apart. The **hoop stress** for a thin rim spinning at angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r^{2}$$

where $\rho$ is material density (kg/m³). For safe operation, $\sigma_{hoop} \le \sigma_{allowable}$. This single constraint defines the **maximum permissible speed** of the flywheel and therefore the **upper bound on stored energy**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$ | Stored kinetic energy | J (or Wh) | Energy capacity of FESS |
| 2 | $I_{rim} = m\,r^{2}$ | Rim inertia | kg·m² | Energy sizing |
| 3 | $I_{disc} = \tfrac{1}{2}m\,r^{2}$ | Disc inertia | kg·m² | Energy sizing |
| 4 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r^{2}$ | Rim tensile stress | Pa (N/m²) | Speed limit constraint |
| 5 | $e_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{2\,\rho}$ | Max specific energy of thin rim | J/kg (or Wh/kg) | Material selection |
| 6 | $e_{max,disc} = \dfrac{2\,\sigma_{allow}}{(3+\nu)\,\rho}$ | Max specific energy of disc | J/kg | Disc design |
| 7 | $P = \tau\,\omega$ | Instantaneous power | W | Power delivery rating |
| 8 | $\tau = I\,\alpha$ | Torque from angular accel. | N·m | Transient sizing |
| 9 | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % | System comparison |
| 10 | $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})$ | Energy exchange | J | Charge/discharge analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In formula 5, the term $e_{max} = \sigma_{allow}/(2\rho)$ is the **single most important result** of flywheel analysis for KTU exams. It shows that energy density depends *only on the material*, not on geometry or speed. This is the "knee-bone" connection between mechanical design and material science.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **F1 KERS (2009–):** The Williams F1 team used a flywheel KERS with specific energy approaching **40 Wh/kg** at 60,000 rpm, providing 60 kW of regenerative boost for 6.6 seconds per lap.
- **Mazda & Chrysler test vehicles:** Composite flywheels with energy density of **60 Wh/kg** and continuous power of **150 kW**.
- **Grid & UPS:** Beacon Power (USA) deployed 100 kWh flywheel units for grid frequency regulation, completing > 100,000 cycles with < 1% performance loss.
- **Hybrid buses & trucks:** Flywheel hybrids in series-hybrid city buses recover 30–40% of braking energy, dramatically reducing urban fuel consumption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in a Thin Rim Flywheel

**Starting Point:** The general expression for rotational kinetic energy.

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2}$$

**Step 1 —** Substitute the moment of inertia for a thin rim ($I = m\,r^{2}$). This is valid when all the mass is concentrated at the mean rim radius (the composite-flywheel approximation).

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,(m\,r^{2})\,\omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}$$

**Step 2 —** Introduce the tangential velocity at the rim, $v = r\,\omega$. Squaring gives $v^{2} = r^{2}\omega^{2}$, so $r^{2}\omega^{2} = v^{2}$. Substituting:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2}$$

**Interpretation:** The stored energy in a thin rim flywheel has the *exact same form* as the kinetic energy of a translating mass. This is a beautiful and important result — the rim flies apart in a tangential "linear" trajectory, so it behaves as a translating particle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Specific Energy of a Thin Rim

**Goal:** Express the energy storage *per unit mass* (specific energy) purely in terms of material properties.

**Step 1 —** Start with the energy expression for a thin rim:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}$$

**Step 2 —** Divide both sides by the mass $m$ to get specific energy $e$:

$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2}\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}$$

**Step 3 —** From the hoop-stress equation $\sigma = \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r^{2}$, solve for $\omega^{2}\,r^{2}$:

$$\omega^{2}\,r^{2} = \frac{\sigma}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute this back into the specific energy equation:

$$e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\rho} = \frac{\sigma}{2\,\rho}$$

**Step 5 —** To find the *maximum* specific energy, set $\sigma = \sigma_{allowable}$ (the design tensile strength with appropriate safety factor):

$$\boxed{\;e_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{allowable}}{2\,\rho}\;}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The factor of 2 in the denominator comes from the rim geometry; for a uniform disc, the corresponding result is $e_{max} = 2\sigma_{allow}/[(3+\nu)\rho]$, which is *lower* than the rim by the factor $4/(3+\nu) \approx 1.16$ (using $\nu = 0.3$ for steel). The thin rim is therefore intrinsically superior for energy storage.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power and Energy Delivery Analysis

Consider a FESS that discharges from $\omega_{max}$ down to $\omega_{min}$. The usable energy is:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,(\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})$$

If the discharge must be completed in time $t_d$ at approximately constant power $P_{avg}$:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{\Delta E}{t_{d}} = \frac{I\,(\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})}{2\,t_{d}}$$

**Step-by-step numerical example** (modelled after KTU numerical problems):

> **Given:** $I = 5\ \text{kg·m}^{2}$, $\omega_{max} = 6000\ \text{rpm} = 628.3\ \text{rad/s}$, $\omega_{min} = 3000\ \text{rpm} = 314.2\ \text{rad/s}$, discharge time $t_d = 10\ \text{s}$.

**Step 1 —** Compute $\omega_{max}^{2}$ and $\omega_{min}^{2}$:

$$\omega_{max}^{2} = (628.3)^{2} = 394{,}784\ \text{rad}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$\omega_{min}^{2} = (314.2)^{2} = 98{,}722\ \text{rad}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the energy released:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2}\,(5)\,(394{,}784 - 98{,}722) = 2.5 \times 296{,}062 = 740{,}155\ \text{J} \approx 0.206\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the average power for 10-second discharge:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{740{,}155}{10} = 74{,}016\ \text{W} \approx 74\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 —** Compute the average discharge torque:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{P_{avg}}{\omega_{avg}} = \frac{74{,}016}{(628.3 + 314.2)/2} = \frac{74{,}016}{471.25} \approx 157\ \text{N·m}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Charge / Discharge Behaviour

| Phase | Speed Change | Energy Flow | Motor-Generator Mode | Torque Sign | Power Sign |
|-------|-------------|-------------|----------------------|-------------|------------|
| **Charging (regen braking)** | $\omega$ increases | Kinetic → Rotational | Generator (driven by drivetrain) | Opposes motion | $P > 0$ (absorbed) |
| **Discharging (boost/traction)** | $\omega$ decreases | Rotational → Kinetic | Motor (drives drivetrain) | Aids motion | $P > 0$ (delivered) |
