| **Idle (storage hold)** | $\omega$ constant (slight decay) | Losses only | Standby | $\tau \approx 0$ | $P \approx 0$ |
| **Top-up hold** | Constant via motor | Electrical → Rotational | Motor (small input) | Maintain $\omega$ | $P > 0$ (input) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** The same M/G unit operates in all phases. The *direction* of power flow decides whether the FESS is charging or discharging. This is the *electromechanical analogy* of a rechargeable battery.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Round-Trip Efficiency

The total energy lost per cycle comes from three principal loss mechanisms:

$$E_{loss,total} = E_{friction} + E_{windage} + E_{electrical}$$

**Step 1 —** Bearing/friction loss (assumed viscous, proportional to $\omega$):

$$P_{fric} = b_{f}\,\omega \quad\Rightarrow\quad E_{fric} = b_{f}\int\omega\,dt$$

**Step 2 —** Windage loss (residual gas drag, $\propto \omega^{3}$ for turbulent flow):

$$P_{wind} = c_{w}\,\omega^{3}$$

**Step 3 —** Electrical losses in M/G and power electronics (assume fraction $\eta_e$ of energy handled):

$$\eta_e = 1 - k_{e}\quad\text{where }k_{e}\text{ is loss fraction per transfer}$$

**Step 4 —** Round-trip efficiency:

$$\eta_{RT} = 1 - \frac{E_{loss,total}}{E_{charged}} = \frac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$$

For a well-designed FESS: $\eta_{RT} = 80\% - 95\%$, which is *higher* than most batteries (60–85% for Li-ion under high-C cycling).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — FESS Architecture for HEV Application

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a flywheel energy storage system integrated into a hybrid-electric drivetrain. It illustrates the energy and signal pathways between each subsystem.

```mermaid
graph TD
    DRV[Vehicle Drivetrain and Wheels] --> MGU1[Motor Generator Unit MGU]
    MGU1 --> PCE1[Power Converter and Inverter]
    PCE1 --> BAT[Primary Battery Pack Slow Energy Reservoir]
    PCE1 --> ROT[Flywheel Rotor High Speed Mass]
    ROT --> BRG[Magnetic Bearings and Spindles]
    BRG --> VAC[Vacuum Containment Chamber]
    VAC --> HSE[Containment Housing Safety Shell]
    ROT --> SNS[Rotor Speed and Vibration Sensors]
    SNS --> ECU[Vehicle Energy Management Controller]
    ECU --> PCE1
    ECU --> MGU1
    BRG --> CPL[Cooling and Lubrication Subsystem]
    PCE1 --> CPL
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The `MGU` (motor-generator unit) is the *electrical port* of the flywheel — it converts electrical energy ↔ rotational energy.
- The `PCE` (power converter) regulates voltage and current to match the high-voltage DC bus of the vehicle.
- The `ROT` (rotor) is the kinetic energy reservoir, isolated from atmosphere.
- The `ECU` orchestrates the entire system, deciding when to charge (during braking) and discharge (during acceleration).
- The `BAT` is shown in parallel to indicate that the flywheel handles *transient high-power* events, while the battery handles *steady-state energy*.

---

### 4.2 Functional Energy-Flow Schematic — Charge and Discharge Cycles

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CHARGE[Charging Phase Vehicle Braking]
        K1[Vehicle Kinetic Energy] -- Regen Brake --> K2[Flywheel Spin Up]
    end

    subgraph DISCHG[Discharging Phase Vehicle Acceleration]
        D1[Flywheel Spin Down] -- Boost --> D2[Vehicle Traction Energy]
    end

    subgraph IDLE[Idle Storage Hold Mode]
        I1[Flywheel Maintains Speed] -- Small Motor Input --> I2[Overcome Friction Windage]
    end

    CHARGE --> IDLE
    IDLE --> DISCHG
    DISCHG --> IDLE
    IDLE --> CHARGE
```

**Reading the Energy-Flow Schematic:**
- Each phase is a *closed loop* with the central `IDLE` state, mimicking how a battery alternates between rest and active states.
- The flywheel is **bidirectional**: the same physical device absorbs and releases energy by reversing the torque on the MGU.

---

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```
        Containment Housing
        |-----------------------------------------------|
        |   Vacuum Enclosure (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar)         |
        |   |-----------------------------------------|  |
        |   |  Upper Magnetic Bearing (radial)        |  |
        |   |        |                                |  |
        |   |   [   Composite Rim Rotor (CF-Epoxy)  ]  |  |
        |   |        |                                |  |
        |   |  Lower Magnetic Bearing (axial+radial) |  |
        |   |        |                                |  |
        |   |   [ Integrated Motor/Generator Stator ] |  |
        |   |-----------------------------------------|  |
        |   Sensors + Condition Monitoring               |
        |------------------------------------------------|
```

This ASCII schematic illustrates the **physical layering** of a typical high-speed composite flywheel: from the outer safety shell inward through vacuum, magnetic suspension, rotor mass, and finally the integrated M/G. Every layer serves a specific function — containment, friction elimination, energy storage, and energy conversion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define specific energy of a flywheel. Why is it considered a key material-selection parameter in flywheel design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Specific energy* of a flywheel is defined as the kinetic energy stored per unit mass of the rotor, expressed in J/kg or Wh/kg. Mathematically,

$$e = \frac{E_{kin}}{m} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{2\,\rho}\quad\text{(for a thin rim)}$$

It is a key material-selection parameter because, from the derivation, the maximum specific energy depends *only* on the ratio $\sigma_{allow}/\rho$ of the rotor material. Geometry and rotational speed do not enter the *upper bound* of energy density. Hence, to maximise energy storage for a given mass, designers must choose materials with the highest possible strength-to-density ratio — for example, **carbon-fiber composites** ($\sigma/\rho \approx 1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$) over **forged steel** ($\sigma/\rho \approx 0.25 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$). **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark material-selection reasoning.)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between low-speed and high-speed flywheel energy storage systems based on rotor material, speed range, and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Low-Speed FESS | High-Speed FESS |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Rotor material** | Steel (forged/rolled) | Carbon-fiber/epoxy composite |
| **Speed range** | 3,000 – 10,000 rpm | 30,000 – 100,000 rpm |
| **Specific energy** | 5 – 30 Wh/kg | 50 – 200 Wh/kg |
| **Specific power** | Low–moderate | Very high |
| **Bearings** | Conventional rolling | Magnetic levitation |
| **Containment** | Standard | Vacuum + burst shell |
| **Typical application** | Industrial UPS, grid storage | EV/HEV KERS, aerospace, F1 |

The high-speed FESS achieves superior energy density because $E \propto \omega^{2}$ — the higher permissible speed (enabled by composite strength) quadratically boosts storage. **(3 marks: 2 marks table comparison + 1 mark concluding justification.)**

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum specific energy of a thin rim flywheel** in terms of the material's allowable tensile strength and density. State the engineering significance of this result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy of thin rim (1 mark):**

The rotational kinetic energy of a thin rim of mass $m$ and mean radius $r$ spinning at angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,(m\,r^{2})\,\omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Hoop-stress constraint (1 mark):**

The hoop (tensile) stress in the rim due to centrifugal force is:

$$\sigma = \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r^{2}$$

where $\rho$ is the material density.

**Step 3 — Express $\omega^{2}r^{2}$ in terms of $\sigma$ and $\rho$ (1 mark):**

$$\omega^{2}\,r^{2} = \frac{\sigma}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Specific energy (1 mark):**

$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2}\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2} = \frac{\sigma}{2\,\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum specific energy (1 mark):**

Substituting the allowable stress $\sigma_{allow}$:

$$\boxed{\;e_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{2\,\rho}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**

For carbon-fiber composite with $\sigma_{allow} = 2.5\ \text{GPa}$ and $\rho = 1600\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$:

$$e_{max} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 1600} = 7.8125 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/kg} \approx 217\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering significance (1 mark):**

The result shows that the energy density of a flywheel is a **material property** — independent of rotor geometry, size, or speed. Therefore, material selection is the *single most important design decision*. High $\sigma/\rho$ materials (carbon composites, T1000-grade fibers) outperform metals by factors of 5–10, justifying their use despite higher cost.

---

**(b)** A **composite flywheel** has a rim of mass **200 kg** and mean radius **0.5 m**, spinning at **20,000 rpm**. The material density is **1600 kg/m³** and the allowable tensile strength is **2.5 GPa**. Calculate: **(i)** the stored kinetic energy, **(ii)** the hoop stress, **(iii)** the specific energy, and **(iv)** the percentage utilisation of theoretical maximum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert angular velocity (0.5 mark):**

$$\omega = 20{,}000\ \text{rpm} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 2\pi}{60} = 2094.4\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Stored kinetic energy (1.5 marks):**

$$I = m\,r^{2} = 200 \times (0.5)^{2} = 50\ \text{kg·m}^{2}$$

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times (2094.4)^{2} = 25 \times 4{,}386{,}496 = 1.097 \times 10^{8}\ \text{J}$$

$$E \approx 109.7\ \text{MJ} \approx 30.46\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Hoop stress (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma = \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r^{2} = 1600 \times (2094.4)^{2} \times (0.5)^{2}$$

$$\sigma = 1600 \times 4{,}386{,}496 \times 0.25 = 1.755 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa} = 1.755\ \text{GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Specific energy (1 mark):**

$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1.097 \times 10^{8}}{200} = 5.485 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/kg} \approx 152.4\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical maximum and utilisation (1.5 marks):**

$$e_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{2\,\rho} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 1600} = 7.8125 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/kg} \approx 217\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

$$\text{Utilisation} = \frac{152.4}{217} \times 100\% = 70.2\%$$

This is the operating margin — a real flywheel cannot be run right up to the theoretical limit because of safety factors, fatigue, and stress concentrations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the major components and working of a flywheel energy storage system (FESS) used in hybrid vehicles. Discuss the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │   Battery Pack  →  DC Bus  →  Power Converter  →  M/G     │
   │                              (Inverter)        (Stator)    │
   │                                                    │        │
   │                                              (Rotor Shaft) │
   │                                                    │        │
   │                              ┌─────────────────────▼──┐     │
   │                              │  Flywheel Rotor         │     │
   │                              │  (Composite Rim)        │     │
   │                              └────────┬──────────────┘     │
   │                                       │                     │
   │                          Magnetic Bearings + Vacuum         │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↓
                 Vehicle Drivetrain
```

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

During vehicle **braking** (or deceleration), the M/G acts as a **generator**: kinetic energy from the drivetrain spins the flywheel up, converting translational KE to rotational KE. During **acceleration**, the M/G acts as a **motor**: it draws rotational energy from the flywheel's deceleration and injects it back into the drivetrain, providing a power boost.

**Subsystem Roles (2 marks):**

| Subsystem | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Flywheel Rotor** | Stores kinetic energy as $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$ |
| **Motor/Generator (M/G)** | Bidirectional electromechanical energy conversion |
| **Power Converter (Inverter)** | Matches high-speed M/G voltage to DC bus |
| **Magnetic Bearings** | Contactless suspension — zero rolling friction |
| **Vacuum Chamber** | Eliminates windage (air drag) losses |
| **Containment Shell** | Safety in case of rotor burst |
| **Sensors + ECU** | Speed, vibration, temperature monitoring + control |
| **Cooling System** | Removes heat from M/G, bearings, electronics |

---

**(b)** A hybrid bus uses a **flywheel KERS** to recover braking energy. The bus has a mass of **8,000 kg** and decelerates from **50 km/h to 20 km/h** in **12 seconds**. The flywheel has a moment of inertia of **$I = 12\ \text{kg·m}^{2}$** and is initially at rest. Calculate: **(i)** the kinetic energy recovered from the vehicle, **(ii)** the final flywheel speed (assuming 100% transfer efficiency), and **(iii)** the average power transferred to the flywheel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to m/s (0.5 mark):**

$$v_{1} = 50\ \text{km/h} = 13.89\ \text{m/s}$$

$$v_{2} = 20\ \text{km/h} = 5.56\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy recovered (2 marks):**

$$\Delta E_{vehicle} = \frac{1}{2}\,M\,(v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2})$$

$$\Delta E_{vehicle} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8000 \times (13.89^{2} - 5.56^{2})$$

$$\Delta E_{vehicle} = 4000 \times (192.93 - 30.91) = 4000 \times 162.02 = 648{,}080\ \text{J} \approx 648\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Final flywheel speed (2 marks):**

At 100% transfer efficiency, all of $\Delta E_{vehicle}$ is stored in the flywheel:

$$\Delta E_{vehicle} = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega_{f}^{2} - 0 = \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega_{f}^{2}$$

$$\omega_{f}^{2} = \frac{2\,\Delta E_{vehicle}}{I} = \frac{2 \times 648{,}080}{12} = 108{,}013\ \text{rad}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$\omega_{f} = 328.7\ \text{rad/s} = \frac{328.7 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 3{,}140\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Average power transferred (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{\Delta E_{vehicle}}{t_{brake}} = \frac{648{,}080}{12} = 54{,}007\ \text{W} \approx 54\ \text{kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The flywheel absorbs **648 kJ** over 12 seconds at an average of **54 kW**, a power level that would otherwise stress the primary battery but is handled effortlessly by a compact FESS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion rpm → rad/s.** A KTU examiner will deduct 0.5–1 mark if you plug $N$ (in rpm) directly into $\omega^{2}$ without converting. Always: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
> 2. **Confusing hoop-stress formula.** The expression $\sigma = \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r^{2}$ is for a **thin rim only**. For a solid disc the formula is different and includes Poisson's ratio. Do not mix them up.
> 3. **Using mass instead of mass moment of inertia in $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$.** A common error: writing $E = \tfrac{1}{2}m\omega^{2}$ instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$. This is dimensionally wrong.
> 4. **Ignoring the factor of 1/2 in $e = \sigma/(2\rho)$.** Students often write $e = \sigma/\rho$ and lose 1 full mark. The factor of 2 comes from the rim geometry.
> 5. **Omitting engineering significance.** A derivation question that *only* lists the algebraic steps without a concluding physical interpretation (e.g., "this shows material selection is the key design lever") typically loses 1–2 marks under the KTU 2024 outcome-based grading scheme.
> 6. **Mixing up charge and discharge signs.** In energy-balance problems, clearly state whether the flywheel is *gaining* or *losing* energy — sign errors in $\Delta E$ lead to nonsensical final answers.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$ — energy depends on the *square* of speed; this is the most exploited feature of flywheels.
- ✅ **Thin-Rim Result:** $e_{max} = \sigma_{allow}/(2\rho)$ — material-property-limited, geometry-independent energy density.
- ✅ **Hoop Stress:** $\sigma = \rho\omega^{2}r^{2}$ — the *governing design constraint* that limits maximum safe speed.
- ✅ **Rim vs Disc:** Thin rim stores **2×** the energy of a solid disc of equal mass and radius.
- ✅ **Charge/Discharge:** M/G unit acts as generator (braking) and motor (acceleration) — the flywheel is **bidirectional**.
- ✅ **Power Rating:** $P = \tau\omega$ — instantaneous; M/G can deliver very high power for short bursts.
- ✅ **Round-Trip Efficiency:** 80–95% (FESS) vs 60–85% (Li-ion at high C-rates) — a key advantage.
- ✅ **Cycle Life:** > 100,000 deep cycles with negligible degradation — far exceeds batteries.
- ✅ **Losses:** Bearing friction (viscous), windage ($\propto \omega^{3}$), and M/G electrical losses.
- ✅ **Safety:** Composite flywheels must be enclosed in a **burst-containment shell**; failure mode is violent fragmentation at high $\omega$.
- ✅ **Applications:** F1 KERS, hybrid buses, grid frequency regulation, satellite attitude control, UPS systems.
- ✅ **Comparison to remember:** Specific *power* of FESS >> batteries; specific *energy* of FESS ≈ Li-ion (composite type) — flywheels win on power, batteries win on long-duration energy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel Accumulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flywheel Accumulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flywheel Accumulator** (also termed a **Kinetic Energy Storage System (KESS)** or **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)**) is an electromechanical energy storage device that stores kinetic energy in a high-inertia rotating mass (the rotor/flywheel) and converts it to electrical energy on demand through an integrated **Motor-Generator Unit (MGU)**. In hybrid and fuel cell vehicles, it functions as a high-power-density, short-term mechanical "auxiliary buffer," complementing electrochemical batteries and supercapacitors during transient acceleration and regenerative braking events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Flywheel accumators are explicitly listed under *Energy Storage Systems in Hybrid Vehicles*. A KTU answer must classify them as a **Mechanical Energy Storage System**, distinguishing them clearly from electrochemical (battery) and electrostatic (supercapacitor) storage. Marks are routinely awarded for correctly identifying the storage domain (Mechanical ↔ Kinetic ↔ Rotational).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Spinning Top Analogy**
> Imagine a child's spinning top: the harder you spin it, the longer it keeps rotating and the more "push" it can deliver if you suddenly grab it to do work. Now scale this idea to an engineering-grade steel or carbon-fiber composite rotor spinning at **30,000 – 100,000 rpm** inside an evacuated chamber, magnetically levitated on frictionless bearings. This is a flywheel accumulator.
>
> - **Charging (storing energy)** = the motor-generator *spins up* the rotor using surplus vehicle/braking energy.
> - **Discharging (releasing energy)** = the same machine acts as a generator, *slowing down* the rotor and converting its kinetic motion back into electricity for traction.

This explains why flywheels are superb for **rapid charge/discharge cycling** (e.g., 100% in < 60 seconds) but less suited for *long-duration* storage where batteries excel.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Specific energy of flywheel | $e_{f}$ | **5 – 100 Wh/kg** |
| Specific power | $p_{f}$ | **1,000 – 5,000 W/kg** |
| Energy stored (kinetic) | $E_{k}$ | 0.01 – 5 kWh (vehicle-scale) |
| Charge/discharge efficiency | $\eta$ | **85 – 95 %** |
| Cycle life | $N$ | > **10⁶ cycles** (theoretically unlimited) |
| Vacuum housing pressure | $P_{v}$ | **< 10⁻³ mbar** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **tensile strength of the rotor material** ($\sigma_{ts}$) is the single most important design metric — it caps the maximum permissible tip speed $v_{tip}$ of the rim, and therefore the maximum storable energy per unit mass.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Principle

The energy stored in any rotating mass is given by its **translational kinetic energy analog**:

$$E_{k} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2}$$

where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia about the spin axis (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

Because energy scales with the **square of speed**, doubling the rpm yields a **quadrupling** of stored energy. This is the fundamental engineering motivation to spin the rotor as fast as material limits allow.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps

The flywheel accumulator operates in three distinct phases inside an HEV/FCEV:

**A. Charging Phase (Energy Capture)**
- Surplus energy available: from **regenerative braking**, **excess ICE output**, or **excess fuel cell output**.
- MGU operates as a **motor** — draws electrical power from the bus.
- Torque is applied to accelerate the rotor: $\tau = I \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$.
- Power input: $P_{in} = \tau \cdot \omega$.
- Rotor angular velocity rises from $\omega_{min}$ to $\omega_{max}$.

**B. Storage (Holding) Phase**
- Rotor is maintained in near-vacuum to eliminate aerodynamic drag.
- Magnetic (active or passive) bearings remove mechanical contact friction.
- Standing losses are extremely low (typically < **1–2 %/hour**).
- Energy is held "ready" with negligible parasitic drain.

**C. Discharging Phase (Energy Delivery)**
- MGU switches to **generator** mode.
- Back-EMF drives current into the vehicle DC bus (typically 200–800 V).
- Rotor decelerates, releasing $E_{k}$.
- Power output: $P_{out} = \tau_{gen} \cdot \omega$.

### 2.3 The Two Material Classes of Flywheels

| Property | **Low-Speed Flywheel (Steel Rotor)** | **High-Speed Flywheel (Composite Rotor)** |
|---|---|---|
| Operating speed | 3,000 – 10,000 rpm | 30,000 – 100,000 rpm |
| Rotor material | High-strength steel / alloy | Carbon-fiber/epoxy composite |
| Material tensile strength | ~ 1,500 MPa | ~ **4,000 – 7,000 MPa** |
| Tip speed limit $v_{tip}$ | < 500 m/s | < **900 – 1,500 m/s** |
| Energy density | Low (≈ 5–20 Wh/kg) | High (≈ 50–100 Wh/kg) |
| Safety on failure | Containment usually sufficient | Burial-type containment required |
| Bearing | Conventional (ball/roller) | Magnetic levitation required |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the tip speed is critical:** When a flywheel fails (overspeed or rim burst), the bursting rim stores kinetic energy proportional to $v_{tip}^{2}$. The empirical **"specific energy" rule** states that stored energy per unit rotor mass scales as $\sigma_{ts}/\rho$, where $\rho$ is material density. Composites win because of their high $\sigma_{ts}/\rho$ ratio (≈ **2–3 ×** that of steel).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{k} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | Stored kinetic energy |
| 2 | $E_{k} = \dfrac{1}{4} m v_{tip}^{2}$ | Energy expressed in terms of rim-tip speed |
| 3 | $v_{tip} = \omega \cdot r$ | Rim tangential velocity |
| 4 | $E_{k,\max} = K \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\rho}$ | Maximum storable energy (material limit) |
| 5 | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Instantaneous mechanical power |
| 6 | $\eta_{cyc} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | Round-trip efficiency |
| 7 | $E_{usable} = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})$ | Usable energy between speed limits |
| 8 | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha = I \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Torque required for a given acceleration |
| 9 | $P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Power drawn from rotor during discharge |
| 10 | $t_{ch} = \dfrac{I (\omega_{max} - \omega_{min})}{2 P_{in}}$ | Time to charge from min to max speed |
| 11 | $F_{c} = m \omega^{2} r$ | Centripetal (burst) stress contributor |
| 12 | $E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi \rho \sigma_{ts} r^{2} L$ | Thin-rim rotor total energy capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions students forget in exams:**
> 1. The flywheel **cannot store energy at zero speed** — minimum speed $\omega_{min}$ must be maintained.
> 2. The **usable** energy is $E_{max} - E_{min}$, not the full $\frac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^{2}$ — so always subtract the residual.
> 3. Tip speed must satisfy $v_{tip} \le \sqrt{\sigma_{ts}/\rho}$ for safe operation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flywheel accumulators are deployed where **high power for short bursts** is critical:

- **Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems)** — recovered braking energy is stored mechanically and re-deployed for overtaking boosts (~ 60–120 kW for ~ 6 seconds).
- **Rooftop / regenerative elevators** — stores braking energy of a descending cabin.
- **Hybrid city buses (e.g., Flybrid, Torotrak, GKN)** — smooths stop-go duty cycles.
- **Grid frequency regulation (Beacon Power, UK)** — second-by-second kW-level balancing.
- **Satellite attitude control** — momentum/energy wheels in spacecraft.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Rotating Disc Flywheel

**Starting point:** A solid uniform disc of mass $m$, radius $r$, rotating about its central axis with angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 1:** Recall the moment of inertia of a solid disc about its central axis:
$$I_{disc} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,r^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the rotational kinetic energy equation $E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$:
$$E_{k} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}\right) \omega^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the coefficient:
$$E_{k} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\, m\, r^{2}\, \omega^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Use the identity $v_{tip} = r\omega$, so $r^{2}\omega^{2} = v_{tip}^{2}$:
$$E_{k} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\, m\, v_{tip}^{2}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though a disc has its mass distributed throughout the radius, only the rim (outermost elements) contributes to high $v_{tip}$, so thin-rim designs store more energy per kg of material.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Safe Energy from Material Tensile Strength

For a thin rim of radius $r$, mass $m$, material density $\rho$, and tensile strength $\sigma_{ts}$:

**Step 1:** The hoop stress (tangential stress in a rotating thin ring) is:
$$\sigma_{hoop} \;=\; \rho \, v_{tip}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** For safe operation, $\sigma_{hoop} \le \sigma_{ts}$:
$$\rho \, v_{tip}^{2} \;\le\; \sigma_{ts}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the maximum permissible tip speed:
$$v_{tip,\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the kinetic energy equation $E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{4} m v_{tip}^{2}$:
$$E_{k,\max} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\, m \, \dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\rho}$$

**Step 5:** Express energy *per unit mass* (specific energy):
$$e_{specific} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{k,\max}}{m} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\, \dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\rho}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical example (KTU-style):** For a carbon-fiber composite with $\sigma_{ts} = 4{,}000 \text{ MPa}$ and $\rho = 1{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$:
>
> $$v_{tip,\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 10^{9}}{1{,}600}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 1{,}581 \text{ m/s}$$
>
> $$e_{specific} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{4 \times 10^{9}}{1{,}600} \;\approx\; 6.25 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/kg} \;\approx\; 174 \text{ Wh/kg}$$
>
> Hence composite flywheels can theoretically store ≈ **170 Wh/kg** — several times lithium-ion batteries (≈ 150 Wh/kg practical).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Usable Energy Between Two Speed Limits

**Given:** $\omega_{min} \le \omega \le \omega_{max}$.

**Step 1:** Total energy at maximum speed:
$$E_{max} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega_{max}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Residual energy at minimum (avoiding zero to keep rotor operable):
$$E_{min} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega_{min}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Usable (extractable) energy:
$$E_{usable} \;=\; E_{max} - E_{min} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, I \left(\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Define the **depth-of-discharge ratio** $k = \omega_{min}/\omega_{max}$:
$$E_{usable} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega_{max}^{2} \left(1 - k^{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A KTU numerical may give you only $E_{usable}$ and ask you to find $E_{max}$. Students often confuse $E_{usable}$ with total stored energy, costing 2–3 marks.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flywheel vs Battery vs Ultracapacitor

| Attribute | Flywheel | Li-ion Battery | Ultracapacitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy density (Wh/kg) | 5–100 | 100–250 | 5–10 |
| Power density (W/kg) | **1,000–5,000** | 200–1,000 | **5,000–10,000** |
| Cycle life | **> 10⁶** | 1,000–5,000 | **> 10⁶** |
| Charge time | Seconds–minutes | 30–60 min | Seconds |
| Self-discharge (per hour) | 1–2 % | 0.1–0.5 % | 5–10 % |
| Operating temperature sensitivity | Low | High | Moderate |
| Best role in HEV | **High-power bursts** | **Long-term energy** | **Transient peaks** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive Flywheel Accumulator (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vehicle DC Bus 200 to 800 V] --> B[Power Electronics Inverter VSI]
    B --> C[Motor Generator Unit MGU BLDC or PMSM]
    C --> D[Coupling Shaft and CV Joint]
    D --> E[High Speed Flywheel Rotor Composite or Steel]
    E --> F[Magnetic Bearings Active or Passive Levitation]
    E --> G[Vacuum Housing less than 10 power minus 3 mbar]
    F --> H[Position Sensors and Controller]
    H --> B
    G --> H
    E --> I[Containment Ring Burst Safety]
    I --> J[Vehicle Chassis]

    classDef power fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#b4d8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#ffb4b4,stroke:#990000,color:#000

    class A,B,C power
    class D,E,F,G mech
    class H,I,J safety
```

### 4.2 Charge / Discharge State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging : Surplus energy from regen or FC
    Charging --> Full : omega equals omega max
    Full --> Idle : Holding vacuum no loss
    Idle --> Discharging : Acceleration demand
    Discharging --> Empty : omega equals omega min
    Empty --> Charging : Recharge
    Discharging --> Charging : Regen during braking
    Empty --> Idle : Standby

    note right of Charging : Motor mode torque applied
    note right of Discharging : Generator mode back EMF
    note right of Full : Near vacuum low drag
```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Map (KTU Exam Drawing Substitute)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Energy Sources]
        REG[Regenerative Braking]
        FC[Fuel Cell Surplus]
        ICE[ICE Surplus]
    end

    subgraph FLYSYS[Flywheel Accumulator System]
        INV[Inverter MGU Controller]
        MGU[Motor Generator Unit]
        ROTOR[Composite Rotor 50000 rpm]
        BRG[Magnetic Bearings]
        VAC[Vacuum Enclosure]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Vehicle Loads]
        TRAC[Traction Motor Drive]
        AUX[Auxiliaries]
    end

    REG --> INV
    FC --> INV
    ICE --> INV
    INV --> MGU
    MGU --> ROTOR
    BRG --> ROTOR
    VAC --> ROTOR
    ROTOR --> MGU
    MGU --> INV
    INV --> TRAC
    INV --> AUX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** In the exam, draw a **clear block diagram** showing the three domains — *Electrical (Bus, Inverter)*, *Electromechanical (MGU)*, and *Mechanical (Rotor, Bearings, Vacuum)*. Use arrows with directional cues and label signal flows. Examiners award 2–3 marks for a well-labelled block diagram even if derivations are incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a flywheel accumulator. List any two advantages it offers over a chemical battery in hybrid vehicle applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A flywheel accumulator is a **mechanical energy storage device** that stores kinetic energy in a rotating mass and converts it to electrical energy through a motor-generator unit.

**Two advantages over batteries:**
1. **Much higher specific power** (1,000–5,000 W/kg vs 200–1,000 W/kg for Li-ion) — ideal for acceleration bursts.
2. **Near-infinite cycle life** (> 10⁶ cycles) with no chemical degradation, and a round-trip efficiency of 85–95 %.

> Any two correct points = 3 marks. Definition alone = 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the formula for kinetic energy stored in a flywheel. Why is it preferable to operate the rotor at very high speeds rather than increasing its mass?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\, m\, v_{tip}^{2}$$

Increasing **speed** is preferred over increasing **mass** because energy scales with the **square of speed** but **linearly with mass**. Doubling speed quadruples stored energy at the same rotor mass; doubling mass only doubles energy and increases gyroscopic, bearing, and containment loads.

> Formula = 1 mark; explanation with $E \propto \omega^{2}$ vs $E \propto m$ = 2 marks.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a flywheel accumulator used in a hybrid vehicle. Discuss the role of the motor-generator unit, vacuum enclosure, and magnetic bearings.

**Model Answer:**

A flywheel accumulator in a hybrid vehicle operates on the principle of **rotational kinetic energy storage** ($E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$). The system consists of the following subsystems:

**1. Motor-Generator Unit (MGU):** A reversible electrical machine (usually a **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor** or **BLDC motor**) coupled to the flywheel shaft. During charging, it acts as a motor and accelerates the rotor using surplus energy. During discharging, it acts as a generator and converts rotational kinetic energy back into electricity via back-EMF, feeding the DC bus.

**2. Flywheel Rotor:** A high-strength steel or carbon-fiber composite disc spinning at 3,000–100,000 rpm inside a sealed housing.

**3. Vacuum Enclosure:** Air is pumped out to **< 10⁻³ mbar** to eliminate aerodynamic drag — at 50,000 rpm, even atmospheric air friction would dissipate the stored energy in seconds.

**4. Magnetic Bearings (Active or Passive):** Replace rolling-element bearings to remove mechanical friction. The rotor is levitated by electromagnetic forces and requires no physical contact.

**5. Power Electronics Inverter:** Bidirectional VSI (Voltage Source Inverter) controlling MGU torque and speed in both directions.

**Working sequence:**
- **Braking → Energy Capture:** Regen energy → Inverter → MGU as motor → Rotor accelerates → Energy stored as $E_k$.
- **Acceleration → Energy Release:** Driver demands power → Inverter → MGU as generator → Rotor decelerates → Electrical energy supplied to traction motor.

[Block diagram as in Section 4.1]

> **Mark split:** Diagram 2 marks; description of MGU 2 marks; Vacuum + Bearings 2 marks; working sequence 1 mark.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flywheel accumulator has a moment of inertia $I = 2.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$. It is charged from 5,000 rpm to 25,000 rpm. Calculate:
(i) the energy stored at maximum speed,
(ii) the usable energy extractable,
(iii) the average power if the discharge takes 30 seconds.

**Model Solution:**

Convert rpm to rad/s using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

**Step 1 — Convert speeds:**

$$\omega_{min} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 5{,}000}{60} \approx 523.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 25{,}000}{60} \approx 2{,}618.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Energy stored at maximum speed:**

$$E_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega_{max}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (2{,}618.0)^{2}$$

$$E_{max} = 1.25 \times 6.854 \times 10^{6} \approx 8.567 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{max} \approx 8.57 \text{ MJ} \approx 2.38 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy at minimum speed:**

$$E_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (523.6)^{2} = 1.25 \times 2.741 \times 10^{5} \approx 3.43 \times 10^{5} \text{ J}$$

$$E_{min} \approx 0.343 \text{ MJ} \approx 0.095 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Usable energy:**

$$E_{usable} = E_{max} - E_{min} = 8.567 \times 10^{6} - 3.43 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\boxed{E_{usable} \approx 8.23 \text{ MJ} \approx 2.29 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Average discharge power for 30 s:**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{t} = \dfrac{8.23 \times 10^{6}}{30}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 274{,}300 \text{ W} \approx 274.3 \text{ kW}}$$

> **Mark split:** Correct ω conversion 1 mark; $E_{max}$ 2 marks; $E_{min}$ 1 mark; $E_{usable}$ 2 marks; $P_{avg}$ 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare low-speed steel flywheels and high-speed composite flywheels. Why are high-speed composites preferred for vehicle applications despite higher cost and complexity?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Low-Speed Steel Flywheel | High-Speed Composite Flywheel |
|---|---|---|
| Speed range | 3,000–10,000 rpm | 30,000–100,000 rpm |
| Specific energy | 5–20 Wh/kg | 50–100 Wh/kg |
| Tip speed | < 500 m/s | 900–1,500 m/s |
| Bearings | Conventional | Magnetic levitation |
| Containment | Standard | Buried/heavy-duty |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Why composites are preferred for vehicles:**

1. **Higher energy density** — specific energy is governed by $\sigma_{ts}/\rho$, which for carbon-fiber composites is **2–3× higher** than steel. This directly reduces vehicle weight.

2. **Smaller package volume** — high rotational speed means less mass and smaller diameter for the same stored energy, enabling under-floor mounting in cars.

3. **Lower gyroscopic effect** — a smaller, lighter rotor reduces unwanted gyroscopic coupling with vehicle yaw/pitch motions.

4. **Lower aerodynamic loss** — though both operate in vacuum, smaller rotor volume reduces required evacuation size.

The higher initial cost is offset by **lifetime value** (no replacement cycles, unlike batteries) and **vehicle packaging benefits**.

> **Mark split:** Tabular comparison 3 marks; four justification points = 4 marks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite flywheel rim has mass $m = 12 \text{ kg}$, radius $r = 0.18 \text{ m}$, and spins at 40,000 rpm. If the material tensile strength is $\sigma_{ts} = 4{,}500 \text{ MPa}$ and density $\rho = 1{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, determine:
(i) the kinetic energy stored,
(ii) the hoop stress, and
(iii) whether the design is safe.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert speed to rad/s:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 40{,}000}{60} \approx 4{,}188.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Kinetic energy stored** (treating rim as thin ring, $I = mr^{2}$):

$$I = 12 \times (0.18)^{2} = 12 \times 0.0324 = 0.3888 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

$$E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3888 \times (4{,}188.8)^{2}$$

$$E_{k} = 0.1944 \times 1.7546 \times 10^{7} \approx 3.41 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{k} \approx 3.41 \text{ MJ} \approx 0.948 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Step 3 — Tip speed:**

$$v_{tip} = r\omega = 0.18 \times 4{,}188.8 \approx 754.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \rho v_{tip}^{2} = 1{,}600 \times (754.0)^{2} = 1{,}600 \times 5.685 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} \approx 9.10 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 910 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Safety check:**

$$\sigma_{hoop} \;=\; 910 \text{ MPa} \;\le\; \sigma_{ts} \;=\; 4{,}500 \text{ MPa} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Factor of Safety:**

$$FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\sigma_{hoop}} = \dfrac{4{,}500}{910} \approx 4.95$$

> **Mark split:** $\omega$ conversion 1 mark; $E_k$ 2 marks; $\sigma_{hoop}$ 2 marks; safety justification with FoS 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert rpm to rad/s, leading to errors of factor $(2\pi/60)^{2} \approx 0.0109$. Always check units first.
> 2. **Using wrong formula for $E_k$:** Some use $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ (linear) instead of $\tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ (rotational). Examiners deduct 1 mark per wrong formula application.
> 3. **Confusing total vs usable energy:** $E_{max}$ is at full speed; the extractable energy in practice is $E_{max} - E_{min}$ (with $\omega_{min} > 0$).
> 4. **Ignoring safety factor:** A KTU 7-mark problem on a composite flywheel **always expects a safety verification**. Not mentioning FoS or $\sigma_{hoop} \le \sigma_{ts}$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Drawing a chemical battery symbol or capacitor symbol in the block diagram:** Examiners specifically look for *rotor + MGU + vacuum chamber* blocks; replacing these with battery/supercapacitor symbols is a 2-mark deduction.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Flywheel Accumulators**

- ✅ A flywheel stores **rotational kinetic energy**: $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{4} m v_{tip}^{2}$
- ✅ Energy scales **quadratically with speed** — speed gain beats mass gain.
- ✅ **Usable energy** is $E_{max} - E_{min}$ (with $\omega_{min} > 0$); round-trip efficiency is **85–95 %**.
- ✅ **Specific energy** is governed by material's $\sigma_{ts}/\rho$ ratio — composites beat steel.
- ✅ Maximum safe tip speed: $v_{tip,\max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{ts}/\rho}$; for steel ≈ 500 m/s, for composites ≈ 1,000–1,500 m/s.
- ✅ **Vacuum + magnetic bearings** are mandatory at high speeds — they cut standing losses to < 2 %/hour.
- ✅ **High specific power** (1,000–5,000 W/kg) makes flywheels ideal for **transient bursts** (acceleration, regen), not long-term storage.
- ✅ **Cycle life > 10⁶**, no chemical degradation — the only wear item is bearing electronics.
- ✅ Failure mode is **catastrophic rim burst** — hence the rule $v_{tip} \le \sqrt{\sigma_{ts}/\rho}$.
- ✅ Automotive applications: **F1 KERS**, **hybrid city buses**, **FCEV transient buffers**.
- ✅ Always include in answers: a block diagram with **Bus → Inverter → MGU → Rotor** and the safety / containment loop.
- ✅ Comparison table: Flywheel = power dense, low energy; Battery = energy dense, lower power; Ultracapacitor = ultra-high power, very low energy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES)

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES)** is an advanced energy storage technology that stores electrical energy directly in the magnetic field of a coil wound from superconducting wire. Because the coil operates in a superconducting state (zero electrical resistance), the current — and therefore the magnetic field and the stored energy — circulates indefinitely without dissipation, provided the cryogenic temperature is maintained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAUT601 Module 3):**
> SMES is a device that stores energy in the form of a DC magnetic field within a superconducting coil that has been cryogenically cooled to a temperature below its **critical temperature (T_c)**. Once the coil is charged, the current persists without loss, and the energy can be released on demand through a power conditioning system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **flywheel** that spins forever once you give it a push because there is no friction. A SMES system is the electrical equivalent of that frictionless flywheel. Instead of kinetic energy (spinning mass), SMES stores energy in a **circulating current of electrons** inside a coil whose wire has been cooled so much that it offers literally **zero resistance** to the flow. Just like a flywheel needs good bearings, SMES needs extreme cold — but once achieved, the energy sits there "for free," ready to be tapped at any instant.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

The fundamental constants governing SMES performance are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$** |
| Critical temperature (Nb-Ti alloy) | $T_c$ | **~9.2 K** |
| Critical temperature (YBCO HTS ceramic) | $T_c$ | **~92 K** |
| Liquid Helium boiling point | — | **4.2 K** |
| Liquid Nitrogen boiling point | — | **77 K** |
| Typical energy density | — | **$\approx 10\text{–}100 \text{ kJ/kg}$** |
| Round-trip efficiency | $\eta$ | **$\approx 95\text{–}98\%$** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these constants matter in KTU context:** The student must remember that $\mu_0$ defines the relationship between magnetic flux density $B$ and magnetic field intensity $H$, and that the energy stored scales with $B^2$. Doubling the magnetic field **quadruples** the stored energy, which is why high-temperature superconductors (HTS) are revolutionizing SMES design.

### 1.4 Principle of Superconductivity

Two fundamental phenomena must be recalled:

1. **Zero Electrical Resistance:** Below the critical temperature $T_c$, the DC resistance of certain materials drops to exactly zero. Current, once initiated, flows forever (persistent mode).

2. **Meissner Effect:** A superconductor expels magnetic flux from its interior when cooled below $T_c$. However, beyond a **critical magnetic field $H_c$**, superconductivity is destroyed and the material reverts to its normal resistive state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Three "critical" limits define the safe operating envelope of any superconductor — **Critical Temperature ($T_c$)**, **Critical Current Density ($J_c$)**, and **Critical Magnetic Field ($H_c$)**. Exceed any one of them and the system reverts to a normal conductor, dissipating all stored energy as heat — a catastrophic failure mode called **"quench."**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

The SMES operating cycle is best understood in five logical phases:

1. **Charging Phase:**
   - AC power from the grid or vehicle bus is rectified to DC.
   - The DC current is ramped up gradually through the superconducting coil via a **chopper-controlled current source**.
   - A magnetic field builds up proportionally to the current ($B = \mu_0 n I$ for a solenoid).

2. **Persistent (Storage) Mode:**
   - Once the desired current $I_0$ is reached, the coil terminals are connected through a **persistent switch** (a small section of superconductor that is thermally shorted or left in superconducting state).
   - The external power supply is disconnected.
   - The current now circulates in a **closed superconducting loop** with $R = 0$. By $V = IR$, the voltage drop is zero, so no power is dissipated.

3. **Standby Mode:**
   - Energy remains stored indefinitely as long as the cryostat maintains temperature below $T_c$.
   - Only the small cryogenic cooling load (typically **0.1–1% of stored energy per hour**) is consumed.

4. **Discharging Phase:**
   - The persistent switch is opened (heated to normal state) or current is diverted through a power-conditioning unit (inverter).
   - The collapsing magnetic field induces a back-EMF, which is rectified/inverted back to usable AC or DC power.

5. **Recovery Phase:**
   - The energy is delivered to the load (electric motor in an EV, or grid in a stationary SMES unit).
   - The cryogenic system continues to operate, ready for the next charge cycle.

### 2.2 Why SMES — The 'Why' Behind the Design

| Engineering Need | SMES Response |
|---|---|
| High power burst for EV acceleration | Discharge time of **milliseconds** |
| Long cycle life | **>100,000 full cycles** with no degradation |
| High round-trip efficiency | **>95%** (limited mainly by inverter losses) |
| No chemical reactions | Zero emissions, no thermal runaway risk |
| Compact for urban/substation use | Energy density up to **~10× lead-acid** per unit volume |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize units as well as expressions. KTU examiners often deduct 1 mark for missing SI units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy stored in inductor | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | Joules (J) | Fundamental definition |
| 2 | Energy density (magnetic) | $w = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}$ | $\text{J/m}^3$ | Field-based form |
| 3 | Total energy in coil volume $V$ | $E = \dfrac{B^{2} V}{2 \mu_{0}}$ | J | When $B$ is uniform |
| 4 | Inductance of solenoid | $L = \mu_{0} \dfrac{N^{2} A}{\ell}$ | H | $N$ turns, area $A$, length $\ell$ |
| 5 | Magnetic field of solenoid | $B = \mu_{0} \dfrac{N I}{\ell} = \mu_{0} n I$ | T | $n = N/\ell$ turns per metre |
| 6 | Critical current condition | $I \le I_{c}(T, B)$ | A | Must not be exceeded |
| 7 | Force on current-carrying conductor | $F = B I \ell \sin\theta$ | N | Used in magnetic stress analysis |
| 8 | Stored energy in terms of $B$ and $H$ | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \int \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}\, dV$ | J | General integral form |
| 9 | Time constant of coil decay | $\tau = L/R$ | s | For SMES, $R\to 0$, so $\tau\to\infty$ |
| 10 | Discharge power | $P = E/t_{discharge}$ | W | Determines rating of inverter |

> **Note:** In all formulas above, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ unless otherwise stated. The vertical bar `|` has been replaced with `\vert` only when not in tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SMES is deployed in:

- **Power Grids:** For grid stabilization, frequency regulation, and buffering transient spikes.
- **Pulsed Power Applications:** Particle accelerators, MRI systems, electromagnetic launchers.
- **Renewable Integration:** Smoothing wind/solar fluctuations in microgrids.
- **Automotive Research:** Experimental high-speed flywheel + SMES hybrid systems for EVs, though adoption is limited by cryogenic complexity. For KTU, the importance lies in understanding that SMES can theoretically provide **instantaneous power** — exactly what an EV needs during hard acceleration.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in a Solenoid from First Principles

We start from the fundamental definition of self-inductance. Consider a coil of $N$ turns carrying current $I$. The magnetic flux linkage $\lambda$ is given by:

$$
\lambda = N \Phi
$$

where $\Phi$ is the magnetic flux through one turn. By definition of inductance:

$$
L = \frac{\lambda}{I} = \frac{N \Phi}{I}
$$

The work done in establishing this current against the back-EMF is the energy stored:

$$
W = \int_0^{I} L i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2}}
$$

This is the most elementary and frequently tested expression in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Density in a Magnetic Field

For a long solenoid of length $\ell$, cross-sectional area $A$, with $N$ turns:

**Step 1.** Write the inductance of a solenoid:

$$
L = \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell}
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute into the energy expression:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell} \right) I^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** Group the factors to reveal $B$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell} I^{2} = \frac{A \ell}{2} \cdot \mu_{0} \left(\frac{N I}{\ell}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4.** Recognize the magnetic field inside the solenoid:

$$
B = \mu_{0} \frac{N I}{\ell}
$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$
E = \frac{A \ell}{2} \cdot \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}
$$

**Step 6.** Since volume $V = A \ell$, the **volumetric energy density** $w$ is:

$$
\boxed{w = \frac{E}{V} = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}}
$$

This expression is profoundly important because it shows energy density depends **only on $B$**, not on geometry. It is why research is directed toward higher-field superconductors.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A SMES solenoid has $N = 2000$ turns, length $\ell = 0.5$ m, radius $r = 0.05$ m, and carries a current $I = 100$ A. Calculate (a) inductance, (b) magnetic field, (c) total energy stored, (d) energy density.

**Given:**
- $N = 2000$
- $\ell = 0.5$ m
- $r = 0.05$ m, so $A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (0.05)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$
- $I = 100$ A
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

**Solution (a): Inductance**

$$
L = \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell}
$$

$$
L = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{(2000)^{2} \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-3})}{0.5}
$$

$$
L = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5}
$$

$$
L = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{4}}{0.5}
$$

$$
L = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (6.2832 \times 10^{4})
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 0.0790 \text{ H} \approx 79 \text{ mH}}
$$

**Solution (b): Magnetic Field**

$$
B = \mu_{0} \frac{N I}{\ell} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{2000 \cdot 100}{0.5}
$$

$$
B = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (4 \times 10^{5}) = 0.5027 \text{ T}
$$

$$
\boxed{B \approx 0.503 \text{ T}}
$$

**Solution (c): Total Energy**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.0790 \cdot (100)^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 395 \text{ J}}
$$

**Solution (d): Energy Density**

Using $w = B^{2}/(2\mu_0)$:

$$
w = \frac{(0.5027)^{2}}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})} = \frac{0.2527}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{w \approx 1.006 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3} \approx 100.6 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using $w = E/V$:** $V = A\ell = 7.854\times10^{-3} \cdot 0.5 = 3.927\times10^{-3}$ m³. So $w = 395 / 3.927\times10^{-3} = 1.006 \times 10^{5}$ J/m³. ✓ **Match confirmed.** Always cross-verify in KTU numericals — examiners reward consistent approach.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SMES vs Other Storage Technologies

| Feature | SMES | Li-ion Battery | Supercapacitor | Flywheel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy form | Magnetic | Chemical | Electrostatic | Kinetic |
| Round-trip efficiency | **~95–98%** | ~85–95% | ~90–95% | ~80–90% |
| Energy density (Wh/kg) | **~10–100** | **~100–265** | ~5–10 | ~20–80 |
| Power density (W/kg) | **~10⁴–10⁶** | ~250–340 | ~1000–10000 | ~400–1500 |
| Discharge time | **ms** | minutes–hours | seconds–minutes | seconds–minutes |
| Cycle life | **>100,000** | ~1000–3000 | **>500,000** | ~20,000 |
| Operating temperature | **4–77 K** | -20 to +60 °C | -40 to +65 °C | Ambient |
| Self-discharge | **Very low** | ~2–5%/month | ~10–20%/day | Friction losses |
| KTU-relevance | High (Module 3) | High (all modules) | Medium | Medium |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SMES System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[AC or DC Power Source Grid or Vehicle Bus] --> REC[Rectifier Stage AC to DC Conversion]
    REC --> CHOP[Chopper Controlled Current Source]
    CHOP --> COIL[Superconducting Coil Energy Storage Element]
    CRYO[Cryogenic Cooling System Liquid He or LN2] -.-> COIL
    COIL --> SW[Persistent Switch Closed in Storage Mode]
    SW --> INV[DC to AC Inverter Discharge Path]
    INV --> LOAD[Load Electric Motor or Grid]
    CTRL[Control and Protection Unit Microcontroller Based] -.-> REC
    CTRL -.-> CHOP
    CTRL -.-> SW
    CTRL -.-> INV
    CTRL -.-> CRYO
    QCH[Quench Detection Circuit] -.-> CTRL
    COIL -.-> QCH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show power flow (electrical energy). Dotted arrows show control signals. The dashed arrow from the cryostat to the coil represents thermal coupling, not electrical. The quench detection circuit is a safety subsystem — it monitors voltage across the coil; any sudden appearance of voltage indicates the coil has gone normal (quench), and the controller must dump the energy safely.

### 4.2 Flowchart — Operating States of SMES

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging: Charge Command Received
    Charging --> Persistent: Current Reaches Setpoint I0
    Persistent --> Charging: Top up Required
    Persistent --> Discharging: Discharge Command Received
    Discharging --> Idle: Energy Depleted
    Charging --> Fault: Quench Detected
    Discharging --> Fault: Quench Detected
    Persistent --> Fault: Quench Detected
    Fault --> [*]: Energy Dumped to Resistor Bank
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in Hybrid EV with SMES

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FC[Fuel Cell Stack] --> BUS[DC Bus]
    BAT[Li ion Battery Pack] --> BUS
    SMES[SMES Unit] --> BUS
    BUS --> MTR[Electric Traction Motor]
    BUS --> AUX[Auxiliary Loads HVAC Lighting]
    REGEN[Regenerative Braking Energy] --> BUS
    BUS --> SMES
    BUS --> BAT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In a hybrid or fuel-cell EV, the SMES handles **transient power demands** (acceleration bursts, regenerative braking spikes), while the fuel cell or battery handles **steady-state cruising load**. This load-sharing architecture exploits SMES's strength in high-power, short-duration events.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES). Mention any two of its advantages over conventional batteries.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
SMES is an energy storage device that stores electrical energy in the magnetic field generated by a DC current flowing through a coil made of superconducting wire. The coil is maintained at a temperature below its critical temperature $T_c$ using a cryogenic system, ensuring zero electrical resistance and therefore lossless current circulation.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Very high round-trip efficiency (~95–98%)** because there is no resistive (I²R) loss in the superconducting coil. Only the inverter and cryogenic system incur losses.
2. **Extremely fast response time (milliseconds)** — ideal for capturing regenerative braking energy and supplying acceleration surges.
3. **Long cycle life (>100,000 cycles)** with no chemical degradation, since no electrochemical reactions occur.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "SMES has 100% efficiency." This is wrong. While the coil itself is lossless, the **power conditioning electronics and cryogenic cooler** consume energy. Always quote ~95–98%, not 100%.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the three critical parameters of a superconductor and explain what happens if any one of them is exceeded.*

**Model Answer:**

A superconductor must operate within three simultaneous limits. If any one is exceeded, superconductivity is lost (a "quench" occurs) and the material becomes a normal conductor with finite resistance.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Nb-Ti) | Consequence of Exceedance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Temperature | $T_c$ | ~9.2 K | Material returns to normal state, $R > 0$ |
| Critical Current Density | $J_c$ | ~10⁹ A/m² at 4.2 K | Joule heating $\to$ thermal runaway |
| Critical Magnetic Field | $H_c$ | ~10–15 T | Magnetic flux penetrates, destroying zero-resistance state |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students remember $T_c$ only. KTU questions frequently test the trio — **all three critical limits must be written.** Memorize the table above.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES) system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A SMES system consists of four major subsystems:

1. **Superconducting Coil** — wound from Nb-Ti or YBCO wire, stores energy in its magnetic field when current flows. Typical inductance is in the range of **1 H to several hundred H** for grid-scale systems.

2. **Cryogenic System** — Dewar vessel containing liquid helium (4.2 K) for LTS coils, or liquid nitrogen (77 K) for HTS coils. Includes thermal insulation (vacuum + radiation shields) and a cryocooler (Gifford-McMahon or pulse-tube type).

3. **Power Conditioning System (PCS)** — composed of:
   - **AC/DC rectifier** (charging),
   - **DC chopper** for current regulation,
   - **DC/AC inverter** (discharging),
   - **Persistent switch** (a small superconducting bridge that is thermally or magnetically activated).

4. **Control and Protection Unit** — microcontroller/PLC-based, monitors current, voltage, temperature, and triggers the **quench protection circuit** (which diverts stored energy to a dump resistor if coil goes normal).

**Working:**
- During **charging**, AC mains is rectified to DC; the chopper ramps up the current in the coil gradually. As current builds, magnetic field $B$ increases and energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^{2}$ is stored.
- During **storage**, the persistent switch closes (superconducting), the external supply is disconnected, and current circulates losslessly in a closed superconducting loop.
- During **discharging**, the persistent switch opens and the stored energy is released through the inverter to the load as AC power.

> **Block diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for energy stored in a superconducting coil in terms of its magnetic field $B$ and volume $V$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** A long solenoid of length $\ell$, cross-sectional area $A$, with $N$ turns carrying current $I$, in a medium of permeability $\mu_0$.

**Step 1.** Inductance of a solenoid:

$$
L = \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell}
$$

**Step 2.** Energy stored in inductor:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell} I^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange to isolate the magnetic field:

$$
E = \frac{A \ell}{2} \cdot \mu_{0} \left( \frac{N I}{\ell} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $B = \mu_{0} \dfrac{N I}{\ell}$, hence:

$$
\left( \frac{N I}{\ell} \right) = \frac{B}{\mu_{0}}
$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$
E = \frac{A \ell}{2} \cdot \mu_{0} \cdot \left( \frac{B}{\mu_{0}} \right)^{2} = \frac{A \ell}{2} \cdot \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}
$$

**Step 6.** Since volume $V = A \ell$:

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{B^{2} V}{2 \mu_{0}}}
$$

**Step 7.** The volumetric energy density is therefore:

$$
w = \frac{E}{V} = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Doubling the magnetic field quadruples the stored energy. This is why HTS materials capable of sustaining higher $B$ fields are preferred for compact SMES designs.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a) Discuss the role of SMES in hybrid and fuel cell vehicles. Compare its performance with battery and supercapacitor-based storage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of SMES in Hybrid/Fuel Cell Vehicles:**

In hybrid and fuel cell vehicles, power demand is highly dynamic. The fuel cell provides steady power, but acceleration requires **short, high-power bursts** (often 3–5× the rated fuel cell power). Similarly, regenerative braking produces large transient currents. SMES excels at both:

- **Acceleration Assist:** SMES releases stored magnetic energy within **milliseconds**, supplementing the fuel cell during peak power demand.
- **Regenerative Braking Capture:** During deceleration, kinetic energy is converted to electrical energy; SMES absorbs this rapidly without the lifetime penalty faced by batteries.
- **Load Leveling:** The fuel cell operates at a constant, efficient setpoint while SMES handles the transients — improving overall system efficiency and extending fuel cell life.
- **Voltage Stabilization:** SMES acts as a buffer, smoothing DC bus voltage fluctuations.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SMES | Battery (Li-ion) | Supercapacitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy density | Low–Medium | High | Low |
| Power density | Very High | Medium | Very High |
| Charge/discharge time | ms | minutes | seconds |
| Cycle life | >100,000 | 1,000–3,000 | >500,000 |
| Efficiency | ~95–98% | ~85–95% | ~90–95% |
| Operating temp. | Cryogenic | Ambient | Ambient |
| Cost & complexity | High | Medium | Medium |

**Conclusion:** SMES is best used as a **hybrid storage element** alongside batteries — never alone in a production EV — handling power transients while batteries manage bulk energy.

---

**(b) A SMES solenoid has 5000 turns, length 1.0 m, and radius 0.1 m. It carries a current of 200 A. Calculate (i) self-inductance, (ii) magnetic field, (iii) total energy stored, and (iv) energy density. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 5000$, $\ell = 1.0$ m, $r = 0.1$ m, $I = 200$ A, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

**Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (0.1)^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(i) Self-inductance:**

$$
L = \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2} A}{\ell} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{(5000)^{2} \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-2}}{1.0}
$$

$$
L = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (3.1416 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
L = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{5})
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 0.9869 \text{ H} \approx 0.987 \text{ H}}
$$

**(ii) Magnetic field:**

$$
B = \mu_{0} \frac{N I}{\ell} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{5000 \cdot 200}{1.0}
$$

$$
B = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (10^{6}) = 1.2566 \text{ T}
$$

$$
\boxed{B \approx 1.257 \text{ T}}
$$

**(iii) Total energy stored:**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.9869 \cdot (200)^{2}
$$

$$
E = 0.5 \cdot 0.9869 \cdot 40000
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 19{,}738 \text{ J} \approx 19.74 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**(iv) Energy density:**

$$
w = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}} = \frac{(1.2566)^{2}}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}
$$

$$
w = \frac{1.5790}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{w \approx 6.28 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3} = 628 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}}
$$

**Cross-check:** $V = A\ell = 3.1416\times10^{-2} \cdot 1.0 = 3.1416\times10^{-2}$ m³.

$$
w = E/V = 19738 / 3.1416\times10^{-2} = 6.28 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Mark-Loss Points):**
> 1. **Forgetting $\pi$ in area calculation** — many students write $A = r^2 = 0.01$ m². Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing $L$ without units** — always append H or mH.
> 3. **In (iv), using $E/V$ instead of $B^2/(2\mu_0)$** — both methods are accepted, but you must **show** the chosen formula explicitly.
> 4. **Not cross-checking** — KTU examiners give **bonus partial credit** if you verify your answer by an alternative route.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — SMES**

- [x] **Definition:** SMES stores energy in the magnetic field of a superconducting coil cooled below $T_c$.
- [x] **Key formula (always memorize):** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$
- [x] **Field-based form:** $E = \dfrac{B^{2} V}{2 \mu_{0}}$
- [x] **Energy density:** $w = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}$ (independent of geometry).
- [x] **Solenoid inductance:** $L = \mu_{0} N^{2} A / \ell$
- [x] **Solenoid field:** $B = \mu_{0} N I / \ell$
- [x] **Three critical limits:** $T_c$, $J_c$, $H_c$ — exceed any one $\Rightarrow$ **quench**.
- [x] **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** — always use this exact value.
- [x] **Round-trip efficiency:** ~95–98%, NOT 100%.
- [x] **Cycle life:** >100,000 (no chemical degradation).
- [x] **Discharge time:** milliseconds — fastest among storage technologies.
- [x] **Four subsystems:** Coil + Cryostat + Power Conditioning + Control/Protection.
- [x] **Vehicle role:** Transient power assist, regenerative capture, DC bus stabilization.
- [x] **Numerical approach:** Always substitute values **after** deriving the symbolic expression.
- [x] **Cross-check:** Use two different formulas (e.g., $E/V$ vs $B^2/2\mu_0$) to verify your answer.

---

**End of Module 3 — SMES Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Accumulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Accumulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **hydraulic accumulator** is a hydro-pneumatic energy storage device that stores potential energy in the form of a compressed, inert gas (typically **nitrogen, N₂**) separated from a hydraulic fluid (typically mineral oil) by a sealed, deformable element (bladder, piston, or diaphragm). When hydraulic fluid is pumped into the accumulator, the gas is compressed, thereby storing energy that can be released on demand to perform useful mechanical work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> A hydraulic accumulator is a pressure vessel containing a non-expansible hydraulic fluid and an expansible, pre-charged gas (usually dry nitrogen) separated by an elastomeric or metallic barrier. It functions as a *hydraulic battery*, supplying flow, dampening pulsations, compensating for leaks, and acting as an **energy buffer** in regenerative braking systems of hydraulic hybrid vehicles (HHVs).

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

Imagine a **soda bottle** half-filled with air and half with water. If you squeeze the bottle (pump fluid in), the trapped air compresses like a spring. Now release the cap — the compressed air pushes water out forcefully. 

A hydraulic accumulator works on the **exact same principle**, but:
- The "air" is **high-purity nitrogen** (non-flammable, chemically inert).
- The "bottle" is a **heavy-duty steel cylinder** rated for hundreds of bar.
- The "squeeze" comes from a **hydraulic pump**, and the "release" drives motors or actuators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nitrogen (N₂)?**
> - Chemically inert → no explosion risk with hydraulic oil.
> - Cheap and abundantly available.
> - Large molecule → low permeability through elastomers.
> - Stable thermal properties → predictable P-V behavior.

---

## 1.3 Primary Functions in a Hybrid Vehicle Context

| Function | Engineering Utility |
|----------|--------------------|
| **Energy Storage** | Stores kinetic energy recovered from regenerative braking (Hydraulic Hybrid Vehicles like the Eaton/Ford UPS truck). |
| **Pulse Dampening** | Smooths out pressure ripples from axial piston pumps. |
| **Emergency Power Source** | Provides instantaneous flow for brake-by-wire fail-op systems. |
| **Leakage Compensation** | Maintains system pressure during idle (pump-off) periods. |
| **Thermal Expansion Buffer** | Absorbs volume changes due to fluid temperature swings. |
| **Shock Absorption** | Cushions water-hammer effects in long hydraulic lines. |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Hydraulic Accumulators (KTU Standard Classification)

```
HYDRAULIC ACCUMULATORS
├── 1. Weight-Loaded (Gravitational)  [OBSOLETE — heavy, low pressure]
├── 2. Spring-Loaded                  [Low pressure, ~ 50–200 bar]
└── 3. Gas-Loaded (Hydro-Pneumatic)   [MOST COMMON — 100–700 bar]
        ├── a) Bladder Type
        ├── b) Piston Type
        └── c) Diaphragm Type
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3)**, the gas-loaded family is the *only* commercially and academically relevant category. Weight and spring types are mentioned only for historical completeness.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Gas-Loaded Accumulator Types — Working Principles

### (a) Bladder-Type Accumulator
- A **seamless rubber/elastomeric bladder** (synthetic rubber, e.g., nitrile-butyl) is fitted inside a forged steel shell.
- The bladder is **gas-charged** through a gas valve (Schrader-type poppet) at the top.
- Hydraulic fluid enters from the bottom through a **poppet valve** (spring-loaded anti-extrusion check) that closes when fluid is exhausted, preventing bladder extrusion through the port.
- **Advantages**: High gas-to-fluid ratio (≈ 75%), fast response, low cost, compact.
- **Disadvantages**: Bladder wear, limited cycle life (~ 10⁶ cycles), temperature sensitive.

### (b) Piston-Type Accumulator
- A **precision-machined metallic piston** with sealing rings (PTFE or rubber) slides inside a honed cylinder.
- Gas chamber on one side, hydraulic fluid on the other.
- **Advantages**: Robust, high pressure capability (up to **700 bar**), long life (> 10⁶ cycles), large capacities (up to **500 liters**).
- **Disadvantages**: Higher friction (hysteresis), slower dynamic response, internal leakage past seals.

### (c) Diaphragm-Type Accumulator
- A **hemispherical elastomeric diaphragm** welded between two forged steel halves.
- Smaller capacity (typically 0.1 – 4 liters), used for low-volume, high-precision applications.
- **Advantages**: Very fast response, no friction, low cost for small sizes.
- **Disadvantages**: Limited capacity, diaphragm rupture risk.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Foundation — The Polytropic Process

The gas inside an accumulator never follows a perfectly isothermal or adiabatic process. The actual behavior lies between the two and is modeled using a **polytropic equation**:

$$P_1 \cdot V_1^{\,n} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{\,n} = \text{constant}$$

where **n** is the **polytropic index** of the gas compression/expansion cycle.

### KTU High-Yield Polytropic Index Table

| Process Type | n Value | When it Occurs in Practice |
|--------------|---------|----------------------------|
| Isothermal | 1.0 | Very slow charge/discharge (> 1 min), or accumulator has large thermal mass allowing heat dissipation. |
| Isentropic (Adiabatic) | 1.4 (N₂) | Very fast charge/discharge (< 5 seconds), no heat exchange with surroundings. |
| Real Polytropic | 1.0 < n < 1.4 | Typical automotive hybrid braking (charge time ≈ 2–10 s). KTU uses **n = 1.4** as conservative default. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule (KTU Examiner Favorite):** If the time constant of the gas process is greater than 60 seconds, assume **n = 1.0**. Otherwise, use **n = 1.4**.

---

## 2.3 The Three Critical Pressure States

Any accumulator analysis involves **three distinct pressure values**:

| Symbol | Pressure State | Physical Meaning |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| $P_0$ | **Pre-charge (Gas) Pressure** | Pressure of N₂ *before* any hydraulic fluid is added. Always < minimum system pressure. |
| $P_1$ | **Minimum (Gas = System) Pressure** | When the gas volume is at its maximum and system pressure has just reached operating minimum. |
| $P_2$ | **Maximum (Gas = System) Pressure** | When the gas volume is at its minimum and the accumulator is fully charged. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-charge Rule (Gold Standard):** For maximum energy storage, the gas pre-charge pressure should be set at **P₀ ≤ 0.9 × P₁** (typically 0.6–0.9 × P₁). If P₀ exceeds P₁, the bladder/piston will be fully compressed before any fluid enters — accumulator becomes non-functional.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}$ | Polytropic gas law | bar·L |
| 2 | $P_0 V_0 = P_1 V_1$ | Isothermal pre-charge condition | bar·L |
| 3 | $V_0 - V_2 = \Delta V$ | Useful fluid volume discharged | L |
| 4 | $E = -\int_{V_2}^{V_1} P \, dV = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$ | Stored energy (polytropic, n ≠ 1) | J |
| 5 | $E = P_1 V_1 \ln \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Stored energy (isothermal, n = 1) | J |
| 6 | $\Delta V = V_0 \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \right] - V_0 \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \right]$ | Total usable fluid volume | L |
| 7 | $V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta V \, (P_1/P_0)^{1/n}}{1 - (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}}$ | Required total accumulator volume | L |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{acc}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{ideal}}} \approx 0.85$ | Accumulator round-trip efficiency | — |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UPS Delivery Trucks (Eaton/Ford Roadranger)** — Hydraulic hybrid using two 70 L piston accumulators at **350 bar** to store regenerative braking energy, yielding **60–70% fuel savings** in stop-and-go delivery cycles.
- **Honda Insight (1st Generation)** — Used hydraulic accumulators in its Integrated Motor Assist (IMA) experimental variants for launch assist.
- **Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems, Pre-2014)** — Some early prototypes used hydraulic accumulators with carbon-fiber reinforced bladders operating at **700 bar**.
- **Aircraft Hydraulic Systems** — Accumulators maintain constant pressure in the **3,000 psi (≈ 207 bar)** flight-control hydraulic system during pump-off flight.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Useful Fluid Discharge Volume (ΔV) from an Accumulator

**Given:** A bladder accumulator of total geometric volume $V_0$ (gas-side maximum), pre-charged to $P_0$, operating between $P_1$ (min) and $P_2$ (max).

**Step 1 — Gas Volume at Minimum Operating Pressure (P₁):**
By Boyle's law (isothermal pre-charge state, where gas is at rest and at the pre-charge value):

$$P_0 \cdot V_0 = P_1 \cdot V_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_1 = \dfrac{P_0 \, V_0}{P_1}$$

**Step 2 — Gas Volume at Maximum Operating Pressure (P₂):**
Using the polytropic equation from the gas-state $(V_0, P_0)$ directly to $(V_2, P_2)$:

$$P_0 \, V_0^{\,n} = P_2 \, V_2^{\,n}$$

Solving for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = V_0 \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Fluid Volume Discharged (ΔV):**
The fluid actually pushed out equals the change in gas volume:

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = V_0 \left[ \dfrac{P_0}{P_1} - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **complete KTU 2024 expression for ΔV** and is the most-tested formula on this topic. Note the **1/n exponent** on the second term — students commonly write it as 1 (isothermal) by mistake, losing full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Stored in the Gas (Polytropic Process)

**Starting Point — Thermodynamic First Law:**
The incremental work done by the gas on the fluid as the gas expands from V to V + dV at pressure P is:

$$dW = P \, dV$$

Total energy released between states 1 and 2:

$$E = \int_{V_2}^{V_1} P \, dV$$
