**Step 1 — Substitute Polytropic Law:**
From $P \, V^n = \text{const} = K$, we have $P = K / V^n$, and $K = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$.

$$E = \int_{V_2}^{V_1} \dfrac{K}{V^{\,n}} \, dV = K \int_{V_2}^{V_1} V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Integral:**

$$E = K \left[ \dfrac{V^{\,1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_2}^{V_1} = \dfrac{K}{1-n} \left( V_1^{\,1-n} - V_2^{\,1-n} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $K = P_1 V_1^n$ and simplify:**

$$E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left( V_1^{\,1-n} - V_2^{\,1-n} \right) = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_1 V_1^n V_2^{\,1-n}}{1-n}$$

Recognizing $V_1^n V_2^{\,1-n} = V_2 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^n \cdot ... $. The cleanest KTU final form is:

$$\boxed{\,E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case (n = 1, Isothermal):** The expression above is undefined for n = 1 (division by zero). Use the limit:

$$E_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sizing an Accumulator for a Required Energy Storage

**Given:** A hydraulic hybrid regenerative braking system must store $E_{\text{req}}$ Joules of kinetic energy. Working pressure range is $P_1$ to $P_2$. Gas pre-charge is $P_0$. Polytropic index is $n$. Find the **minimum required accumulator volume $V_0$**.

**Step 1 — Express Energy in Terms of V₀:**
From Derivation 2 and using $V_1 = P_0 V_0 / P_1$ and $V_2 = V_0 (P_0/P_2)^{1/n}$:

$$E = \dfrac{1}{n-1} \left[ P_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_1} - P_2 \cdot V_0 \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \right]$$

$$E = \dfrac{V_0}{n-1} \left[ P_0 - P_0^{1/n} \, P_2^{\,1 - 1/n} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $P_0$ and $P_2$:**

$$E = \dfrac{V_0 \, P_0}{n-1} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{(n-1)/n} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for V₀:**

$$\boxed{\,V_0 = \dfrac{E_{\text{req}} \, (n-1)}{P_0 \left[ 1 - (P_0/P_2)^{(n-1)/n} \right]}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Question)

**Problem:** A bladder-type accumulator has geometric volume $V_0 = 10$ L, pre-charge $P_0 = 100$ bar, and operates between $P_1 = 150$ bar and $P_2 = 350$ bar. Assume **polytropic index n = 1.4**. Calculate:
(a) Usable fluid volume ΔV.
(b) Energy stored.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Usable Volume:**

$$\Delta V = V_0 \left[ \dfrac{P_0}{P_1} - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \right]$$

Plugging values:

$$\dfrac{P_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{100}{150} = 0.6667$$

$$\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{100}{350}\right)^{1/1.4} = (0.2857)^{0.7143}$$

Computing the exponent:

$$\ln(0.2857) = -1.2528 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -1.2528 \times 0.7143 = -0.8948$$

$$\Rightarrow \exp(-0.8948) = 0.4086$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta V = 10 \, \bigl[ 0.6667 - 0.4086 \bigr] = 10 \times 0.2581 = 2.581 \text{ L}$$

### **Answer (a): ΔV ≈ 2.58 L of usable hydraulic fluid.**

**Part (b) — Energy Stored:**

First, compute $V_1$ and $V_2$:

$$V_1 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{100 \times 10}{150} = 6.667 \text{ L}$$

$$V_2 = V_0 (P_0/P_2)^{1/n} = 10 \times 0.4086 = 4.086 \text{ L}$$

Now apply the energy equation (convert bar·L → J: 1 bar·L = 100,000 Pa × 10⁻³ m³ = **100 J**):

$$E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$$

$$P_1 V_1 = 150 \times 6.667 = 1000 \, \text{bar·L}$$

$$P_2 V_2 = 350 \times 4.086 = 1430.1 \, \text{bar·L}$$

$$E = \dfrac{1000 - 1430.1}{1.4 - 1} = \dfrac{-430.1}{0.4} = -1075.25 \, \text{bar·L}$$

(The negative sign indicates work done *by* the gas on the fluid; magnitude is energy stored.)

$$E = 1075.25 \, \text{bar·L} \times 100 \, \text{J/(bar·L)} = 107,525 \, \text{J} \approx \textbf{107.5 kJ}$$

### **Answer (b): E ≈ 107.5 kJ of stored energy.**

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Accumulator

| Selection Criterion | Bladder | Piston | Diaphragm |
|---------------------|---------|--------|-----------|
| Max Pressure Rating | 350 bar | 700 bar | 250 bar |
| Typical Capacity | 0.1 – 50 L | 1 – 500 L | 0.1 – 4 L |
| Dynamic Response | Fast (< 30 ms) | Slow (≈ 100 ms) | Very Fast (< 15 ms) |
| Gas-to-Fluid Ratio | High (~75%) | Medium (~50%) | High (~75%) |
| Cycle Life | ~ 10⁶ | > 10⁶ | ~ 5 × 10⁵ |
| Cost (per liter) | Medium | High | Low |
| Best Use in HV | Mid-pressure regen | High-pressure launch | Pressure dampening |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Accumulator in a Hybrid Regenerative Braking Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[VEHICLE KINETIC ENERGY] --> node2[BRAKE PEDAL ACTUATION]
    node2 --> node3[HYDRAULIC PUMP MOTOR UNIT]
    node3 --> node4[HIGH PRESSURE HYDRAULIC LINE]
    node4 --> node5[CHECK VALVE 1]
    node5 --> node6[BLADDER ACCUMULATOR]
    node6 --> node7[N2 GAS PRE CHARGE 200 BAR]
    node6 --> node8[HYDRAULIC FLUID STORAGE]
    node8 --> node9[CHECK VALVE 2]
    node9 --> node10[PRESSURE REGULATION VALVE]
    node10 --> node11[HYDRAULIC MOTOR FOR LAUNCH ASSIST]
    node11 --> node12[DRIVETRAIN RE-ENGAGEMENT]
    node13[ENGINE] -. Idle Cut Off .-> node11
    node4 --> node14[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE 350 BAR]
    node14 --> node15[RESERVOIR TANK]
    node15 --> node3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above represents a **Hydraulic Hybrid Vehicle (HHV)** drivetrain. The accumulator (node6) sits at the energy-storage node, with the gas pre-charge (node7) and fluid (node8) as its two internal states. The check valves (node5 and node9) prevent reverse flow during charging and discharge respectively.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of the Three Gas-Loaded Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AccumTypeA[BLADDER ACCUMULATOR]
        nodeA1[FORGED STEEL SHELL] --> nodeA2[ELASTOMERIC BLADDER]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3[N2 GAS CHARGE TOP VALVE]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA4[HYDRAULIC OIL BOTTOM PORT]
        nodeA4 --> nodeA5[POPPET VALVE ANTI EXTRUSION]
    end
    subgraph AccumTypeB[PISTON ACCUMULATOR]
        nodeB1[HONED STEEL CYLINDER] --> nodeB2[SEALED METALLIC PISTON]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[N2 GAS CHAMBER TOP]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB4[HYDRAULIC OIL CHAMBER BOTTOM]
        nodeB4 --> nodeB5[PTFE SEAL RINGS]
    end
    subgraph AccumTypeC[DIAPHRAGM ACCUMULATOR]
        nodeC1[TWO PIECE FORGED SHELL] --> nodeC2[HEMISPHERICAL DIAPHRAGM]
        nodeC2 --> nodeC3[N2 GAS ABOVE]
        nodeC2 --> nodeC4[HYDRAULIC OIL BELOW]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 P-V (Pressure-Volume) Working Diagram for a Polytropic Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[STATE 0 PRE CHARGE P0 V0] --> nodeS2[STATE 1 MIN PRESSURE P1 V1]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[STATE 2 MAX PRESSURE P2 V2]
    nodeS3 -. Gas Expansion Release .-> nodeS2
    nodeS2 -. Gas Compression Charge .-> nodeS3
    nodeS1 -. Initial Inflation .-> nodeS2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the P-V Diagram:** The horizontal axis is **gas volume** (decreasing as fluid is pumped in). The vertical axis is **gas pressure** (increasing during charge). The area under the curve from $V_2$ to $V_1$ is the **stored hydraulic energy E** released when the accumulator discharges from state 2 back to state 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between bladder-type and piston-type hydraulic accumulators. Mention two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bladder Type | Piston Type |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Sealing Element | Elastomeric rubber bladder | Metallic piston with PTFE/rubber seals |
| **Advantage 1** | Higher gas-to-fluid ratio (~75%) → compact for given energy | Higher pressure capability (up to 700 bar) |
| **Advantage 2** | Faster dynamic response due to no friction | Longer cycle life (>10⁶ cycles), robust construction |
| **Disadvantage 1** | Bladder wear and limited cycle life (~10⁶) | Higher internal friction → slower response, hysteresis |
| **Disadvantage 2** | Limited pressure rating (~350 bar max) | Higher cost per liter capacity |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is meant by "pre-charge pressure" of a hydraulic accumulator? Why must it always be less than the minimum system pressure?**

**Model Answer:**
The **pre-charge pressure ($P_0$)** is the pressure of the nitrogen gas sealed inside the accumulator *before* any hydraulic fluid is introduced into the system. It is set during factory charging using a gas valve (Schrader-type).

**Why $P_0 < P_{\min}$:**
If the pre-charge pressure $P_0$ were equal to or greater than the minimum system working pressure $P_1$, the gas would already be at or above the system's lower operating limit even before charging begins. Consequently:
- The gas would not be able to expand and discharge fluid at $P_1$.
- The accumulator would be inoperable in its pressure window.
- The bladder/piston would be pre-compressed against the hydraulic port, blocking fluid inflow.

**Design Rule:** Typically, $P_0 \leq 0.9 \times P_1$ for maximum energy density, and $P_0 \geq 0.25 \times P_2$ for safe mechanical margins.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, with 7+7 Sub-parts)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic accumulator is required to deliver **6 liters** of fluid between pressure limits of **$P_1 = 140$ bar** and **$P_2 = 280$ bar**. The nitrogen pre-charge pressure is **$P_0 = 100$ bar**. Assuming **polytropic index n = 1.4**, calculate:
  (i) The required geometric volume $V_0$ of the accumulator.
  (ii) The energy stored in the gas during charging.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the application of hydraulic accumulators in a **Hydraulic Hybrid Vehicle (HHV)** for regenerative braking. Discuss why piston-type accumulators are preferred over bladder-type for heavy-duty HHVs like the Eaton UPS delivery truck.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **useful fluid discharge volume** $\Delta V$ from a gas-loaded hydraulic accumulator operating between pressures $P_1$ and $P_2$ with pre-charge $P_0$. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5-liter piston-type accumulator is pre-charged to **80 bar** of nitrogen. The hydraulic system operates between **150 bar and 300 bar**.
  (i) Compute the volume of fluid discharged between the two pressure limits (assume n = 1.4).
  (ii) Calculate the energy released if the discharge is **isothermal** (n = 1.0).
  (iii) Compare the two energy values and comment on which assumption is more conservative.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Question A

### Question A — Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**
$V_0 = ?$ (to find), $P_0 = 100$ bar, $P_1 = 140$ bar, $P_2 = 280$ bar, $n = 1.4$, $\Delta V = 6$ L.

**Step 2 — Apply the ΔV formula:**

$$\Delta V = V_0 \left[ \dfrac{P_0}{P_1} - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate terms:**
$$\dfrac{P_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{100}{140} = 0.7143$$

$$\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{100}{280}\right)^{1/1.4} = (0.3571)^{0.7143}$$

Compute:
$$\ln(0.3571) = -1.0296$$
$$-1.0296 \times 0.7143 = -0.7354$$
$$\exp(-0.7354) = 0.4792$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V₀:**

$$6 = V_0 \, [0.7143 - 0.4792] = V_0 \times 0.2351$$

$$V_0 = \dfrac{6}{0.2351} = \textbf{25.52 L}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $V_1$ and $V_2$:**
$$V_1 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{100 \times 25.52}{140} = 18.23 \, \text{L}$$

$$V_2 = V_0 \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = 25.52 \times 0.4792 = 12.23 \, \text{L}$$

**Step 6 — Energy Stored:**
$$P_1 V_1 = 140 \times 18.23 = 2552.2 \, \text{bar·L}$$
$$P_2 V_2 = 280 \times 12.23 = 3424.4 \, \text{bar·L}$$

$$E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1} = \dfrac{2552.2 - 3424.4}{0.4} = \dfrac{-872.2}{0.4} = -2180.5 \, \text{bar·L}$$

Taking magnitude and converting (**1 bar·L = 100 J**):

$$E = 2180.5 \times 100 = \textbf{218,050 J} \approx \textbf{218 kJ}$$

**Final Answer (a):**
(i) $V_0 \approx 25.52$ L
(ii) $E \approx 218$ kJ

---

### Question A — Part (b) Solution

**Block Diagram of HHV Regenerative Braking:**

```
[Wheel Brake Demand] → [Pump/Motor Unit] → [High-Pressure Line] → [Accumulator]
                                                                       ↓
                                                          [Stored Hydraulic Energy]
                                                                       ↓
[Launch Assist / Acceleration] ← [Hydraulic Motor] ← [Pressure Regulating Valve]
```

**Explanation:**
1. During **braking**, the kinetic energy of the decelerating vehicle drives a **hydraulic pump** (acting as a motor), forcing high-pressure fluid into the accumulator. The nitrogen gas compresses, storing energy.
2. During **launch or acceleration**, the high-pressure fluid is released from the accumulator, driving a **hydraulic motor** that assists the engine (or fully propels the vehicle in low-speed urban cycles).
3. The accumulator acts as an **energy buffer**, decoupling the instantaneous braking energy from the energy release profile during propulsion.

**Why Piston-Type is Preferred for Heavy-Duty HHVs:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Higher Pressure Rating (700 bar)** | Required for storing the large kinetic energy of heavy trucks (~ 10–20 tons). Bladders typically max out at 350 bar. |
| **Larger Capacity (up to 500 L)** | UPS-class trucks need 50–100 L of fluid storage per accumulator; bladder types usually cap at 50 L. |
| **Robustness & Cycle Life** | Heavy-duty vehicles undergo 1000s of braking events per shift; pistons endure > 10⁶ cycles without seal failure. |
| **Lower Permeation Loss** | Metallic piston seals are impermeable, unlike elastomeric bladders that slowly leak N₂ over years. |

---

## 5.4 Complete Model Solutions for Question B

### Question B — Part (a) Solution (Derivation — see Section 3.1)

**Assumptions Stated:**
1. The pre-charge gas obeys the **ideal gas law**.
2. The compression/expansion process is **polytropic** with index $n$ (1.0 ≤ n ≤ 1.4).
3. The hydraulic fluid is **incompressible** (bulk modulus is effectively infinite).
4. **No heat transfer** to surroundings within the cycle (or quasi-isothermal — depends on $n$).
5. The accumulator is **perfectly sealed** — no gas permeation through the bladder/piston.

**Final Derived Expression:**
$$\Delta V = V_0 \left[ \dfrac{P_0}{P_1} - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \right]$$

---

### Question B — Part (b) Solution

**Given:** $V_0 = 5$ L, $P_0 = 80$ bar, $P_1 = 150$ bar, $P_2 = 300$ bar.

**(i) Polytropic Discharge (n = 1.4):**

$$\Delta V = 5 \left[ \dfrac{80}{150} - \left(\dfrac{80}{300}\right)^{1/1.4} \right]$$

$$\dfrac{80}{150} = 0.5333$$

$$\left(\dfrac{80}{300}\right)^{1/1.4} = (0.2667)^{0.7143}$$

Computing:
$$\ln(0.2667) = -1.3218$$
$$-1.3218 \times 0.7143 = -0.9441$$
$$\exp(-0.9441) = 0.3890$$

$$\Delta V = 5 \times [0.5333 - 0.3890] = 5 \times 0.1443 = \textbf{0.7215 L}$$

**(ii) Isothermal Energy (n = 1.0):**

First, compute $V_1$ and $V_2$:
$$V_1 = \dfrac{80 \times 5}{150} = 2.667 \, \text{L}$$
$$V_2 = 5 \times (80/300) = 5 \times 0.2667 = 1.333 \, \text{L}$$

Apply isothermal energy formula:

$$E_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$P_1 V_1 = 150 \times 2.667 = 400 \, \text{bar·L}$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{300}{150}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

$$E_{\text{iso}} = 400 \times 0.6931 = 277.24 \, \text{bar·L} = 277.24 \times 100 = \textbf{27,724 J} \approx \textbf{27.72 kJ}$$

**(iii) Comparison:**

| Parameter | Polytropic (n = 1.4) | Isothermal (n = 1.0) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| ΔV | 0.7215 L | 0.7215 L (same, only depends on $V_0$ & pressures) |
| Energy E | Computed below | **27.72 kJ** |

Polytropic energy:

$$V_2 \text{ (poly)} = 5 \times (80/300)^{0.7143} = 5 \times 0.3890 = 1.945 \, \text{L}$$

$$E_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1} = \dfrac{150(2.667) - 300(1.945)}{0.4} = \dfrac{400 - 583.5}{0.4} = \dfrac{-183.5}{0.4} = 458.75 \, \text{bar·L}$$

$$E_{\text{poly}} = 458.75 \times 100 = \textbf{45,875 J} \approx \textbf{45.88 kJ}$$

**Comment:** The isothermal assumption **under-predicts** the energy stored (27.72 kJ vs. 45.88 kJ actual). This is because in reality, when the gas is compressed rapidly during braking, it heats up and reaches a higher pressure than the isothermal case — storing **more** energy. Thus, **the isothermal assumption is more conservative** for safety factor sizing, but the **polytropic/adiabatic assumption is more realistic** for fast-charge hybrid braking cycles.

---

## 5.5 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1/n exponent** in the $(P_0/P_2)^{1/n}$ term — students frequently write it as $(P_0/P_2)$, leading to incorrect answers. **Lose 3–4 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing units**: bar·L is NOT Joules. The conversion **1 bar·L = 100 J** must be explicitly stated. Examiners deduct if conversion is omitted. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Using n = 1 for fast processes**: For accumulator sizing in hybrid braking (typically < 10 s cycles), **n = 1.4 must be used**. Using n = 1 under-estimates gas pressure rise and gives oversized (unsafe) accumulators. **Lose full 7 marks on Part (a).**
> 4. **Sign confusion in the energy formula**: The numerator $P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2$ is **negative** during discharge (since $V_2 < V_1$ but $P_2 V_2 > P_1 V_1$). Students often report a negative energy value, losing the conceptual mark. **Always take magnitude and write "E_stored = |...|".**
> 5. **Pre-charge rule violation**: Setting $P_0 \geq P_1$ results in zero usable volume. KTU frequently tests this as a "trick" sub-question. **Lose 2 marks.**

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Hydraulic accumulator = **hydro-pneumatic energy storage device** using compressed N₂ separated from hydraulic oil by a deformable element.
- **Three Types:** **Bladder** (most common, fast response, mid-pressure), **Piston** (high-pressure, high-capacity, robust), **Diaphragm** (small, fast, low-pressure).
- **Why N₂:** Chemically inert, non-flammable, cheap, low permeability through elastomers.
- **Pre-charge Rule:** $P_0 \leq 0.9 \times P_1$ — if violated, the accumulator becomes inoperable.
- **Polytropic Law:** $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ — index **n = 1.0** (isothermal, slow), **n = 1.4** (adiabatic, fast, KTU default).
- **ΔV Formula:** $\Delta V = V_0 \left[\dfrac{P_0}{P_1} - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}\right]$ — **the 1/n exponent is critical**.
- **Energy Formula (n ≠ 1):** $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$.
- **Energy Formula (n = 1):** $E = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- **Unit Conversion:** **1 bar·L = 100 J** — always state this in derivations.
- **HV Applications:** Regenerative braking energy storage (UPS Eaton trucks), launch assist, brake-by-wire emergency power.
- **Efficiency:** Round-trip accumulator efficiency ≈ **85%** due to friction and gas hysteresis.
- **Pressure Ratings:** Bladder ≈ 350 bar, Piston ≈ 700 bar, Diaphragm ≈ 250 bar.
- **Cycle Life:** Piston > Bladder > Diaphragm.
- **Assumption Chain (KTU Standard):** Ideal gas + Incompressible fluid + Polytropic process + Sealed system + Uniform gas temperature.
- **For energy sizing in a hybrid vehicle accumulator, ALWAYS use n = 1.4 unless explicitly stated otherwise.**

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Hydraulic Pumps and Motors — Core Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** Hydraulic Pumps/Motors form the *actuation heart* of every hybrid hydraulic accumulator system, regenerative braking loop, and hydraulic launch-assist driveline. Mastering displacement, flow, torque and efficiency relations here directly powers Module 4 (Energy Storage) and Module 5 (Hybrid Drivetrain Integration).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **hydraulic pump** is a *positive-displacement (PD)* or *non-positive-displacement* machine that converts **mechanical energy** from a prime-mover (typically an Internal Combustion Engine or an Electric Traction Motor) into **hydraulic energy** in the form of pressurized, metered fluid flow.

A **hydraulic motor** is the exact energy-reversal counterpart — it converts the **hydraulic energy** of a pressurized fluid back into **rotational mechanical energy** (torque × angular velocity) to drive a mechanical load such as a wheel hub, an actuator, or a generator in a regenerative system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Symmetry Principle:** A *fixed-displacement hydraulic pump* can be operated as a *hydraulic motor* (and vice-versa) by simply reversing the inlet/outlet ports — the governing geometry is identical. Variable-displacement units require directional port swaps due to their swash-plate control logic.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **calibrated syringe connected to a closed water pipe**:

- When you **push the plunger inward** with force, water shoots out under high pressure. You supply mechanical work; you get pressurized fluid. **You are acting as a PUMP.**
- Now imagine **high-pressure water enters the syringe from the pipe** and pushes the plunger outward on its own — that rod will now move and could turn a wheel. **The syringe is now acting as a MOTOR.**

A hydraulic pump does the *pushing*. A hydraulic motor is *being pushed by the fluid* and gives back rotation. In a Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive or a parallel hydraulic hybrid bus (e.g., Eaton hydraulic launch assist), the same physical pump/motor unit swaps role repeatedly — pumping during regenerative braking and motoring during launch assist.

## 1.3 Why Hydraulic Pumps/Motors in Hybrid Vehicles?

| Application | Role of Pump | Role of Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Hybrid Launch Assist** | Pressurizes accumulator during braking | Drives wheels from stored pressure during launch |
| **Regenerative Braking Storage** | Pumps fluid into Nitrogen-charged accumulator | — |
| **Active Suspension / Actuators** | Supplies flow to ride-height actuators | Drives linear/rotary motion of suspension |
| **Hydrostatic Transmission** | Replaces gearbox — driven by engine | Drives differential from engine's hydraulic energy |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Board-Exam Buzzwords:** Always remember — *Displacement (V_D)*, *Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)*, *Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)*, *Overall Efficiency (η_o)*, *Pump input power*, *Motor output power*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic Pumps and Motors

Hydraulic machines are broadly classified based on how fluid is moved through them:

### A. Non-Positive Displacement (Hydrodynamic) Pumps
- Centrifugal pumps, axial-flow pumps.
- Deliver flow proportional to speed; pressure is a *function of system resistance*.
- **Rarely used** in HEV/FCEV hydraulic accumulators because they cannot build the high, stable pressures needed for energy storage.

### B. Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps/Motors — *Dominant in HEV*
PD machines **trap a fixed volume of fluid** and mechanically *force* (or *are forced by*) it from inlet to outlet. This makes them ideal for high-pressure accumulator charging in hybrid vehicles.

#### B1. Gear Pumps/Motors (External & Internal)
- Simple, cheap, robust. Two meshing gears trap fluid between teeth and casing.
- **Use-case in HEV:** Low-to-medium pressure auxiliary systems (power steering in hybrids).

#### B2. Vane Pumps/Motors
- A slotted rotor with sliding vanes inside an elliptical cam ring.
- Used in **variable-displacement** form via cam-ring tilting — popular in **hydraulic hybrid buses (UPS, Eaton systems)**.

#### B3. Piston Pumps/Motors (Axial & Radial)
- **Axial-piston swash-plate type**: Most common in HEV regenerative systems due to high efficiency (>90%) and ability to operate continuously at 350–420 bar.
- **Radial-piston cam-ring type**: Used in very high torque, low-speed applications (e.g., in-wheel hub motors).

## 2.2 Governing Physics — Theory of Operation

For any positive-displacement machine, the **displacement volume per revolution (V_D)** is the *single most important geometric parameter*. It dictates *how much fluid is moved per cycle*.

- **Pump:** A mechanical shaft drives the pumping element; fluid is *expelled* from the outlet at high pressure.
- **Motor:** Pressurized fluid *enters* and pushes the pumping element; the same element now rotates a shaft against an external load.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Flow Rate (Pump) | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ | m³/s or L/min | $V_D$ in m³/rev, $N$ in rev/s |
| 2 | Actual Flow Rate (Pump) | $Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | $\eta_v$ = Volumetric Efficiency |
| 3 | Theoretical Torque (Motor) | $T_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across machine |
| 4 | Actual Torque (Motor) | $T_{actual} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m$ | N·m | $\eta_m$ = Mechanical Efficiency |
| 5 | Hydraulic (Mechanical) Power | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W (or kW) | Universal input/output power |
| 6 | Shaft Power (Pump input) | $P_{shaft,pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta_t}$ | W | $\eta_t$ = Overall efficiency |
| 7 | Shaft Power (Motor output) | $P_{shaft,motor} = \Delta P \cdot Q \cdot \eta_t$ | W | Reverse application |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | < 1 due to internal leakage |
| 9 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | < 1 due to friction |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | dimensionless | Critical for HEV energy budgeting |
| 11 | Pump Input Torque | $T_{pump,in} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$ | N·m | |
| 12 | Motor Output Torque | $T_{motor,out} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m$ | N·m | |
| 13 | Specific Power (Pump) | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P}{\text{Mass}}$ | kW/kg | Metric for HEV power-density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Validity (Always State in Exams):**
> - Formulas assume **steady-state** operation; transient losses (cavitation, fluid compression) neglected.
> - $V_D$ is **constant** for *fixed-displacement* machines; varies with swash-plate angle in *variable-displacement* units.
> - $\eta_v \to 1$ as $N$ increases (leakage is roughly constant in volume); $\eta_m \to 1$ as $\Delta P$ decreases (less friction work).
> - **Maximum operating $\Delta P$** for axial-piston units: ~420 bar (typical HEV accumulator charging).

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in HEV Engineering

When sizing the hydraulic pump/motor for an accumulator launch-assist system, the engineer must solve three nested problems:

1. **Energy balance** — the accumulator stores $\frac{1}{2} P_{max} V_{accum}$ of hydraulic energy; the pump must fill it within a target brake-time window.
2. **Power sizing** — $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$; this directly drives the choice of electric traction motor driving the pump.
3. **Thermal budgeting** — losses $P_{loss} = P_{in} (1 - \eta_o)$ become heat that must be rejected by the hydraulic fluid cooler; in FCEVs, this heat can partially be harvested to warm the fuel cell stack — a beautiful *cascaded energy-recovery* strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Flow Rate of a Positive-Displacement Pump

> **Goal:** Prove that $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$.

Consider a single pumping chamber of volume $V_D$ (in m³/rev) that fills once and empties once every full rotation.

**Step 1 — Define volume per revolution.**
The geometric *displacement* $V_D$ is the volume of fluid physically enclosed between the inlet and outlet sealing zones of the pumping element per revolution. For a piston pump with $z$ pistons of area $A$ and stroke $s$:

$$
V_D = z \cdot A \cdot s
$$

**Step 2 — Define rotational speed.**
Let $N$ be the shaft rotational speed in *revolutions per second (rev/s)*.

**Step 3 — Set up the flow equation.**
In one revolution, the chamber displaces $V_D$ cubic metres of fluid. Over $N$ revolutions per second, the total volume displaced per second is:

$$
Q_{th} = (\text{Volume per rev}) \times (\text{Revs per sec})
$$

$$
Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N
$$

**Step 4 — Check units.**
$[V_D] = \text{m}^3/\text{rev}$, $[N] = \text{rev/s}$. Multiplying gives m³/s. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Torque of a Hydraulic Motor

> **Goal:** Prove that $T_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}$.

The hydraulic motor is the *energy-reversed* twin of a pump. Energy must balance.

**Step 1 — Energy crossing the motor per revolution.**
In one revolution, a volume $V_D$ of fluid crosses the motor at pressure differential $\Delta P$. The hydraulic work done *on the rotor* is:

$$
W_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

**Step 2 — Convert hydraulic energy to mechanical rotation.**
Mechanical work per revolution is torque multiplied by angular displacement per revolution:

$$
W_{mech} = T_{th} \cdot (2\pi)
$$

The factor $2\pi$ is the angular displacement (in radians) traversed in one full revolution.

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of energy (frictionless ideal motor).**
Setting hydraulic work = mechanical work:

$$
T_{th} \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for torque.**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}
$$

This is the **ideal theoretical torque** with 100% mechanical efficiency.

## 3.3 Derivation: Actual Pump Flow with Volumetric Efficiency

> **Goal:** Prove that $Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$.

**Step 1 — Real machines leak.**
In a real PD pump, pressurized fluid always *leaks* back from the outlet chamber to the inlet chamber through clearances (between piston and bore, between gear tips and casing, etc.). This leakage $Q_L$ is approximately **constant** (independent of speed) and depends on $\Delta P$.

**Step 2 — Theoretical vs. Actual flow.**
Theoretical flow is $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$. The actual *useful* flow delivered to the system is theoretical minus internal leakage:

$$
Q_{actual} = Q_{th} - Q_{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out theoretical flow.**

$$
Q_{actual} = Q_{th} \left(1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_{th}}\right) = V_D \cdot N \left(1 - \frac{Q_L}{V_D \cdot N}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define volumetric efficiency.**

By definition, $\eta_v = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{V_D \cdot N}$. Substituting:

$$
Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for Board Exam:** Because leakage $Q_L$ is roughly constant, $\eta_v \to 1$ as $N$ increases. This is *why* hydraulic hybrid systems always operate the pump at **high shaft speed** — it maximizes volumetric efficiency.

## 3.4 Derivation: Overall Efficiency as the Product of Volumetric and Mechanical Efficiencies

> **Goal:** Show $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.

**Step 1 — Define each efficiency precisely (using pump convention).**

- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{V_D \cdot N}$
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{theoretical}}{T_{actual,pump}} = \dfrac{T_{th}}{T_{in}}$  (pump takes *more* torque than ideal due to friction)

**Step 2 — Write actual pump input power.**

$$
P_{in,actual} = T_{in} \cdot 2\pi N
$$

**Step 3 — Write useful hydraulic power output.**

$$
P_{hyd,useful} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{actual} = \Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{in}$ from mechanical efficiency relation.**

From $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{th}}{T_{in}}$, we get $T_{in} = \dfrac{T_{th}}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$.

Therefore:

$$
P_{in,actual} = \left(\frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}\right) \cdot 2\pi N = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P \cdot N}{\eta_m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute overall efficiency.**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{hyd,useful}}{P_{in,actual}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v}{\dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P \cdot N}{\eta_m}} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m}
$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** An axial-piston hydraulic pump has a geometric displacement of $V_D = 28 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and is driven at $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$. The system pressure is $\Delta P = 350 \text{ bar}$. Volumetric efficiency = 0.96, mechanical efficiency = 0.93. Calculate:
> (a) Actual flow rate in L/min
> (b) Input shaft torque in N·m
> (c) Input shaft power in kW
> (d) Overall efficiency

**Given:**
- $V_D = 28 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $N = 3000 \text{ rpm} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$
- $\Delta P = 350 \text{ bar} = 350 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 3.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta_v = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.93$

**(a) Actual flow rate:**

$$
Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v = (28 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 50 \cdot 0.96 = 1.344 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to L/min (1 m³/s = 60 000 L/min):

$$
Q_{actual} = 1.344 \times 10^{-3} \times 60\,000 = 80.64 \text{ L/min}
$$

**(b) Input shaft torque:**

$$
T_{in} = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m} = \frac{(28 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^7)}{2\pi \cdot 0.93}
$$

$$
T_{in} = \frac{980}{5.842} = 167.74 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(c) Input shaft power:**

$$
P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot 2\pi N = 167.74 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 50 = 52\,694 \text{ W} \approx 52.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**(d) Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.96 \times 0.93 = 0.8928 \approx 89.3\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{actual}$:**
> $P_{hyd} = 3.5 \times 10^7 \cdot 1.344 \times 10^{-3} = 47\,040 \text{ W} = 47.04 \text{ kW}$.
> $\eta_o = P_{hyd}/P_{in} = 47.04/52.69 = 0.8928$ ✓ Matches the product.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pump & Motor Roles in HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine or EV Traction Motor] -->|Mechanical Shaft| PUMP[Variable-Displacement Axial Piston Pump]
    PUMP -->|Pressurized Hydraulic Fluid 350 bar| ACCU[High-Pressure Accumulator Nitrogen Pre-charge]
    ACCU -->|Stored Hydraulic Energy| MOTOR[Variable-Displacement Piston Motor]
    MOTOR -->|Mechanical Shaft Torque| LOAD[Wheel Hub or Generator]
    PUMP -.->|Internal Leakage Q_L| LEAK[Leakage Path]
    PUMP -.->|Friction Loss| FRIC[Friction Loss]
    FRIC --> HEAT[Hydraulic Fluid Cooler]
    HEAT -.->|Waste Heat Recovery| STK[FCEV Fuel Cell Stack Pre-warming]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Positive-Displacement Pumps & Motors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PD[Positive Displacement Pumps and Motors]
    PD --> GEAR[Gear Type]
    PD --> VANE[Vane Type]
    PD --> PIST[Piston Type]

    GEAR --> EXT[External Gear]
    GEAR --> INT[Internal Gear]

    VANE --> FIXV[Fixed Cam Ring]
    VANE --> VARV[Variable Cam Ring Tilted]

    PIST --> AXI[Axial Piston Swash Plate]
    PIST --> RAD[Radial Piston Cam Ring]

    AXI --> FIXA[Fixed Displacement]
    AXI --> VARA[Variable Displacement by Swash Plate Tilt Angle]
```

## 4.3 Energy-Flow Schematic of a Hydraulic Hybrid Launch-Assist System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brake Pedal Event] --> B[Wheel Decelerates]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Pump Engaged]
    C --> D[Pump Pressurizes Fluid]
    D --> E[High Pressure Fluid Charges Accumulator]
    E --> F[Energy Stored at 350 bar]

    F --> G[Vehicle Needs Launch Assist]
    G --> H[Hydraulic Motor Engaged]
    H --> I[Pressurized Fluid Drives Motor]
    I --> J[Motor Outputs Torque to Wheels]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for valuation:** In KTU board answers, you can reproduce simplified versions of any of the above block-diagrams for full marks in the *schematic* portion of a 14-mark question. Always **label arrows with energy type** (mechanical / hydraulic / thermal) and **annotate pressures** where applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Define the term "volumetric efficiency" of a hydraulic pump. State one physical cause of its deviation from unity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$) of a hydraulic pump is defined as the ratio of the *actual delivered flow rate* to the *theoretical (geometric) flow rate*:

$$
\eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{V_D \cdot N}
$$

**Physical cause of deviation:** Internal leakage of pressurized fluid from the outlet chamber back to the inlet chamber through the small clearances between moving parts (e.g., between piston and bore, between gear-tooth tips and casing). This leakage reduces the net flow delivered to the external circuit, causing $\eta_v < 1$.

*(Award: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark cause.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Why is an axial-piston swash-plate pump preferred over a gear pump for hydraulic hybrid energy-storage systems in heavy vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher operating pressure** — axial-piston units routinely operate at 350–420 bar; gear pumps are typically limited to ~200 bar. Higher pressure means **more energy stored per unit accumulator volume** (Energy $\propto P$).
2. **Higher overall efficiency** — well-designed axial-piston units achieve $\eta_o > 90\%$, while gear pumps typically operate at 75–85%.
3. **Variable displacement capability** — tilting the swash plate allows continuous adjustment of $V_D$, giving precise control of flow and power during regenerative braking (variable vehicle deceleration).

*(Award: 1 mark per point.)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** from a Module-internal choice of two.

---

### ❓ Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Choice Q1]

**Q. A (a)** Derive the expression for the theoretical torque developed by a hydraulic motor in terms of its geometric displacement $V_D$ and pressure differential $\Delta P$. State clearly the assumptions used.

**Q. A (b)** A variable-displacement axial-piston hydraulic pump delivers fluid to a hydraulic motor. The pump displacement is $V_{D,p} = 45 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, driven at $N_p = 2400 \text{ rpm}$, with volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v,p} = 0.95$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_{m,p} = 0.92$. The motor has $V_{D,m} = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $\eta_{v,m} = 0.97$, $\eta_{m,m} = 0.94$, and runs at $N_m = 1800 \text{ rpm}$. The system pressure is $\Delta P = 320 \text{ bar}$. Calculate:
- (i) Flow rate delivered by the pump (in L/min)
- (ii) Theoretical and actual torque of the motor
- (iii) Input shaft power to the pump and output shaft power of the motor

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. A (a) — [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation.
2. Fluid is incompressible.
3. No internal leakage (ideal machine).
4. No mechanical friction losses.
5. Pressure differential $\Delta P$ is uniform across the motor's working chambers.

**Derivation:**

In one revolution of the motor, a volume $V_D$ of fluid at pressure $\Delta P$ crosses the motor. The hydraulic energy delivered per revolution is:

$$
W_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

This energy is converted into mechanical work over an angular displacement of $2\pi$ radians:

$$
W_{mech} = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi
$$

Applying conservation of energy (assuming 100% efficiency):

$$
T_{th} \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

Solving for $T_{th}$:

$$
\boxed{T_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}}
$$

*(Marks: 2 for assumptions, 4 for step-by-step derivation with energy balance, 1 for final boxed expression.)*

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. A (b) — [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $V_{D,p} = 45 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $N_p = 2400 \text{ rpm} = 40 \text{ rev/s}$
- $\eta_{v,p} = 0.95$, $\eta_{m,p} = 0.92$
- $V_{D,m} = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $N_m = 1800 \text{ rpm} = 30 \text{ rev/s}$
- $\eta_{v,m} = 0.97$, $\eta_{m,m} = 0.94$
- $\Delta P = 320 \text{ bar} = 3.2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Pump actual flow rate:**

$$
Q_{p} = V_{D,p} \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_{v,p} = (45 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 40 \cdot 0.95
$$

$$
Q_p = 1.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 102.6 \text{ L/min}
$$

**(ii) Motor theoretical and actual torque:**

$$
T_{th,m} = \frac{V_{D,m} \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} = \frac{(60 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (3.2 \times 10^7)}{2\pi}
$$

$$
T_{th,m} = \frac{1920}{6.2832} = 305.58 \text{ N·m}
$$

Actual motor torque (output) = $T_{th,m} \cdot \eta_{m,m}$:

$$
T_{actual,m} = 305.58 \times 0.94 = 287.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Pump input power and motor output power:**

Pump input power:

$$
P_{in,p} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_p}{\eta_{o,p}} \quad \text{where} \quad \eta_{o,p} = 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874
$$

$$
P_{in,p} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 1.71 \times 10^{-3}}{0.874} = \frac{54\,720}{0.874} = 62\,609 \text{ W} \approx 62.61 \text{ kW}
$$

Motor output power:

$$
P_{out,m} = T_{actual,m} \cdot 2\pi N_m = 287.25 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 30
$$

$$
P_{out,m} = 287.25 \cdot 188.496 = 54\,151 \text{ W} \approx 54.15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Transmission efficiency of pump-motor pair:**

$$
\eta_{system} = \frac{P_{out,m}}{P_{in,p}} = \frac{54.15}{62.61} \approx 86.5\%
$$

*(Marks: 1 setup + 1 each for (i)(ii)(iii) + 0.5 unit conversions + 0.5 final cross-check.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Examiner's Warning:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $V_D$** — converting cm³/rev to m³/rev is the *#1 place students lose marks*. Use the factor $10^{-6}$ for cm³ → m³.
> 2. **Confusing pump vs. motor convention** — For pumps, $T_{in} = T_{th}/\eta_m$; for motors, $T_{out} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$. Mixing these *costs 2 marks instantly*.
> 3. **Forgetting overall efficiency is the product** — Many students write $\eta_o = (\eta_v + \eta_m)/2$. **Never!** It's a *product*: $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.
> 4. **rpm vs rev/s** — Forgetting the factor 60 here gives answers off by 60× and *invites zero in valuation*.

---

### ❓ Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice Q2]

**Q. B (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a hydraulic launch-assist system used in heavy hybrid vehicles. Clearly identify the pump mode and motor mode operations and state the role of the high-pressure accumulator.

**Q. B (b)** A hydraulic pump delivers $Q = 90 \text{ L/min}$ at $\Delta P = 300 \text{ bar}$. The drive motor supplying the pump is an electric traction motor operating with $\eta_{em} = 0.94$. The pump's overall efficiency is $\eta_o = 0.88$. Calculate:
- (i) Hydraulic power generated
- (ii) Electrical input power required by the traction motor
- (iii) Power lost as heat in the hydraulic fluid (in kW)
- (iv) If this heat is used to pre-warm a 50-L FCEV coolant loop from 20 °C to 60 °C, calculate the time required (Coolant $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, density $\rho = 1.05 \text{ kg/L}$).

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. B (a) — [7 Marks]

A hydraulic launch-assist (HLA) system replaces or augments the conventional drivetrain during the high-fuel-consumption *launch* phase (0–30 km/h).

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Engine / Brake Event] → [Pump] → [High-Pressure Accumulator] ↔ [Hydraulic Motor] → [Wheel Hub]
                                      ↑
                              [N₂ Pre-charge 180 bar]
```

**Working principle (4 marks):**

- **Pump mode (regenerative braking / deceleration):** The vehicle's kinetic energy drives the hydraulic pump (often coupled directly to the transmission). The pump pressurizes hydraulic fluid and forces it into a Nitrogen pre-charged accumulator. Energy that would otherwise be wasted as brake-heat is stored as **pressure energy**.
- **Motor mode (launch assist):** During acceleration from standstill, pressurized fluid is released from the accumulator into the hydraulic motor, which now drives the wheels through the transmission. The IC engine can remain off or at very low load, dramatically cutting urban fuel consumption.
- **Role of high-pressure accumulator:** Acts as the *energy buffer* — a hydraulic equivalent of a battery. It decouples the *transient braking power* from the *transient launch power demand*, allowing both to be time-shifted.

*(Marks: 3 for diagram with labels, 2 for pump-mode explanation, 2 for motor-mode explanation.)*

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. B (b) — [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $Q = 90 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{90 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\Delta P = 300 \text{ bar} = 3.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta_{em} = 0.94$ (electric motor)
- $\eta_o = 0.88$ (pump overall)

**(i) Hydraulic power generated:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 3.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 45\,000 \text{ W} = 45 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Electrical input power to traction motor:**

First, shaft power required at pump input:

$$
P_{shaft,pump} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_o} = \frac{45}{0.88} = 51.136 \text{ kW}
$$

Electrical input:

$$
P_{elec} = \frac{P_{shaft,pump}}{\eta_{em}} = \frac{51.136}{0.94} = 54.40 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Power lost as heat in hydraulic fluid:**

Hydraulic loss (within pump):

$$
P_{loss,pump} = P_{shaft,pump} - P_{hyd} = 51.136 - 45 = 6.136 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Note on interpretation:* The pump loss = $P_{shaft}(1 - \eta_o) = 51.136 \times 0.12 = 6.136$ kW ✓

**(iv) Time to pre-warm 50 L of coolant from 20 °C to 60 °C:**

Mass of coolant:

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = 1.05 \cdot 50 = 52.5 \text{ kg}
$$

Temperature rise:

$$
\Delta T = 60 - 20 = 40 \text{ K}
$$

Heat energy required:

$$
Q_{heat} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 52.5 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 40 = 7350 \text{ kJ}
$$

Time required (assuming 100% heat transfer):

$$
t = \frac{Q_{heat}}{P_{loss,pump}} = \frac{7350 \text{ kJ}}{6.136 \text{ kW}} = 1197.9 \text{ s} \approx 20 \text{ min}
$$

*(Marks: 1.5 for (i), 2 for (ii), 1 for (iii), 2.5 for (iv) with each step shown.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Examiner's Warning for Q.B:**
> 1. **Sign errors in efficiency chain** — Always cascade *up* the energy chain (electrical → shaft → hydraulic). Dividing by efficiency *increases* the upstream power required.
> 2. **Mixing kW and kJ** — Students frequently forget to convert power units when computing time. Power in kW = Energy in kJ divided by Time in seconds.
> 3. **Heat-loss = $P(1-\eta_o)$ vs $P\eta_o$ confusion** — Many students wrongly subtract $\eta_o \cdot P$ instead of $(1-\eta_o) \cdot P$. **Memorize:** Loss = Input − Output.
> 4. **Density for coolant** — Water has $\rho = 1.0$ kg/L; ethylene-glycol mixtures have higher densities (1.05–1.08). Use the *given* value.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Hydraulic Pumps/Motors**

- 🔹 **Pump** = Mechanical → Hydraulic energy. **Motor** = Hydraulic → Mechanical energy. *Same physical unit, opposite role.*
- 🔹 **Geometric displacement $V_D$** is the single most important parameter; it ties *geometry* to *performance*.
- 🔹 **The Four Master Equations** — Burn these into memory:
  - $Q = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$
  - $T = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m$ (motor output) or $\cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_m}$ (pump input)
  - $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$
  - $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$
- 🔹 **Pump convention vs. Motor convention for torque** — Don't mix them up.
- 🔹 **Axial-piston swash-plate** = gold standard for HEV accumulator systems (high pressure, high $\eta_o$, variable $V_D$).
- 🔹 **Why high pressure?** — Stored energy $\propto P_{max}$. Doubling pressure doubles stored energy at constant volume.
- 🔹 **Leakage is roughly constant in volume** → high shaft speed = high $\eta_v$. Hence HEV pumps run at high rpm.
- 🔹 **Heat losses are reusable** — In FCEVs, pump heat can pre-warm the fuel-cell coolant — a *cascaded recovery* strategy worth mentioning for bonus marks.
- 🔹 **Common board-exam trap:** Confusing $\eta_v$ with $\eta_m$. Volumetric = flow-based; Mechanical = torque/friction-based. *They are independent physical phenomena.*
- 🔹 **Always show unit conversions explicitly** — cm³/rev × 10⁻⁶ = m³/rev; bar × 10⁵ = Pa; L/min ÷ 60 × 10⁻³ = m³/s.
- 🔹 **Draw the block diagram early** in any 14-mark answer — it earns 2–3 marks independently of calculation correctness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic Hybrid Engine Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT601 - HYBRID AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic Hybrid Engine Systems**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pneumatic Hybrid Engine System (PHES)**, also referred to as a **Compressed Air Hybrid (CAH) System**, is a kinetic energy recovery and propulsion architecture that stores braking/waste energy in the form of **high-pressure compressed air** inside a reinforced storage tank, and subsequently reconverts that compressed air into useful mechanical work via a pneumatic motor/expander to assist the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) during traction phases.

The working fluid is ordinary atmospheric air (≈ 78% N₂, 21% O₂ by volume), and the system follows the **Boyle-Mariotte** and **Polytropic** gas laws during charging and discharging cycles. Operating pressures in modern PHES prototypes range from **20 bar to 300 bar**, depending on the vehicle class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> A Pneumatic Hybrid is fundamentally a *mechanical-flywheel analog built from gas compression* — the air tank is the "flywheel," the compressor is the "input clutch," and the expander is the "output clutch." It is most often paired with a conventional ICE in a *parallel-hybrid topology*, though fully pneumatic vehicles (e.g., MDI Air Car concept) also exist.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressurized Balloon Car"

Imagine a **giant, super-strong balloon** attached to a car's drivetrain.
- When you **brake** the car, instead of throwing kinetic energy away as heat (like in a normal car), a small pump **squeezes air** into the balloon. The balloon gets harder and harder to squeeze as it fills (rising pressure).
- When you **press the accelerator**, the balloon is allowed to push air back out through a small turbine, spinning the wheels for free — the air "gives back" most of what you put in.
- The balloon (compressed air tank) is the **energy reservoir** — it just holds potential energy in the form of pressurized gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why air, not battery?**
> Air is everywhere, non-toxic, non-flammable, and you can refill a tank in 2–3 minutes using an ordinary garage compressor. The penalty is **low energy density** (≈ 20 Wh/kg at 300 bar) compared to lithium-ion batteries (≈ 150–250 Wh/kg), which is why PHES is best suited for **short-range urban delivery vehicles, refuse trucks, and city buses** that brake and accelerate often.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Specific Gas Constant (Air) | R_air | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| Ratio of Specific Heats (Air) | γ (kappa) | **1.4** |
| Atmospheric Pressure | P_atm | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** |
| Standard Temperature | T_std | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| PHES Tank Operating Pressure | P_tank | **20 – 300 bar** |
| Polytropic Index (real compressor) | n | **1.25 – 1.35** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture (Parallel Pneumatic Hybrid)

A typical PHES comprises the following primary sub-systems:

1. **High-Pressure Air Storage Tank (HP Tank):** Typically a carbon-fiber-wrapped composite cylinder rated for 200–300 bar service pressure and ≈ 450 bar burst pressure.
2. **Air Compressor (Charging Unit):** A multi-stage reciprocating or scroll compressor driven by the engine/transmission during regenerative braking or engine-on phases.
3. **Pneumatic Expander / Air Motor (Discharging Unit):** A reciprocating piston, vane, or turbine type that converts compressed-air enthalpy into rotational mechanical work.
4. **Pressure Regulation & Valve Manifold:** Regulates HP tank → LP actuator pressure using pressure-reducing valves, check valves, and solenoid shut-offs.
5. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU):** Manages the *State of Charge (SoC)* of the air tank, decides split between ICE and pneumatic motor, and monitors safety thresholds.
6. **ICE (Base Powertrain):** Provides primary propulsion and is supplemented by the pneumatic expander.

## 2.2 Three Operating Modes — Step-by-Step Logic

### Mode 1: Regenerative Braking (Charging — Compressor Active)

- Driver releases accelerator; vehicle kinetic energy tries to drive the wheels.
- ECU disengages the ICE from the wheels (clutch open or through CVT) and engages the **compressor** to the drivetrain.
- The compressor pulls atmospheric air (P₁ ≈ 1 bar) and compresses it to tank pressure (P₂ ≈ 200 bar).
- Energy input to compressor: W_comp = (n / (n − 1)) × P₁V₁ × [(P₂/P₁)^((n−1)/n) − 1]
- Heat is generated as a by-product; in ideal isothermal compression it must be removed (intercoolers), but in real automotive systems it is mostly rejected to ambient.

### Mode 2: Traction Assist (Discharging — Expander Active)

- Driver demands torque; ECU opens the HP tank valve.
- High-pressure air flows through the regulator to the **pneumatic expander / air motor**.
- The expander produces torque on the drivetrain parallel to the ICE.
- The air exhausts to atmosphere at ≈ 1 bar after delivering mechanical work.
- Useful work extracted: W_exp = (n / (n − 1)) × P₂V₂ × [1 − (P₃/P₂)^((n−1)/n)], where P₃ = 1 bar (atmospheric exhaust).

### Mode 3: Idle / Engine-Off Coasting

- Vehicle is rolling; ICE is decoupled and shut down (Stop-Start function).
- Pneumatic system maintains accessory loads (power steering, brake assist, HVAC) using tank-stored air.
- This eliminates engine idle fuel consumption — a major source of urban-cycle inefficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** PHES is favoured in **urban delivery vehicles** (UPS, FedEx, DHL pilots) and **garbage trucks** because their duty cycles involve **30–50 stop-start events per hour** — exactly the operating envelope where regenerative pneumatic recovery yields the highest fuel-economy gains (typically **20–35 % improvement in city MPG**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are valid for **ideal gas** behavior (valid for air up to ≈ 50 bar; for higher pressures, real-gas compressibility factor Z must be applied).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | P [Pa], V [m³], m [kg], T [K] |
| 2 | $PV^{n} = C$ | Polytropic process relation | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_{1}V_{1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Compressor work (per cycle) | Joules [J] |
| 4 | $W_{exp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_{2}V_{2}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{3}}{P_{2}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\right]$ | Expander work (per cycle) | Joules [J] |
| 5 | $T_{2} = T_{1}\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Temperature after polytropic compression | Kelvin [K] |
| 6 | $\eta_{isothermal} = \dfrac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{actual}}$ | Compressor isothermal efficiency (60–75%) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{W_{adiabatic}}{W_{actual}}$ | Compressor adiabatic efficiency (70–85%) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{stored} = P_{tank} \cdot V_{tank} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_{tank}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$ | Useful energy stored in air tank | Joules [J] |
| 9 | $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P}{R_{air} \cdot T}$ | Air density at any P, T | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{P_{tank} \cdot V_{tank}}{R_{air} \cdot T_{tank}}$ | Mass of air in HP tank | kg |

> [!NOTE]
> When **n = 1**, equations 3 and 4 reduce to the **isothermal** case: W = P₁V₁ ln(P₂/P₁). When **n = γ (1.4)**, they give the **adiabatic/isentropic** case — this is the *theoretical minimum* work for an insulated compressor.

## 2.4 Why Polytropic Index 'n' Matters

The polytropic index `n` captures the *real* thermodynamic path between true isothermal (n = 1, infinite cooling) and true adiabatic (n = γ, no cooling):
- **n = 1** → isothermal (best efficiency, requires massive cooling)
- **n ≈ 1.25–1.30** → actual multi-stage reciprocating compressor with intercooling
- **n = γ = 1.4** → adiabatic (worst work input, no cooling)

KTU exam tip: if the problem says "insulated cylinder" or "no heat transfer" → use **n = 1.4**. If it says "perfectly cooled" → use **n = 1**. Otherwise assume **n = 1.3** unless specified.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done During Polytropic Compression of Air

**Statement:** A reciprocating compressor draws atmospheric air at P₁ = 1 bar and T₁ = 300 K, and compresses it polytropically (n = 1.3) to P₂ = 9 bar. Calculate the work input per kg of air and the discharge temperature.

**Given:**
- P₁ = 1 bar = 1 × 10⁵ Pa
- T₁ = 300 K
- P₂ = 9 bar = 9 × 10⁵ Pa
- n = 1.3
- R_air = 287 J/(kg·K)

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**
For a polytropic process, the work done per unit mass of gas is:

$$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_{1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 2: Compute the pressure ratio.**

$$\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \dfrac{9}{1} = 9$$

**Step 3: Compute the exponent term (n − 1)/n.**

$$\dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{1.3 - 1}{1.3} = \dfrac{0.3}{1.3} = 0.23077$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the pressure ratio raised to the exponent.**

$$9^{0.23077} = e^{0.23077 \cdot \ln 9} = e^{0.23077 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.5071} = 1.6606$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the work equation.**

$$W = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 287 \times 300 \times \left[1.6606 - 1\right]$$

**Step 6: Simplify the coefficient.**

$$\dfrac{1.3}{0.3} = 4.3333$$

**Step 7: Compute stepwise.**

$$W = 4.3333 \times 287 \times 300 \times 0.6606$$

$$W = 4.3333 \times 86100 \times 0.6606$$

$$W = 372966.6 \times 0.6606$$

$$W \approx 246{,}402 \ \text{J/kg} \approx 246.4 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8: Compute the discharge temperature.**

$$T_{2} = T_{1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times 1.6606 = 498.18 \ \text{K}$$

**Step 9: Convert to °C for sanity check.**

$$T_{2} \left(^{\circ}\text{C}\right) = 498.18 - 273.15 = 225.03 \ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** A discharge temperature of 225 °C is too hot for practical single-stage compression and explains why real PHES compressors use **multi-stage compression with intercooling** — each stage limits the temperature rise to ≈ 60–80 °C and dramatically reduces the *specific work input*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Compressed Air Tank

**Statement:** A PHES tank has volume V = 100 L, charged to P = 300 bar at T = 300 K. Atmospheric pressure is 1 bar. Compute the *useful* mechanical energy that can theoretically be extracted if the air is expanded isothermally back to atmospheric pressure.

**Given:**
- V_tank = 100 L = 0.1 m³
- P_tank = 300 bar = 300 × 10⁵ Pa
- P_atm = 1 bar = 1 × 10⁵ Pa
- T = 300 K (isothermal)
- R = 287 J/(kg·K)

**Step 1: Isothermal expansion work formula.**

For an isothermal process (n = 1), the boundary work is:

$$W_{iso} = P_{1} V_{1} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}\right) = m R T \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{tank}}{P_{atm}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the mass of air in the tank.**

$$m = \dfrac{P_{tank} \cdot V_{tank}}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{300 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1}{287 \times 300}$$

$$m = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{6}}{86100} = 34.84 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Compute the natural log term.**

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{300}{1}\right) = \ln(300) = 5.7038$$

**Step 4: Compute the work output.**

$$W_{iso} = 34.84 \times 287 \times 300 \times 5.7038$$

$$W_{iso} = 34.84 \times 86100 \times 5.7038$$

$$W_{iso} = 2{,}999{,}724 \times 5.7038$$

$$W_{iso} \approx 17.11 \ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 5: Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ).**

$$E = \dfrac{17.11}{3.6} = 4.75 \ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 6: Compute gravimetric energy density.**

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{4.75 \ \text{kWh}}{34.84 \ \text{kg}} = 0.136 \ \text{kWh/kg} = 136 \ \text{Wh/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice this is the *theoretical maximum* under ideal isothermal conditions. In practice, due to adiabatic losses, friction, and tank-empty cut-off pressure (usually 30–50 bar), the **delivered energy is only 30–40 % of this value**, giving an effective energy density of about **40–55 Wh/kg** — competitive with NiMH but far below Li-ion (150–250 Wh/kg).

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Polytropic vs Isothermal vs Adiabatic

| Parameter | Isothermal (n=1) | Polytropic (n=1.3) | Adiabatic (n=γ=1.4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Work Input (per kg) | **Lowest** (≈ 175 kJ/kg for 9:1) | Intermediate (≈ 246 kJ/kg) | **Highest** (≈ 263 kJ/kg) |
| Discharge Temperature | T₂ = T₁ = 300 K | T₂ ≈ 498 K | T₂ ≈ 562 K |
| Cooling Requirement | Massive (intercoolers needed) | Moderate (multi-stage) | None assumed |
| Realistic for PHES? | Ideal but impractical | **Most realistic** | Theoretical limit |
| Compressor Efficiency | 60–75 % | 70–80 % | 80–90 % |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Atmospheric Air P at 1 bar] -->|Induction| nodeB[Compressor Unit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The bidirectional dotted lines from the HP tank indicate the tank acts as a *bidirectional energy buffer* — it *receives* compressed air from the compressor (charging) and *supplies* it to the expander (discharging). The ECU continuously monitors tank pressure to determine the **State of Charge (SoC)** of the pneumatic system, analogous to a battery's SoC in a conventional hybrid.

## 4.4 Table — Component Roles in the PHES Architecture

| Component | Primary Function | Energy Form Handled |
|---|---|---|
| Air Intake Filter | Removes particulates from atmospheric air | Mechanical → Cleaned Air |
| Multi-Stage Compressor | Converts kinetic energy to pneumatic energy | Mechanical → Pneumatic (↑P) |
| Intercooler | Removes heat of compression | Thermal → Ambient |
| HP Storage Tank | Stores potential energy in compressed air | Pneumatic (high-P) |
| Pressure Regulator | Reduces HP to usable LP for expander | Pneumatic (P control) |
| Pneumatic Expander | Converts pneumatic energy to mechanical torque | Pneumatic → Mechanical |
| Safety Relief Valve | Prevents over-pressure rupture | Pneumatic → Atmospheric |
| ECU & SoC Logic | Manages mode transitions and safety | Electrical → Control Signals |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]**
**Define a Pneumatic Hybrid Engine System. List any two advantages it has over a battery-electric hybrid.**

**Model Answer:**
A Pneumatic Hybrid Engine System (PHES) is a hybrid powertrain that stores energy in the form of high-pressure compressed air in a storage tank, recovering kinetic energy during braking via a compressor and reusing it during acceleration via a pneumatic expander to assist the ICE.

**Advantages over battery-electric hybrid:**
1. **Faster refueling** — A 200 bar tank can be refilled in 2–4 minutes using a garage compressor, compared to 30–60 minutes for Li-ion battery charging.
2. **No thermal runaway risk** — Air is non-flammable and non-toxic, eliminating the fire/explosion hazards of Li-ion packs.
3. **Lower capital cost** — Compressed-air tanks and air motors are cheaper per kWh-stored than battery packs.
4. **Long cycle life** — Air tanks endure > 10,000 charge/discharge cycles without degradation, vs ≈ 1000–2000 for Li-ion.

*(Any two well-explained points = full 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is the role of the polytropic index 'n' in pneumatic hybrid analysis? What value of 'n' represents a perfectly cooled compressor?**

**Model Answer:**
The polytropic index `n` defines the thermodynamic path between isothermal (n = 1) and adiabatic (n = γ = 1.4) compression. It appears in the relation **PV^n = constant** and directly determines the **specific work input** and **discharge temperature** of the compressor.

A higher `n` indicates less heat loss to the surroundings during compression (more adiabatic), while a lower `n` indicates more effective cooling (closer to isothermal).

A **perfectly cooled compressor** corresponds to **n = 1 (isothermal compression)**, where all the heat of compression is instantaneously removed, yielding the theoretical minimum work input: **W = P₁V₁ ln(P₂/P₁)**.
