## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** *List the six major types of fuel cells and state the operating temperature range and electrolyte used in each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Electrolyte | Temperature | Charge Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| AFC — Alkaline FC | KOH (30–45 % aqueous) | 50–200 °C | OH⁻ |
| PEMFC — Proton Exchange Membrane | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 50–100 °C | H⁺ |
| DMFC — Direct Methanol FC | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 50–120 °C | H⁺ |
| PAFC — Phosphoric Acid FC | Liquid H₃PO₄ in SiC matrix | 150–200 °C | H⁺ |
| MCFC — Molten Carbonate FC | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ | 600–700 °C | CO₃²⁻ |
| SOFC — Solid Oxide FC | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia) | 800–1000 °C | O²⁻ |

*(Marks: 1 — listing; 1 — electrolyte identification; 1 — temperature correlation.)*

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *Define Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of hydrogen. Why is LHV preferred for vehicle fuel-cell efficiency calculations?*

**Model Answer:**

- **HHV of H₂** = 141.9 MJ kg⁻¹ — assumes the combustion water is condensed to liquid and its latent heat recovered.
- **LHV of H₂** = 119.9 MJ kg⁻¹ — assumes water exits as vapour; the latent heat is *not* recovered.

LHV is preferred in vehicle FC efficiency calculations because the exhaust water leaves the stack as vapour above 100 °C and the latent heat cannot realistically be captured within the vehicle's thermal envelope. Reporting efficiency on HHV basis would inflate the apparent performance.

*(Marks: 1 — definitions; 1 — numerical values; 1 — justification for LHV.)*

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Question A]**

### Question A — (a) 7 Marks

**Derive the Nernst equation for an H₂/O₂ fuel cell operating at non-standard partial pressures and partial humidification. Compute the cell EMF at $T = 353$ K if $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm, and water exits as vapour at its saturation pressure $P_{H_2O}^{sat}(353\,K) = 0.467$ atm. Take $E°(353\,K) = 1.185$ V.**

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst expression for the H₂/O₂ cell:**

$$ E(T) \;=\; E^{\circ}(T) \;+\; \dfrac{R T}{n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2 O}}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Insert values.** $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹:

$$ \dfrac{R T}{n F} \;=\; \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} $$

$$ =\; \dfrac{2934.8}{192970} \;=\; 0.01521\;\text{V} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the argument of the logarithm:**

$$ \dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2 O}} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \times 1^{0.5}}{0.467} $$

$$ =\; 6.424 $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the natural logarithm:**

$$ \ln(6.424) \;=\; 1.860 $$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms:**

$$ E \;=\; 1.185 \;+\; 0.01521 \times 1.860 $$

$$ =\; 1.185 \;+\; 0.02829 $$

$$ =\; 1.2133\;\text{V} $$

**Result:** $E \approx 1.213$ V at the elevated operating temperature and partial pressures.

*(Marks: 2 — Nernst write-up; 2 — thermal voltage; 1 — log evaluation; 1 — final number; 1 — unit & physical interpretation.)*

---

### Question A — (b) 7 Marks

**With the aid of a polarisation curve, explain activation, ohmic, and concentration polarisation losses in a PEM fuel cell. For a stack of 200 cells, given per-cell $E = 1.21$ V, activation drop = 0.05 V, ohmic drop = 0.08 V, concentration drop = 0.04 V, and stack current = 250 A, calculate the stack power output and the HHV-based electrical efficiency.**

**Step 1 — Compute the actual cell voltage** by subtracting polarisation losses from the reversible EMF:

$$ V_{\text{cell}} \;=\; E \;-\; \eta_{\text{act}} \;-\; \eta_{\text{ohm}} \;-\; \eta_{\text{conc}} $$

$$ V_{\text{cell}} \;=\; 1.21 \;-\; 0.05 \;-\; 0.08 \;-\; 0.04 \;=\; 1.04\;\text{V} $$

**Step 2 — Stack voltage:**

$$ V_{\text{stack}} \;=\; 200 \times 1.04 \;=\; 208\;\text{V} $$

**Step 3 — Stack power:**

$$ P_{\text{stack}} \;=\; V_{\text{stack}} \times I \;=\; 208 \times 250 \;=\; 52{,}000\;\text{W} \;=\; 52\;\text{kW} $$

**Step 4 — Compute HHV-based efficiency.** The cell voltage ratio (voltage efficiency) multiplied by the HHV thermodynamic efficiency:

$$ \eta_{\text{HHV,thermo}} \;=\; 1.229 / 1.481 \;=\; 0.830 $$

$$ \eta_{\text{voltage}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.04}{1.229} \;=\; 0.846 $$

$$ \eta_{\text{overall}} \;=\; 0.830 \times 0.846 \;=\; 0.702 \;=\; 70.2\,\% $$

*(Marks: 2 — polarisation losses with diagram; 2 — stack power; 2 — efficiency computation; 1 — physical interpretation.)*

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Question B — Alternative Choice]**

### Question B — (a) 7 Marks

**Explain the working principle of a Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC). Write the half-cell and overall reactions. Compare DMFC vs PEMFC in terms of energy density, efficiency, operating temperature, and application.**

**Step 1 — Half-cell reactions (acidic membrane, methanol fed directly to anode):**

$$ \text{Anode:}\;\; CH_3OH \;+\; H_2 O \;\rightarrow\; CO_2 \;+\; 6H^{+} \;+\; 6e^{-} $$

$$ \text{Cathode:}\;\; \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \;+\; 6H^{+} \;+\; 6e^{-} \;\rightarrow\; 3H_2 O $$

$$ \text{Net:}\;\; CH_3OH \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \;\rightarrow\; CO_2 \;+\; 2H_2 O $$

**Step 2 — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | DMFC | PEMFC |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel state | Liquid methanol | Gaseous H₂ |
| Anode reaction | CH₃OH + H₂O → CO₂ + 6H⁺ + 6e⁻ | H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ |
| Charge carrier | H⁺ | H⁺ |
| Operating T | 50–120 °C | 50–100 °C |
| Theoretical EMF | 1.21 V | 1.23 V |
| Power density | 50–200 W/kg stack | 500–1000 W/kg stack |
| Energy density (fuel) | 6.1 kWh kg⁻¹ methanol | 33.3 kWh kg⁻¹ H₂ (LHV) |
| Efficiency | 20–40 % | 50–60 % |
| Methanol crossover | Major loss pathway | Not applicable |
| Application | Portable electronics, laptop chargers | Automotive traction, backup power |

**Step 3 — Key advantage of DMFC:** Liquid fuel with high specific energy; no reformer required.

**Step 4 — Key disadvantage:** Methanol crossover through Nafion membrane lowers coulombic efficiency by 10–20 %.

*(Marks: 2 — reactions; 2 — comparison table; 2 — application & engineering trade-off; 1 — conclusion.)*

---

### Question B — (b) 7 Marks

**Describe steam methane reforming (SMR) for hydrogen production. With reference to a typical industrial reformer, list the sequential unit operations, the reactions in each stage, and the typical purity of H₂ achieved. Why is SMR the dominant technology for fuel-cell-grade hydrogen today?**

**Step 1 — Overall reforming reaction (endothermic):**

$$ CH_4 \;+\; H_2 O \;\rightarrow\; CO \;+\; 3H_2 \quad \Delta H \;=\; +206\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 2 — Sequential Unit Operations:**

1. **Desulfurisation** — Natural gas is passed through a ZnO bed to remove H₂S (which poisons downstream Ni catalyst).
   $$ H_2S \;+\; ZnO \;\rightarrow\; ZnS \;+\; H_2 O $$
2. **Steam mixing** — Steam-to-carbon molar ratio = 3:1 to suppress coke formation.
3. **Primary reformer** — Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 800–850 °C; the SMR reaction above occurs.
4. **High-temperature shift (HTS)** — Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ catalyst at 350 °C:
   $$ CO \;+\; H_2 O \;\rightarrow\; CO_2 \;+\; H_2 \quad \Delta H \;=\; -41\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$
5. **Low-temperature shift (LTS)** — Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 200 °C; same reaction, pushes equilibrium further right.
6. **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** — Multi-bed adsorption removes CO₂, CH₄, CO; H₂ purity reaches 99.99 % (40 ppm total impurities).

**Step 3 — Why SMR dominates:**

- Mature technology with >50 years of industrial optimisation.
- Lowest cost of H₂ in mid-to-large scale (~1.5–3 USD per kg currently).
- Compatible with existing natural gas pipeline infrastructure.
- Allows integration with carbon capture (CCS) for blue hydrogen.

**Step 4 — Disadvantages for fuel cells:**

- CO must be reduced to <10 ppm for PEMFC anodes to avoid Pt catalyst poisoning — extra cleanup stages required.
- Fossil-fuel dependent; considered "grey hydrogen" unless paired with CCS.

*(Marks: 2 — overall reaction; 2 — sequential stages; 2 — PSA purity; 1 — dominance justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong sign in Nernst equation** — remembering whether product or reactant goes in the numerator. Always re-derive from $\Delta G = -nFE$ in the exam.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV cell potentials** — 1.481 V is for HHV/liquid water, 1.253 V is for LHV/vapour. Mixing them gives the wrong efficiency.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{P_{O_2}}$ term** — the 0.5 exponent on $P_{O_2}$ is often missed, producing an answer off by ~5 %.
> 4. **Ignoring stack-level vs cell-level units** — KTU expects both cell voltage and stack voltage stated separately.
> 5. **PSA H₂ purity** — frequently mistaken as 99 %; correct value for fuel-cell grade is **≥ 99.99 %**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fuel cell** = electrochemical converter, *not* a battery; electrodes are invariant.
- **Core cell reaction:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$; reversible EMF $E° = 1.229$ V at 25 °C (HHV basis gives 1.481 V).
- **Nernst equation** relates EMF to operating partial pressures: $E = E° + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{0.5}/P_{H_2O})$.
- **Polarisation losses** — activation (Tafel), ohmic (linear), concentration (mass-transport). Real cell voltage: $V = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$.
- **Six types** — AFC, PEMFC, DMFC (low-T, H⁺ or OH⁻); PAFC (intermediate-T, H⁺); MCFC, SOFC (high-T, CO₃²⁻ or O²⁻).
- **PEMFC is the automotive choice** because of low-T quick-start, high power density, and solid electrolyte.
- **SOFC/MCFC** are stationary and CHP-favoured because of high waste-heat quality.
- **AFC** is the legacy technology (Apollo program); cheap but CO₂ intolerant.
- **Hydrogen storage** options: 350/700 bar compressed gas, cryogenic liquid (−253 °C), metal hydrides, complex hydrides, MOF adsorption. 700 bar compressed gas is the current automotive standard.
- **Reformers** — SMR is the dominant industrial route; ATR (autothermal) is faster-starting; POX is used in heavy-duty truck APUs.
- **Key constants** for the exam: $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹; $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹; $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 119.9$ MJ kg⁻¹; $\text{HHV}_{H_2} = 141.9$ MJ kg⁻¹.
- **PSA H₂ purity** = 99.99 %; CO tolerance for PEMFC anode < 10 ppm.
- **SMR reaction:** $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ (endothermic, 800–850 °C, Ni catalyst).
- **HTS/LTS** convert residual CO to H₂ via water-gas shift; required because CO poisons Pt catalysts.
- **DMFC advantage** — liquid fuel, high specific energy; **disadvantage** — methanol crossover through Nafion.
- **Reversible efficiency formula:** $\eta_{ideal} = \Delta G / \Delta H = 1 - T\Delta S / \Delta H$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Fuel Cell Vehicles — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV)**, also designated as a **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)**, is an advanced propulsion platform classified under **Zero-Emission Vehicles (ZEVs)** that generates onboard traction electrical energy through an **electrochemical reaction between a continuously supplied fuel (typically hydrogen, H₂) and an oxidant (typically oxygen, O₂ from ambient air)** within a controlled electrochemical reactor termed a **Fuel Cell Stack**.

Unlike conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles — which rely on thermochemical (heat-based) conversion following the Carnot cycle — or Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) — which store electrical energy in rechargeable electrochemical cells — an FCV functions as an **energy converter** in which the chemical energy bound within the hydrogen molecule is converted *directly* into electrical energy without any intermediate mechanical, thermal, or rotating stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorise Verbatim):**
> *“A Fuel Cell Vehicle is an electric vehicle that uses a fuel cell, powered mostly by hydrogen and oxygen, to produce electricity for traction. The only by-products are water, heat, and electricity — no greenhouse gases, no particulates, no NOx, no CO₂ at the tailpipe.”*

The governing electrochemical half-reactions occurring within a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** — the dominant topology for automotive deployment — are formally expressed as:

**Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):**
$$\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-}$$

**Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):**
$$\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O}$$

**Net Cell Reaction:**
$$\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — “The Hydrogen Breath of a Battery”**
>
> Imagine a **conventional AA battery** that you never have to *recharge*. Instead of plugging it into a wall socket, you simply **feed it hydrogen gas through a hose** and **let it breathe in oxygen from the surrounding air**. As long as you keep feeding it hydrogen, it keeps producing electricity continuously. The waste product? **Pure drinkable water (H₂O)** that drips out the exhaust pipe.
>
> That is precisely how an FCV operates:
> - **Fuel hose (H₂ tank)** → replaces the wall-socket charger of a BEV.
> - **Fuel cell stack** → replaces both the battery *and* the engine of a conventional car.
> - **Exhaust** → emits only water vapour, not CO₂.
>
> In essence: **An FCV is an electric car that generates its own electricity on board — quietly, efficiently, and cleanly — as long as hydrogen is supplied.**

---

## 1.3 Why Hydrogen? — The Strategic Fuel Choice

Hydrogen is selected as the primary automotive fuel for four engineering rationales:

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Gravimetric Energy Density** | **≈ 120 MJ/kg** (~33.3 kWh/kg) | Highest of any practical fuel — ~3× gasoline |
| Volumetric Energy Density (liquid H₂) | 8.5 MJ/L | Low — necessitates high-pressure (350–700 bar) tanks |
| Molecular Weight | 2.016 g/mol | Lightest molecule → fastest kinetics |
| Oxidation Product | H₂O | Zero harmful emissions |
| Flammability Limit in Air | 4–75 % (vol) | Wide range → safety engineering challenge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants (Bolded for Exam Recall):**
> - Universal Gas Constant, **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Faraday's Constant, **F = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Standard Cell Voltage (H₂/O₂), **E⁰ = 1.229 V**
> - Higher Heating Value of H₂, **HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol**
> - Lower Heating Value of H₂, **LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Automotive Fuel Cells

KTU Module 4 emphasises the following classification. Students are expected to remember the operating temperature and electrolyte:

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp. | Automotive Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane / Polymer Electrolyte Membrane) | Solid polymer membrane (Nafion™) | **60–100 °C** | ✅ **Preferred for FCVs** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) |
| AFC (Alkaline Fuel Cell) | KOH solution | 60–90 °C | ❌ Used in space applications (Apollo) |
| PAFC (Phosphoric Acid) | Liquid H₃PO₄ | 150–220 °C | ❌ Stationary only |
| MCFC (Molten Carbonate) | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | 600–700 °C | ❌ Stationary power plants |
| SOFC (Solid Oxide) | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia) | 800–1000 °C | ❌ Heavy-duty / auxiliary power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PEMFC dominates automotive applications?**
> 1. Low operating temperature → fast cold-start capability (essential for consumer vehicles).
> 2. Solid electrolyte → no leakage, no corrosion.
> 3. High power density → compact packaging under vehicle floor.
> 4. Excellent dynamic response → matches variable driving load profiles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4: Fuel Cell Vehicles — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Operational Stages of an FCV Powertrain

An FCV powertrain converts hydrogen's chemical bond energy into wheel rotation through a strictly defined sequence:

### Stage 1 — Hydrogen Storage & Delivery
- Compressed gaseous hydrogen (**CGH₂**) is stored in **Type IV composite tanks** at **350 bar (standard) or 700 bar (high-pressure)**.
- A **Pressure Regulator** reduces tank pressure (~700 bar) to stack inlet pressure (~2–5 bar).
- A **Hydrogen Recirculation System** returns unreacted H₂ from anode exit back to the anode inlet — improving fuel utilisation from ~85 % to **>99 %**.

### Stage 2 — Electrochemical Conversion (Fuel Cell Stack)
- H₂ molecules split at the **anode catalyst (Pt nanoparticles, 0.1–0.5 mg/cm² loading)** into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻).
- Protons migrate through the **proton exchange membrane** (Nafion™-117 thickness 50–175 μm).
- Electrons are forced through the **external circuit** — generating usable DC electrical current.
- At the cathode, O₂ + H⁺ + e⁻ combine → H₂O (product water, drained as vapour).

### Stage 3 — Power Electronics Conditioning
- Fuel cell output is **unregulated DC** with voltage that sags non-linearly with load current.
- A **DC-DC Boost Converter** (typically interleaved or full-bridge topology) steps up and regulates voltage to a stable high-voltage DC bus (**typically 250–450 V**).
- An **Inverter (DC → 3-phase AC)** drives the traction motor.

### Stage 4 — Electric Traction
- A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** or **Induction Motor (IM)** propels the vehicle.
- Peak power ratings typically **113–134 kW (150–180 hp)** for mid-size FCVs.
- Single-speed transmission (no multi-gear complexity).

### Stage 5 — Energy Buffering (Hybridisation)
- Pure fuel cells respond slowly to transient loads (hydrogen flow dynamics, thermal inertia).
- Therefore, every modern FCV includes a small **Lithium-Ion Battery (1–2 kWh)** or **Supercapacitor Bank** for:
  - **Regenerative braking energy capture**.
  - **Load levelling** — battery handles transient peaks, fuel cell handles steady-state.
- This makes the FCV technically a **Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle (FCHEV)** — often the question setter's trap.

---

## 2.2 The Reversibility Principle — Why a Fuel Cell is the "Reverse" of Electrolysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **William Grove's 1839 Discovery:**
> An electrochemical cell operated **in reverse** of an electrolyser is a fuel cell. If electrolysis uses electricity to split water → H₂ + O₂, then supplying H₂ + O₂ recombines them → electricity + water.

Mathematically, the equilibrium is governed by the **change in Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG)**:

$$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E_{\text{cell}}$$

where:
- **n** = number of electrons transferred per mole of fuel (= **2** for H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻)
- **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- **E_cell** = reversible cell voltage (V)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** This table consolidates every equation KTU has tested in the last 5 FCV examinations. Memorise the variable symbols and standard-condition values.

| # | Formula | Equation | Variables / Standard Values | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | E⁰ = **1.229 V**, n = 2 | Theoretical open-circuit voltage under non-standard conditions |
| 2 | Lower Heating Value Efficiency | $\eta_{LHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{LHV}} = \dfrac{237.2}{241.8} = \mathbf{0.981}$ | ΔG = 237.2 kJ/mol, LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol | Maximum theoretical efficiency of H₂/O₂ cell |
| 3 | Higher Heating Value Efficiency | $\eta_{HHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{HHV}} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = \mathbf{0.830}$ | HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol | Used for steam-producing fuel cells |
| 4 | Polarisation Curve (Cell Voltage) | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | η_act, η_ohmic, η_conc = activation, ohmic, concentration overpotentials | Predicts real-world cell voltage under load |
| 5 | Fuel Cell Power Output | $P_{\text{stack}} = N_{\text{cells}} \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I$ | N_cells = 200–400 typically | Stack sizing for vehicle power demand |
| 6 | Faraday's Law (H₂ Consumption) | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot I}{n \cdot F}$ | M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol | Calculates hydrogen mass flow rate for given current |
| 7 | Energy Density (Hydrogen Tank) | $E_{\text{tank}} = m_{H_2} \cdot LHV$ | LHV per kg = 120 MJ | Vehicle range estimation |
| 8 | Carnot Reference Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$ | T in Kelvin | Why fuel cells exceed ICE efficiency |
| 9 | Vehicle Range | $R = \dfrac{E_{\text{tank}} \cdot \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}{P_{\text{aux}}}$ | η_drivetrain ≈ 0.55–0.65 | Mirai: ~650 km range |
| 10 | Cell Voltage vs Current Density | $V = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{\alpha F}\ln(i) - iR_{\text{internal}}$ | α = transfer coefficient (≈0.5 for Pt) | Tafel slope derivation for activation loss |

---

## 2.4 Why Fuel Cells Beat Internal Combustion Engines — The Efficiency Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:** *“Why is a fuel cell more efficient than an IC engine even though both consume fuel?”*

| Parameter | ICE (Otto Cycle) | PEMFC |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion Pathway | Chemical → Thermal → Mechanical | Chemical → Electrical (direct) |
| Theoretical Max Efficiency | Carnot limit ≈ 35–40 % | **η_LHV ≈ 98.1 %** (thermodynamic) |
| Real-World Vehicle Efficiency | 25–35 % | **50–60 %** (at wheel) |
| Tailpipe Emissions | CO₂, NOx, CO, HC, PM | **H₂O only** |
| Operating Temperature | 600–1200 °C | 60–100 °C |
| Moving Parts | ~2000+ | 0 (in stack) |

**Engineering Insight:** The fuel cell bypasses the thermal intermediate (heat → mechanical work), thus evading the Carnot ceiling entirely. This is the *single most important conceptual advantage* — board examiners award marks specifically for articulating this point.

---

## 2.5 Real-World FCV Engineering Examples (Industry Reference)

| Manufacturer | Model | Fuel Cell Stack Power | H₂ Tank Pressure | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Toyota | **Mirai (2nd Gen, 2020)** | 128 kW × 174 kW system | 700 bar (3 tanks, 5.6 kg) | **650 km** |
| Hyundai | **Nexo (2018)** | 95 kW × 135 kW system | 700 bar (3 tanks, 6.33 kg) | **666 km** |
| Honda | Clarity Fuel Cell | 103 kW | 700 bar | 590 km |
| Mercedes-Benz | GLC F-CELL | 155 kW (plug-in hybrid) | 700 bar | 478 km |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Challenges and Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board-Specific Trap:** Students often write *“Fuel cells are emission-free, hence perfect”* — losing 2 marks. You must acknowledge the **upstream challenge**: hydrogen is *currently* produced mostly via **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)**, which generates CO₂. Only *green hydrogen* (electrolysis using renewable electricity) yields true zero-emission status. Mention this nuance to score full marks.

The principal engineering hurdles are:
1. **Hydrogen Production & Infrastructure** — global H₂ refuelling stations numbered only ~1,000 in 2024 vs. millions of petrol pumps.
2. **Onboard Storage Density** — 700 bar tanks occupy significant vehicle volume.
3. **Catalyst Cost** — platinum loading (~0.125 mg/cm² in 2024, down from 0.4 mg/cm² in 2008) remains expensive.
4. **Water Management** — membrane flooding (excess water) and drying (insufficient) both degrade performance.
5. **Cold-Start Performance** — water inside membrane can freeze below 0 °C, blocking proton conduction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4: Fuel Cell Vehicles — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermodynamic Reversible Cell Voltage (E⁰)

**Objective:** Derive the *theoretical maximum* voltage of a single H₂/O₂ fuel cell operating at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm).

### Given Data
- Reaction: H₂(g) + ½ O₂(g) → H₂O(l)
- Standard Gibbs Free Energy change, **ΔG⁰ = −237.2 kJ/mol** (negative = spontaneous)
- Number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂, **n = 2**

### Step 1 — Establish the First-Law Energy Partition
The total energy released from hydrogen oxidation is captured by the **enthalpy change (ΔH)**. This enthalpy is split into two useful energy forms:

$$\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$$

where:
- **ΔG** → maximum *useful electrical work* obtainable.
- **TΔS** → unavoidable *heat* released to surroundings.

### Step 2 — Compute ΔS for the Cell Reaction
Using standard entropy values:
- S⁰(H₂O, l) = 69.9 J/(mol·K)
- S⁰(H₂, g) = 130.7 J/(mol·K)
- S⁰(O₂, g) = 205.1 J/(mol·K)

Calculate:

$$\Delta S^{0} = S^{0}_{H_2O} - S^{0}_{H_2} - \tfrac{1}{2}S^{0}_{O_2}$$

$$\Delta S^{0} = 69.9 - 130.7 - \tfrac{1}{2}(205.1)$$

$$\Delta S^{0} = 69.9 - 130.7 - 102.55$$

$$\Delta S^{0} = -163.35 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

### Step 3 — Verify ΔG Using the Identity
Substitute into ΔH = ΔG + TΔS at T = 298 K:

$$\Delta H_{LHV}^{0} = -241.8 \; \text{kJ/mol} \; \text{(water as vapour)}$$

$$-241{,}800 \; \text{J/mol} = \Delta G^{0} + (298)(-163.35)$$

$$-241{,}800 = \Delta G^{0} - 48{,}678.3$$

$$\Delta G^{0} = -241{,}800 + 48{,}678.3 = -193{,}121.7 \; \text{J/mol} \approx -193.1 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

This is for H₂O *vapour* product (LHV). For liquid product (HHV):

$$\Delta G^{0}_{HHV} = -237.2 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

### Step 4 — Convert ΔG to Cell Voltage
The fundamental electrochemistry relation linking Gibbs energy to voltage:

$$\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$$

Solve for E⁰:

$$E^{0} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{nF}$$

$$E^{0} = -\dfrac{-237{,}200 \; \text{J/mol}}{(2)(96{,}485 \; \text{C/mol})}$$

$$E^{0} = \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^{0} = 1.229 \; \text{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise:** **1.229 V** is the *theoretical open-circuit voltage* of one H₂/O₂ cell at standard conditions. Real cells operate at **0.6–0.7 V** under load due to irreversible losses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Nernst Equation for Variable Operating Conditions

**Objective:** Generalise E⁰ to arbitrary temperature, pressure, and humidity.

### Step 1 — Start with the Chemical Reaction Quotient
For the net reaction H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O, the reaction quotient Q is:

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot (a_{O_2})^{1/2}} = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot \sqrt{P_{O_2}}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Generalised Gibbs Free Energy Expression

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln(Q)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute ΔG = −nFE and ΔG⁰ = −nFE⁰

$$-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln(Q)$$

$$nFE = nFE^{0} - RT \ln(Q)$$

$$E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Q Expression

$$\boxed{E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}\right)}$$

Equivalently (using negative sign inside log):

$$E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Temperature-Corrected Standard Voltage
KTU frequently tests the empirical form valid for PEMFC temperature range (60–90 °C):

$$E^{0}(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298) \; \text{[V]}$$

For T = 353 K (80 °C):

$$E^{0}(353) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \times 55 = 1.229 - 0.0468 = 1.182 \; \text{V}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Theoretical Efficiency from Thermodynamics

### Step 1 — Define Two Efficiencies
- **Thermodynamic (Ideal) Efficiency** — based on Gibbs free energy.
- **Carnot Efficiency** — based on heat engine cycle.

### Step 2 — Compute Thermodynamic Efficiency (HHV basis)

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{HHV}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{-237.2 \; \text{kJ/mol}}{-285.8 \; \text{kJ/mol}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{ideal, HHV}} = 0.830 \; \text{(83.0 \%)}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Thermodynamic Efficiency (LHV basis)

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{LHV}} = \dfrac{-237.2}{-241.8}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{ideal, LHV}} = 0.981 \; \text{(98.1 \%)}}$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Carnot Reference
Assume fuel cell operates at T_hot = 353 K (80 °C), T_cold = 298 K (25 °C):

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{298}{353} = 1 - 0.844 = 0.156 = 15.6 \; \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight:** A fuel cell's *thermodynamic* efficiency (83–98 %) **vastly exceeds** the Carnot limit of any practical heat engine — *because it does not convert heat to work at all*. This is the conceptual basis for KTU's repeated "compare FC vs ICE" questions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydrogen Mass Flow Rate from Faraday's Law

**Objective:** Determine H₂ consumption for a 100 kW FCV cruising at 50 km/h.

### Step 1 — Convert Power to Current (Assumed Average Cell Voltage)
Assume V_cell = 0.7 V, N_cells = 300, stack voltage V_stack = 210 V.

$$I = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{V_{\text{stack}}} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{210} = 476.2 \; \text{A}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot I}{n \cdot F}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{(2.016 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/mol})(476.2)}{(2)(96{,}485)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.9600}{192{,}970} = 4.975 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kg/s}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kg/hour

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 4.975 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 0.01791 \; \text{kg/h}$$

### Step 4 — Estimate Range
If tank holds 5 kg of H₂:

$$\text{Range} = \dfrac{5 \; \text{kg}}{0.01791 \; \text{kg/h}} = 279 \; \text{hours of cruising at 50 km/h} \times 50 \; \text{km/h} \approx \text{inconsistent — recalibrate}$$

**Correction (Practical Cruise):** At 50 km/h, FCV power demand is closer to ~15 kW:

$$I_{15kW} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{210} = 71.43 \; \text{A}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,15kW} = \dfrac{(2.016 \times 10^{-3})(71.43)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 7.46 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{kg/s} = 0.00269 \; \text{kg/h}$$

For 5 kg tank:
$$\text{Range} = \dfrac{5}{0.00269} \times 50 \approx 92{,}937 \; \text{km (unphysical — confirms assumption error)}$$

**Resolution:** Real FCVs consume ~1 kg H₂ per 100 km. This matches **Toyota Mirai's published 1.0 kg/100 km**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Voltage Losses (Polarisation Curve)

Real cell voltage V_cell as a function of current density i (A/cm²):

$$V_{\text{cell}}(i) = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \underbrace{\dfrac{RT}{\alpha F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)}_{\eta_{\text{activation}}} - \underbrace{i \cdot R_{\text{internal}}}_{\eta_{\text{ohmic}}} - \underbrace{\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)}_{\eta_{\text{concentration}}}$$

| Loss Mechanism | Physical Origin | Dominant At | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation (η_act) | Sluggish ORR kinetics at cathode | **Low current densities** | ~0.1–0.3 V |
| Ohmic (η_ohmic) | Ionic (membrane) + electronic resistance | **Mid-range currents** | ~0.05–0.15 V |
| Concentration (η_conc) | Mass transport limitation of reactants | **High current densities** | ~0.05–0.2 V |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem Templates

### Template Problem 1: Cell EMF at Non-Standard Conditions
**Given:** T = 353 K, P_H₂ = 3 atm, P_O₂ = 1 atm, P_H₂O = 0.5 atm.

**Solution:**

$$E = 1.182 + \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3 \cdot \sqrt{1}}{0.5}\right)$$

$$E = 1.182 + (0.01522) \ln(6)$$

$$E = 1.182 + (0.01522)(1.7918)$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.209 \; \text{V}}$$

### Template Problem 2: Stack Power Output
**Given:** 300-cell stack, V_cell = 0.65 V, I = 250 A.

$$P_{\text{stack}} = 300 \times 0.65 \times 250 = 48{,}750 \; \text{W} = 48.75 \; \text{kW}$$

### Template Problem 3: Vehicle Range
**Given:** 5 kg H₂ tank, LHV = 120 MJ/kg, drivetrain efficiency 55 %.

$$E_{\text{usable}} = 5 \times 120 \times 0.55 = 330 \; \text{MJ}$$

At 15 kW average demand:

$$t = \dfrac{330{,}000}{15{,}000} = 22 \; \text{hours cruising at 50 km/h} \Rightarrow \text{Range} = 1100 \; \text{km}$$

(Real range is lower due to auxiliary loads, transient peaks, accessory power → ~650 km in practice.)

# Module 4: Fuel Cell Vehicles — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Fuel Cell Vehicle. State any two advantages of FCVs over BEVs.**

**Model Answer:**

A Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV) is an electric vehicle that uses a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** to generate electrical energy onboard through the electrochemical reaction of hydrogen (from a high-pressure tank) and oxygen (from ambient air). The electrical energy drives an electric motor for propulsion, with **water and heat as the only by-products**.

**Two advantages over BEVs:**

1. **Refuelling Time:** An FCV refuels in **3–5 minutes** (comparable to petrol cars), whereas a BEV requires **30 minutes to 8 hours** for charging depending on charger rating.
2. **Driving Range:** FCVs achieve **500–700 km** per refuel, comparable to ICE vehicles, while BEVs typically achieve **200–500 km** per full charge. Additionally, FCVs do not suffer battery degradation over years, whereas BEV batteries lose 20–30 % capacity over 8–10 years.

*(Word count: ~110 words. Diagram NOT required for 3-mark questions.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the major components of a PEM fuel cell stack and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bipolar Plates** | Distribute reactant gases, conduct current between cells, remove product water |
| 2 | **Anode Catalyst Layer** (Pt/C) | Splits H₂ into protons and electrons (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction) |
| 3 | **Cathode Catalyst Layer** (Pt/C) | Combines O₂ + H⁺ + e⁻ to form water (Oxygen Reduction Reaction) |
| 4 | **Proton Exchange Membrane** (Nafion™) | Allows only H⁺ ions to pass from anode to cathode |
| 5 | **Gas Diffusion Layers (GDL)** | Distribute gases evenly and conduct electrons to/from catalyst |
| 6 | **Gaskets and Seals** | Prevent gas leakage and mixing of H₂ and O₂ |
| 7 | **End Plates and Tie Rods** | Provide uniform compression across the stack |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two alternative questions (Q-A or Q-B). Each question is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle (FCHEV). Describe the role of the energy storage system (battery/supercapacitor) in the hybrid powertrain.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
A Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle (FCHEV) is a vehicle in which the primary energy source is a **hydrogen fuel cell**, supplemented by a **secondary energy storage system (battery or supercapacitor)** to handle transient power demands. Pure fuel cell systems respond slowly to load changes due to hydrogen flow dynamics and thermal inertia. The energy storage system (ESS) acts as a **power buffer**, providing peak power during acceleration and capturing regenerative braking energy during deceleration.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
H₂ Tank → Pressure Regulator → Humidifier → Fuel Cell Stack
                                                      ↓
                                              DC-DC Boost Converter
                                                      ↓
                                              High-Voltage DC Bus ←→ Battery/Supercapacitor ESS
                                                      ↓
                                                  Inverter
                                                      ↓
                                                PMSM Motor
                                                      ↓
                                            Single-Speed Gearbox
                                                      ↓
                                                   Wheels
```

**Working Description (2 marks):**
- During **steady cruising**, the fuel cell supplies all propulsion power.
- During **acceleration**, the battery/supercapacitor delivers additional peak power in parallel with the fuel cell.
- During **braking**, the motor acts as a generator, charging the battery.
- A **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** orchestrates power split between the fuel cell and the ESS based on State of Charge (SOC), driver demand, and fuel cell efficiency map.

**Role of ESS (1 mark):**
The ESS improves overall vehicle efficiency by allowing the fuel cell to operate near its **maximum efficiency point** (typically 30–50 % of rated power) while letting the ESS handle transient peaks, thus avoiding low-efficiency operating regions of the fuel cell.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the Nernst equation for an H₂/O₂ fuel cell. Calculate the open-circuit voltage at T = 353 K and partial pressures P_H₂ = 3 atm, P_O₂ = 1 atm, P_H₂O = 0.5 atm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The Nernst equation relates the cell potential to standard conditions and reactant/product activities. For the reaction:

$$\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$$

The reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot (P_{O_2})^{1/2}}$$

Starting from the thermodynamic identity:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln(Q)$$

Substitute ΔG = −nFE and ΔG⁰ = −nFE⁰:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln(Q)$$

Solving for E:

$$\boxed{E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}\right)}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

At T = 353 K, the corrected standard potential (using the empirical form for PEMFC):

$$E^{0}(353) = 1.229 - 0.00085(353 - 298) = 1.229 - 0.04675 = 1.182 \; \text{V}$$

Substitute into Nernst:

$$E = 1.182 - \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.5}{3 \cdot \sqrt{1}}\right)$$

$$E = 1.182 - (0.01521) \ln(0.1667)$$

$$E = 1.182 - (0.01521)(-1.7918)$$

$$E = 1.182 + 0.02725$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.209 \; \text{V}}$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain with suitable equations the polarisation curve of a PEM fuel cell. Identify the three major voltage losses and their physical origins.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The polarisation curve (also called the **I-V characteristic**) of a fuel cell plots cell voltage against current density. It reveals three distinct regions of voltage loss from the ideal thermodynamic value E_Nernst.

**Equation (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{cell}}(i) = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}}(i) - \eta_{\text{ohmic}}(i) - \eta_{\text{conc}}(i)$$

where:
- **Activation Loss** (Butler-Volmer / Tafel equation):

$$\eta_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

- **Ohmic Loss** (Ohm's law):

$$\eta_{\text{ohmic}} = i \cdot R_{\text{internal}}$$

- **Concentration Loss** (mass-transport limit):

$$\eta_{\text{conc}} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

**Three Major Voltage Losses (3 marks):**

| Loss | Physical Origin | Magnitude at Rated Current | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Activation Polarisation** | Energy barrier for ORR at cathode; sluggish Pt kinetics | ~0.3–0.4 V | Higher Pt loading, novel catalysts (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni), elevated temperature |
| **Ohmic Polarisation** | Membrane ionic resistance + electronic contact resistance | ~0.1–0.2 V | Thinner membranes, higher-conductivity Nafion™ variants |
| **Concentration Polarisation** | Mass transport limit when H₂/O₂ supply cannot keep pace with current | ~0.1–0.3 V | Optimised flow field design, higher reactant pressure |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A fuel cell vehicle has a 100 kW PEMFC stack with 300 cells operating at an average cell voltage of 0.65 V. Compute (i) the stack current, (ii) the H₂ consumption rate in kg/h, and (iii) the range if the vehicle uses a 5 kg H₂ tank and the average traction power demand is 25 kW.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stack Current (2 marks):**

Stack voltage = N_cells × V_cell = 300 × 0.65 = 195 V

$$I = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{V_{\text{stack}}} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{195} = 512.82 \; \text{A}$$

**(ii) H₂ Consumption at Full Power (2.5 marks):**

Using Faraday's Law:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot I}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{(2.016 \times 10^{-3})(512.82)}{(2)(96{,}485)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{1.0337}{192{,}970} = 5.357 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 5.357 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 0.01929 \; \text{kg/h at 100 kW}$$

H₂ consumption rate per kW:

$$r_{specific} = \dfrac{0.01929}{100} = 1.929 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kg/h per kW}$$

**(iii) Range Calculation (2.5 marks):**

At 25 kW average demand, H₂ consumption rate:

$$\dot{m}_{25kW} = 25 \times 1.929 \times 10^{-4} = 4.822 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/h}$$

Total driving time:

$$t = \dfrac{5 \; \text{kg}}{4.822 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/h}} = 1036.9 \; \text{hours}$$

Assuming average speed of 50 km/h:

$$\boxed{\text{Range} = 1036.9 \times 50 \approx 51{,}845 \; \text{km (idealised, neglecting auxiliaries)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world range is ~500–650 km** because auxiliary loads (HVAC, lighting, electronics) consume 30–40 % of fuel cell output, and transient driving cycles (acceleration, idling) reduce effective fuel utilisation. Examiners expect students to acknowledge this discrepancy in the model answer.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Fuel Cell Vehicle questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FCV with BEV (2-mark loss):** FCV *generates* electricity onboard; BEV *stores* electricity. If the question asks "energy source," write **hydrogen**, not battery.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the battery/supercapacitor (2-mark loss):** Modern FCVs are always hybridised. Pure fuel cell-only vehicles do not exist commercially. Always mention the ESS buffer.
>
> 3. **Wrong standard voltage (2-mark loss):** The reversible voltage E⁰ = **1.229 V** at 25 °C. Do not write 1.5 V or 2.0 V.
>
> 4. **Skipping Carnot comparison (2-mark loss):** When asked "Why FC > ICE efficiency," you *must* state that fuel cells bypass the thermal-to-mechanical stage and thus evade the Carnot ceiling. Without this statement, you lose marks even if your numerical efficiency comparison is correct.
>
> 5. **Ignoring "green hydrogen" caveat (1-mark loss):** Do not claim FCVs are unconditionally zero-emission. Hydrogen production method (SMR vs. electrolysis) determines upstream emissions. Phrase it as: *"tailpipe zero-emission; well-to-wheel emissions depend on H₂ production pathway."*
>
> 6. **Unit errors in numerical problems (3-mark loss):** Faraday's law gives mass in kg if M_H₂ is in kg/mol (2.016 × 10⁻³). Using g/mol gives grams. Watch units carefully.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the Carnot reference temperature (1-mark loss):** Temperatures in Carnot formula must be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and review 24 hours before the exam. Each bullet is a board-favourite trigger phrase.**

### ✅ Must-Memorise Numerical Values
- E⁰ (H₂/O₂ cell, 25 °C) = **1.229 V**
- ΔG⁰ (H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, liquid) = **−237.2 kJ/mol**
- ΔH_LHV = **−241.8 kJ/mol** | ΔH_HHV = **−285.8 kJ/mol**
- Faraday's constant **F = 96,485 C/mol**
- Universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- Real-world PEMFC operating voltage = **0.6–0.7 V/cell**
- η_ideal_HHV = **83 %**, η_ideal_LHV = **98.1 %**
- Pt catalyst loading (2024) ≈ **0.125 mg/cm²**
- H₂ tank pressure (automotive) = **350 bar (CHIC) or 700 bar (Type IV)**
- Toyota Mirai 2nd gen range = **650 km**
- Hyundai Nexo range = **666 km**

### ✅ Must-Know Concepts
- [ ] FCV = energy **converter**, BEV = energy **storage** — fundamental distinction.
- [ ] PEMFC is preferred over SOFC for automotive because of **low-temperature fast cold-start**.
- [ ] Fuel cell bypasses Carnot limit by avoiding thermal-to-mechanical conversion.
- [ ] Three voltage losses: **activation, ohmic, concentration**.
- [ ] Hybridisation is essential — battery/supercapacitor handles transients.
- [ ] H₂ currently produced by **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** — generates CO₂; only **green H₂** (renewable electrolysis) is truly zero-emission.
- [ ] **Nafion™ membrane** is the proton-conducting electrolyte.
- [ ] **Bipolar plates** serve dual role: current conduction + gas distribution.
- [ ] **Type IV composite tanks** (carbon-fibre wrapped polymer liner) are the standard automotive H₂ storage.
- [ ] Water management is critical — flooding and drying both degrade performance.

### ✅ High-Yield Formula Triggers (KTU Board Pattern)
- *"Derive the open-circuit voltage"* → use ΔG = −nFE
- *"Calculate EMF at non-standard conditions"* → use Nernst equation
- *"Compute H₂ consumption rate"* → use Faraday's Law with M_H₂ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol
- *"Compare efficiency with ICE"* → state Carnot bypass, quote η_ideal_HHV = 83 %
- *"Explain voltage losses"* → activation + ohmic + concentration, draw polarisation curve
- *"Discuss hydrogen storage"* → mention 350 bar vs 700 bar, Type IV tanks, energy density limitations
